### Post by pbpersson on 2008-05-17
The reboots and lockups.  I am using Kubuntu Gutsy Gibbon and they are few and far between but they are still annoying.

On the lockups when the mouse is still working I guess I could restart XServer but I can never remember the key combination.

However, the last time this happened I was in Firefox typing a post in this forum, I clicked on "preview post" and the machine locked up so hard even the mouse was dead.

On the plus side, when Kubuntu restarted all my data was saved, including the post I was working on - that was very impressive.  :)

### Post by bash on 2008-05-17
I don't hate anything about Ubuntu. But I have some things that I think could be improved or to use a fancy marketing term: optimized. Although some are just things upstream needs to fix. Like tabbed nautilus, saving sessions (both coming in GNOME 2.24), the still shaky suspend/hibernate support. And some little cosmetic things. Like why isn't automatically your theme also the root theme. You can easily set this through symbolic links. But for users that don't know it, they might wonder why all the "sudo" applications are in ugly grey.

### Post by DoctorMO on 2008-05-17

### Post by quanumphaze on 2008-05-17
I suppose I hate that Nautilus doesn't support different wallpapers per desktop and I have to disable it from drawing the desktop and let Compiz handle it consequentially loosing desktop icons. But there was some patch where Nautilus must be recompiled but it was for the old version before 2.22.
This is mostly Gnome's fault and should have been implemented years ago (even if it didn't work with Compiz).

Then there are just the bugs which are not really something to hate the whole distro for:
Pulseaudio and Flash don't get along but I don't know who's fault that is.
The ath-hal (or related) module occasionally locks up my system but that's not Ubuntu's fault.

Not really much to hate, just the little annoyances.

### Post by Npl on 2008-05-17
Its way to easy to shoot yourself in the foot. For example I got 1 couple NTFS-Partitions which aint get mounted automatically, you can mount them by clicking on them in Nautilus - then, if you open the properties you can edit the mountpoint (actually theres no description or help what it does exactly). I did so on one drive with the result that I was unable to mount it and as the property-dialog is only available when mounted you are unable to fix it the same way. Only way out was to search around in the registry for a key that sounded like it was responsible.

Which brings me to the next point: Documentation/Help is totally poor (the only thing thats documented good is commandline-stuff) and way worse if you have a different language than english selected.

And another point: Inconsistent configuration. you set a mountpoint in device-properties? That setting goes in Gnome-Registry and NOT in /etc/fstab.
Trying to disable the Standby-Button? - well, there are config-files that allow to disable ACPI-Sleep (=Standby), and there are scripts which appear to be called when you press the Button. Both are documented and should do what I wanted but both have no effect if Gnome is running (the setting is once more in the registry, and since a couple Versions you can also alter that setting manually - but that wasnt possible when I tried to disable it).

If you force install on a Package it typically breaks PAckage-Management bad enough so you cant run updates or do much stuff with apt-get, except running apt-get install -f and get your package removed again.

### Post by xtreme- on 2008-05-17
> **EdThaSlayer said:**
> No encryption...by default? It's not even that vital though, and I heard Ubuntu 8.04 can do that so.

Support for encrypting root/home/swap partition is included in the alternate install cd from version 7.10. I have it set up, and it asks for password immediately after booting - real smooth. I'm quite certain that it's included in 8.04 also (you may need the alternate cd, though).

Additionally, I have created a LUKS-partition (encrypted partition) on an external hard drive, and each time I plug it in, I get asked for the password (a dialog box pops up).

So I think encrypting disks is very well supported.:)

### Post by jrusso2 on 2008-05-17
Things I dislike about Ubuntu.

1. The use of meta packages.  This makes it hard to uninstall certain apps without uninstalling the whole gnome desktop.  Its also bad from a resource use stand point and slows Ubuntu down as it loads unnecessary things into memory.

2.  The free software only stance makes it difficult for people who are new users.  It should be a stance like we will use free software wherever possible but not to the point to inconvenience the users.

3.  Six month release cycle means buggy software releases.  You see this every time there is a new release. Instead of doing six months releases they should do yearly and upgrade the current distro better as far as newer apps and even newer kernel where needed.

4. Ugly Brown theme, nuff said.

---

### Post by Bungo Pony on 2008-05-17
- Pulse Audio. I'm still having a horrible time getting my inputs and outputs to work properly. The only time everything works *somewhat* correctly is when I have Jack audio control running.

- The lockups. On some boots, I get really awful temporary lockups when I do something. I'm really hoping that this gets fixed in the near future.

- Getting the serial port working. I've had lots of trouble getting Minicom to talk with other computers, just because Ubuntu doesn't want me using my serial port for anything useful.

Hardy is probably going to be my last version of Ubuntu. I was playing around with PCLinuxOS a few months ago, and no matter how much crap I added to it, it remained rock solid. I went with Hardy with the hope that it would be just as good as Feisty. It's way better than Gutsy, but not as good as Feisty was.

### Post by diablo75 on 2008-05-17
I thought about starting a thread like this, but decided not to because of how many people would reply with this same useless answer:  "Nothing!  I like it just the way it is."  Really?  Well, shut up for a second because the OP isn't interested in your "perfect" system configuration, nor does he want some fanboy telling him his perspective on things is what's out of alignment.  He's looking for others to vent about things that need polishing, and that's a good thing in the end.  It helps to bring attention to Ubuntu's weak spots.

So here goes my rant:  I've said this again and again, but I don't like Pulse Audio.  Sometimes it causes more problems than it solves, and it's delivered to you via an upgrade to 8.04, regardless of whether or not you actually need it.  Tweaking it to work right often requires "sudo gedit" to be involved at some point, but it still isn't glitch-free.  Pulse Audio kind of makes 8.04 feel like its still in Beta, in my opinion.

Something else I don't like is the whole concept of Software Sources being placed on the shoulders of new users.  New users don't care where it comes from, as long as it works.  Have you ever found yourself having to manually change your source to be a different server because the Main server (or whatever one it was pointing at) wouldn't respond or wouldn't send all the files update manager requested?  Nobody wants to deal with that; mirror servers should be sought out automatically without the need for manual intervention to get things rolling again.  I've submitted an [idea]("http://brainstorm.ubuntu.com/idea/8376/") about this on Ubuntu Brainstorm already.

Another thing I hate is my numberpad switching into Mouse-Keys mode and nothing appearing on the screen to notify me of this sudden change or why it happened in the first place.

I have high expectations for 8.10, namely a new, sexy desktop theme that will appeal to more new users than before (the current default theme is, in a word: boring).  Pulse Audio will need to be glitch-free, auto-detect and support more than 2 speakers out of the box without the need to manually edit a config file as well as have a built in global (system-wide) equalizer.

That's all for now.

### Post by Sinkingships7 on 2008-05-17
I used to have tons of issues with Ubuntu, and then I saw the light. Turns out, doing a minimal command-line install and then adding things like the GDM and window manager and stuff makes Ubuntu a LOT more stable than a normal install from the CD. I've been thinking about writing a guide to doing a minimal install and working your way to customization. It's a lot easier than it seems, and I think it might clear up a lot of some people's problems.

That being said, I do have one problem, and it's a kernel problem so doing a minimal install won't fix it...

after resuming from hibernation, sound doesn't work. And the only way to get it to work again is to reboot. If your someone who's like me and is a sucker for the 'uptime' command in the terminal, this is quite an issue...

### Post by init1 on 2008-05-18
Heh I find it funny how so many people say they don't hate anything about Ubuntu. Here's a list of my complaints: 

**Automount**
If I want to mount a device, I'll run the command. This wouldn't be such a big deal if they would just have an option in the "Removable Storage" properties window that allowed one to disable it. There is a way to do it, but it's rather unintuitive and took some searching around.  

**Brightness**
Hardy changes my brightness during shutdown and startup, and it really annoys me. I see no practical reason why it would do that. 

**GDM Font**
The font size inside the user name and password field for GDM is huge and almost unreadable. 

**Flash Plugin**
Neither swfdec nor Gnash work correctly for Youtube, and the official adobe plugin still makes Firefox crash (less often than Debian Etch though)

### Post by quinnten83 on 2008-05-18
> **DoctorMO said:**
> I have plenty to vent about evolution-data-service (EDS), lots of vitriol for kde-pim and not very nice things to say about thunderbird. Linux and by extension Ubuntu is the worst software for managing addresses, contacts, phone numbers, notes, emails, events and other meta information. It's so badly thought out, so badly hodge podged and left to upstreams who only care about beating the other guys with their private format wars.

I'm not fond of PulseAudio, Alsa, OSS or ESD either. Linux sound is immature and the play thing of geeks instead of laymen and musicians.

The pidgin developers, what more can you say. A bad example of software developers, rude and stubborn at least in my experience with them.


< Major rant warning, not just about Ubuntu but Linux ecosphere in general>

+1 on the evolution thing. It is still very buggy. The only reason I use it is because it is integrated. I hate the fact that even though it is announced, my google calendar does not work well in evolution on hardy. I really wanted that feature. I really wanted to have my calendar with me at all times.... Why does it not work?????

My gripes are mostly application related, not so much with Ubuntu itself. In ubuntu I hate that you can't easily (read graphically) blacklist an on board sound card and that your peripherals all have cryptic names. i still haven't been able to figure out which is my web cam and which is the web cam's microphone. 

I also hate it,that programs are not updated as soon as upstream releases a stable candidate. I understand they have to test things out, but sometimes you just wonder why it is taking sooo long for some things to get updated.

Also sometimes the design of some of the software we use is just really really bad. Or that the same software on windows, seems to have a better layout than on linux, why??? I hate the fact that there is little accountability for software makers on linux. Yes I can email them to request features or whatnot, but if they don't want to help, then tough luck. Have a look at the whole funpidgin fork to see what I mean.

I also hate the fact that there sometimes is no good alternative to software I need. perosnally I need a good Visio clone. But Kivio and Dia look hopelessly outdated and the icons in the templates they use are just plain ugly. OO.o draw would have been a good candidate, but they don't support the templates I need. If they do,then I haven't found the option to turn this on.

What I also hate is that documentation for software is appalling. Even for Ubuntu there is a lot of irrelevant documentation on the website. Try having to wade through Google to find information on the release that you need and not something written for hoary or warty and therefore pretty much obsolete. I don't think canonical realizes that this gets worse every six months when a new version is released. 
Manuals are non-existent sometimes or so badly written that a non native English speaker has to get a dictionary for every other word and still he manages to not understand what the writer of the manual is trying to say. Not to mention the huge amounts of l337 5p34k and geek jargon. I am only half geek, so I am not really that good at understanding all this. I imagine this to be an dyslexics nightmare.

The thing is I realize most of this is not Ubuntus fault, but it is driving me crazy. I love this OS and I love the FLOSS culture, but sometimes it feels like an uphill struggle to get things to change in upstream, and since I am not a coder I can't change it myself....

I really whish this would change...

<end rant>

### Post by Sprax on 2008-05-18
I've been using ubuntu for a few hours now and so far I've got the following:

1. Ubuntu is using the wrong soundcard to output sound. Changing the settings in alsamixer, system -> pref -> sound, picking the correct card as default in /etc/modprobe.d/alsa-base and disabling the wrong won't help.

2. Pressing hibernate hangs the computer. There is no way to resume.

3. Changing the refresh rate of the screen hangs the computer. I guess it should reset after about 30s but it doesn't.

4. Rebooting from live cd causes the computer to hang instead of rebooting.

5. Installing the "correct" (?) ATI drivers ruins video making it pixelated. 

Unfortunately vista is no better. For one thing it's not compatible with my NIC (is this just a way of forcing users to upgrade?). There are definaely things I like about ubuntu. But... sigh.

### Post by daengbo on 2008-05-18
Top 10 things I hate about Ubuntu (Hardy)

10) Rhythmbox ships with plugins that don't work as configured.

9) Network failure for retrieving my Google calendar (in the clock) hangs the entire Gnome panel until I kill it.

8 ) gvfs doesn't mount servers correctly anymore.
7) gvfs fails to umount a yanked removable drive, leaving the entry in Nautilus and creating a duplicate when it is reinserted.

6) 64 bit is a second class citizen.

5) Canonical decided to make the choice between Flash breaking audio or libflashsupport crashing FF.

4) Hardy came with a beta web browser.

3) The default photo application doesn't work in 64 bit.

2) The bug-fixing attitude

And the #1 thing I hate about Hardy is ...
1) that Canonical though it was more important to ship their LTS on time than to have it actually work well.

Linux and by extension Ubuntu is the worst software for managing addresses, contacts, phone numbers, notes, emails, events and other meta information. ### Post by the8thstar on 2008-05-18
I keep Windows to do what Ubuntu can't:

1. Run Office 2007. I gave it several tries in Ubuntu with Wine, without success unfortunately. Therefore I use OO in Ubuntu, which I must say I'm very happy with.

2. Run Google Earth 4.3, as the same version doesn't work in Ubuntu. I'm forced to use v4.2 in Ubuntu, that is quite a bummer because I don't know why 4.3 fails.

3. Run other software, to do my taxes or to play games that don't run in Wine, etc. It's not Ubuntu's fault of course, but I don't like that.

4. Stream media on the Internet. All the how-tos I've tried for Ubuntu fail at some point. There are streams I just can't get. I guess Windows has better codecs. At least they all WORK.

5. Play DVDs. In some cases, the encryption cannot be forced and some DVDs won't play in Ubuntu. They all play in Windows.

### Post by karellen on 2008-05-18
saying I hate something would be too harsh, but what I profoundly dislike is this:
> Once installed your system is immediately ready-to-use. On the desktop you have a full set of productivity, internet, drawing and graphics applications, and games. from [http://www.ubuntu.com/products/whatisubuntu](http://www.ubuntu.com/products/whatisubuntu)
and this
> Ubuntu 8.04 Long Term Support (LTS) provides a stable **platform **for software and hardware vendors, developers and users. With three years of support and maintenance on the desktop, 8.04 LTS is a great choice for large-scale deployment.
and more > “Ubuntu 8.04 LTS Desktop Edition is a very significant release as it will take Ubuntu squarely into the **business environment**", both from [http://www.ubuntu.com/news/ubuntu-8.04-lts-desktop](http://www.ubuntu.com/news/ubuntu-8.04-lts-desktop)
if it's stable just on some machines (even if it counts for 80% of them), then it's not stable enough for business. if a graphic card is not recognized out of the box, then it's not immediately ready to use...
don't get me wrong, I have no problems with getting my hands dirty (after all configuring Fedora after installation is more time consuming than Ubuntu) but I don't like this deceiving marketing. I don't expect that all things to work out of the box and every piece of hardware to be fully supported, but stop pretending otherwise. at least write some warning/caution note "some extra tweaking may be required after installation" or "check to see if you hardware gets on well with Ubuntu"...
just my inner thoughts

### Post by Peterix on 2008-05-18
The way xorg.conf is set up after install. Every (FSCKING EVERY) install I did had bad pre-set monitor settings.
Sometimes this prevents even the LiveCD from booting into GUI.

After install
```
Section "Monitor"
	Identifier "whatever"
        HorizSync    SOME_FSCKING_MAGIC_NUMBERS
        VertRefresh  SOME_OTHER_FSCKING_MAGIC_NUMBERS
	Option "DPMS" "true"
EndSection
```
What I end up doing:
```
Section "Monitor"
	Identifier "whatever"
	Option "DPMS" "true"
EndSection
```

And then things start to work again. Overall, my main gripe with Ubuntu is badly done autoconfiguration (in wrong places), apt breakage (DON'T INSTALL GFORGE) and various little things.
FAIL BulletProofX that NEVER works.
That network applet thing that won't let me use VPN with a static IP address.
And it's slow. Being an i386 distro, it's a bit more compatible with old machines, but it misses a lot of the optimizations -- i686 is better in this regard.
That's why I switched to Arch after a year of using Ubuntu. I need to know more about the system, the install was way more complicated, but at least there's no config 'stuff' getting in my way.

---

### Post by doobydave on 2008-05-18
Though I have been affected by a fair few bugs, this is to be expected. Bugs happen. This only becomes a nuisance if they don't get fixed.

I seems to me that having a six-monthly release cycle is giving the developers quite a lot of work, and possibly, dare I say, overwhelming them?

I am not sure how the people who make the decisions think that a six-monthly release is suitable for the target consumers who at the end of the day require stability and predictability over anything else.

Recently, when I've been selecting a version of Ubuntu to install, it's been a choice of staying in my comfort zone with the well-established bugs or the excitement of experiencing new ones.

I'm currently looking at other distros too. Whether I'll find a better one, who knows? But will have a good look.

### Post by linuxlizard on 2008-05-19
Well, I'm a long time fan of ubuntu going back to breezy badger. The only thing I've hated about ubuntu has been the release of hardy heron.

I've installed hardy 4 times on exactly the same machine and had different problems each time. It's like playing the lotto- will the next install be a big winner and have fewer problems or more, some will be the same and some will be different.

Not only that, but get this- After battling with it some and winning some things and getting them working and loosing some things and just living with it for the next 5 or 6 months until the next release comes out, I set up my 2 sons user accounts. So now I've got 3 user accounts on this machine. Believe it or not- we've got different problems in each user account, and different levels of compatibility with some features like the drive mounter and compiz- One of my sons accounts compiz works with everything, on the other two accounts we have to turn it off for anything that goes full screen. Another has smooth dvd playback and the other two accounts do not- it stalls for 1/4 second every few seconds and eventually crashes. One of my sons the middle click in firefox doesn't work to open new tabs, it works fine in the other two, and there is a list of several other things that work differently in each of our account, even though we all have the exact same permissions(!) and this is exactly one machine (!)

Hardy is a crap shoot.

Looks beautiful, is very fast, but from reading I think they used a bad choices that were unstable for firefox, the kernal, and x, and the latter prevented nvidia drivers from working properly some of the time which of course leads to compiz problems and probably problems elsewhere for anything using the driver, such as anything running on x itself. Not to mention the buggy kernal version and the beta testing version firefox, which is constantly used on most users machines and has forced us all to become beta testers.

Hardy is full of gremlins on this machine which has worked smoothly and problem free since breezy...

No big deal as long as the next release uses a more stable base structure and hopefully we'll be back to ubuntu love as usual. If not, oh well, I guess I'll get back to the distro hopping days I did for years before breezy.

---

### Post by Keyper7 on 2008-05-19
> **karellen said:**

 from [http://www.ubuntu.com/products/whatisubuntu](http://www.ubuntu.com/products/whatisubuntu)
and this

and more , both from [http://www.ubuntu.com/news/ubuntu-8.04-lts-desktop](http://www.ubuntu.com/news/ubuntu-8.04-lts-desktop)
if it's stable just on some machines (even if it counts for 80% of them), then it's not stable enough for business. if a graphic card is not recognized out of the box, then it's not immediately ready to use...
don't get me wrong, I have no problems with getting my hands dirty (after all configuring Fedora after installation is more time consuming than Ubuntu) but I don't like this deceiving marketing. I don't expect that all things to work out of the box and every piece of hardware to be fully supported, but stop pretending otherwise. at least write some warning/caution note "some extra tweaking may be required after installation" or "check to see if you hardware gets on well with Ubuntu"...
just my inner thoughts

Something stated in the advertisement of a product does not apply to every conceivable situation in reality. News at eleven.

In related news, the milk cart I'm holding right now states that milk is full of benefits for your health and does not state anywhere that people with intolerance to lactosis might die if they drink it. Police is considering the arrest of evil milk vendors for deceiving marketing.

We'll be right back after the commercials.

### Post by Franko30 on 2008-05-23
I hate:

pulseaudio

It drives me crazy - doesn't keep any volume settings (drumroll at full blast when logging in - try that on a train...), doesn't survive a hibernate - and all this on a laptop that was working perfectly with a 7.10 install.

### Post by sebbouckaert on 2008-05-25
> **Franko30 said:**
> I hate:

pulseaudio

It drives me crazy - doesn't keep any volume settings (drumroll at full blast when logging in - try that on a train...), doesn't survive a hibernate - and all this on a laptop that was working perfectly with a 7.10 install.

Why did I even bother with a fresh install of 8.04?

Franko30

You could try to select ALSA in audio preferences, then go to System > preferences > sessions > add (then give a name something like kill pulseaudio) and as command enter *killall pulseaudio*. It will kill pulseaudio on next reboot. For me that worked well, my audio system behaves like Gutsy again. 

You could also roll back to a full gutsy install, of course ;)

### Post by ben2talk on 2008-05-26
I hate that I can't talk to my computer and have it understand and answer me, I hate that I can't communicate by using my hands, I have to use a mouse - I like using the keyboard but it's still rather clumbsy. Mouse is also very clumbsy, I'd prefer to use a pen but it's rather expensive.

I hate that it doesn't read my mind and am rather upset that all the software for the 'default' windows can't run natively on ubuntu. 

I hate that people are so ignorant, and say 'what Internet Explorer are you using?' and when you say you're not using Windows, they glaze over and walk away... I hate that everyone in the world is mostly interested in eyecandy, and really have not much idea why they spent so much money on it. 

In truth, I love anything that runs on my computer, but it's not perfect and never can be. Best focus on the benefits - mostly for me in my school, my computer is always useable. Windows degrades as the year progresses, but it's necessary for running the software that is written for it... but it can't connect to internet because I refused to let them install the drivers for the wireless device! Ubuntu had no problem with the ZyXEL device, so internet is safe for me.

I like my gnome desktop, but have no trouble with KDE if you prefer it.... If I want to do some work, gnome seems easier. I have no menu bars, I just use launcher shortcuts ALT+ F1/F2/F3, terminal ALT+T, and so on... Menu's are outdated,but needed for times that you don't remember what you need. Imagine Synaptic running a menu system (maybe a few hundred pages of information to search manually).

Mostly I hate the people who talk crap about it all.

Who read this? I must confess, I didn't bother to read most of the other posts on here either.

The rest about Ubuntu is awesome: it's fast, reliable and secure. I will never pay for windows software again!

### Post by 2hot6ft2 on 2008-05-26
I don't hate anything about Ubuntu (other than maybe the wallpaper). There are a few things that I wish worked a little better but sometimes that's not the Distros fault.

Printer compatibility. It's ok with my HP 3900 but doesn't play well with the Epson or Lexmark. I haven't had time to really try and make it work with the Epson and from what I've read forget about the Lexmark.

Have to agree with others on rushing a new buggy Distro out. I don't do beta testing for windblows and don't want to do it with Linux. At least not until I get more experienced with it. For the good of the community.

I guess I have been very fortunate and have had only a few minor issues using Ubuntu. Some of which are being looked into like the Fn keys on the Vaio Laptops.

Stability is paramount!!! Followed by security.

I would like to see the repositories updated a little more when a more stable version of an app. is released. Not just when a more secure version is released.

I must have tried over 20 Linux distros before deciding to go with Ubuntu.
I dual and multi boot all my pc's with xp and at least one flavor of Linux and when the day comes that I can do the same things on Linux that I can on windblows I'll be more than happy to reformat their partitions.

Until then keep up the good work and please focus a little more on making it a rock solid distro that I won't regret introducing people to.

I already put it on my Stepdaughters pc.
Will be putting it on my Nieces and on her old mans pc's
Would have put it on my Moms if it would have installed
And have yet to talk my Brother into installing it. But I will.

I am considered their tech. support since I have been messing with pc's since the early 1980's back before DOS 4.0 and Windows 3.1 so stability is extremely important to me.

Priorities should include:
1.Stability
2.Security
3.Compatibility
4.Usability
5.Flexibility

After that comes the eye candy which Ubuntu already has plenty of.
1 new release a year should be plenty. A Beta then at least 2 Months for all the testers to make it stable is a lot more realistic than every 6 Months.

How many updates are there to the original Hardy 8.04 release? Close to 200 yet? Then it's time to re-release the livecd with the bug fixes already applied.

I for one would be happy to torrent them so they could be distributed faster and I'm sure others would too. Torrenting a buggy Distro is like sharing misery with others. Doesn't sound like the definition of Ubuntu to me.

There it is for what it's worth. And like I said keep up the good work.

### Post by dapps2k on 2008-06-10
What I hate?

I hate trying to configure the system using xorg.conf and having killed my system countless times, the drivers that are missing, having to hack files to make them do what I want them to do, and random, inconsistent crashes.

A problem I have with open-source projects in general is that most of these projects are done solely with programmers who are working on their own free time.  Obviously development depends on how much time they can spend working on the project.  Most of the time it's not user-friendly nor good to look at, but they don't exactly have a huge team working on those aspects of the program who are also getting paid to do it. 

Don't get me wrong, I like the OS.  In fact, I find it really impressive that they've got a free OS working with a lot of hardware support with free, alternative software from a bunch of programmers who are doing all of this on their own free time.

### Post by Tuxoid on 2008-06-10

### Post by jukingeo on 2008-06-19
I only been on Ubuntu for about a month now.  I have been using it more than Windows now and overall I do like the operating system.

However, I am a fan of gaming and also I am heavy into audio recording work.  I do some video work but not much.

So the key word there is "audio".

Ubuntu seems to have MAJOR issues with sound.  More so than video.  It seems like development is WAY behind when it comes to sound.  Now I am not saying the applications (programs) are being, but the actual method of getting those programs to interact with sound cards.

It seems like the most "flexible" sound card is the Sound Blaster Live.   I have the Sound Blaster X-Fi which is at least 4 Sound Blaster devices ahead of Live.

As of now there seems to be a switch over in the preferred driving method two.  So far the choices are ALSA and OSS.   OSS works with the X-Fi but it is very limited.  ALSA supposedly offers more, but it is nearly impossible to get operating.

Another thing that I don't like about the sound is that if you configure one thing, you can knock out something else.  For example.   When I initially installed OSS, I couldn't get any sound from my games whether it be Linux or an emulator (Dos, NES, Atari 2600, GENS, etc).  However, I did have sound with Totem and streaming audio on the internet.

There was a fix for this though, and while I subsequently had sound for my games, it knocked out my streaming internet audio and it made streaming video very choppy.   So there was a fix for this, but I found out too late that the fix was for ALSA only...which my card will not do.  So I ended up knocking out my sound completely.

After taking everything sound related off my machine and starting over (now with a never updated version of OSS), I have sound again...but only partially.   My internet sound still doesn't work and none of my Ubuntu Studio programs are working well.

As of now it seems like Ubuntu Studio mostly relies on a JACKrack configuration and my goal is to somehow get JACK to talk to OSS.  Then I can work with my Ubuntu Studio applications.

So far my experiences here and with Ubuntu are very good.  Other problems I had usually had a one or two postings here and I had a fix for them and the fixes were good (mainly video issues and system configuration).  However, for over a month now, I cannot get everything working with sound.  I have to 'hunt' for the right people to talk to and once I do have a solution, I am worried that any alterations I do will knock something else out.

So since I am such a BIG fan of audio production and I do like my games, I am second guessing Ubuntu for it's sound handling.

In fact I am thinking about using a different variant of Linux that is better suited for audio production.  I hope I don't have to considering that Ubuntu is the best supported Linux variant.  But as of now, the problems with getting sound to work is very difficult.

I can understand if I had an "offbeat" sound device, such as my other unit, an Alesis IO-26 which is a firewire device.  But the Sound Blaster X-Fi is a very well known high end sound card.   Many gamers and people into multi-media systems use this card.  So I am surprised that considering the length of time this card has been out that no one has reversed engineered the thing and written a good Linux driver for it (granted, I guess that IS easier said than done).

So overall, I guess when it comes to audio production, for now I am going to stick with my Ablton/Alesis setup in Windows XP.

But in terms of everything else; surfing with Firefox, using Evolution for Emails and using Open Office for documents, Ubuntu gets two thumbs up there.  In fact overall Ubuntu feels like a 'nicer' and less bloated version of Windows.   Sure there is a learning curve with somethings but once you get the hang of it, you are good to go.

But let me warn those that want to work with sound and you don't have a Sound Blaster Live card...you are in for one rough road to travel on.

Geo

### Post by igoddard on 2008-06-29
This is a general Linux issue, not just Ubuntu.

What do we complain about in Windows? - DLL hell.  ISTM that Linux has achieved exactly that.

The core libc libraries have a lack of backward compatibility.  If you need to run an app compiled in an older kernel version it may well fail on you due to that lack.  The LD_ASSUME_KERNEL hack might well have fixed that back in Dapper.  It certainly doesn't under Hardy because the back-compatibility libraries are gone.

There's also what I call a lack of sideways compatibility.  Several releases of Informix server, compiled on RH, crash on Debian derivatives.  The problem seems to be that the RH pthread library has an entry point which isn't in the Debian and that those releases call it.  Either one version fails to meet the library's spec or else the spec itself is too lax.

This means that I'm locked out of those versions of Informix.  The backward compatibility issue breaks Kylix and maybe other stuff that I've not discovered yet.

At this point no doubt some readers will be reaching for their keyboards to say I wouldn't have this problem if I stuck to open source S/W.  If you're one just consider this before you start typing:  What else do we complain about in Windows? - user lock-in.  And you're just about to propose locking me into open source!  There are a few other aspects to this.

In the case of Informix even if I had the source I'd still have to hack it to work around the call to a non-existent entry point.  And if I were using it to run a major business app (and that's the sort of thing which Informix is used for) I'd (a) be obliged to do an enormous amount of testing to convince myself that I hadn't broken it and (b) quite possibly be hung out to dry by an auditor if he discovered I'd hacked a fundamental component of the system.

In the case of Kylix there really isn't any open source alternative that I'm aware of.  Lazarus might get there but at the moment it doesn't incorporate one of Kylix's (& Delphi's) best tricks, the ability to treat a form and its associated code as a base class from which to develop variants.  So right now I have to have W2K to run Delphi if I want to write a GUI app for my own use; I wish I didn't but the other IDEs I've looked at seem too primitive to bother with in comparison.

If you're still with me there's also the problem of inconsistent support for H/W.  It's not just a question of checking what's supported before you go out to buy it.  An upgrade or even a routine update will break what was previously working.  I switched to Ubuntu from another distro because a routine update broke my wireless.  Twice I've had Ubuntu upgrades (Feisty and Hardy) break support for my camera.

Bottom line: updates and upgrades shouldn't break working S/W or H/W.  We expect breaks on Windows and complain about it.  But we shouldn't get them on Linux if it really is better.

Ian

---

### Post by UncleFoo on 2008-06-30
There is a timely article in linux.com titled "Ten sticking points for new Ubuntu users"

[http://www.linux.com/feature/139214?](http://www.linux.com/feature/139214?)

It mirrors my experiences with what is otherwise a very good Distro. Here is my comment to that article;

"I have been using Ubuntu for just over a year at work and at home. I just switched to Suse 11 at home because of the sound and video issues. My requirements at work are pretty basic so 1025x768 is fine and I can live without sound (but why can't I get sound?).

At home however my kids watch videos & play games, so I have a some decent speakers and and NVidia 7600GS video card. The sound issues noted in the article are dead on, either no sound, or sound on one application only (log out if you want to listen to Rhythmbox after watching a video). And video just keeps getting worse and worse. I was afraid to do updates because it kept killing my video card (640x480 in a 19' monitor indeed), and getting the drivers to work again is a exercise in patience. It is hard to be a Linux advocate when you are spending countless hours just trying to make it work.

Happily Suse 11 fixed everything out of the box. I was the easiest OS install I have ever done (windows or Linux), and everything just worked.

I like Ubuntu, and I like the inroads that it has made, but I think I will wait a few more releases before I try it again."

### Post by phidia on 2008-07-03
"Hate" is a little too strong a word. I see myself always supporting and using linux & ubuntu in some form. 
I am fairly disappointed in the uneven and haphazard support for laptops in ubuntu & linux generally. Someone is sure to point out the there are plenty of howtos, guides and the laptop testing page. 
The problem I've discovered, after have used those resources, is that **"it works, but"** is implied in all that. In my own experiment with using linux on a laptop I found too many time consuming and overlapping troubles with sound, suspend-resume-hibernate, and networking. While there are recent madwifi solutions for the specific network card the performance of madwifi tested over many days was worst than dismal and ndiswrapper works well but conflicts with suspend. The sound problems can be corrected by building alsa from source and rebuilding with each kernel update. 
So I think you can get the picture from all that. I am really unlikely to consider using linux on a laptop again anytime soon.

### Post by Tomatz on 2008-07-03
> **lil_elvis2000 said:**
> is there a way to remove GRUB if ubuntu is the only OS on the system?



Just change the timeout to **0** in the grub config file:

```
sudo gedit /boot/grub/menu.lst
```

(i recommend you change it to **1** second as you may need recovery mode :))

### Post by igoddard on 2008-07-10
> **Tomatz said:**
> Just:

```
sudo su
```

It has the same effect...

A lot of old-time sysadmins like myself consider the Ubuntu way of doing things retrograde.  It means that a normal user password gets access to root functionality.  If that password is compromised so is the entire system.  Having a separate root password means that two passwords need to be compromised; it's an extra layer of security (unless, of course, you allow root logins).  If you set a root password you could then set up another user ID for everyday use, disable the Ubuntu semi-root user ID and revert to something close to a traditional Unix security model.

This is not to ignore the advantages which sudo provides but the Ubuntu way of using it is less than ideal.

Ian

---

### Post by UncleFoo on 2008-07-10
> **igoddard said:**
> A lot of old-time sysadmins like myself consider the Ubuntu way of doing things retrograde.  It means that a normal user password gets access to root functionality.  If that password is compromised so is the entire system.  Having a separate root password means that two passwords need to be compromised; it's an extra layer of security (unless, of course, you allow root logins).  If you set a root password you could then set up another user ID for everyday use, disable the Ubuntu semi-root user ID and revert to something close to a traditional Unix security model.

This is not to ignore the advantages which sudo provides but the Ubuntu way of using it is less than ideal.

Ian

Yes, but you can limit the functionality that each user can use sudo for. In that respect I think it is an improvement over the old Unix model.

---

### Post by spacelem on 2008-07-11
Ubuntu really doesn't seem to like the command line, and it feels like little attention has been paid to making the command line feel polished (e.g. $PS1 colours).

I'm sure it's fine for new users, but I don't enjoy going into /etc/fstab, and seeing it configured in ways that need work before I can start adjusting it. I can't figure out where xorg.conf gets its values from, and whether they'll change change after I edit them.

/etc/grub/grub.conf (or menu.lst) seems to revert to its original setting every time there's a kernel upgrade, which is annoying since those settings don't boot on my machine.

It's been mentioned before, but sudo just seems dangerous, and I'd advocate removing it immediately. As soon as the normal user has been compromised, you have root access. Also seeing all these commands prefixed by sudo just seems odd.

### Post by Tomatz on 2008-07-11

> Ubuntu really doesn't seem to like the command line, and it feels like little attention has been paid to making the command line feel polished (e.g. $PS1 colours).

I'm sure it's fine for new users, but I don't enjoy going into /etc/fstab, and seeing it configured in ways that need work before I can start adjusting it. I can't figure out where xorg.conf gets its values from, and whether they'll change change after I edit them.

/etc/grub/grub.conf (or menu.lst) seems to revert to its original setting every time there's a kernel upgrade, which is annoying since those settings don't boot on my machine.

It's been mentioned before, but sudo just seems dangerous, and I'd advocate removing it immediately. As soon as the normal user has been compromised, you have root access. Also seeing all these commands prefixed by sudo just seems odd.

Are you serious?

### Post by UncleFoo on 2008-08-14
As for me 3 months later, after switching to Suse 11 at home, and using Gutsy at work, here are my impressions:

Suse:
Pros:
1. sound and graphics work flawlessly, no issues at all.
2. It's still Linux and is stable.

Cons:
1. They are absolutely paranoid about restricted formats & drivers. MP3 is a large pain in the butt, and don't even think about watching a MPAA DVD. You can really feel MicroSoft's influence here.
2. Their menu system is really clunky compared to Ubuntu (I'm using Gnome).

So, what to do? I much prefer the look/feel/experience with Ubuntu, but back when I decided to switch every update would kill my sound and graphics (really really nasty). In fact on my work machine (running Gutsy) I still don't have sound (no errors or any indication why either).

---

### Post by xouns on 2008-08-15
I started using Ubuntu (my first Linux) half a year ago, and since then I've had nothing but troubles. What bothers me is that all the sites and programs still treat you as if you are a high skilled linux user, and I had to learn for myself what the sudo command does, so to say. After half a year, this is the first freaking site I'm browsing on a Linux installation, and it took me 7 (!) reinstalls. But that was mostly Gutsy Gibbons fault, for being unable to recognize my WiFi adapter.

Maybe things will get better in the future, but still, I'm looking through a 800x600 resolution screen, because my graphics card refuses to be installed. I can see that Hardy is an improvement, but it's sill lacking...

edit: So, my graphics card seems to work (after going through four reinstallations of the same package). Sound is oke (bit crackly) and internet is working. This is a first for me, I'm looking at the screen in astonishment...

### Post by jukingeo on 2008-09-10
> **UncleFoo said:**
> As for me 3 months later, after switching to Suse 11 at home, and using Gutsy at work, here are my impressions:

Suse:
Pros:
1. sound and graphics work flawlessly, no issues at all.
2. It's still Linux and is stable.

Cons:
1. They are absolutely paranoid about restricted formats & drivers. MP3 is a large pain in the butt, and don't even think about watching a MPAA DVD. You can really feel MicroSoft's influence here.
2. Their menu system is really clunky compared to Ubuntu (I'm using Gnome).

So, what to do? I much prefer the look/feel/experience with Ubuntu, but back when I decided to switch every update would kill my sound and graphics (really really nasty). In fact on my work machine (running Gutsy) I still don't have sound (no errors or any indication why either).

What kind of sound device you have?

I have a similar story to yours except I tried 64Studio.  I found 64 Studio's file management to be a pain.  So I came back to Ubuntu.  However, it seems like unless you have a supported on board sound, a Soundblaster Live, an M-Audio card, or an RME Hammerfall card, nothing seems to work right.  Even ALSA supported devices will not work.   I specifically bought an ECHO Mona sound device after reading that ALSA supports it.  I installed it and not only found out that it isn't "automagically" recognized, but that it is a royal pain to get set up.

So things I like about Ubuntu...Quite a lot and there are too many things to list.

Things I hate about Ubuntu

NO GOOD SOUND SUPPORT FOR DAW APPLICATIONS!!!

So for DAW work either you settle for the cheapy M-Audio, or foot the $600 or more for one of the RME Hammerfall cards.  There is no middle road unfortunately.

I AM hoping things will get better once FFADO gets underway and perhaps I can experiment using Firewire devices with Jack.

So I hope the Ubuntu Devs are reading this:

FIX THE SOUND, MAKE ALL ALSA DEVICES AUTOMAGICALLY RECOGNIZED!!  GOT THAT?  GOOD!

### Post by UncleFoo on 2008-09-11
I just thought of another thing I like about Ubuntu (Vs Suse). The forums are way more user friendly with Ubuntu. If you Google a specific issue for Ubuntu the link take you to the entry in the forum that you are looking for. In Suse the link takes to the the Suse forum main page!!?? Then you need to search again within the forum (good luck).

Other things, but with Linux in general are the tools available for simple tasks. For example PDF manipulation, using pdftk and Gambas I can toss together some very powerful custom tools very quickly (for free). Try that with windows. Your either pay Adobe a small fortune or you need to buy and learn the whole .NET mess just to do some simple apps.

### Post by fballem on 2008-09-13
> **gmedina said:**
> Ubuntu tries to do everything for me! Man, I hate that. As a Debian user I got used to read and search for information and got used to that feeling of "being in control". I still use Debian in two Boxes and I feel like "I'm home there" (**my** home, if you know what I mean); I only use Ubuntu in my laptop and everyday I thank Ian and Debra.

It's all in the perspective: think of the new user coming from Windows with minimal (or no) exposure to Linux. As a place to start, is Ubuntu or Debian easier for them. If they want an operating system that works (or mostly works) in a relatively pain-free manner, they are likely to be more comfortable with ubuntu.

Once they have gotten comfortable, and more exposed to other possibilities, they may want to try other distros with varying characteristics, at which point Debian may be exactly the right choice for them.

I haven't tried Debian - yet. I'm not fully comfortable with Linux and I use restricted stuff (nVidia drivers and various codecs). I may in the future, but I am finding ubuntu to be very comfortable. But then I'm a relatively new user coming from Windows.

### Post by SeePU on 2008-09-14
I hate a few things:

1) I don't like sudo.  I hope someone can tell me how to get rid or it or in other words, so I can use the traditional su method.  Please.  

2) I don't like how it's against the law to be negative or frustrated in Linux/Ubuntu forums.  Linux users have to face the fact that people will have problems especially people not as familiar with the particular distro or Linux in general.  Unless you're the brother of the developer, please try to be sympathetic to people's problems and frustrations.  I think dealing with the negativity and trying to help will benefit us all and allow potential for continuous improvement of whatever distro we use

3) I hate that Ubuntu seems to be noted for bugs.  Seriously.  If you google or read comments about Ubuntu, they have a rep for being slow in dealing with bugs.  

I found a bug in 8,10.  I hope they will fix it.  One was:
'Sorry, the program "jockey-gtk" closed unexpectedly'

Also, when I looked at the 'hardware drivers' for the nvidia drivers, at one point, the windows 'froze.'  Also, I tried the so-called equivalent Kubuntu Live CD (of Intrepid) 8,10 but I couldn't even get video.  Why could I boot up with Ubuntu but not Kubuntu?  I perceive neglect of Kubuntu there.  Any ideas?    

The good thing is that Ubuntu is one of the few besides Fedora that has my usb wireless adapter working out of the box and was an easy configuration.  Bravo.  Now, if they can just fix those bugs and work on my points above.  Then I would switch (to Ubuntu) for my main system.

### Post by growingneeds on 2008-09-15
Hi all,

I have been browsing through a number of posts in this thread, and I must say I agree with most of them. Ubuntu has been the first Linux distribution that had persisted alongside my Windows installation since 2006. I love the way it seems to be able to handle everyday tasks. Like Apple, there seems to be a lot of innovation with each release.

However, one thing irks me about Ubuntu: I dislike upgrading to a new release for fear that some things will get broken. I was a happy camper of Feisty Fawn right up to the point when Gutsy was released. Then it was an extended weekend nightmare. I could not explain why my system became unacceptably slower. I could type faster than the words appear on the screen (it was system-wide, including Terminal). Neither could I do anything about my then-dysfunctional Linksys WUSB54Gv4. Finally, I gave up and went back to Feisty. I had waited for over 4 months since the release of Hardy before deciding to try this release. (Ubuntu would only support Feisty via security upgrades till Oct 2008.) Hence, I made the jump. I must say that I am very pleased with Hardy. Even my WUSB54Gv4 works well with ndiswrapper. But since, I've been on the lookout for a way to install Linux (hopefully Debian-derived) and continuously build on it without having to fear that a previously functional package would get broken in a new release.

I have contemplated Debian stable and Gentoo. But it's still preliminary. (I'm still very comfy with Hardy. Perhaps too comfy.) As with most Linux distributions, Debian seems to follow the philosophy of “release distro”. I'm hoping Gentoo doesn't require such major system overhauls. But compilations from source aren't really my thing.

May all future releases of Ubuntu be as sturdy as Hardy.

---

### Post by N00b-un-2 on 2008-09-16
1) NO FINAL FANTASY VII!!! Well... actually I suppose that you could always play FFVII on a PSX emulator, but I want to play the PC version with all the cool mods that squaresoft didn't have the foresight to program into the original game.  Who's brilliant idea was it to use those lego block characters?  

2) No sound on the web cam. Still looking into that one.  

3) My scanner is inop.  But I think I've used it maybe 2 or 3 times in the 5 years I've owned it.

4) The fact that when you log in as root you aren't able to open or modify some programs that are attached to a SU profile (WTF?!?!? But that's not Ubuntu, that's just Linux...  Seems to me that root should be God on a Linux box)

That being said, they said it couldn't be done. But I got my Lexmark X1150 printer to work.  They said it couldn't be done but I got my Atheros ar242x (AR5007E chipset) wifi card to work.  They said it couldn't be done (easily), but I got my nVidia graphics card to work just fine (only gets goofy while playing games.  It tends to prefer to run in dual screen mode, despite the fact that I don't have two screens)

What do I love about Ubuntu?

1) Chicks dig it!

2) It's NOT Windows Vista!

3) It's totally 100% free!  as in cost-free, virus-free, Micro$0ft-free, free-source, free-balling... you get the point.

### Post by WeddleWebguy on 2008-09-17
I foresee a time in the very near future when it is going to come down to a war between microshaft and the civilized world.  I guess a few features which bug me about ubuntu is the following:

1. Ubuntu doesn't purr when I pet it.  My kitty bill purrs, and I would like to see a purring Ubuntu in the 8.10 release.

2.  The price.  I mean if it's free it can't be worth much right? I mean take oxygen for example, it's free but if you get too much you get too high.  Ubuntu should cost more, but I think that is something the marketing department is going to have to research.

3. Ubuntu doesn't have the blue screen of death.  I like the color blue and it bugs me that my computer doesn't occasionally scream at me that an invalid page fault, or system boot was interrupted.

4.  Uptime -- I mean seriously, I occasionally like to hear my computer make the start up sounds.  When you don't need to reboot for days on end, it has me so tempted to shut down and restart just for the heck of it so that I can hear my beloved start up sounds.  Ubuntu should come up with a feature where Ubuntu takes control and reboots your computer, so you get that early morning surprise that your system restarted sometime during the night and your site has been down for hours on end.

~That Weddle Web Guy

### Post by Lord Udedenkz on 2008-09-18
Honestly?
A few.
1) Performance. Boot, Video, Shutdown, Sleep/Standby are all slower then XP. My myspace profile is much slower in Ubuntu. Video and Flash have performance problems. Everything graphical is faster in XP.
2) RAM and CPU usage. XP64 + FireFox = 228MB, Ubuntu uses like 300MB RAM on boot. XP64+Firefox = 1-4% CPU Usage, Ubuntu64+Firefox = 8-20% CPU usage.
3)It just seems, not crisp. It seems blurry for some reason. XP seems to have more contrast or maybe problem V&H refresh rates?
4) Bugs. Ubuntu seems to have some serious bugs unlike XP. For example sometimes on start it does not detect that I have a network card, after sleep no sound, and lots of times I have press Enter to get to boot up.
So, in a summary: Slow, Unoptimized, and Buggy compared to XP64 or XP32. Don't get me wrong, I don't think XP is not optimized either as it just does not take advantage of SSE2/3/4 it seems to speed itself up and also it has bugs as well...

Positives,
- Free.
- Might soon have actual different desktops that I could switch from and to which is cool. For now, the multi-desktop is cool but from what I read it will get better.
- ALT-TAB looks awesome
- Not MAC or WINDOWS, so people ask me wtf I am running.
- Like Video Games, addicting because you just want it to work right and it refuses but you keep on trying.

I decided to keep it limited.

---

but its a really nice os for programming and office use i think.

### Post by waltclay on 2008-10-08
> Since open-source projects tend to attract expert users, they often lack clear user interfaces and readable documentation, making them unfriendly to mere mortals. But Buytaert understood from the beginning how important usability is to the cycle of improvement, adoption, and more improvement that drives the development of open-source software. The core Drupal installation comes with voluminous help files. The central team regularly polls users as well as developers (which is unusual in an open-source project) to decide what to improve next. The process reveals not just features to add, but ones to remove, and ways to make existing features easier to understand. For example, the project's website has been redesigned to help people new to Drupal figure out how to get up and running.

Source: [http://www.technologyreview.com/TR35/Profile.aspx?Cand=T&TRID=688]("http://www.technologyreview.com/TR35/Profile.aspx?Cand=T&TRID=688")

How does ubuntu measure up? I don't think it is helped by the mindless hobbyist/enthusiasts.

### Post by Irihapeti on 2008-10-08
> **waltclay said:**
> Source: [http://www.technologyreview.com/TR35/Profile.aspx?Cand=T&TRID=688]("http://www.technologyreview.com/TR35/Profile.aspx?Cand=T&TRID=688")

How does ubuntu measure up? I don't think it is helped by the mindless hobbyist/enthusiasts.

In the Gnome help files (help icon at the top of the screen), there's a series of well-written documents about how to set various things up, including stuff that needs to be taken into account when migrating from Windows.

I think it would be an excellent idea if these docs were accessible from an icon on the live CD, e.g. "Read this before you install" kind of thing.

Also, I'd like to see a better explanation of what the different partitioning options mean when one is installing. I can remember not being quite sure what each option was going to do. Fortunately, I didn't screw up, but clearly quite a number of newcomers do.

### Post by FLyzoola on 2008-10-23
There's a couple of things I don't "like" in Ubuntu.

It's not, when comptared to something like windose, very user friendly.

Installing apps that aren't in the package manager is an absolute pain. "Oh, it didn't work? dang, I gotta go through code to figure out WHY it didn't work, and then see how I CAN make it work"

I'd rather double-click .exe and that be it. 

Linux has a lot of overly-complicated ways to do very simple things. Getting some apps to work together is also a pain, and getting some to just plain out work is hard, even if it's from the add/remove option.

I think ubuntu should me made WAY more userfriendly. Installing apps shouldn't be so hard. Overall, it's still more complicated to use than a windows computer, and a mac (lacking super user-friendly apps). I love my Ubuntu computer, because I customized everything on it, and chances of me going back to windows are slim, but even upgrading it turned out to be a hassle. 

Ubuntu's lack of user friendliness makes me sad panda ):

### Post by Crick on 2008-10-24
Hate is a strong word. If there was something I hated about Ubuntu, I wouldn't be running it. I might take up a bit of time with things like not being able to VPN while having a static IP, but that will get dealt with over time like all the other issues that kept me from running Linux for the first 10 years I had known of it. The biggest asset of Ubuntu is the community, and scouring the forum almost ALWAYS yields a workaround. I haven't gotten involved in coding for Ubuntu, but I like to give back by posting guides for things when I figure out how to do something.

What I unreservedly **hate** is running an operating system that is closed source, phones home without authorization, probably has back doors, and works for the interest of itself and other companies instead of mine, while papering over that with Orwellian marketing speak (e.g. Genuine Advantage). 

I hate running an OS that is a malware magnet and has had security features hacked on rather than inherent in the design, in order to seize early market share and gain through network effect.

I hate being treated like some sort of bovine consumer waiting to be milked for my money every 3 years or so, with forced, planned obsolescence. I hate the waste involved in that process. I hate having a dumbed down base install with less features simply because someone views me as a market segment and wants to extract as much cash from my pocket as possible.

I hate software companies who think it is wise to corrupt governments against the greater interests of all humanity, when those governments and organizations try to choose a truly open document format.

I hate all of those things, and that is why I made the switch to Ubuntu. Of course, it (like all operating systems) has features that you find difficult to live without once you learn them, but that is not why I switched to Ubuntu.

---

### Post by kimda on 2008-12-11
That I cannot save to a samba share with several programs. Some programs do not use gvfs at the moment and this is pissing me off. I have to save my openoffice documents in my home directory and then move it to the proper location. I love Linux. I am using it since 2000 and I've seen it improve with leaps and bounds but this bug is annoying me a lot. This is something I really hate in Ubuntu! Now I am considering other options like ditching samba all together and going for nfs or maybe using cif mounts to mount the "multiple" shares I cannot save to. I feel it has been a big mistake to implement gvfs while not all the programs are ready for this. I wish browsing/opening/saving to a samba share would be as easy under Ubuntu as it is under Windows because its a mess. ### Post by ibutho on 2008-12-17
I don't particularly dislike anything about the distro (apart from the fanboys who have little respect for other distributions.), but I wish it had better support for wireless devices. Another gripe of mine is the lack of good system configuration tools like those found in other distros e.g. Xorg can be a pain to configure on Ubuntu if X settings are not auto detected correctly which may result in manual editing of files being needed. Other distros have builtin command line and graphical tools that help configure X correcting without tinkering with config files.

### Post by Tomatz on 2008-12-17
> **ibutho said:**
> I don't particularly dislike anything about the distro (apart from the fanboys who have little respect for other distributions.), but I wish it had better support for wireless devices. Another gripe of mine is the lack of good system configuration tools like those found in other distros e.g. Xorg can be a pain to configure on Ubuntu if X settings are not auto detected correctly which may result in manual editing of files being needed. Other distros have builtin command line and graphical tools that help configure X correcting without tinkering with config files.

Ubuntu has a gui for configuring xorg. Its called:

```
displayconfig-gtk 
```

(run the command in a terminal)

The old now defunct way of confuguring xorg from the command line was:

```
sudo dpkg-reconfigure xserver-xorg
```


No modern distro is without these tools ;)

### Post by OzzyFrank on 2008-12-19
Hehe... you guys are hilarious! Someone asks what you hate about Ubuntu and you all queue up frantically to announce you hate nothing about it? Hmmm... methinks you are in the wrong thread, hehe. (I know, I know, we all love Ubuntu, but this is how some threads blow out to 370 pages, hehehe!)

Me, well I won't post here about things that could be better in apps (like many others do) as I know that is not the province of Ubuntu developers but that of the developers of the programs.

What I will complain about here is the booting into **low-graphics mode** that has happened with the last couple of upgrades. This has happened before on my P4 with ATI card and my Quad-core with nVidia card. Me, I can just go "Ugh... not again!" and fix it, but that situation seriously freaked out a couple of friends I have converted to Ubuntu. Especially when the screen size is so small, and while you can see the menus in the top panel, clicking them actually activates the launchers underneath them (not that you can see those). If it wasn't for me rushing around there, these guys would have given up on Ubuntu just based on that (only so much you can do in a 640x480 or 600x800 desktop with menus you can't open).

Oh, and this is now the 2nd time **Usplash** has disappeared on me after an upgrade. They could do more work on 64-bit and widescreen for Usplash... or just get rid of it in favour of Splashy. Not a big deal, but obviously an issue.

### Post by amheuwr on 2008-12-21
After having to try and load XP back onto a second hard drive I had lying around so that others in the house could log into a dual boot system I ended up totally knackering my spare drive and also my main linux drive. I had to resort to using my children's computer and they us XP. 
At first I wasn't happy with the very slow system, my Gnome use do fly on a lower spec system. Lovely and clean with no bells or whistles. But then I started using my Wacom tablet on this other machine, to work on some photographs. I used photoshop elements for the first time as well and something just hit me. I realised that I spend an awful lot of time on Ubuntu trying to get things to work, don't get me wrong here, I do like a good tinker,(two broken hard drives are evidence of this) but I seemed to have been wasting an awful lot of time lately just trying to get things to work. Some will say that you mange to get more control over your system this way and you also learn at the same time. To an extent I agree, however, I have a life and some interests out side of the study.So what I am getting at is this, I hate the fact I have to waste so much time tinkering, just to get things to work OK rather than perfectly in Ubuntu. Will get myself a couple of new hard drives soon and will dual boot. Will pop into both until the day comes that I can use things with the minimum of meddling.:?

### Post by StudioDave on 2008-12-22
I bought an HP G60-125NR because it has an on-board nVidia 8200M (I need 3D graphics for my work) and a Lightscribe disc drive. I tried various distros and eventually settled on Intrepid. Initially I liked 8.10 enough to recommend it in an article I wrote for the Linux Journal. I now hate it so thoroughly I plan to retract my recommendation. Here's why:

Shutdown and Restart don't work. I have to use ctrl-alt-del (if I'm lucky enough to make it to the login page after exiting Fluxbox, sometimes the system dies) and then boot into recovery mode to effect a clean shutdown.

Logout doesn't work and will totally destabilize my system. I have to power cycle to get the system back.

I use Fluxbox: If I click on the Help for a GNOME app I get an infinite flow of borked Help screens until I press the power switch on my machine.

HAL polled my CD/DVD drive and a non-existent drive to the point of borking my system until I disabled HAL polling.

SHMConfig was not configured, I couldn't disable my touchpad until I learned that 8.10 now uses the HAL system instead of xorg.conf to set up shmconfig. I could (and did) use the GNOME Control Center to turn off the pad, but every time I did it also reset my Fluxbox background. I wanted to use synclient instead, and quickly discovered the shmconfig fiasco.

Pulseaudio=PITA. I use JACK, so I had to search the Web for directions on cleanly removing Pulseaudio.

NetworkManager might be the cause of some of my troubles, but by this time I'm tired of messing around with Ubuntu 8.10. I've already deleted it and installed OpenSUSE 11, which is also brain-damaged due to unconfigurable shmconfig (no way to disable touchpad) and Pulseaudio. Can anyone suggest a usable modern Linux distribution without these problems ?

I don't want a lot of what distro designers seem to think are good things, and the distros are getting harder to configure for even experienced users (I've been using Linux for about 14 years). The touchpad issue is indicative: What good reason can maintainers have for *not* enabling shmconfig by default ? Surely they can't think that no-one wants to turn off the touchpad ? And why install synclient by default in an environment where it's sure to not work ?

So farewell, Ubuntu, I'll try you on another day, maybe three or four releases down the line.

Back to square one.

**Added 12/25/08**: Merry Christmas ! Well, after wrestling with OpenSUSE 11 for a few days I learned that there are worse systems than Ubuntu out there. I installed the 64-bit version of OS 11, with GNOME, and ran across the same problems I had in Intrepid, but with no apparent workarounds. SHMConfig couldn't be set up (I never figured it out, despite using a HAL fdi file), Pulseaudio is more difficult to remove than in Intrepid, and the installer didn't install the binary nVidia driver properly. Also, the Lightscribe software doesn't work under 64-bit systems, and I want to use it for some holiday discs. So I'm back to Intrepid. I'm sticking with GNOME this time (even though I don't especially like it). I much prefer Fluxbox but I'm wondering if it isn't exacerbating some of the problems. Btw, I did try installing Arch on this machine but the installer pukes with a complaint about hardware. I'll gladly try it again (yes, I do like a lot of control over my system) if I can figure out the right boot options. One thing more: All praise to the Ubuntu devs for getting some things right, and I'll continue to hope for solutions to the rest of my problems with 8.10.

---

I don't know whether it's just Fluxbox....I'm far from an expert....but I switched off the touchpad...at least the tap to click...through the GUI (in Gnome that is), so not sure why you're having trouble with it.

### Post by jbysmith on 2008-12-23
> **StudioDave said:**
> ... Can anyone suggest a usable modern Linux distribution without these problems ?

I've yet to find the absolutely perfect distro that worked 100% for me, they *all* have some quirks or another.  (Which of course isn't unique to Linux.. at least it didn't make me want to cry and self-immolate like Vista.)

Sounds like you want a lot of control over what goes into your system.  One of my personal favorites, along with Ubuntu and Debian of course, is Arch Linux.  

Definitely takes longer to set up, as you're building it from the ground up starting from a console only boot.  But it's not hard, their wiki has an excellent how-to that you just follow along with, and you get *exactly* the setup you want as you're putting it together a piece at a time.  They actually have a few features that I prefer over the Debian style, like their init system, a global rc.conf configuration and the like.  Decent package management.  A "ports system" of sorts where you can pull in and compile various out-of-repository software. Absurdly lean and fast unless you bloat it up.  It's also a rolling release system; there's no "six month upgrade roulette" to worry about. 

If there's something in there you don't like it's your fault, as you installed it :D

### Post by Yellowbelly on 2008-12-26
Extra partitions you have are mounted by the names of their size instead of their actual partition names. Incredibly stupid, especially when it didn't used to be like this.

Finding myself using the command line to fix what should be stupid and simple stuff . . . like automounting those partitions on start up. Funny thing is, all the code I tried didn't work so I found out about that gui program and it worked flawlessly.

Using the command line to install .bin's and .run's. This is the year 2008. I don't shouldn't need to look up how to's to install programs just because I forget a piece of code. It needs to be stupid proof to be quick and easy for everyone.

I don't like the idea of compiz as a program on top of the window manager. I think KDE has it right when it's integrated. With that, it's way too ugly to be mainstream.

Can't see other computers on a network without mounds of bull crap to get through

that's about it for now.

### Post by luvr on 2009-01-04
I found Ubuntu a great, refreshing experience when I first tried it (that was the 6.06 release), though I wasn't ready to take it seriously just yet at that time.

The first release that I actually used, was 7.04, and I loved it.

Then came 7.10, but that was too much of a hassle to get working on my hardware. Most annoying were the display issues, but there were other troubles as well. I must admit that, with some effort, I could resolve the issues, but I simply considered it not worth it; after all, 7.04 worked great, and I didn't feel forced to upgrade.

I was quite happy with the 8.04 release (though I'm not convinced that including the Firefox 3 beta was such a great idea--but that eventually got fixed). I thought that the **Long-Term Support** qualification would make it a safe choice--but it turns out I was mistaken: One day, I found that USB devices no longer worked (must have something to do with some update, since the Live CD doesn't have these problems); I soon discovered that USB devices no longer got automatically registered, and that I had to run the *lsusb* command to get them to be seen.

Initially, the 8.10 release seemed to solve the USB issues: It automatically mounted USB devices again. Unfortunately, it tends to *unmount* them at random while I'm using them, telling me that it ran into I/O errors and that maybe the USB cable is bad (which it isn't--under Fedora 10, Slackware 12.1 and 12.2, and even Windows XP, I cannot reproduce this issue). By now, the 8.04 release has begun to present the same problems, and the problems seem to get worse with every update, so I have learned to avoid using USB devices under Ubuntu like the plague.

(There are more than enough bug reports filed about these USB issues, so they should be known by now.)

**Conclusion:** I would love to love Ubuntu, since I quite enjoy it if it works, but it doesn't work well enough. I *will* try out the next release (should be 9.04, I guess) when it arrives, though I'm seriously considering taking a closer look at Slackware; even if, in the end, I may conclude that it's not right for me, I **will** have learned a lot about Linux, and in the mean time, Ubuntu may have resolved its worst stability issues (or not, as the case may be).

I'm becoming convinced that a six-month release cycle won't ever produce a *stable* product--which is fine with me, provided that we can all agree that these releases are *"experimental"* (i.e., by definition, not necessarily *"stable"*). I do believe that Ubuntu should consider a *"stable"* branch, outside of the six-month release cycle--the current *"Long-Term Support"* releases just don't cut it. Why not go for a model like what Red Hat does: a bleeding-edge *"experimental"* release like Fedora, and a *"stable"* release like the actual Red Hat distribution. Heck, there's even an *"unofficial stable"* release, called CentOS, in the Red Hat world.

### Post by Geekkit on 2009-01-05
Up until 8.04 (haven't tried with 8.10 since the last time I tried to install 8.10 it locked up on me), the following limitations bother me:

[LIST]
[*]copying files/directories from a CD-ROM to my home directory requires me to perform the following command: chmod -R u+w  ... apparently the non-write option from CD-ROM is copied over
[*]I **still** have video tearing and it **still** happens on four different computers, from 7.04 to 8.04, compiz or no compiz, compiz unredirect fullscreen windows on/off, compiz detect refresh rate on/off, compiz refresh rate 60 - 200, compiz sync to vblank on/off, totem, vlc, xorg.conf Option "TexturedVideo" "off" or Option "TexturedVideo" "on", NVidia card, Matrox card, ATI card, Intel card, AVI, MOV, MPEG 1/2/4, WMV, FLV, full screen/window, doesn't flipping matter, video tearing.
[/LIST]

The first one is a mere annoyance that is easily resolved with the above posted command (assuming you know it) but the second one makes multimedia in Linux/Ubuntu (for me) flawed compared to if I watch video in Windows or OSX. It's difficult for me because the OSS people blame the proprietary drivers and of course the creators of the proprietary drivers blame OSS.

Btw, if anyone has come up with solution that I haven't mentioned (I have raked the web and forums for hours to no avail), I'm all ears.
