### Post by Montblanc_Kupo on 2009-01-14
The things I hate about ubuntu are generally problems with most linux distros (ubuntu gives me the least grief so I've stuck with it as My main distro).

1. Installing / uninstalling applications. Yes... once you figure out how to use the add / remove / synaptic / deb / make / whatever you can do it... but it's definitely the least intuitive method of the three major desktop os's (mac, windows, linux). It's close to how windows works actually... but using a mac... that is the gold standard IMHO. One icon... drag and drop for 99% of applications. File management and application management still need work to be truly user friendly. I love how repositories work and that I can add/remove things easily in ubuntu with the add/remove app... but it's still not where it should be.

1.5 /usr/lib/blackmagic/hiddenfolder/.wherearemyprogramsanyway... uhm... nuff said.

2. Adobe. Not linux's fault... but still a problem I face with linux.

3. Hardware driver installation and management. There's not **good** centralized hardware management in ubuntu. I wouldn't say that windows is good... uhm... for anything... but having a centralized hardware management location is one of the few ideas I like. Installing even something that should be as simple as a graphics driver (a one double click operation on mac and windows) is an all day event in linux... and it STILL didn't work right (that was nvidia's fault partially... but it took Me an hour to find out if it was even functional... linux's fault). Basic things like...seeing your screen... or being able to slow down My mouse tracking to a usable rate should **NOT** require editing cryptically named and located files deep in seedy redlight district of the OS. This is a stigma that linux has had for a long time... and it's still there. You should be **able** to access the core of the system with the terminal... you shouldn't [/b]need to[/b].

3. Centralized UI. If I hit cmd+q on a mac... My application quits. Every time. Every app. If I hit cmd+w on a mac... My current window closes... every app... every time. On linux... well... I eventually got some things programmed in that did that... through lots of poking around... and it works in .. probably 80% of the things that I use... but each and every app requires configuration just to do it. And now that I've gone through and tweaked everything... something else is broken and I can't locate it. Mac has it's issues... but they pioneered the centralized UI and they did a damned good job of it.

95% of the time I love linux... I'm using it as My primary OS... but yeah... it could use some tweaking.

$0.02

---

### Post by soelk on 2009-01-14
*The repositories could be a bit more up to date with bug fixes. For example, my Nokia phone can't connect properly to USB in Intrepid. There is a fix, but I don't fancy re-compiling the kernel in order to find out if it works for me.

Besides the lack of quick bug fixes for minor issues I don't think I have any problems that are specific to Ubuntu. But here is the **complete** list of annoying problems that I've encountered since I switched to Ubuntu from Windows XP one year and a couple of months ago:

*Drivers and driver updates can sometimes mess things up in ways you're not used to in Windows. The ATI drivers gave me plenty of headaches until I learned that the common sense (use the latest driver) in Windows does not apply in Linux. This is hard to quantify.

*Digital sound out from my old Creative Audigy 2 ZS did not work. Solved by *disabling* the enable switch. :confused: Think it's fixed since Hardy.

*No sound in the Adobe flash plugin. Solved after half an hour of googling, sudo gedit.

*No picture in any video player in Compiz Fusion. Google, sudo gedit.

*Extra mouse buttons does not work as expected. Google, sudo gedit. Problem fixed since Hardy.

*Can't drag and drop application launchers to the applications menu. Think many future Windows switchers will miss this functionality, althought it's not something that I miss personally now that I am used to Gnome.

*Installing Matlab is a somewhat hard-ish task for a Windows user. Can alternatively be solved by switching to Sage. (Sage is free software, perhaps some of the most amazing software that you can get for free besides GNU/Linux).

*No games.

*The XBOX360 game controller was a bit tricky to set up, until some great guy made a userspace driver. Lovely. :)

Not much to whine about, I'd say.

---

### Post by JC Cheloven on 2009-01-15
Not Ubuntu specific, but I hate how sound works (perhaps "works" is not the best word to use) in linux. It is an absolute mess of confusing design in the basics and half-cooked software on top. If you add these to poor hardware support (not linux' fault indeed), you get a system which is hardly recommendable to a musician or someone else professionally involved in digital audio. 

I know, I could contribute instead of moaning. I do, to a humble extent. But I've the feeling that a whole, unified, simpler but powerful and robust sound system has to be built for gnu-linux, ground up. It's far away from my capabilities.

As a musician, I tried hard, but didn't put things to work properly. This is my only regret. Other than that, me and my many 'linux converted' friends are more than happy with ubuntu. Gnu linux based systems are awesome for a wide range of tasks, and the effort made at ubuntu has blown that goodness to a broader audience. Good things are to come from that. Thank you to everyone.

---

### Post by stairwayoflight on 2009-01-15
Ever since I tried GNU/Linux, I was hooked. Sure it was fun to pull out the calculator to create openbsd partitions, or play distribution developer with gentoo and change up use flags, but the "set it and forget it" Ubuntu experience is much nicer.

Which leads to my only real gripe. I can deal with hardware problems, occasional compatibility problems, etc.--but what I absolutely hate is when I have set up everything and it works, updating the kernel breaks something (like losing sound output, which is a serious problem).

Sure I can roll back to an older kernel, (and I do) but there has to be a better way. How about setting up some kind of hardware profile. If the latest kernel will break sound on my system, it should be "masked" out in the repository until one works. Or at least I should have the kernel installed, and then it should ask me:

1) On the next boot
2) In a few days

if everything seems to be working, and if I say no, it should simply revert to the last good kernel, mask the broken one as an upgrade choice, and try again on the next kernel. (yes, now that I am learning shell I can work out how to script this myself.)

I want to use my computer to develop and interact with applications, not spend time administering the system. If it wasn't for this, I would get my parents on Ubuntu. (I don't live with them, and don't have time to fix their computer more than once a year.) When this happens to the average user, they are totally stuck.

When Ubuntu deals with this (and I believe they will, because their team is fantastic) then they will truly be Linux for human beings.

My $0.02.
Stairwayoflight

---

### Post by mnmus on 2009-01-20
First, I do like Ubuntu a great deal. I've retired (or retasked for simpler uses) all my other machines and use this Ubuntu machine daily for over 90% of my computing use--apart from real music (more on that in a sec). The rest is mostly performed in various other OSes in VMs on my Ubuntu box.

That said, I hate the way updates/upgrades reliably *heh* break things... on a regular basis. Update? Sound stops working properly. Or flash player flakes when something else is updated, or video drivers/resolutions are "reset" and I'm left with 800X600 on a 1680X1050 display, or he shared printer attached to my Ubuntu machine becoming unusable by ANYONE else after an update (until after much shaking of chicken bone rattle while wearing dreadlock wig)... you get my drift. Fiddle around at the command line for a while, sometimes hit up Google or these forums for solutions to problems _caused_ by an update, sometimes just stumble around in the dark for a while because NOTHING works the way it's supposed to when fixing problem X.

*feh*

Then there are the flaky install routines required to get some things going. Want to run an Ubuntu box as an HTPC? Better build it that way from the ground up, because adding Mediabuntu or Freevo or darned near anything else and you WILL spend an inordinate amount of time at the command line, and you'd darned well better not make any typos or it'll be more than merely an inordinate amount of time. Now, I don't mind that all that much, but imagine the proverbial "Aunt Tilly" whose grand-nephew enthusiastically installs Ubuntu on her media pc when Windows flakes out one too many times. Oops. He'd darned well better be a command line wizard, cos she's not going to be happy until he gets it right.

Or take the simple little thing of getting midi playing properly. In Windows, anyone--and I do mean anyone with more than two active brain cells--could plug my external synth right on in to their Windows box and get it going with no sweat at all, almost automagically. In Ubuntu, I've found that it is possible, but it's not friendly. Heck, for quite some time, I found it far easier and a more efficient use of my time to keep my synth plugged into an OLD Win98 box than mess with jumping through all the hoops it took to finally get things working in Ubuntu--and even then it was a Windows program running under WINE that managed to work for me.

So that leads to... there are some indispensable Windows/Mac softwares for which there is neither a port nor a suitable Linux substitute... yet. But that'll come, I'm sure.

Drivers. Finally gave up on my USB IR receiver/remote and simply replaced a perfectly good set with something that was supported. Not that the IR receiver/remote are uncommon--no! They were just not a set anyone had cared to write for or that the IR Control applet in Control Center could detect. So, amazon.com and another $40 for a less functional remote/receiver that IS on the list. Not a biggie, but irksome. I guess if I wanted seamless HTPC functionality as well as a normal computing setup, I could just have scrubbed everything and started over with LinuxMCE... or Vista, but no, I had to beat my head against a brick wall for a while instead. S'll right; I seem to enjoy pain. After all, I was a Windows user for 16 years before I made a(n almost) complete break. Sure, I eased into Ubuntu over the last few years, a bit, but a clean break from Windows starting with Ubuntu 8.04 has been, on balance, a positive thing.

And all that said, I find the only thing I really, really miss about Windows is Freecell. (The crappy freecell game that's available OOB in Ubuntu is, well as I said, crappy.) But since I don;t really care that much abut any game, that's not too bad at all, now is it? For the rest, I put it all down to growing pains and am still more than pleased to run Ubuntu (8.10 now, even with the upgrade headaches) as my daily computing platform.

One last thing I really hate: having to figure out what to do with the "Microsoft tax" I'm NOT paying... what to spend it on, what to spend it on... *heh*

---

### Post by Tomatz on 2009-01-29
> **beerguzzler said:**
> I don't upgrade, I reinstall too, but I still have to reinstall all my applications though. yes the settings I had within the application stay as they were but doing sudo apt-get install for all my apps every six months after a fresh install is a bit of a pain. That said, I guess I may be at fault there for not doing the Ubuntu install correctly?

You can upgrade packages from the commandline:

```
sudo apt-get update && sudo apt-get dist-upgrade
```

;)

**EDIT:**

Ahhh i see what you mean now. Updating ubuntu to the next version _usually_ works flawlessly if you have a basic installation but as soon as you start adding extra repos and packages things get messy when you update ;)

1. It's slower than WinXP (Wubi 8.04)

3. Wubi 8.10 won't boot after several tries (I get as far as quick display of GRLDR, then "unrecognized...".  I can't read the rest it goes by too fast to read.  I'm thinking it has something to do with partition.

Burned the .iso file to CD (twice), uninstalled 8.04 per instructions, selected partition into which 8.10 would load (15GB).  All seems to go well until after reboot, when I get the "GRLDR, etc." flashby, then just a blinking cursor.

As I said, 8.04 seemed to work OK, except for printer issue and slowness under XP, but it also stopped recognizing my GeForce 5200 card except in 640x480.  I'd love to try 8.10 on my previous 8.04 system, but it's been a real hassle and lots of lost time trying to get 8.04 to work with printer and graphics, and now no 8.10 on my older system (2-55GB partitions-NTFS--are free. ### Post by Tomatz on 2009-01-30
> **docklc said:**
> 1. It's slower than WinXP (Wubi 8.04)
2. Wubi 8.04 wouldn't recognize my Canon IP1700 Printer
3. Wubi 8.10 won't boot after several tries (I get as far as quick display of GRLDR, then "unrecognized...".  I can't read the rest it goes by too fast to read.  I'm thinking it has something to do with partition.
4. It takes forever to download, even with a highspeed Comcast connection

I have installed 8.10 on my other desktop with little fuss, after the long download.  Burned the .iso file to CD (twice), uninstalled 8.04 per instructions, selected partition into which 8.10 would load (15GB).  All seems to go well until after reboot, when I get the "GRLDR, etc." flashby, then just a blinking cursor.

As I said, 8.04 seemed to work OK, except for printer issue and slowness under XP, but it also stopped recognizing my GeForce 5200 card except in 640x480.  I'd love to try 8.10 on my previous 8.04 system, but it's been a real hassle and lots of lost time trying to get 8.04 to work with printer and graphics, and now no 8.10 on my older system (2-55GB partitions-NTFS--are free. Hardy Heron 8.04 is uninstalled. I have a Celeron 2.2 w.512 RAM and the aforementioned video.  I don't care about games, I just want to get away from Microsoft as soon as feasible.  I will work with 8.10 on my other system, but 8.10 should install on this system, too.  I'll try another download and reinstall tonight, but if there's a partition problem, how to fix?  I see no problems using a partition manager, nor with XP--all partitions are recognized under it.  Why is 8.10 hanging on install???


Sorry to hear about your problems with the geforce 5200. I have been trying to help another user with exactly the same problem:

[http://ubuntuforums.org/member.php?u=150727](http://ubuntuforums.org/member.php?u=150727)

[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?p=6641428#post6641428](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?p=6641428#post6641428)

He has filed a bug report at launchpad. If you pm him i am sure he will give you the url to the bug report so you can confirm it and hopefully get help.

It seems older nvidia hardware (geforce 4, fx) is being a bit problematic in ubuntu as of late. This is because nvidia are no longer supporting the fx series under linux:

Taken from wikipedia:

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/GeForce_FX_Series](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/GeForce_FX_Series)

> Linux driver support

The GeForce FX series is no longer supported [20] by the binary NVidia drivers as of version 177.80. Owners of GeForce FX hardware now have to use the legacy drivers, which cannot be used at the same time as the regular drivers and will not be updated to work with newer Linux kernels or Xorg releases.


So basically its not really a problem with linux more poor support from nvidia :(

That said mandriva still supports the 5200 because the kernel is more compatible with the older (legacy) drivers. Maybe you should give mandriva a go?

[http://www.mandriva.com/](http://www.mandriva.com/)

### Post by mnmus on 2009-01-30
1. Updates frequently screw up either sound or video. Reinstalling the nVidia drivers, re-specifying my monitor--what's up with that?
2. Updates, again--this a.m., for example, everything working fine, "Oh, there are updates waiting. OK." ran the update manager, rebooted as requested. No disk space left. "WTF?!?!? I had more than 220GiB of free disk space this a.m., but now I have zero?!?!?"
3. No useful error messages reference. Search the forums. Run across helpful people more often than not, but also self-important twits who have nothing useful to contribute as well, just ridicule for those with problems. Now *that* encourages Ubuntu use. Not. *heh*
4. Really lousy, obscurantist midi implementation. *feh* I want to play the notes into my transcription apps, not spend hours getting midi to work decently (though never really well) and *then* hours more reconfiguring it after an update nukes everything.
5. Updates again: I can count on it, any major update will make ALL my VM's inaccessible. Maybe it's a VMWare problem, but Every Stinking Time?!?! Install recommended Ubuntu updates. Oops. Suse Linux (or PCBSD or... any of 'em) VM won't boot--the panel won't even load at all, at all. *feh* It's all right, though, since the updates have likely broken something else important as well.

(Is Windows much better in that regard? Well, yes. Me$$y$oft frequently breaks things with updates, but at least most times *someone* has an answer readily accessible within a few hours. The Ubuntu Forums are pretty decent, but funding anything here is... an exercise in frustration as often as not, really no more useful than the screwy always SNAFUed Microsoft site, and there are so very, very many more unofficial Windows help sites that there's no real comparison, unfortunately. Will I ever get an answer--or even a hint--about my missing 220GiB of storage nuked by this a.m.'s update? Not if the first response I had on the forums is any indication.)

For general stuff, web browsing, email, office apps, Ubuntu is pretty good. I like it better than any Windows I've used, for those kinds of things. But for the kind of media creation I like to do, for day in and day out REAL stability (that is, updates don't create near SNAFU conditions with my system), I may have to find a different distro. Sad. Eight months plus of exclusive Ubuntu use has left me less satisfied with it than I was right "out of the box".

---

### Post by DrakeGis on 2009-01-30
1- After a  Kernel update, drivers Updates (wacom, ATI) usually must be done by hand. Maybe i would be nice to have a way to automatically generate some kind of script to do what I'm doing by hand.

2- ATI drivers work incorrectly with compiz for video reproduction. I hate the fact that AMD (and ATI, the same thing) are not creating an alternative to WINTEL.

3- Webcam support.

4- Messenger with webcam and audio support compatible with yahoo .

5- Flash support and the fact that there is not a "youtube" like web site where linux-formatted-videos are published.

6- Poor touchscreen support and no applications. 

7- Ubuntu forums should be organized in a better way and perhaps some threads should be "cleaner". Or at least support search within search within search, etc.... It's becoming hard to find things... and It's also hard to decide where to post.

8- The linux (or maybe the free-software) community (not only ubuntu) is loosing initiative and not promoting innovation. Remember that linux used to provide new features, new interfaces, new things to do... now, it seems is copying a lot of stuff from windows and mac. The best example is Firefox, but also OpenOffice, amarok (why there is no amarok for gnome? ), GPG, etc. 

Don't get me wrong. I love Linux and I like Ubuntu, but I have to recognize that there are thing that can be done in a better way. When I was younger I love to spend hours making the computer fully functional, but now... I only want a computer that works correctly. I guess I'm getting old :)

### Post by moopere on 2009-01-30
> **will1911a1 said:**
> Wow, that sucks.  Ever figured out why?

Yeah, mostly the up to date Linux's are just getting fatter and fatter and fatter.  I can't believe that XP feels slim in comparison.

This is my personal number one pet peev.  Legend has it that Linux (in general) runs fast even on 2-3 or 4 generations old PC's but its just not the case unless one runs Debian Woody or something equally old.  The usual counter argument is that the newer releases offer so much more functionality, but this I find to also be a false argument, yes, newer Linux's are more functional than early Linux's but to compare apples with apples you need to look at early Linux's versus say Windows NT.

I'm in IT and deal with a huge mix of systems including embedded stuff (both linux and windows embedded) and I'm annoyed and getting more annoyed that despite the various distro's physical growth over the years both in outright size and also resource requirements, in general Linux's ability to integrate with other systems (windows + other more arcane systems) is pretty much as broken and difficult as its always been.  Heaps more bling everywhere but little in the way of whole-of-network integration.

Cheers,
Craig

### Post by mnmus on 2009-02-01
> **jsearles said:**
> I hate the 12 hours that I have put in today solving the Nvidia adapter problem for a specialty application... 

Yeh, as I mentioned, I dislike having to reconfigure my video almost every time I update (nVidia card). Of course, I use EnvyNG (Available in one of the repos--don't recall offhand which), now, to reconfigure my driver, saving some hassles by NOT going the System>Administration route and installing the crappy drivers Ubuntu grudgingly makes available, and once the right nVidia drivers are installed and configured, running

```
sudo displayconfig-gtk
```

*usually* takes care of the display resolution selection. Usually. Of course, the danger of displayconfig messing up the driver selection is very real... *sigh* But at least EnvyNG sorts that out pretty quickly and easily.

Of course, if your specialty needs exceed whatever's available from the latest nVidia drivers EnvyNG can pull of nVidia's site for you, then a long haul might well be in the works.

As for Ubuntu/Linux being a "toy" OS, well, I suppose the same could be said for Win95, 98, and XP. (Vista's a generally high-cost "plastic" plaything, IMO) NT4 and Win2K were pretty robust, as are the recent Windows Server OSes, but despite my cavils about some Ubuntu quirks and irks, it stacks up well, IMO, against any desktop Windows version--except for some specialty, niche situations. (Disclaimer: longtime DOS/Windows user speaking here--even Novell NOS user. Only versions of Windows past 3.0 I've missed using on my own computers have been Me (Muppet Edition), NT before 4.0 and Vista/Server 2003/2008, although I've done maintenance on systems built using those last three, and support for altogether too many home users of the slightly creepy "Muppet Edition".)

Ubuntu's no toy, as much as I may from time to time decry its rough edges. Now, I could understand how some folks might mistakenly call something like Puppy Linux (a whopping 95MB Live CD) a toy, because it's so slim and elegant by today's standards, but even there such an estimation would be off-base, IMO.

### Post by solwic on 2009-02-01
> **jsearles said:**
> In my book,, except for specialty apps like Servers and not much else, Linux is a toy and very dangerous for most business environments.


Only if you're too lazy or stupid to learn.  Otherwise, Linux is great for business environments (stable, fast, cost-effective).  Unless, of course, you need really specialized applications or hardware.  Then that's out of Ubuntu's and Linux's hands; gotta gripe to the hardware manufacturer/software vendor.

> **jsearles said:**
> 
The second thing that I hate is that purveyors of Linux don't admit to this and end up misleading a lot of people.

Of course not.  Windows doesn't admit to all of their bugs and flaws and insecurities, either.  Install Windows XP some time.  While it's copying files it'll tell you all about how Windows XP is more secure than ever, which anybody with a functioning brain knows is laughable.  

It's business, and no business that expects to survive is going to make a habit of saying, "Hey, even though there's really nothing we can do about this, the following hardware/software may have issues: ...."

In essence, though, you'll get out of Linux what you put into it.  Ask any programmer:  it's the GIGO Rule (Garbage In, Garbage Out).  

It is what you make of it.  :)

Cheers!

### Post by OzzyFrank on 2009-02-02
OOo3 was a bit of a pain to install via guides I found, but it worked. But slowly with each system update it died. First, files wouldn't open from email attachments, but would if I double-clicked the saved files... then they wouldn't open that way, but would if I opened Writer or Impress or whatever then used File > Open... then OOo3 died altogether and none of the programs would open at all. Now I've uninstalled it, but can't reinstall the Ubuntu-friendly one in the repos! So now I have no OOo at all.

As for sound, I never had a problem till the kernel update last night. Before, my Audigy card was much more easily picked up than in Windows, where it needed drivers installed, but then I lost sound. But at least booting into the previous kernel at GRUB gives me my sound back.

---

### Post by solwic on 2009-02-02
> **OzzyFrank said:**
> OOo3 was a bit of a pain to install via guides I found, but it worked. But slowly with each system update it died. First, files wouldn't open from email attachments, but would if I double-clicked the saved files... then they wouldn't open that way, but would if I opened Writer or Impress or whatever then used File > Open... then OOo3 died altogether and none of the programs would open at all. Now I've uninstalled it, but can't reinstall the Ubuntu-friendly one in the repos! So now I have no OOo at all.


Sounds like you're running Kubuntu, or some other distro using KDE.  It's notorious for problems with OOo3.  

The key is to make sure you remove OOo2.4, and use a good guide, like [this one.]("http://www.howtoforge.com/how-to-install-openoffice-3.0.0-on-ubuntu-8.04")  It says it's for 8.04, but I used it for 8.10 too, and it worked fine. 

Running Ubuntu with Gnome, that is.  Kubuntu breaks OOo3.

A simple ```
 sudo apt-get remove openoffice* 
``` did the trick for me, though there are other, safer ways.  

But you may have to reinstall the system before any of that will work.  And if you're using Kubuntu, it won't work at all. 

Good luck!

---

Anyhow, glad you got it back.  Just remember that if you're using Kubuntu - even the kubuntu-desktop package in regular Ubuntu - you have to expect things not to work.  I would venture to say that 95% of problems you have with Kubuntu/kubuntu-desktop are KDE related.  

:)

### Post by atatistcheff on 2009-02-03
I hate that there are so many audio/video apps and none of them seem to be able to play all the formats.  I just try them one after another until one finally plays the mp3, wav, mpeg, mov, wmv, wma or whatever file I want to see/hear. ### Post by Tomatz on 2009-02-03
> **atatistcheff said:**
> I hate that there are so many audio/video apps and none of them seem to be able to play all the formats.  I just try them one after another until one finally plays the mp3, wav, mpeg, mov, wmv, wma or whatever file I want to see/hear.  Why can't the base installation come with a media player that just works?

You need to install all the codecs, then you can do everything in totem Including DVD:

```
sudo apt-get install gstreamer0.10-plugins-ugly gstreamer0.10-plugins-ugly-multiverse gstreamer0.10-plugins-bad gstreamer0.10-plugins-bad-multiverse gstreamer0.10-ffmpeg libdvdcss2
```

Reboot (for good measure)

### Post by jbysmith on 2009-02-05
> **acidboot3r said:**
> The thing I hate about in Ubuntu is the fact that it isn't exactly noob friendly. For some computers, they will experience problems using the OS, and to fix it, you always have to configure some important file (most of the time xorg.conf or GRUB), or use terminal or do a whole lot of complex stuff. I've always found a problem while using Linux Distributions, but I'm still sticking to it! ^_^

Good that you're sticking with it.  #1 tip to remember that it's not Windows; just different ways of doing thing.  I personally prefer having individual configuration files located in obvious places versus the "registry dumpster" of Windows.  I find it a lot easier to edit settings in a single text file than chasing down some obscure class ID in the registry.  Some settings are a no brainer in the registry if you know where they are, but for the computer novice it can be a nightmare, and potentially lethal to the OS if they mess up.

Some people say the terminal is a hindrance. For the absolute first timer, maybe, but you definitely want to get used to it.  Once you get the hang of what its capable of, it's absurdly brilliant and powerful.  Sure I have a pretty GUI and all its 3D rendered goodness, but I can just bang out tasks faster in a console. Forget anything you know about Window's command prompt.  Comparing the terminal to Cmd is like, I dunno, comparing a Ferrari to bellybutton lint.  Well that's not exactly fair; I find the lint more useful than Cmd.exe.

> **OzzyFrank said:**
> As for sound, I never had a problem till the kernel update last night. Before, my Audigy card was much more easily picked up than in Windows, where it needed drivers installed, but then I lost sound. But at least booting into the previous kernel at GRUB gives me my sound back.

I ran into this once with my Audigy as well. I regularly compile my own kernel against the Ubuntu repo source. I have a couple USB devices that I really don't want powered up when the system is turned off.  (An LCD display among other things.)  I apply a patch that tells USB to power down when the kernel shuts down.  One of the Ubuntu kernel updates, for whatever reason, had a lot of the PCI audio cards disabled, which killed my Audigy.  Had to turn them back on, re-recompile, and was good to go.  Go figure. 

> **Miss_Hopeful said:**
> I miss Windows Media player and Windows movie maker...havn't fogured out how to install one that runs with Ubuntu...then again...maybe it's easy and I'm just retarded. Who knows.

Heh no, not retarded.  Just goes to getting used to a non Windows environment.  A Windows user jumping in feet first into a Mac for example will be equally lost.

Personally I can't stand Media Player,aka WiMP, as there's some formats it just won't play, and lots and lots of phoning home.  (In Windows, I prefer Xion or WinAmp for audio, and video you just cant beat Media Player Classic).  There's some quality Linux native programs to try though.  Amarok is arguably one of the best players, period.  Personally, under Gnome, I prefer Exaile.  Pretty light, sits in the tray, and pops up a notification with album art on a track change.  Very simplistic, which is all I want.  There's a *lot* of others that are worth checking too.  

I won't argue about Movie Maker.  Working with the evil WMA format aside, it's actually rather nice for a light movie editor for people who don't need a full blown Premiere type solution. Alternatives might be Kino or LiVES perhaps. Little more in-depth than Movie Maker though.

One site that I like for looking through more popular apps is getdeb.net.  Many of those applications are in the repository already (or are there but may be out of date), but if not, you can download a .deb archive and install it that way.

### Post by mikeize on 2009-02-05
I don't hate anything about Ubuntu really.  It does some things better than, and some things worse than Windows.  Overall, I'd say the balance is in favor of Ubuntu, but it's pretty close... especially considering all the tweaking I've *had* to do to get Ubuntu working right.

Having said that, my wife has occasional gripes about Ubuntu.  Perhaps most importantly is mediocre support for Chinese characters (display, input and printing). Unfortunately, printing in general works MUCH better with Ubuntu, but when she transfers her files from her windows eeepc, some formatting and characters fail to print.

### Post by SeePU on 2009-02-07
> **RaiCoss said:**
> Yeah I had a bug kinda like that. The first time I used 8.10, and installed the graphics driver, it took out my WIFI, and refused to detect it afterwards, so I went back to XP, until that got nailed by another virus, came back to Ubuntu and never had had a problem again until just recently!

What about trying another distro?  I knew someone who used Gentoo and had WINE going and games.  I think he played COD 4, WOW and others.  Which ones do you play?  I don't play games but if I did, I'd probably dual boot and have XP on a partition.  It would be much easier.  Surely, you don't have to worry about viruses if you are only booting up Windows to play games unless you are playing online games?  

I think Fedora, Gentoo and Debian (and other Debian-based distros) would be okay for games/WINE other than the *ubuntus.  But, even if you use WINE, aren't you losing a few fps, anyway?  I am not sure but I wonder if everything is optimized even if you use WINE.  But, there are Linux gamers who find it acceptable.  

I don't like how Ubuntu has decided to remove options and do a 'Windows-like' strategy of reducing the number of functions or customization options.  The most recent issue of not allowing extra utilities or customization of my alsa / sound driver is what is turning me off most right now.  I have had problems installing binary Nvidia drivers before and only twice have I ever lost sound (completely).  The other case was in Debian and I used alsaconf and it restored it immediately.  Ubuntu has this forum and a zillion posts for how to deal with sound issues but none have helped me.  Debian still has the alsaconfig utility which RESTORED sound so guess which one is more frustrating?  What am I supposed to do?  Re-install because no matter what I do, I don't have sound?  So, I have to do all the customization, software installs et al. all over again because Ubuntu decides to take away a sound utility?  What if the binary Nvidia driver install removes sound AGAIN?!?  I like to have up-to-date video drivers but I can't have them on Ubuntu because I risk losing sound and if I do, I have to read for days to try to find a solution?!?

### Post by jjpcexpert on 2009-02-19
> **jrusso2 said:**
> Things I dislike about Ubuntu.

1. The use of meta packages.  This makes it hard to uninstall certain apps without uninstalling the whole gnome desktop.  Its also bad from a resource use stand point and slows Ubuntu down as it loads unnecessary things into memory.

2.  The free software only stance makes it difficult for people who are new users.  It should be a stance like we will use free software wherever possible but not to the point to inconvenience the users.

3.  Six month release cycle means buggy software releases.  You see this every time there is a new release. Instead of doing six months releases they should do yearly and upgrade the current distro better as far as newer apps and even newer kernel where needed.

4. Ugly Brown theme, nuff said.
This is turning into a flamewar. Can the admins help? Steer to the right direction (in this case lock it)

### Post by anthonie on 2009-02-23
On a daily basis I use: Ubuntu on my workmachine, dualbooted with Vista and I use XP on the other machines here in the office. At home, so, privately, I run a Gentoo install that I never dared to use as a production environment.

Things have changed so now, in 2009, when the question pops up: What do you hate about Ubuntu?, the answer will be

I hate how hard it is to say it out in the open: Ubuntu is slowly getting worse.

I've been a user since 6.04 and what started as a fine system where you had to tweak a couple of things to have a fully stable system after that, I now am stuck with a 64bit version of 8.10 that suffers from constant lockups (the gray/black out screens not reacting) to fullblown crashes.

I hate the fact that no longer I can get other people to start using Linux, in order to save myself some time not having to clean up their cluttered machines, repeatedly. I used to install Ubuntu on people's machines, after I had cleaned theirs out more than two times. It used to be a process of installing, a little bit of tweaking in order to get all the multi-media running and after that receiving the compliments from a very surprised owner that never expected it's machine to be able to run so fast and reliable. A full fledged operating system was installed and generally speaking, I never had to look at the machine again: It would always run fine.

Recently I've had to spend a lot of time fixing people's machines and it becomes increasingly harder to convince people that Ubuntu or Linux in general is actually a time-saver. Several people have asked me to reverse the migration process. Surely, no joy in that.

Today, after several crashes, I started thinking about installing Gentoo on my production machine. Sure, it's a lot of work and it takes quite a bit of time. However, my Gentoo machine back home has been running stable for years now and perhaps it deserves at least the chance that Ubuntu has been given over the years.

Sadly, there is no way, with my limited knowledge, that I would start spreading the Gentoo install, the same way I used to with Ubuntu installs.

Now that truly sucks.

---

runs so slow compared to some other distro's

everything else rocks :guitar:

### Post by jbysmith on 2009-02-24
> **northwestuntu said:**
> runs so slow compared to some other distro's

everything else rocks :guitar:

It's not tooooo bad once you take things into your own hands; disable unnecessary services, rebuild the kernel (Master Kernel Thread is god in that department), things like that.  But yea the out of the box performance has been getting slow; I definitely notice a difference from say Feisty Fawn.  

If I have a complaint against Ubuntu, it would be the "everything and the kitchen sink" attitude.  But since they're trying to cater to *everyone*, I can see the reasoning behind it.  A quote from another forum put it nicely, this isn't by me, *"I got away from XP because I felt forced into their idea of how my computer should be, and I feel that Ubuntu is becoming slightly more like that.  You will install pulse audio, you will have synaptic, you will have 14 million packages that you may not need or want, just because we say you want them." *

Great for the perfect out of the box just get it going setup, but I prefer a more minimalistic approach, hence Arch being my desktop.  Start with the bare bones and build up, not start with everything and build down. Most definitely not bashing Ubuntu; love the distro and for people who just want to install and go as quickly as possible, it's perfect.

### Post by jbysmith on 2009-02-25
> **jukingeo said:**
> As I mentioned to Anthonie above, version 8.04 wasn't all that it was cracked up to be.  In fact at the time of it's release many were touting IT was the worst Ubuntu release.  I was about to agree, but then 6 months later most of the issues were resolved.  So when Intrepid came out I was hesitant to switch.  Good thing I didn't.  I found out that Intrepid has quite a bit of problems with USB devices.   After I spent months to get my USB devices to work in 8.04, I am certainly not about to switch any time soon.  When the next version of Ubuntu comes out, THEN I will look into Intrepid. Not before.Geo

That's certainly a very viable option, and I know some people who feel the same way; they either stick solely with the LTS builds, or stay one version behind under the rationale that by the time the next one is released, the previous one is fairly solid.  They won't go to 8.10 till 9.04 is released, and so on.  Shoot I know a guy who still runs Feisty Fawn, just because it's rock solid, works fast, and doesn't let him down.  He doesn't care about the latest versions of Compiz and the like, just wants it to work.

You got to deal with the trade-off between bleeding edge and stability; more often than not you won't get both.  Windows is kind of the same way. XP is very solid because it's been out for *years*; it had its fair share of problems when it was released.  Vista was a bug ridden disaster when it went gold, nearing service pack two and now I'd consider it only a partial train wreck.  Win7 looks good (for Windows) but I'm sure it'll have its fair share of problems too at first.

My servers run the Ubuntu Server 8.04 LTS build.  I don't need latest and greatest, I want it to run for a million years without a glitch, which it does nicely.

### Post by Name change on 2009-02-25
OK be warned rant comes this way.
First of all let me get one thing clear I like (K)Ubuntu but sometimes I just really irritated by many things.
So this is the list of things that irritate me:
I sometimes feel like second-class citizen using KDE.
When I look at some other distros like SuSE I feel that most of dev efoort goes to Gnome instead of KDE.
And also on Ubuntu Brainstorm KDE specific ideas get a lot less votes than the most useless Gnome ideas.

I passionately hate the first version of Kubuntu I came across - Gutsy Gibbon I almost lost my mind with it. It broke if you tried to use adept to upgrade install or anything else. But on the other hand it threw me into the water and I had to learn how to swim. So the first thing I learned was
sudo apt-get update / upgrade and install.

I also (and I fear this will bring many bad responses to this post) hate some Ubuntu users. Not in particular, no. But as an group.
If you remember the whole Woman can't go to school due to Ubuntu incident and the whole "hate e-mail" that that TV station got from it.
I've heard the term "Linux Youth" before that incident but until then I was sure it's just a joke. But then it became just too real.
I don't like all that my OS (Ubuntu) is the best on the world and anyone that defies that will be flamed (burned).
It might work well for you but that's no excuse to go around and try to "convert" strangers to Ubuntu. Some might (yes) still prefer Windows (oh the humanity) over Ubuntu. And your constant pushing of Ubuntu will only dissuade then from using it.
I didn't start using Ubuntu and joined the community to be a part of "spiritual" community, but to be a user of a normal OS.
That's why I feel that not promoting Ubuntu, but more casually mentioning it to those that are interested in such stuff will go further than installing it on your grandmother's dog computer.

OK I ranted enough. I'm at peace now.
So back to "loving" Kubuntu :D

---

### Post by jbysmith on 2009-02-25
> **Primož Papi&#269 said:**
> OK be warned rant comes this way.
First of all let me get one thing clear I like (K)Ubuntu but sometimes I just really irritated by many things.
So this is the list of things that irritate me:
I sometimes feel like second-class citizen using KDE.

I'm not a fan of KDE myself, but I do try it from time to time to see how KDE4 is progressing.  I have to agree with you though, the KDE side of things just never felt really complete with Ubuntu.  At least for me, I've always had issues here and there with it.  I've read posts too where people have had nothing but a great experience with it; that's the nature of Linux, gotta pick what works for you.

Their Gnome implementation is flat out *excellent*, no arguments there.  But for KDE4, I personally felt that openSUSE has done a better job.  (I *really* don't like SUSE's Gnome though.)  Another KDE4 distro that's really really good is Chakra, which is a liveCD based on Arch and KDEMod, which is a nice alternative if you just wanted to try it but not go through the whole manual install procedure.  (Defeats the purpose of Arch, granted, but it's still well done.)

### Post by jbysmith on 2009-02-25
> **Primo&#382 said:**
> I agree with you on cahkra did try the Alpha CD and I love it!
Next time I'll install Linux I might even change to Chakra or maybe Arch.
I still feel that Arch might be better as you're in "charge" what to install and what not.

It's definitely not for everyone, as it was designed with a different audience in mind than Ubuntu.  (Build it yourself vs click click done)  But I found it an excellent balance of a rolling release, a binary repository, the BSD style init system (I *love* the rc.conf file), and it has a Gentoo-like Portage system is available if you want to build from source.  Doesn't hold your hand in any way, but the wiki is fantastic.  Absolutely zero bloat, unless you put it in there.  You build it from the ground up; when the installer is done, you're left with a terminal login, nothing more.

Chakra is really nice, but it also hides some of the stuff that you really should know as a first-timer, especially if you're coming from a different distro.. things just don't work the same as with a Debian based distro.  It's an excellent build, but if you run into trouble, you can get lost pretty easily if you don't understand how Arch works under the hood.

If you like to tinker, Arch fits the bill perfectly.  If you just want to get it done *now*, Ubuntu is stellar there.  Can't really say one is better than the other, as they have different people in mind as the intended audience, and in the end they both perform amazingly well.

---

### Post by yyyc186 on 2009-02-26
> **jespdj said:**
> I don't understand. There's Ubuntu and Kubuntu, so GNOME and KDE are both available. You can also install them both on your machine. For example, if you start with Ubuntu, install the package **kubuntu-desktop** and you'll be able to choose GNOME or KDE from the login screen.

The issue is that when you purchase or otherwise obtain an installation DVD, you don't get the option to install both front ends like you do with SuSE and most other distros.  Most of us have download limits.  When you obtain a Ubuntu installation CD/DVD and install it, you have to manually select the KDE stuff after installation is complete, then download over 600Meg.  A lot of ISPs limit users to 300MEG per day.

A combined DVD needs to be made for each release.  I have asked On-Disk to look into making one of these.  They also see the need.

### Post by yyyc186 on 2009-02-26
> **sharks said:**
> What are the things that u hate in Ubuntu?
In order of annoyance:

1)  No combined DVD available.  KDE and icky-nasty-Gnome need to be on the same installation DVD so they can both be installed at the same time.  This split package thing causes a lot of problems.

2)  Evolution seems to have Microsoft quality developers working on it.  Every automatic update seems to break something else on it.

3)  t1-cyrillic package not installed by default.  I don't know who thought that up since most people need the fonts.

4)  CUPs team seems to be tossing the dice a lot.  In the past month I have received automatic updates which broke nearly all of my printing.  KWord was the only thing working correctly, so naturally there was a broken automatic update which removed KWord from my system.  Thanks guys.  I can type all I want in OpenOffice, I just can't print to a decent printer.

5)  Automatic Updates and Synaptic installations don't have a built in download manager.  A lot of ISPs have a 300Meg/day DL limit.  Those "features" need to have settings where a user can configure them to only pull down 150Meg per day and they continue the installation once they have everything.

6)  64-bit version seems to get a very short end of the stick when it comes to development and bug fixes.

### Post by jbysmith on 2009-02-26
I can agree with that; openSUSE's KDE4 implementation was done *very* well.  Not sure who I like better, theirs or Chakra (Arch KDEMod).  Ubuntu's version, not so much.  I'm not a KDE fan anyway, but Kubuntu always felt a little rough and not quite finished.  Just my opinion; I know some people love it to death, and that's a good thing.

Gotta agree with YaST too.  On paper, it's excellent.  A centralized management system that does it all is a good thing.  In reality, the thing irks the hell out of me and keeps openSUSE off my desktop full time.  Again, just my opinion, tons of people use it, and I have no problems recommending it to people as it's a quality distro for sure.  At least for KDE; Gnome not so much.  As far as I'm concerned, the Gnome team may as well rename the project Gnobuntu, as I've yet to find a distro that does Gnome better.

I can agree about Evolution too.. it's just a littly quirky yet.  Has a lot of potential for sure, but personally I install Thunderbird right off.  Then again, I don't need the calendar and stuff anyway.

One other thing I dislike about Ubuntu is the release schedule.  It's just too rigid.  I still prefer a rolling release anyway, but I can live with a six month cycle.  But more often than not the releases go public with a bunch of bugs in them.. The CD eject bug with 8.10 for example, no idea how that could have been missed.  I'd much rather see a "It's ready when it's ready" attitude.. not as far as Debian takes it though :D

I really hate the current deal(s) with Amazon.  Anyone doing business with Amazon will eventually find themselves living on the street and begging for food.  They put just about everyone out of business who chooses to do business with them.  I fear the same fate will befall the makers of Ubuntu.

### Post by spliffeh on 2009-02-27
Ubuntu's installation stinks at dealing with partitions and multiple drives, setting up grub, dealing with the mbr.

Besides that, when you're installing there are 7 steps. On the final step if you dont hit the 'Advanced' button and tell it how you want grub set up then your just TOTALLY screwed. The grub settings should not be hidden away in an 'advanced' button... at the least the button should be labelled 'CLICK HERE OR I'LL TOTALLY MESS UP YOUR BOOT SETTINGS'

Maybe it's just me.. here's my setup

sda1 - windows
sda2 - ntfs storage
sda3 - ubuntu
sda4 - swap
sdb - ntfs storage

Grub lives on sda3. Ubuntu has screwed up this installation 5/5 times I think... Sabayon doesn't do this to me... Fedora didn't do this to me... endless hours of fixing grub and other b.s.... the last install it decided to wipe my sda2 partition... it just ate the fat table (ntfs table?).. other then that it installed normally.... as I type this im running a Diskinternal's partition recovery thingy to get my 750,000 ntfs files back.... this is bullsh-t... if I was new to linux I would run back to windows and never look back.... great way to permenantly scar someone for life

[B]Ubuntu needs to be more friendly when there are multiple o/s and partitions and harddrives involved. It should be 1000% clear what Ubuntu is about to do to partitions and things that affect boot up. It's a very bad first impression for linux when you can't boot and that VERY UNFRIENDLY grub> prompt greets you (if your lucky)... there is NO EXCUSE for mistakes here, and it is not the user's fault... your installation should be bulletproof.  This should be a TOP PRIORITY for the Ubuntu team... if noobs can't install safely/properly _nothing_ else matters. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
[/B]

---

### Post by jbysmith on 2009-02-27
Maybe I was lucky, dunno, but I've never had an issue with the installer's partition editor, and I've done it with multiple operating systems and storage formats in the mix.  My current setup is XP, Windows7 just for giggles, Vista for testing stuff, and two Linux distros;  My main, and another for my "messing around" distro that gets changed on a near daily basis.  Plus a few NTFS partitons that I can access across the board from all the OS's.  This is split up across two 500GB drives.

Not sure why it was looking at putting GRUB on SDA3.  You want that on your primary boot partition, regardless of the OS.  For me anyway, it's always done that correctly.  You want it to replace the Windows MBR, or you'll never be able to boot into Ubuntu.  (Of course, you'll need a bootable Winders CD and run FixMBR if you remove Linux again to restore it's original boot loader.)  Once in a while it'll incorrectly identify a storage partition as a full fledged OS and it'll add a boot entry; no biggie there, simply remove that line and move on.  It does that quite a bit with my storage stuff.

As far as it eating a partition outright goes, that shouldn't have happened unless you accidentally told it to.  (A power outage during the partitioning is always funny too.)  As long as you tell it to *use* a partition as NTFS or FAT32, but not format it, it won't touch it.  (IE, make sure that little check box is clear, and triple check the last page before you commit, it tells you whats getting formatted.)

### Post by kansasnoob on 2009-02-28
> **lukedupree said:**
> I hate that I have been trying to get it to run on my system for months and there is no answer. 8.04 sound issues and 8.10 video driver issues. Who ever said it just works? Hmmm!  Not

Actually those two things were sort of what I was thinking about when I saw this thread title as I was heading over to Jaunty testing!

There was a period of time (about 3 to 4 months) that Pulse Audio just drove me nuts in 8.04. Sometimes it would seem that the earliest versions of this tutorial would get things straightened out:

[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=789578](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=789578)

But then occasionally things would still crop up so I kept quite a lot of machines running Gutsy ......... nothing wrong with that! And it now seems that Pulse Audio is working great in 8.04.2 (for me anyway)!

In Intrepid xorg drives me nuts! Specifically in Hardy I could manually add "openchrome" to the "Devices" section of '/etc/X11/xorg.conf' and obtain the proper resolution for the 23 machines I built with VIA UniChrome video chipsets.

A recent thread about that:
[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1041680](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1041680)

But still, I've always found a "work around". At first it was sticking with Gutsy that worked flawlessly with these 23 machines when I built them. Now it seems to be sticking with Hardy (8.04.2).

But having tried many other distros: Fedora, Mandriva, PCLOS-Gnome, MEPIS, SUSE, etc, etc. I must say that Ubuntu and it's derivatives (both official and otherwise - Linux Mint and Crunchbang both deserve honorable mentions) has been the absolute best.

This community is one of the biggest reasons I say that! With rare exception help comes in a friendly and informative manner!

I could give many, many more reasons why I love Ubuntu than I could "what I hate about Ubuntu"!

### Post by jbysmith on 2009-02-28
> **SeePU said:**
> Okay, let's get this straight.  There are a lot of distro forums out there but the Ubuntu ones have the most 'negative' posts and yes, it also has people bashing those who criticize it and report on their problems.

Well they'll all have their faults, no OS is perfect.  I also frequent the Arch forums, and they get their fair share too.  Granted, no matter where you go, there's always going to people who a) Don't know how to ask for help politely, they rant and assume it's their God given right, or b) Those who have a low tolerance to people asking questions.  

I kinda sorta lean towards option B, but I keep it to myself :D  Chances are, you're not the first person having that particular problem.  Man, Google, and/or a forum search will 99.9% of the time have the solution.

Personally, I'd chalk up more negative posts due to Ubuntu being the most popular and it's typically the first distro that a "Linux newbie" tries. They'll have zero experience, unreasonable expectations, and just don't know what to do after double-clicking a setup.exe file.  

This isn't pointed at anyone in particular, just a broad generalization.

### Post by amylase on 2009-03-02
I used Microsoft OS from DOS 3.0 -> 3.3 -> 4.0 -> 4.2 -> 5.0 -> 6 -> 6.22 -> Windows 3 -> 3.1 -> 95 -> 98 -> ME -> XP -> Vista. A month ago I switched to Ubuntu. One of the key things that really made it hard for me to switch earlier was Office. I have been hooked on Office for school and work. 

I started using Microsoft Office since some 15 years ago. Each version just gets better. The latest Office 2007 tried to group things a bit differently which took a bit of getting use to, but turned out much more efficient than before. OneNote 2007 was also absolutely wonderful as a note keeping tool. In contrast, Open Office even though open source, cannot compare with Microsoft Office in terms of functionality, flexibility, general appeal, and ease of use. So what I hate most about Ubuntu or Linux would be not able to run the latest Microsoft Office or not having something as good as it.

Even now I am still using Microsoft Office 2007 through WINE. From time to time things crash or some functions totally don't work, but I'll just have to accept that for now. 

Other minor things I dislike about Ubuntu is lack of drivers for devices. My Canoscan LiDE 70 for example is sitting there gathering dust while I contemplate either read up on some tute about Vmware and Samba just so I can access it via Windows again, or buy LiDE 60 which is supported by SANE. Neither of these seem ideal to me. Also some games require several steps to install. First you lack this, then you lack that. To install this you need to compile. To compile you need to configure it properly etc. etc. It's fun to work on these things and read HOW-TO's when I am free but not when I want things to work straight away with just one click.

Having complained so much, that's about it really. Overall, Ubuntu is a much more enjoyable experience than Windows (and I feel cool using Ubuntu, that's the most important haha).

---

### Post by jbysmith on 2009-03-02
> **Frak said:**
> I hate that every linux user thinks they're immune to malware.
(sarcasm in true form)

Quite true really, I see people say they love being immune a lot.  The occasional bug can be a security issue; SSH, FTPd, Apache and the like all had their share of critical bugs.  Exposing your box to the internet when it's not configured properly is a hacker playground.  Running untrusted code from outside the repository is just like Windows; could be good, could smash your system.  Anyone manage to get a virus in Wine yet?  Going to happen sooner or later.  Not directed at anyone, but Linux's security system won't cover against user stupidity either; running daily as root and the like.

> **jrock612 said:**
> I think jbysmith is a she.  I think.

Right?
Right.

### Post by jbysmith on 2009-03-02
> **jrock612 said:**
> How right you are.  I read somewhere once that a smart user on a Windows system is safer than a dumb user on a Linux system.  The developers can't predict *every* possible stupid move a stupid user can make.

I'd believe that.  I've used Windows since forever.  My general security is a basic two-way firewall, an on-demand only spyware scanner (plus occasionally the VirusTotal web site), and Sandboxie as a safety net when needed.  That's it.  No antivirus, no resident antispyware, no HIPS, nothing dragging my system down to a crawl.  My current XP installation has been crapware free for years without a reinstallation just from not being a dumbass when it comes to download habits.  My number one level of defense is just knowing what I'm doing and not being careless.

On the other hand, my Linux knowledge is still fairly mid-level at best.  I can easy miss some little configuration somewhere, and my system could be wide open waiting for a hacker to break in and wreck the system.  I just don't know enough yet to claim that I'm 100% secure, and brag "feel free to try and hack me."  I keep my server services to a minimum, blocked from the Internet via the router, I don't run as root and cross my fingers.  Anything above that, I tread very very lightly.

Just based on that, to be honest I feel more secure in Windows, just because I know what's going on.  In Linux, I'm not sure yet, so I have that doubt I'm 100% secure.

> **jrock612 said:**
> Woohoo, do I win a prize for knowing that? 
No, I saw that you visited my "About Me" page, cheater :D

### Post by LoREZ on 2009-04-30
I like ubuntu enough that I've been following it release-by-release until it worked properly on my machine (several years).  To this day I'm forced to remove one of my SATA cables in order to install it properly w/ dual boot (probably a GRUB issue).

My real problem with ubuntu is probably really a linux problem in general:

If you are working in the GUI, you should never have to drop to the CLI (within reason); if you are working in the CLI you should never have to go to the GUI (within reason).

If a program has a graphic interface, it should always request the root password if it can't get permission to do something; it should never force you to find and edit a configuration file manually.  That's just stupid.  I'm not against being allowed to edit these files directly (I'm in favor of freedom), but let's get smart about interface design and workflow.

If basic functionality (like playing a DVD) isn't pre-installed, the user should be warned and told how to correct the issue when he tries to use that feature.  The fix should be automated.  I shouldn't have to dive through forums for that sort of information (though I'm happy it is here to be found).  In fact, if you try to do anything the system isn't set up for, it should tell you how to do it and assist you.  Such things are getting better, but still not there yet, imo.

Such things get in the way of the user experience and are an unnecessary barrier to efficiency.

---

### Post by laltopi on 2009-05-01
1. Lack of support for encrypted partitions in the standard gui installer. Using an encrypted partition requires using "alternate install disk" with a text based installer; and asks too many questions. Fedora's anaconda handles encrypted partitions a lot better.
2. Burning coasters (CDs) is still the most prominently displayed option when downloading for install. Net install or writing USB flash drives should be more preferentially displayed - to save the environment.

### Post by caravel on 2009-05-20
> **growled said:**
> Same here. It's really screwed up.
It's improved vastly in terms of support and in terms of the code ATI have released to open source developers.  Nvidia have better drivers but do not release any code.

I have a Radeon HD3650 running on Debian Lenny with the stable fglrx driver from the repository, and I can run games like Quake4, Doom3, UT2K4 and Neverwinter Nights all natively on the highest quality settings with a good FPS.

ATI's fglrx driver is not perfect but it's a lot better than it was.  The moral is that if you want it to just *work* - run stable software.  Ubuntu is not stable software.

### Post by caravel on 2009-05-20
> **growled said:**
> I have a x1300 card and it's now considered legacy in Linux. No fglrx for me, only the open source drivers. That's why I said it was screwed up.
No, since Catalyst 9.4 (the latest is 9.5) the ATI Linux drivers have not supported 7/8/9/X series graphics cards.  You can install an older version of the fglrx driver, such as in the 9.3 release, and it will support your card, but won't run on your current xorg version.  The only solution is not to upgrade to 9.04 - stay 8.10 or move to another distro.  Personally if I was in that position I would choose the slightly older driver and keep my card *working* rather than just upgrade for the sake of upgrading.

### Post by Sewje on 2009-05-20
Well I can only say what I hate about my Ubuntu installation. I notice there are some posts that mention things that isn't really Ubuntu's fault and can probably be fixed easily..., hopefully all the ones I list are genuine things I see wrong with my Ubuntu installation that currently have no easy solution to.

I'm really going to nit pick here!

First, minuet gui polish. Small glitches that happen during normal use like settings not getting saved, for example when I put my volume on low then reboot, the volume starts in mute mode. Switching videos to fullscreen then using player controls in fullscreen causes a brief flash of the desktop.
Sometimes custom taskbars don't unhide when I mouse over them, or they don't auto-hide properly.
I suppose GFX driver support isn't very strong currently. for anything other than nVidia for 3D.
Ubuntu runs nearly 10 degrees hotter than in Vista.
Firefox scrolling with mouse wheel or arrow keys isn't smooth enough.

Hmm thats about all I can think of... Even tho these things wouldn't stop me using Ubuntu they do detract from a better experience.
However the things I like about Ubuntu far outweight the dislikes and thats what matters most.

### Post by GuiGuy on 2009-05-20
Here's something to really hate; it's arrived with 9.04

* Burn a DVD on smart hardware, 20x SATA DVD writer
* media is quality disk, rated 16x write
* If I'm lucky, the first disk will write at about 10x. 
* If I am unlucky (most times), it writes < 2.4x
* Any subsequent disk will write at < 2.4x, unless I reboot.

In fairness, seeing the same thing on OpenSuse on the same hardware. Windows, ever reliable of course ;), burns at max speed of media. 

Cheers

### Post by MBybee on 2009-05-23
> **rkirk said:**
> 
...and, on the what I hate (it's a short list).
[LIST]
[*]6-month release cycle (openSUSE's 8-month release cycle is rather nice)
[*]nano is the default text editor in the commandline (vim's the only "real" editor)
[*]lack of root user (yeah, **sudo bash** kind of does the trick... but, still...)
[/LIST]

The root user is there (unix/linux won't work without it):
user@system:~$ sudo su -
root@system:~# 

Feel free to assign it a new password, whatever. The point is that it *shouldn't* be used directly on a regular basis, especially not by unix newbies.

Don't like the default text editor? Change it in your profile (.bashrc, etc). Just put the line "export /usr/bin/vim" or whatever at the end, and bang.
<Edit - that should have been "export EDITOR=/usr/bin/vim", which is why I shouldn't post tired :D>
Alternatively, type "sudo update-alternatives --config editor" and pick your fave that way. The maintainers of a distro need to pick what is easiest for the most people, and assume that people with a preference will set it themselves.
:D

### Post by joelholio on 2009-05-27
After 3 weeks now with Ubuntu 9.0.4, it's clear to me that it's still a geek thing. No insult intended. If I want to use my pc as a browser and a word processor, all's well.

There's an assumption that the command line is still a viable solution - outdated for most of us - having spent 20 years in a DOS/Windows world, the last thing I wanna do is master another environment with a host of arcane commands.

Apps don't install easily (ie HJSplit)

Apps aren't readily 'there' after a supposedly error-free install (ie Unision). I wanna click an icon and let 'er rip.

No drivers for certain devices (ie scanner that's a few years old)

Apps just don't work as their Windows counterparts do (ie Avidemux ain't VirtualDub)

I could go on,,, but I'm too busy trying to get 'stuff' to work ...

Not giving up yet, but on the verge of going back to the dark side...

### Post by rasmus_ on 2009-05-27
I dislike (no hate here):

[LIST]
[*]The lack of binary compatibility as seen on (sorry) Windows.
[*]That I had to download and install drivers from NVIDIA using obscure methods in order to get the system to stop freezing using my GPU. The supplied drivers were to old for my GTX 280.
[*]That the aforementioned drivers made my system mad during a kernel upgrade (no surprise though).
[*]That the login screen got screwed by my 30" screen and made the font extremely small. (Manual edit of xorg.conf fixed it)
[*]That ubuntu comes with an old version of emerald.
[*]That I need to edit configuration files manually for many simple customizations.
[*]I miss Photoshop and 3ds max.
[/LIST]

I'm aware that some of the above points is out of control for ubuntu as a distribution. But it's the things that annoy me. Take it or leave it ;)

---

### Post by luvr on 2009-05-27
> **joelholio said:**
> There's an assumption that the command line is still a viable solution - outdated for most of us - having spent 20 years in a DOS/Windows world, the last thing I wanna do is master another environment with a host of arcane commands.

I would be somewhat more careful about making this comment. The new Windows server releases are moving to a command-line environment. Microsoft has apparently figured out that servers work better **without** a GUI.

That's not to say that you may be right as far as client PCs are concerned--though that depends on your viewpoint. Many procedures can be documented infinitely more easily when executed from the command line, and that is often the faster solution as well. Still, I can do much (though not necessarily all) work from the GUI under Linux--but I'm more comfortable with the command line for many tasks.

> Apps aren't readily 'there' after a supposedly error-free install (ie Unision).
In the case of Unison, that's really just a matter of installing the appropriate package--i.e., **unison-gtk** (use *Synaptic Package Manager* if you want to avoid the command line for this operation.) That will create a Unison icon in your Applications menu. If you want it on the desktop, then just right-click on the Unison entry in the menu, and select *"Add this launcher to desktop."*

> Not giving up yet, but on the verge of going back to the dark side...
I can only give you the advice to use whatever works best for you... I know that Linux may be hard to get used too (been there, too...), and some things likely won't work as easily or as well as under Windows. Ultimately, it's your decision to make. But if you point out any concrete issues that you struggle with, then the Linux community (or any single knowledgeable Linux user that understands your specific frustration) will at least have a chance to look into them; and then, it's up to them do decide if they want to do anything about them.

---

### Post by joelholio on 2009-05-27
> **luvr said:**
> I would be somewhat more careful about making this comment. The new Windows server releases are moving to a command-line environment. Microsoft has apparently figured out that servers work better **without** a GUI.

I cannot speak to the user community in Belgium, but server operators are a small portion of the user community here in states. I can't imagine any corporate IT manager suggesting that end users return the a command prompt to conduct business.... Same goes for the home computer user so until the Linux community moves away from it's dependency on Terminal to make things happen, it will be relegated to the server community and the hardcore - not on end user desktops - the vast majority of us...

> 
In the case of Unison, that's really just a matter of installing the appropriate package--i.e., **unison-gtk** (use *Synaptic Package Manager* if you want to avoid the command line for this operation.) That will create a Unison icon in your Applications menu. If you want it on the desktop, then just right-click on the Unison entry in the menu, and select *"Add this launcher to desktop."***My point exactly**. I installed it using SPM without errors - yet nowhere on my desktop or under any menu will you find a reference to Unison. 

Oh, and let's talk about using alien to get some software to the point we can even use SPM - now there's a feature every one is begging to use.

The topic of the thread was what we hate about Ubuntu - I don't hate anything about it - it's actually pretty cool and stable, but it's way far from being an OS for the end user despite press to the contrary and I blame that primarily on arcane application install routines and the mind set of developers that 'theres always Terminal' ...

---

### Post by luvr on 2009-05-28
> **joelholio said:**
> **My point exactly**. I installed it using SPM without errors - yet nowhere on my desktop or under any menu will you find a reference to Unison.
Strange... I install Unison (the *"unison-**gtk**"* package on just about all of my Ubuntu systems, and I have never run into that problem. Well, to be completely honest, I **did** run into it the first time that I installed Unison--but that was because I installed the plain *"unison"* package, instead of  *"unison-**gtk**"*...

> Oh, and let's talk about using alien to get some software to the point we can even use SPM - now there's a feature every one is begging to use.
I don't get the point of *"alien"* either... If a software package was not meant to be installed on your system, then why bother? If you really, **really, _really_** want it that badly, then compile it from source. But that's nothing for an end user to undertake, of course...

> it's way far from being an OS for the end user despite press to the contrary and I blame that primarily on arcane application install routines
Hmmm... Depends on how you define *"end user,"* I suppose. To me, *"end users"* are those people how couldn't care less about installation procedures, because they don't want to *install* software--they want to just **use** it instead. I keep running into what I call *"end users"* who are **terrified** by the suggestion that they may have to install some software under Windows... even if I assure them that it's just a simple matter of *click, next, next, done.* For one such end user, I had installed Ubuntu in dual-boot, and at one point, he was looking for some software package that wasn't installed on his Ubuntu sytem, but that was available in the repositories. I walked him, via the phone, through the *Synaptic Package Manager* installation process, and he replied: "Wow! Is it *that* simple to install software in Ubuntu?"

> the mind set of developers that 'theres always Terminal' ...
Well, yes, there **is** always Terminal--for the developers and for advanced users. The people that I classify as *"end users"* don't need the Terminal, though.

---

### Post by juanoleso on 2009-05-28

### Post by NightwishFan on 2009-05-30
> **Onizuka89 said:**
> Only thing I hate with Ubuntu is that it seems to hate games that take the whole screen :( except that it's not much to complain about ^^

That is likely a compiz problem. Just run metacity --replace in the run command dialog, and when you are done gaming run compiz --replace. Or do what I do and add a keyboard shortcut for those commands. I use ctrl+alt+c and ctrl+alt+m.

### Post by zugu on 2009-06-02
I hate repositories and the centralised way of installing software. I hate the job purpose of the middlemen called package maintainers. I hate the fact that I can't install stuff like in OS X, by dragging and dropping, that I can't easily have two different versions of the same app installed side by side. I hate compiling. I hate the command line. I hate the default, two taskbars layout in GNOME. I hate KDE more than anything. I hate CLI commands with cryptic names written in lowercase. I hate the fact that in some versions of Ubuntu the official way of updating Firefox or OppenOffice.org or even VLC media player required a dist-upgrade. I hate to upgrade every 6 months. I hate the repository mega-freeze development style. I hate zealots and fanbois.

I could go on and on and on.
