### Post by Saint Angeles on 2009-06-02
> **zugu said:**
> I hate repositories and the centralised way of installing software. I hate the job purpose of the middlemen called package maintainers.
then use .deb files. honestly, i'd RATHER have the OS take care of installing the updates for the hundreds of files on my computer without me having to browse countless websites hunting down .deb files. for me and many others, this is a superior option than the windows way of doing things and one of the many reasons i prefer ubuntu to windows. package maintainers make our jobs easier by doing the mundane work of collecting updates and dependencies for us.
> I hate the fact that I can't install stuff like in OS X, by dragging and dropping, that I can't easily have two different versions of the same app installed side by side.well i think drag+drop is childish.but it sounds like you dislike ubuntu because it doesn't work the way that your proprietary OSes work and you think of it as a bad thing. well, maybe OS X is for you then. and just curious, what applications would you like to install 2 different versions? i'm curious because i've never needed more than one version of something on my computer at a time. except, i do have firefox 3.1 and that 3.5beta thing on my computer... hmmm.
> I hate compiling. I hate the command line.don't compile and don't use the CLI. its that simple. i've installed ubuntu for several of my family members who have nothing but good things to say about ubuntu. they've never even seen a CLI or even know what it does. and compiling software is something that can be done on windows and mac as well... it just happens to be a hell of a lot easier on linux.
> I hate the default, two taskbars layout in GNOME. I hate KDE more than anything.i dislike the 2 panels as well. thats why, the first thing i do after an install is remove the top one and put everything i need on the bottom one. it takes less than a minute. it's prolly just because i'm super-used to the windows style and its easier for me, but for people without any prior windows experience, they won't be used to a single panel and might like the 2 panels. i have 3 friends who i converted to ubuntu who love the 2nd panel though...

and don't use KDE. that was easy.
> I hate CLI commands with cryptic names written in lowercase.linux differentiates between capital and lower case letters. this makes typing commands more clear and more exact. somebody can give you a short command and tell you exactly what it does and guess what? it does exactly what it is designed to do. the linux CLI is a super powerful tool that many many professionals love and i wouldn't remove it for the world. plus, are you honestly saying you like capital letter more than lowercase? i think capitals are hideous. thats why i don't type capitals that often.
> I hate the fact that in some versions of Ubuntu the official way of updating Firefox or OppenOffice.org or even VLC media player required a dist-upgrade.or just get a .deb file from getdeb. this will essentially make your update process exactly like the windows way and your problem is solved. 
> I hate to upgrade every 6 months. I hate the repository mega-freeze development style.
well maybe ubuntu isn't for you. because you have such a strong opinion on the matter, i would recommend a rolling release distro like Arch.
> I hate zealots and fanbois.they exist in the windows and ESPECIALLY in the mac world as well. you know what i hate? trolls. specifically, people who come to a forum with nothing but negative things to say because they get off on starting fights and causing chaos. from looking at your posts, it is obvious that this is your only reason for coming to these forums. plus, people who HATE ubuntu and go around telling people how bad it is are just as bad as people who LOVE it and tell people how good it is. actually... its prolly worse because you are not helping the world of open source any by your actions and will prolly turn people away from open source just because of your silly ideas of whats wrong with it.
> I could go on and on and on.so could i... but for everybody's sake, i'm glad you didn't.

### Post by zugu on 2009-06-03
@Saint Angeles: thanks for taking the time to reply, but there's nothing you can do to change my mind. I have legitimate reasons for disliking most of the things I dislike in Ubuntu.

> **Saint Angeles said:**
> then use .deb files.You didn't quite get my point. I can't stand repositories because they force a centralised way of installing software on the user. Of course, anyone can get debs from various other sources, but the official, recommended way is using the official repositories. I know it's easier to manage and install software using a repository, but it's a big trade-off. Some things have to be sacrificed and one has to be at the mercy of the package maintainer when it comes to updated software. I like installing software straight from upstream, fresh, just-released stuff. I hate waiting for the package maintainer to make a deb for me. In most of the cases there won't be newer versions of a package during the life of a Ubuntu release, because backporting stuff can destroy the fragile equilibrium of the package ecosystem. Repositories have advantages and disadvantages. I am prepared to install and update the programs I use by myself, as I do in Windows, and I want to be able to access latest versions of everything. Compiling stuff is not safe, since the package manager is not usually aware of compiled packages. Using third-party repositories is also not recommended. If getdeb offers updated stuff, why isn't that stuff already in the official backports? I'll tell you why: because the safety of that software cannot be guaranteed.


> **Saint Angeles said:**
> honestly, i'd RATHER have the OS take care of installing the updates for the hundreds of files on my computer without me having to browse countless websites hunting down .deb files. for me and many others, this is a superior option than the windows way of doing things and one of the many reasons i prefer ubuntu to windows.That's just how you like it. Most people are used to do things the Windows and OS X way. I would expect the same from a small OS trying to take on the giants.

> **Saint Angeles said:**
> package maintainers make our jobs easier by doing the mundane work of collecting updates and dependencies for us.Oh, please, they do it because there's no real alternative, other than all distributions uniting efforts and standardising on a base system (a la BSD) and a standard package manager and format. And we all know that won't happen. I used Firefox on Windows XP since v0.4. I didn't need to upgrade to Vista to use the latest version. Ubuntu would rather have me upgrade every 6 months to achieve the same thing. How is this convenient and logical?

> **Saint Angeles said:**
> well i think drag+drop is childish.but it sounds like you dislike ubuntu because it doesn't work the way that your proprietary OSes work and you think of it as a bad thing.Childish? Are you kidding me? It's not perfect, I agree, but it's as close to it as it can be. Retail utilities, video games, webware, anything can be installed in the most easy way. Deployment is a breeze, all dependencies are inside the app folder, one can have the latest stable versions of anything - no need to wait for someone to package it! Having it other way IS a bad thing.

> **Saint Angeles said:**
> and just curious, what applications would you like to install 2 different versions? i'm curious because i've never needed more than one version of something on my computer at a time. except, i do have firefox 3.1 and that 3.5beta thing on my computer... hmmm.Oh, the classic "I never needed this, so why should you?" rebuttal. Just because you don't have this problem, it doesn't mean other people don't.

> **Saint Angeles said:**
> don't compile and don't use the CLI. its that simple.Eventually, something will go awry and one will have to dive into the CLI. Sometimes there's no updated package for some application one needs, so compiling is the last hope. In a community where many upstream projects are just spitting sources, there just won't be enough manpower to create packages for everything. Look at Ubuntu Dapper: its 5 year support is ending this month. Its death is a painful one: it has **96 open CVE bugs, some of them are over 2 years old**. It's not that upstream didn't fix the bugs, it's just that there aren't enough people to backport fixes and security patches. I would dare to say it's also too difficult a task to do. Please, don't use the "who uses Ubuntu Dapper today, anyway" rebuttal, it's not about Dapper in particular, it's about the way of doing things in general.

> **Saint Angeles said:**
> i've installed ubuntu for several of my family members who have nothing but good things to say about ubuntu. they've never even seen a CLI or even know what it does.Good for them.

> **Saint Angeles said:**
> and compiling software is something that can be done on windows and mac as well... it just happens to be a hell of a lot easier on linux.Are you kidding me? Compile any big software you want in Ubuntu then tell me you're sure you know where all the compiled files reside. Tell me it's as easy to remove them as deleting a folder. Compiling is the same process on all OSes, it's the little things around the compiling process that matter.

> **Saint Angeles said:**
> i dislike the 2 panels as well. thats why, the first thing i do after an install is remove the top one and put everything i need on the bottom one. it takes less than a minute. it's prolly just because i'm super-used to the windows style and its easier for me, but for people without any prior windows experience, they won't be used to a single panel and might like the 2 panels. i have 3 friends who i converted to ubuntu who love the 2nd panel though...You think I'm so stupid I don't know how to remove the extra panel? It's the default arrangement that I hate. It's the decision of the GNOME developers I can't stand. It's also Ubuntu's decision, since distros are free to modify the interface as they wish before releasing.

> **Saint Angeles said:**
> and don't use KDE. that was easy.Of course I don't use it. But that doesn't mean I hate it less.

> **Saint Angeles said:**
> linux differentiates between capital and lower case letters. this makes typing commands more clear and more exact. somebody can give you a short command and tell you exactly what it does and guess what? it does exactly what it is designed to do. the linux CLI is a super powerful tool that many many professionals love and i wouldn't remove it for the world. plus, are you honestly saying you like capital letter more than lowercase? i think capitals are hideous. thats why i don't type capitals that often.Bla, bla, bla, I still hate the case sensitivity of the command-line. And the command-line itself.

### Post by zugu on 2009-06-03
> **chrisme52 said:**
> Not being able to place confidance in the the latest release.
Theres always a new problem or something else that doesn't work.
Ubuntu should be not be officially released until tested properly.
Dont get me wrong, i love using ubuntu but such simple things such as delaying official releases could improve uptake and consumer satisfaction.Delay it too much and you get Debian.

Ubuntu: relatively recent software, stability issues, poor QA
Debian: obsolete software, no stability issues, QA on steroids
Windows XP: software as recent as I want, no stability issues, QA that's at least decent. Having your cake and eating it, too.

### Post by caravel on 2009-06-03
> **zugu said:**
> Delay it too much and you get Debian.

Debian: obsolete software, no stability issues, QA on steroids
This is why Debian has unstable (always called "Sid"), testing (next stable release, this will be called "Squeeze") and the current stable ("Lenny") releases.  Ubuntu is based on the Debian unstable release.  Depending on the type of user you are or how much you want to tweak/mess about with the OS you can use either stable or unstable.  It's up to the end user.  Debian unstable is not as "unstable" as you would assume either so it's a valid alternative for those moving on from Ubuntu.  Personally I stick with stable at the moment because I'm playing through a few games (which are running very well), but I intend to move to unstable sooner or later.

Compiling stuff is not safe, since the package manager is not usually aware of compiled packages. Using third-party repositories is also not recommended. If getdeb offers updated stuff, why isn't that stuff already in the official backports? I'll tell you why: because the safety of that software cannot be guaranteed.yes, ubuntu doesnt always have the latest versions of each piece of software in the repos. thats why we can get debs for the apps we want and install them. i'm, sorry, maybe i'm just tired but i don't understand what you are trying to say. we have the power of the repos plus the ability to install a package from a deb file. we have it both ways and i happen to think thats good.
> That's just how you like it. Most people are used to do things the Windows and OS X way. I would expect the same from a small OS trying to take on the giants.wait, you'd EXPECT them to blatently copy their inferior version of package management? oh wait, they dont have any form of package management and you have to get every app's exe file seperately. but we can do that with deb files too! so how is having different options of package management a bad thing?
> Oh, please, they do it because there's no real alternative, other than all distributions uniting efforts and standardising on a base system (a la BSD) and a standard package manager and format. And we all know that won't happen. I used Firefox on Windows XP since v0.4. I didn't need to upgrade to Vista to use the latest version. Ubuntu would rather have me upgrade every 6 months to achieve the same thing. How is this convenient and logical?no alternative? debs work, right? they could've just focused on creating debs for everybody to hunt down and install themselves.
with ubuntu, each new version is not nearly as big as the leap from XP to Vista. you can upgrade ubuntu without losing your files. can you use DirectX10 on XP? i think you'll have to upgrade to vista for that.
> Childish? Are you kidding me? It's not perfect, I agree, but it's as close to it as it can be. Retail utilities, video games, webware, anything can be installed in the most easy way. Deployment is a breeze, all dependencies are inside the app folder, one can have the latest stable versions of anything - no need to wait for someone to package it! Having it other way IS a bad thing.look, its just my personal opinion. i was raised on the mac classic, and when we got color (system 7?) it was the greatest thing ever! then in college, i got a mac, and it reminded me of being a kid again. i said that I THINK its childish. i'm not saying that everybody does or should think the same way.
> Oh, the classic "I never needed this, so why should you?" rebuttal. Just because you don't have this problem, it doesn't mean other people don't.wow, you totally misread my post didn't you? i asked "what applications would you like to install 2 different versions?" i wanted to know because i'm curious... thats all. like i said, i don't have too much experience doing that so i wanted to know what software you do that with. i never said "i never needed this so why should you?". you put those words in my mouth and started an argument where there was none.

but because you don't want to share on the subject, i am assuming its a touchy subject. maybe 2 versions of the same software killed your parents? i don't know. i'm sorry i asked a simple question and i'm sorry if it brought up any repressed memories of childhood abuse.
> Eventually, something will go awry and one will have to dive into the CLI.oh the "it happens to me so it happens to everybody" argument? i know about a dozen people that have used ubuntu for over a year and have never touched CLI.
> Sometimes there's no updated package for some application one needs, so compiling is the last hope. In a community where many upstream projects are just spitting sources, there just won't be enough manpower to create packages for everything.if a certain application isn't available on linux, people usually have to go back to windows. if they use linux primarily BECAUSE theres a certain piece of software they need... but they have to compile it, if they were dedicated enough to switch OSes over it, i'm sure they'll be dedicated enough to learn how to compile something. and yes, theres never ever enough developers for certain projects. maybe they should reach into the developers bin and just pull one out?
> Look at Ubuntu Dapper: its 5 year support is ending this month. Its death is a painful one: it has **96 open CVE bugs, some of them are over 2 years old**. It's not that upstream didn't fix the bugs, it's just that there aren't enough people to backport fixes and security patches. I would dare to say it's also too difficult a task to do. Please, don't use the "who uses Ubuntu Dapper today, anyway" rebuttal, it's not about Dapper in particular, it's about the way of doing things in general.windows XP has bugs too... thats the nature of software. software has bugs! no software is perfect! ubuntu has a pretty good track record of fixing important bugs though... i mean, compared to XP. there was a comparison study with charts and stuff i saw a little while ago that compared them... i can't find it now but i'll look for it because its really interesting.
> Good for them.yep. they were able to prove your argument was flawed. they did a good job.
> Are you kidding me? Compile any big software you want in Ubuntu then tell me you're sure you know where all the compiled files reside. Tell me it's as easy to remove them as deleting a folder. Compiling is the same process on all OSes, it's the little things around the compiling process that matter.ok. put them in subdirectories in /opt. all done!
> You think I'm so stupid I don't know how to remove the extra panel? It's the default arrangement that I hate. It's the decision of the GNOME developers I can't stand. It's also Ubuntu's decision, since distros are free to modify the interface as they wish before releasing.putting more words in my mouth. i never said you were stupid... in fact, I WAS AGREEING WITH YOU. wow, you always seem to get things backwards. during this part of our discussion, i was agreeing with you how i don't like the 2 panels either so i change it. i don't like the default theme or arrangement either, so i change it. i can't be any more clear without repeating myself again.
> Of course I don't use it. But that doesn't mean I hate it less.i don't hate it... but i dislike it too.
> Bla, bla, bla, I still hate the case sensitivity of the command-line. And the command-line itself.so your answer to an intelligent argument is "bla bla bla"? like somebody stated above, this is pointless.
> You're damn right it's not! But I always try to voice my frustration, hoping that some day things will start to change.so you don't like ubuntu, but you spend your free time in an ubuntu forum bashing it hoping it will change so one day you will like it? i feel bad for you. i wouldn't spend all my free time in a windows forum bashing windows because, well, i have better things to do besides being a troll. yes, thats the definition of troll.
> Arch sucks.what distro do you like? just curious, please don't put words into my mouth this time.
> So I'm a troll now? Just because I happen to hate some things in Ubuntu? Oh, dear, I thought the title of the thread was "Things that you hate in Ubuntu". Read my previous message in this thread again. It's just me listing the things I hate. How does that make me a troll?so maybe this wasn't the best thread to call you out in. but you've specifically stated you hate ubuntu, and you have many many OTHER posts in many threads bashing ubuntu. you are on an ubuntu forum. it just seems like you get off by going to a board for something you dislike, just to let everybody know that you dislike it. i don't like disney, so instead of going to the disney website and forums (do they have forums?) just to let everybody know how i feel, i don't visit the site at all.

why else would you be sharing how much you hate ubuntu, unless you just wanted some sort of attention?
> Should you have any problems with the messages I posted in other threads, please, please go there and reply to them. Your prejudice against me is most... disturbing.not worth my time... as you've clearly demonstrated
> So disliking a piece of software makes me a bad person? I never thought about this!you love putting words in my mouth. its a very childish fallacy.
you stated that you hate fanbois. why? because they love a piece of software and preach about it? so loving a piece of software makes them a bad person?
thats not good logic, so please state why you hate fanbois so we can understand why you've judged them all as bad people.
> If my ideas are silly, only silly people will turn away from Ubuntu. I'm sure you don't need that kind of users around here.i've never heard that quote. i mean, it doesn't make sense but it sounds nice. did you see it on a bumper sticker?

### Post by khelben1979 on 2009-06-04
> **GuiGuy said:**
> Do you mean the OS is unstable or some of the applications you run in the OS are unstable. 

By my estimation the problem tends to be more the latter. For example, Firefox's propensity to chew up resources, applications using sqlite DB getting bogged down in processing data etc etc, "Amacrok2" locking up.

Cheers

I mean the whole Ubuntu system. I installed the system for one of my brothers so I myself only had control of the system in the beginning. He decided to use Wine for basically everything he wanted to do with the system and several times a week it just freezed. He described the stability as worse than Windows XP. It feels like a bad Windows system, he said.

I later installed Debian and I myself experienced it to be more stable than Ubuntu, but still, it wasn't good enough for him, so he went back to Windows Vista after this.

He said to me: I might consider going to a dual boot solution in the future.

It is understandable in his situation, because he wants to do so much with Windows Vista which he just can't do with a Linux distro. And as every other computer user out there: the user chooses upon what he/she wants. I just don't like Vista myself, so I've told him that I won't give any support on it (and I don't master Vista myself, so..).

### Post by Tamale on 2009-06-17
Worst part in my experience is dealing with drivers, of any kind.  I hate trying to make hardware work in ubuntu.. it's absolutely painful.

Video driver support is finally *pretty* good.. but the day Ubuntu matches other operating systems in this regard will be the day it's ready for the everyman.

The test for this is simple.. 

google "ubuntu <version> <make/model of hardware> driver"

one of the first results needs to have a link to have a *.deb that installs just as easily as a windows *.exe.

That's it.

No kernel mods, no reading through pages and pages of forum arguments, no "use the windows driver through ndiswrapper", and most certainly.. NO BUILDING OF CODE!  The average user doesn't even KNOW what compiling is.. let alone how to do it.

At worst, a reboot should be required.  Then the device should just work.

---

### Post by radtek on 2009-06-23
I absolutely love Ubuntu and it has come a long way since 2006.  I'm preparing for 9.04...

Probably, the main thing I don't like (hate) is the lack of a post-installation interactive tutorial on how to utilize the command-line with concrete and relative examples of commands. For example: how to install a package/program at the command-line. Synaptic's GUI is great, but it does little to expand my skills. I'd say it is a concession to MS users.

I've been there and will continue to: pore through innumerable man & web-pages and confusing forum posts; then cutting and pasting. It does little to advance my abilities and knowledge. It is more like 'casting a spell' without knowing what it actually does.

Instead of expecting total noobs to convert through this often-times discouraging process why not give them an easier leg-up and **teach**? Those that are used to a totally GUI based system have a hard time with the steep learning curve and manuals and commands are often incomprehensible to those without extant skills to interpret them.

### Post by hammer v2 on 2009-06-25
Things I hate in ubuntu.....

well i love ubuntu...BUT...

I still hate installing apps. It's still a PITA in 2009...add/remove is great but only goes so far. Google earth is a .bin skype is a .deb, some apps are still a script....

i can do it easily enough (as most of you can I'm sure) but It still pisses me off that it's still techy in 2009.
I hope the ubuntu app centre fixes this. I want ONE repo for everything.

I also want a way to install apps without a direct net connection ([working on that]("http://code.google.com/p/superdeb/")) 

- Pulse Audio....WHY??? I'm a sound engineer. Sound on computers is my job. Pulse audio is ALWAYS the first thing to go on my ubuntu install. That extra mixing layer just adds extra complexity and shouldn't be part of the desktop. People just want sound to work.

- Development. It's getting better but I want the really, REALLY want basic VISUAL IDE's to work properly on linux. Runtime revolution and realbasic are fantastic and supposedly work on linux. But their performance is sub-par and there's annoying bugs that stop me everytime. Gambas is great but the documentation isn't and mono and java are still out of a beginner's league. I want a drag and drop program creator designed for monkeys that works. (python, java and freebasic etc don't cut it from a Visual programming POV - next level up).

- the menu. It should be a directory like windows. Having to use alacarte to edit it is just stupid. I should be able to arrange it all from the menu itself, without the use of alacarte.

I agree with the network bit.  Still can't get Nautilus to read my network the way it should.  It mostly just hangs.  Don't like that - of course.  Seems such a simple and obvious thing a file browser should be able to do.

Moved to Gnome Commander.  That one has no problem with networks.  Still like the concept of Nautilus - obviously don't like the network inability.

### Post by Bodsda on 2009-06-26
> **hammer v2 said:**
> Things I hate in ubuntu.....

well i love ubuntu...BUT...

I still hate installing apps. It's still a PITA in 2009...add/remove is great but only goes so far. Google earth is a .bin skype is a .deb, some apps are still a script....

i can do it easily enough (as most of you can I'm sure) but It still pisses me off that it's still techy in 2009.
I hope the ubuntu app centre fixes this. I want ONE repo for everything.

I also want a way to install apps without a direct net connection ([working on that]("http://code.google.com/p/superdeb/")) 

- Pulse Audio....WHY??? I'm a sound engineer. Sound on computers is my job. Pulse audio is ALWAYS the first thing to go on my ubuntu install. That extra mixing layer just adds extra complexity and shouldn't be part of the desktop. People just want sound to work.

- Development. It's getting better but I want the really, REALLY want basic VISUAL IDE's to work properly on linux. Runtime revolution and realbasic are fantastic and supposedly work on linux. But their performance is sub-par and there's annoying bugs that stop me everytime. Gambas is great but the documentation isn't and mono and java are still out of a beginner's league. I want a drag and drop program creator designed for monkeys that works. (python, java and freebasic etc don't cut it from a Visual programming POV - next level up).

- the menu. It should be a directory like windows. Having to use alacarte to edit it is just stupid. I should be able to arrange it all from the menu itself, without the use of alacarte.

1) .bin files are binary, you can just run them via ./file.bin , .debs can be installed via dpkg or double clicked on and gdebi will install, and as for "some apps are still a script...." you say that like its a bad thing?

2) As for installing apps without web connection, there are a few, such as aptoncd

3)Pulseaudio: I dont get your point, sound support is geting better with every release.

4)Why not help out by writing documentation, and btw, python is pretty darn useful for GUI apps. if thats what you meant by Visual.

5)If I used Gnome, and didnt use the terminal, I would probably agree

I hate the fact that everything is centered on GNOME, I use fluxbox and the only change I have seen in the last 3 releases is this new notify system and increase boot times... Doesnt seem that impressive for 18 months work.

### Post by NightwishFan on 2009-06-27
As far as I know, and that is little, I believe it offers software mixing that bypasses hardware, you can use it to pipe all audio output through (alsa, oss, esound), and newer versions offer lower latency and less power use. (Ubuntu Jaunty has this feature disabled by default, as it is buggy.) I believe you can try it by removing text in a configuration file called tsched=0, however it will not fix anything if you have problems.

It is what is called a 'step in the right direction' by some. However many many users report problems, because Pulseaudio seems to expose a lot of bugs in alsa code, etc..

Someone more familiar can show you the end user benefits. The main one I know of is the ability to adjust the volume of individual streams. For example I can adjust individual channels on my 5.1 surround sound audio, or just mute/unmute applications as a whole.

### Post by newbuntuxx on 2009-11-27
Well, as an average user, and someone who used Windows (for many many years) before using Ubuntu, I do have a few things to complain about.

But before I do that, I want to say that I started using linux (ubuntu) because of its stability and security. I then found other good things about it which only made me abandon Windows as my main PC.

So, things to complain about?

Installing new software:

As simple as it may sound to the experienced user, I find it, at times, very difficult to get a piece of software installed and properly working (without nasty errors asking for a kernel module to be installed etc..), and that's due to many reasons:

1. There is more than one way to install a piece of software. 
- You can use the terminal (apt-get)
- You can use synaptic/new software center in 9.10
- You can download the .deb file 
etc...
This can create a lot of confusion to new users and can even discourage them from continuing to use ubuntu. 

2. Updating the sources.list file every time you install new software

- When you install a piece of software, you will need to use "sudo". So, would it not be better to have the said software modify the sources.list file with the appropriate links? The user can be notified that the software is modifying sources.list. This is much easier and more user-friendly (i think) for new users. 

This is of course just my opinion. I base my judgements on my own experience with ubuntu. I had the same hard-ware since ubuntu 7.04. When I installed 7.04, I had to edit many config files to get sound, video card, flash etc...to work. And with every new release, I do a fresh install to see if things have improved, and sure they did!

With 9.10, I didn't even need to edit any config files. Ubuntu worked right out of the box. And thats the beauty of linux!

---

-A extended guide is not available immediately(heck,even 1 unofficial *buntu guide [[COLOR="RoyalBlue"]like this[/COLOR]]("http://kubuntuguide.org/Karmic") or [[COLOR="Blue"]this[/COLOR]]("http://ubuntuguide.org/wiki/Ubuntu:Karmic") is a lot more complete.)
-Lack of having a comparisons file between FLOSS and CPS(Closed/proprietary software).
-Some apps/packages can crash randomly(not the entire system). 
-When you download 1 package,others will being added.
-Not enough testing(bugs and all).

:guitar:

### Post by GuiGuy on 2009-12-23
> **Psumi said:**
> 
ext# only, fat systems still transfer fast on my end.

Not here. trying to xfer a 2G file to an 8G fat formatted USB drive (destined for a WinXP PC) was going to take 2 days. I was quicker to find a Windows computer and transfer from Ubuntu > ethernet > Windows7_PC > USB Stick. 

This has been going on for more than a year now. It is despairing.

---

### Post by Psumi on 2009-12-23
> **GuiGuy said:**
> Not here. trying to xfer a 2G file to an 8G fat formatted USB drive (destined for a WinXP PC) was going to take 2 days. I was quicker to find a Windows computer and transfer from Ubuntu > ethernet > Windows7_PC > USB Stick. 

This has been going on for more than a year now. It is despairing.

I don't transfer over a network, and I don't transfer huge files like that usually. :| Both Fat and ext filesystems aren't supposed to transfer huge files in fast times. It's actually the hardware that matters. You might want to try using SSDs. It took me over an hour to transfer 2 GB of data from my harddrive to a flash drive as well, with the flash drive being formatted to ext2. But then I went back and used fat32, and guess what? It went much faster (most files, if not all, were under 20 MB in size. Nothing over 50 MB.)

### Post by stefcep on 2009-12-24
Lack of iPod/touch support.
Fonts are poor.
Default human theme is amateurish.
6 month release cycles: should be no shorter than 12 months, and definately NOT rolling.
Names for different releases every 6 months eg Intepid Ibex, Jaunty Jackalope blah,blah.  I don't remember which is older or newer.
dependence on CLI.
I can't install software by downloading it and double clicking it, but have to rely on repo maintainers

---

### Post by N00b-un-2 on 2009-12-24
> Lack of iPod/touch support.
Fonts are poor.
Default human theme is amateurish.
6 month release cycles: should be no shorter than 12 months, and definately NOT rolling.
Names for different releases every 6 months eg Intepid Ibex, Jaunty Jackalope blah,blah. I don't remember which is older or newer.
dependence on CLI.
I can't install software by downloading it and double clicking it, but have to rely on repo maintainers

While I do bemoan the spotty iPod support on Linux I can't agree with your other statements.

The six month rolling release cycle ensures that users have the latest and greatest available to them.  If you don't like the latest releases, then stick with the LTS.  That's what I do.  I have 8.04 installed on all of my computers without issue.

The version naming scheme is alphabetical and has been so since Fiesty (7.04). Fiesty, Gutsy, Hardy, Intrepid, Jaunty, Karmic, Lucid, etc.

You are incorrect about not being able to download software from the internet.  Check out [www.getdeb.net](www.getdeb.net)

if you don't like the default free fonts in Ubuntu, install msttcorefonts to get default Windows fonts.

Human isn't the default anymore.  It is Humanity and the icon set is based on elementary which is probably the most complete and professional looking icon set I've come across on Linux yet, and there are plenty of themes you can use.  Check out [www.gnome-look.org](www.gnome-look.org)

Linux relies on the CLI only as much as you choose to make it do so.  By now, there is very little you can't do using the GUI that can be accomplished by the CLI.  It's just a matter of personal preference.

### Post by GuiGuy on 2010-01-16
> **Psumi said:**
> I don't transfer over a network, and I don't transfer huge files like that usually. :| Both Fat and ext filesystems aren't supposed to transfer huge files in fast times.

:( I despair; My point was that it was FASTER to transfer a large file across from the ubuntu box to a Win7 PC than copying the same file on the Ubuntu box from its SATA HDD to a USB memory device. 

More to the point UBUNTU SATA HDD > SATA HDD isn't much faster. 

And we're talking files between 500M and 4G, no bigger.

Finally, it is demonstrable that Windows or my daughter's MacBook will transfer a 4G file faster by a factor of 5 to 10 than my Athlon Phenom/ 4G/ ASUS MOBO running Ubuntu is capable of. 

**In my experience**, given similar hardware, when it comes to speed and performance Win7, even XP, leaves Ubuntu in the dust. 

But, I'll accept it for what it is. Ubuntu/ Linux is free in the first instance so maybe I should just bear up and accept floppy drive speed is the new standard.

---

### Post by seeker5528 on 2010-01-16
> **GuiGuy said:**
> :( I despair; My point was that it was FASTER to transfer a large file across from the ubuntu box to a Win7 PC than copying the same file on the Ubuntu box from its SATA HDD to a USB memory device. 

More to the point UBUNTU SATA HDD > SATA HDD isn't much faster.

Booting with System Rescue CD and using ddrescue to clone disks, I commonly see 15 to 35 MBps sustained transfer rates, depending on the drives and controllers, I have seen some better rates with better hard drives. I'm not usually sitting there watching it. These are general numbers, not limited to SATA, since I am often dealing with some combination of older and newer stuff, PATA to SATA transfers can still see some pretty decent rates, PATA to PATA is most commonly from what I remember 15 to 25 MBps.

This is raw sustained transfer speed, not taking the overhead of dealing with different file systems and file managers in to account. 

Not questioning that you have a problem with your specific hardware in Ubuntu, just questioning whether this is an Ubuntu problem with your specific hardware/driver or an upstream problem with your specific hardware/driver.

Later, Seeker

### Post by yester64 on 2010-01-17
There is nothing really i hate about Linux (or Ubuntu).
The problem is, that you have to deal with the windows world, where standard getting defined as to how things work.
Example, gaming. So you can not run Windows games straight from within linux. Is it linux fault? No, its because the closed world requires you to use Windows.
Or certain hardware that does not work out of the box. Is it the fault of linux? No, its the fault of the manufactures who don't consider linux a platform at all and require you to run either Windows or Mac.
Ubuntu worked for me fine. I do not game a lot and i do not have to have certain applications running like Photoshop or the like. So perhaps i am blessed or i just do not need that many features.
A lot of complains i see have something to do with what user expect the OS should do, but very view see the fault in the windows world and rather paint it on Linux for not delivering.
So, i love Ubuntu (or any other Linux Distro) over Windows any time. Because i know that problems you have are mostly related to Windows, even if you said goodbye to it, it still haunts you.
Of course there are buggy software or thing that just don't run like they should, but things get fixed.
So far everything i have running works, but it took me awhile to have the system i love.
I rather have some pain, but don't have to run Windows anymore.

Thats my take, and i am sure not everyone agrees with me on this.

---

### Post by Techsnap on 2010-01-17
> No, its the fault of the manufactures who don't consider linux a platform at all 

Sometimes it's just not worth their money to support an OS with such a low market share because it's going to cost them to produce Linux drivers which they will have to keep up to date all the time because they could break with a kernel update or something similar, there's no stable driver API in Linux and considering the small market share it's not worth their time, resources or money to do this.

---

### Post by yester64 on 2010-01-17
> **Techsnap said:**
> Sometimes it's just not worth their money to support an OS with such a low market share because it's going to cost them to produce Linux drivers which they will have to keep up to date all the time because they could break with a kernel update or something similar, there's no stable driver API in Linux and considering the small market share it's not worth their time, resources or money to do this.

Mm... that is true to an extent. But i argue that a company, if it wants to sell a product to you, should consider to offer alternatives for you.
Or does the free choice exhaust in 'yes, you have free choice of OS' 'Windows Home, Pro, Ulitmate, 32bit or 64bit'.
If thats the case, these companies lost me as a customer.
I am fed up with companies to tell me, what i can run or not. So if their precious resources are not worth to help me, than they can sell their stuff to someone else.

It is not that i do not recognize the problem, but i rather ignore a product. Of course i am lucky, since i am not forced by an employer to use a certain software or hardware. I understand that some people have to suffer through this because of their job requirements.

I had the same problem with a printer i want it to hook up onto my Amiga 500 (way back). Every printer pretty much supported only Dos or Windows and it was kinda hard to find printerdrivers to get some printers working. Those were the dot matrix times. Thats why i hate companies deeply, because they always limit your choice in what you can use and how.

### Post by GuiGuy on 2010-01-19
> **seeker5528 said:**
> 
Not questioning that you have a problem with your specific hardware in Ubuntu, just questioning whether this is an Ubuntu problem with your specific hardware/driver or an upstream problem with your specific hardware/driver.

It is not the hardware. I can demonstrate the issue on a number of different computers available to me. The symptoms are always the the same:

[LIST]
[*]Prepare a file copy process of several larger files (250M - 4G)
[*]Initiate the copy, either using cp or using a file manager
[*]The file copy starts reasonably briskly, if not at the full potential of the hardware
[*]Progressively the transfer speed slows until it is at an absolute trickle
[/LIST]

All known suggestions on cures, including buying new hardware, have been tried. The problem remains[URL="http://www.google.com.au/search?q=Slow+SATA+USB+Ubuntu&ie=utf-8&oe=utf-8&aq=t&rls=com.ubuntu:en-US:official"]

Google will show I am not alone[/URL].

Just now I have had to push the reset button; the PC was transferring several video files from an internal SATA drive to eSATA drive when the cursor stutters started. 

Thankfully I have just invested in a Gigabit router and thankfully I have a Windows7 notebook to pull me out of the brown stuff.

### Post by BETATEST on 2010-01-24
The serious lack of "Commercial" Support or Buy-in for Ubuntu (or any Linux derivative for that matter) by the majority of Application developers, Game publishers, and Device manufacturers.  We have the BetaMax of Operating Systems right here - better quality than anything else available on any platform.  Not to menation a price that's easy on the wallet during these crappy economic times.  Yet - Ubuntu users are a market that the computer industry, for the most part, just kind of shrugs at like it's a fad.  They know we're here and all, but all the good stuff is made for the Windows and MAC markets first.  We're kind of like this red-headed step-OS, an after thought, an annoyance to Application developers, Game publishers, and Device manufacturers. We have to settle for "clones" of commercial applications - want to use CS3 and CS3 plugins?  Sorry, Linux isn't supported by CS3 ... use GIMP.  (I like GIMP BTW, but one of my jobs requires CS3 and it's collaboration toolsets that WINE doesn't work with.)  Why should I have to use a darn virtual machine to "emulate" a Windows or MAC system on my Ubuntu!!!  I don't want Windows anything.  I want to use commercial applications like CS3 and MAYA natively on Ubuntu and get away from Microsoft all together.  :^)  I've got the Cadillac of Operating Systems here ... and all it has is the AM Radio!

---

We have the BetaMax of Operating Systems right here - better quality than anything else available on any platform.  Not to menation a price that's easy on the wallet during these crappy economic times.  Yet - Ubuntu users are a market that the computer industry, for the most part, just kind of shrugs at like it's a fad.  They know we're here and all, but all the good stuff is made for the Windows and MAC markets first.  We're kind of like this red-headed step-OS, an after thought, an annoyance to Application developers, Game publishers, and Device manufacturers. We have to settle for "clones" of commercial applications - want to use CS3 and CS3 plugins?  Sorry, Linux isn't supported by CS3 ... use GIMP.  (I like GIMP BTW, but one of my jobs requires CS3 and it's collaboration toolsets that WINE doesn't work with.)  Why should I have to use a darn virtual machine to "emulate" a Windows or MAC system on my Ubuntu!!!  I don't want Windows anything.  I want to use commercial applications like CS3 and MAYA natively on Ubuntu and get away from Microsoft all together.  :^)  I've got the Cadillac of Operating Systems here ... and all it has is the AM Radio!

If you work in photography try Bibble. I do agree with most of your post though even though it's not "ubuntu's" fault as it's up to third party vendors.

### Post by the_prof on 2010-02-04
I just wanted to inject my two-penneth as a (very) recent convert to Linux, and specifically Ubuntu.  

I have 'used' Linux as a secondary play thing for quite a while now, but until earlier this week, never as my primary operating system.  Karmic was one of the deciding factors in my decision to change over, as was my retisence to put yet another version of Windows on my machine (and more specifically, pay Microsoft for the privilege).  

Actually moving over to Ubuntu was nowhere near as painful as I thought - quite the opposite in fact.  I'm totally impressed with how slick the installation for it is, and how easy it is for somebody who's relatively new to Linux.  Absolutely excellent.  I have to say for the very first time in the 8+ years I've messed about with Linux, I actually didn't feel that I needed a dual boot with Windoze.  

Which brings me onto my next point.  Whilst there are still weak areas in Ubuntu (and Linux in general), you really have to admire what's been achieved in the last few years.  Considering the sheer amount of money Microsoft get for their operating systems, I think this puts them to shame.  Why would you pay for Windows when you get this for free?

"Well, duh!", I hear you say...  And I know this is a point that almost goes without saying these days, but I feel I have to say it at least once.  

In the process of getting my Ubuntu installation how I want it, I've tripped up a few times to say the least.  However, pretty much nothing was unachievable (which certain things were a couple of years ago).  The audio capabilities are still a tad disappointing, as is some of the hardware support (and the way Ubuntu deals with hardware), but that's all IMHO (as a user coming over from Windows anyway).  

Given that most manufacturers STILL do not consider Linux support to be of importance, I find the number of supported devices incredible though.  

The only other bugbear is the number of things you have to fiddle with from the command-line.  I'm no stranger to the Terminal, but even I sometimes find it confusing.  I'm sure it's something I'll get used to in time, but I still don't find it all particularly logical...  That said, I usually seem to find the answer on these forums, or elsewhere on the Internet.  

I think this is another area where the Linux users and developers are their own worst enemy.  Rather than addressing the root cause of certain issues, I think they tend to go and change config files manually, recompile kernels, and do things that your average new user wouldn't have a clue about.  

It would be nicer if these people recognised that this stuff is unknown territory for a lot of people, and scares a lot of other people off, and addressed it in a user-friendly way.  I can see this aspect is getting better, but it still needs a fair bit of work.  

Anyway, my main point is, I've just come from being primarily a Windows user, and I haven't got too stuck so-far.  There isn't anything I hate about Ubuntu, and I hope to keep Ubuntu as my primary OS for the forseeable future.  I very much look forward to seeing how things progress.

---

### Post by jurialmunkey on 2010-02-07
Audio,. Definitely.
Thankfully at least Karmic has support for the X-fi out of the box. But it still doesn't sound as good as in windows, nor does it use all the X-fi features but at least I don't have to do a stupid hack to get audio and I have 5.1 sound now.

There's a complete lack of decent audio production/recording apps. And anyway the support for the X-fi is so shaky it doesn't even matter. I can't even record in Audacity from a line-in source properly,, it records both the line in and the audio from the speakers... there goes multi-tracking out the window.

And having to do ridiculous unintuitive things to get simple things to work like needing to go into alsa-mixer and unmute channels and raise the volume on the x-fi on a vanilla install... why aren't the channels already unmuted and the volume up??

And lack of standards (but I suppose this happens in Windows too..),, like how firefox and openoffice don't sport standard menus and thus do not work with global-menu.

Other than that, most of my grievances have been mostly fixed by Karmic,, and its good to see the NVIDIA drivers getting much better. I'm not too keen on the new GDM-login screen... the old one was much more customisable and didn't require a semi-hack to customise. All that said, I barely ever go into Windows any more because I just can't deal with using it, its just so slow and horrible to use. I only keep Windows to play the occasional game and to do audio work.

### Post by MadCow108 on 2010-02-09
this is more general GNU/Linux rant:
the lack of a good and fast GUI file browser!
Not one of the browsers in the repository, be it the heavyweights like nautlius, konqueror or the lightweights like thunar, xfe or pcmanfm come close to the speed of the windows 98/xp explorer.

Ok my pc is rather outdated, but opening a folder with a few thousand files in it takes 2-3 secs in windows xp and friggn 30 seconds in any file browser if tried (quite a few)

and the download/Save As dialog is just a pain in all I tried. Windows xp has this neat feature of bookmarks which you can click and in half a second the file lands there.
This also exists in most of the GNU/linux browser, but its so SLOW which makes it unusable ... (and in most the dialog takes the whole screen forcing you to move the mouse miles, wtf...)

I guess the speed is related to how the fbrowsers determine the filetype, explorer uses the dumb approach of looking at the extension only, stupid but I guess thats what makes it so fast.

Also only konqueror seems to have a webbrowser style personal bar, a personal must feature for file and webbrowsers (although I haven't used the other heavyweights in a while maybe they have this feature by now?)

### Post by humphreybc on 2010-02-13
There are a few things that I think need to be addressed in the **default install.**

The default theme and colour scheme is outdated and fairly ugly. Not just my opinion.

Application consistency needs to be looked at - there are a lot of different ways of doing things. Like zooming in on things for example. gThumb uses ctrl + scroll, while Gimp uses ctrl and + or -

Default applications. F-Spot, in particular. It's a horrible photo management application.

Ubuntu/Gnome (probably Gnome more so) needs a clean up - there is so much crap still left over from years ago - like all the useless panel applets (fish eyes!) and the huge amount of ugly window borders that come with the default install. I reckon about 50mb of space could be freed on the CD by just cleaning out stuff that is obsolete in Gnome and Ubuntu.

It annoys me that I can't easily edit the colours used in gnome-menu. I can change all the other colours through the appearance menu for a theme, but if I want to change the menu then I have to edit the theme itself. Stuff like that is actually quite common - some areas/applications have a huge level of customization while others have virtually none.

Consistency is one of the downsides of FLOSS software - having thousands of people with their own opinions on what looks good writing code will lead to a tonne of randomness.

Hardware drivers still play havoc with many printers, older graphics cards and wireless. I predict it will still be a year or two before we can truly boast that we have excellent hardware support on par with the competition.

Education of Ubuntu users is severely lacking. So is help with contributing and getting involved with the community. If you're not already in IT, then it's not apparent at all how people can get involved. What goes on behind the scenes. Luckily there are a lot of cool projects going on at the moment that are aiming to increase education - the manual project, Ubuntu Learning project etc. Also, Ground Control, Lernid, Quickly etc are all helping "every-day" users fix bugs and submit patches or feedback.

---

### Post by homeuser1 on 2010-02-14
So, let me preface this reply by saying I'm not a IT person or have any programming knowledge with windows or linux.  

What I like about Ubuntu:
1)  It's free
2)  I can load it on older computers and be able to use them for internet browsing without too much work. 
3)  When I'm bored, I can research fixes for hours.   
What I don't like about Ubuntu:
1)  The hours I have too spend researching fixes to figure things out.  Such as,how to use juno dialup.  Still don't have that one working
2)  Kernel upgrades can drastically change things. Such as,the kernel upgrade from 9.04 to 9.10 caused my dell laptop to kill the network manager everytime I close the laptop.  I have spent timeless hours trying to fix this problem with no luck.  Luckily, it's free to reload 9.04 to get things working again.  
3) Confidence in the OS.  I would never use linux as my primary OS.  Too many things can happen that cause you to have to reload the OS.  
4) Program use.  Again, I would never be able to use linux/Ubuntu as my primary OS. I buy one program a year to do my taxes.  I don't want to spend the time trying to figure out how to load it on Ubuntu. This could be said about any windows based application.

Well that's it for now. Ubuntu works pretty well for Internet browsing.  Other than that, it's too much work/frustrating for the non-IT homeuser to use as it's primary OS. :)

### Post by OobNosferatu on 2010-02-17
I love ubuntu, but ther eare somethings about it i absolutely detest:

1- upgrades break stuff.
2- i ALWAYS have to do tweaking or workarounds to make it work with my hardware (sound issues, anyone!???)
3- after using workarounds for a billion different things over the course of a few months, the whole system becomes unstable because of so many bandaids! it's the equivalent of a man starting out fine but accumulating tiny paper cuts and bruises, so he uses bandages and casts until he's eventually fully covered in them and has difficulty moving about anywhere.

Sigh.

hypothetical example: remember that libfusion.so thing that you replaced, modified or patched to custom fit that one software you needed it to work with...? well, it's making a couple of other NEW software crash and there's no forum that can help you because your issue is unique, as you have customized your entire pc to exactly suit your needs and personality... so it's a unique, one of a kind problem.

ARGH!

Oh and what's with no system recovery tool built in to ubuntu??? I've worked around this issue by making an rsync script that backs up my filesystem minus the home dir... daily. But it would be so nice to have that piece of mind that comes with a pre-installed, fully supported and INTEGRATED recovery system!

and yes, lack of gaming power sucks.

and i hate to say this, even though i've modified my system using custom themes, compiz setting changes ranging to the esoteric, cairo dock and screenlets... it doesn't compare to the beauty of windows 7 customization that comes with rainmeter and windows' own customization ability.

Sure, i can customize ubuntu MORE, but the options just don't give me as pretty a look as minimal-effort customization of windows 7 will. That sucks.

AND I HATE THE BROWN THEME that comes with ubuntu! STOP IT already! :P

annnnnd.... annnnd... uhm.... 

oh yeah, it doesn't find printers on a windows network using a keyword search, the way windows does. That's a very convenient option... wish ubuntu had it.

and file sharing over the network is a pain: samba shares have only let me share ONE way.. which blows. maybe i'm doing something wrong here, but still.

customization comes with another price: sensitivity to screwing things up. Ubuntu has crashed more than vista and xp combined.. for me at least. mostly because i've messed with the system so much (editing usplash, partition-tweaking, nvidia driver updates, etc) but I'd like it to have a crash recovery system... built in, please. i imagine i could make a script or something, to escape crashes or lockups, but i'd like to not have that headache.

there's probably more to complain about. but i have to go. ranting is so cathartic... :)

For the consumer:
1. Fewer updates.  Seems like every time I power up, Update Manger monopolizes my CPU with[/COLOR][COLOR=Black] _[COLOR=Blue][umpteen]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indefinite_and_fictitious_numbers")[/COLOR]_ updates to install.
2. Improve wireless performance.  For the most part, my laptop serves as a Hulu and South Park Studios delivering device in my garage as I ride my[/COLOR][COLOR=Black][ _[COLOR=Blue]trainer[/COLOR]_]("http://www.performancebike.com/bikes/Product_10052_10551_1063136_-1___") in anticipation of better[/COLOR][COLOR=Black] _[COLOR=Blue][weather]("http://www.midatlanticweather.com/")[/COLOR]_.  Though I'm constrained by HW, even the "low bandwidth" option is choppy.
3. Finally, nothing.  The Ubuntu community (developers and users that post) is almost completely exhaustive.  I haven't tried to do anything (see laptop usage above) that hasn't been done before, has active trouble shooters, or is a limitation that can't be attributed to Ubuntu.  Great job developers and posters, new and old.
For the professional:
1. Enterprise acceptance.  I work in a specialized IT environment in which only a cadre of OSes are supported (MS, RHEL, & SuSe).  However, I do not believe this is a fault of Ubuntu's since they had a booth at[ _[COLOR=Blue]VMWorld '09[/COLOR]_]("http://www.vmworld2009.com/community/conferences/2009/sponsors-exhibitors/") and were actively touting their integration from a technical standpoint.  Maybe some work in the PR/corporate collaborative dept's is in order.  For example, try to get Ubuntu to work with and support _[COLOR=Blue][ESRI]("http://www.esri.com")[/COLOR]_ software.

Keep up the good work and I look forward to upcoming improvements & increased collaboration within the IT community.[/COLOR]

### Post by prodigy_ on 2010-03-09
Since Ubuntu is just a Linux distro, I prefer talking about general Linux/Gnome/FOSS problems. 

---

1. **Persistent issues with drivers**. Intel HDA sound chips seem to be cursed with all sorts of problems: sound disappears randomly or changes or starts screeching. Sometimes volume changes w/o user intervention. Creative X-Fi is less problematic but extended configuration options (provided by Creative Console Launcher in Windows) are inaccessible. Wireless support is also far from perfect - many devices are reported to be unusable due to missing or buggy drivers. And of course there are ATI video cards and FGLRX... no I don't even want to talk about FGLRX.

2. **False dependencies**, i.e. when your package manager is forced to believe that a package depends on something that isn't actually needed for it to work. A classic example is Wine "dependency" on avahi-daemon. Plymouth (added in Lucid Lynx) is even more notorious in this regard. Admittedly, this is a design/philosophy issue that doesn't affect usability in most cases.

3. **Video tearing**. Especially with ATI cards. Although, I've also seen video tearing on Intel GMA950, for example. It's less noticeable, but still. So who needs GUI when there's no reliable v-sync even in 2D?

4. **Gnome 3 and Unity are less configurable** than Gnome classic. None of them can be enabled for VMWare guests. Worse yet, Gnome "fallback" is less configurable too (at least from what I've seen in Natty and Fedora 15). At least KDE4, as buggy as it was at release, didn't have such trivial problems. 

5. There's **no convention about network configuration files**. Almost all distros use files in /etc/sysconfig/network scripts folder but Debian and its derivatives use /etc/network/interfaces file with a completely different syntax. There are also other inconsistencies. For example all distros have different ifup/ifdown implementations. While some allow you to add your own ifup-local/ifdown-local scripts, others don't. All of this makes writing portable scripts a much more tricky endeavor than it should be.

6. **Gnome network manager** is garbage and should be discontinued. Basically, two user-friendly GUI tools are needed instead:
- some kind of frontend to parse and edit network configuration files and /etc/dhcp3/dhclient.conf;
- a wizard-like tool to configure logical connections (VPNs, etc.).

7. For now the only proper way to handle batch file operations properly in a bash script is to use ASCII NUL character as the field separator. Almost any other character (yes, even LF aka newline!) may technically appear in file names. Unfortunately, **not all CLI tools can read or generate NUL-delimited strings**. The whole issue would simply disappear if newlines weren't allowed in file names but nobody cares.

8. **FUSE/GVFS** is implemented in the worst way imaginable. Whoever thought that the whole "virtual directories" crap is a good idea, needs to be hanged by the neck. And whoever thought that making user mounts inaccessible for root by default is also a good idea, needs to be quartered. Maybe Linux isn't UNIX but it's also not Windows. Superuser (root) is here for a reason, and by default it must be able to access all inodes.

9. There is **no common package management system**. Debian and its derivatives use apt/dpkg. RHEL and Fedora use yum/rpm. Mandriva - urpmi/rpm. OpenSUSE - YaST/rpm. You switch to another distro and you suddenly feel like your hands are cut off because you have no clue about how to handle software installations from command line. Of course there are GUI tools, but what if you want to write a portable script that installs something? Or if you need to check the status of some package from command line?

10. We're not in 1970s anymore but **remote filesystems still cause problems**. From the top of my head: [SSHFS](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/sshfs-fuse/+bug/159031) and [CIFS](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/samba/+bug/211631). NFS is no better, especially if you try to access an NFS share over the Internet.

11. **Avahi-daemon**. Mostly useless but is installed by default in Ubuntu. Why must it nag me with pointless and annoying GUI (!) notifications every time I restart networking? Yeah, I can get rid of those, but my point is: this shouldn't be the default behavior for any daemon. Log files exist for a reason.

12. **The lack of a full-fledged ext4 driver for Windows**. It's a much-needed feature but nobody cares.

13. **Inability to switch gfx mode for ttys on the fly**. Furthermore, it's hard to make them work with anything else than 640x480. Ok, uvesafb works, but available options are limited and it takes a reboot to switch from one resolution to another. It's 2011 out there, why should this still be such a problem?

13.1 Speaking of 2011, **I still keep stumbling upon systems where vi (!) is the default system editor**. This is a hate thread, isn't it? Well, I hate, hate, HATE vi. In theory, I know how to use it but I can't bother to remember those stupid commands. Every time I have to google them. When I open file for editing, I want to type something right away not to think about how to get into that typing mode. That's why nano is so great. Let's face it: vi is the worst, the most annoying computer program ever written.

14. Configuring **Grub2 is a nightmare for any newcomer**. Was it so hard to place one .conf file with all user-definable settings (and comments about how to use them) in /etc so that update-grub just parsed it? Yes there's /etc/default/grub but it doesn't include all settings and it doesn't explain their meaning.

15. **Directories clutter**. All those /bin, /sbin, /usr/bin, /usr/share and so on. We have a 40 years old obsolete and obviously wrong concept that is still here only because of some misplaced sentimentality. Managing your OS and installed apps becomes an absolute horror when you need to find something specific. This has to stop! There should be precisely 5 default locations:
a) /programs (for system-wide applications installed with root privileges, system-wide config files should be in subdirectories); 
b) /home (for users configuration files and data);
c) /home/%username%/programs (for user-installed applications and user-specific config files);
d) /system (for system files);
e) /media (for mount points).
Yes, exactly like in Windows. Like it or not, this would be much more human-friendly. And I honestly don't understand why nobody is even trying to move in this direction.

---

### Post by vmsda on 2010-03-16
I have been using Linux (and, by extension, Ubuntu) for the last 7 or 8 years. I find the idea of openness so appealing and so compelling that it has in fact become the only reason why I still stick with Ubuntu.

1. It is not for the money saved, since I actually donate to every organization whose work I benefit from; and if I were to compute the endless hours I have spent reading stuff about things which don't work at all or don't work the first, second or third time around, then my business case would look even more incurably bleak.

2. It is not for the desktop experience either. If there is any accusation I level at Ubuntu (or any other Linux distro for that matter), is its *failure to provide a fundamentally different and innovative desktop experience*; basically it is the same as Windows or Apple, with its own set of shortcomings, just as they have theirs. It is my belief that as long as Ubuntu just "clones" those rivals, it will not make many inroads; after all, if Ubuntu is incapable of firing desktop users' imagination, their migration shall not be forthcoming any time soon. 

3. Canonical is not financing or producing philosophy (what the desktop of the future should embrace) or design (how to go about implementing that philosophy in a Linux environment); instead, it is throwing money at a strategically losing enterprise, by keeping alive a run-of-the-mill desktop experience which, by surfeit of choices, tries to be all things to all people but is not (application and hardware-wise) a reliable and comprehensive platform for anyone, save the few technically savvy (a bitter sweet irony that it has managed to achieve such a feat while basing itself on the most reliable of operating systems available!).

It is not Microsoft's fault that they are now selling for the *n*th time essentially the same crap they have been selling for the last twenty years; they are only taking advantage of others' shortcomings. Let us not be too surprised if they come to the conclusion that they have milked that cow long enough and decide to bet on some other platform to revolutionize the way we experience personal computing. Which platform: why not Linux?

---

> The whole setback for Linux making it bigtime has been the damn command line. I'm not going to regress to learning quasi-MSdos to debug a bug-riddled  OS. WHat's holding it back now is that many crucial things don't work as they should, so instead of adding new stuff, make sure what's already there works properly, which it simply doesn't.

i think linux is a loooooong way away from being command line free for everyone.
for a lot of people, whose hardware all works without tinkering, could comfortably live with out ever going near the command line if they're only doing things like web browsing, music, email, just basic computer stuff that most users these days use their computers for. but one dodgy piece of hardware leads you straight to the command line for info like you said.
my laptop works, everything bar the wireless (which only took a download of backports) worked without any tinkering, including flash.  but my desktop graphics card (ati) takes a bit of messing around, which is even more frustrating considering every new kernel upgrade means i have to install it again.

if you want a command line free life, linux won't be the way to go for a while yet for most users.

---

> 
One feature that isn't included is the calendaring add-on, Lightning. Originally, Mozilla had planned to bake the extension into the program, but decided back in February 2009 to change course and leave it up to users to download. Although Thunderbird natively comes with Microsoft Exchange support, there's no calendar and therefore no meeting support in the default Thunderbird installation. Along with Lightning, there's an essential Google Calendar add-on for Lightning that gives Google users calendar support in Lightning.

from here:  [http://www.daemonnews.org/2009/12/08/mozilla-lets-thunderbird-3-fly.html](http://www.daemonnews.org/2009/12/08/mozilla-lets-thunderbird-3-fly.html)

### Post by YMS_1975 on 2010-03-26
Not sure if this qualifies as "hate" for Ubuntu (because I absolutely love it!) but I am really disappointed & frustrated in the instability of various versions of Ubuntu.
[B]
Example :[/B] Just 5 days ago I needed to reinstall Ubuntu 9.10 on my children's desktop. I encountered a problem though; the networking card was not being recognized. [Click here for full details]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1424045&page=4") (third post from the top). 

After several days worth of technical support with **chili555**, whom I consider a true friend & _a definite asset to the Ubuntu community_ (don't wanna sound like a broken record chili555, but THANK YOU!) the progress of fixing my children's PC was at a stand still. He had ruled out what wasn't the problem but after trying things over and over again, I got flustered and was ready to give up and just install Windows XP back onto their system.

The funny thing is, just as I was about to do this I realized that I never had any of these problems with Ubuntu 9.04 (Jaunty Jackalope). I remember Jaunty's install was fast and very easy, and once it was installed, I upgraded to 9.10 (Karmic Koala) through the Update Manager without any problems.

Getting back to the instability of various versions...I found it very frustrating that doing a clean install of 9.10 yielded this very annoying problem, yet installing an older version was easier. One would think that as software evolves it gets better (and includes **more** drivers for a greater selection of hardware out of the box), but in this particular case, it got worst. This hiccup was enough to convince me to look out for some hard-copy books (not ebooks) that will teach me more about things like the command line, drivers, compiling, etc.

Anyways....to shorten an already long story, I finally got 9.10 up and running (by installing 9.04 and upgrading to 9.10) and my love for Ubuntu continues.  :D

---

### Post by MarkX on 2010-03-27
Apart from what I said in a previous post, like:
1)No easy way to set your own screen resolution etc when it doesn't detect it.

2) Changing stuff that isn't broken when upgrading to a new release and breaking a whole load of new stuff instead of fixing what was broken in the first place.

3) Being told that I "don't have permission" to access my own damn drives instead of just asking for a password. And no instructions on how to get damn permission.

4) Flashplayer STILL being unusable 70% of the time in Firefox. It just effs up and displays a blank box where flash should be after opening more tabs or opening more firefox windows. Yes, I've tried various "fixes" and they didn't work. 

5) Bugriddled apps.

6) Now "software center" has been downgraded from what was fine previously. This is what I mean, why change stuff that's not broken? Now everyone will have to find their way around this pointless change. 

I don't use Ubuntu because I like it, I use it because I hate M$ and the insecurity of their bloatware. I "upgrade Ubuntu versions not because of "exciting new features" (get a life)  but only because I hope some of the countless bugs have been fixed. Invariably it's just a bug change.

### Post by PC_load_letter on 2010-04-01
> **sudoer541 said:**
> I hate the use of the command prompt (drives me crazy!)
I hate the lack of software and drivers. I think ubu will improve in the near future!:p:D

Not to sound like a jerk, but the command line is one of the things I do LOVE about Ubuntu. Give it more time, and learn it anew. Don't compare it with the command line in Windows. The command line in Linux does wonders and it can be a very powerful and productive tool, more productive than GUI tools in many cases, e.g. when you have to apply a certain action to several files.

Back to topic, I wouldn't say I "hate" the following, but I would probably feel better if these issues were addressed.

**1- Resizing a window w/ the mouse:** One has got to have 20/20 laser vision to navigate the mouse's pointer to the window border or corner, which is a very thin line. Could it be changed to become much easier for the mouse pointer to latch onto the window sides?

**2- The small download window in Synaptic:** After you choose a certain package to install, the download progress window looks silly, it looks like a square and as a result it doesn't show much information. Aesthetically, it's painful!

**3- The disappearance of icons in the top panel:** And they come back if I just resize the panel back and forth. 

**4- Pulse Audio:** Enough said :D

1. **Over simplification**, if I want to do any real customization (usually to fix some problem) I have to google down instructions and copy+paste those in terminal. For example power management has lots of options disabled for no good reason.

2. **Updating software** that came with Ubuntu, like Firefox, ALSA, ScummVM, Wine or OpenOffice.org. Again the solution is to google and type some magic words in terminal and I'm left with feeling the correct way would have been to wait for the next release of Ubuntu.

3. **When I encounter a problem**, I'm not sure whether it qualifies as a bug or not and if I should report it in launchpad as own report or just comment on some similar bug, or maybe as a suggestion in brainstorm. In the end I usually just find a workaround and be satisfied that *it works for me!*

4. Almost forgot, the **lack of international English** support. It's annoying when the spell checker whines when I write color, instead of colour or the other way around. And it sucks that the day of the week is dependant on what language you choose!

### Post by beerguzzler on 2010-04-21
> **pgordon said:**
> I hate that I have to know about xconf.org. If there is a GUI way to create a xorg.conf file when auto detect doesn't work then please let me know. Maybe I can create one and won't have to use nvidia-settings to reset my resolution every time I boot up.

From Terminal enter "sudo nvidia-settings" 
Set yr resolution, hit the "Save to X configuration file" and then exit.

Then go to System, Preferences, Start up Applications and add the following:
Name - nvidia settings
Command - nvidia-settings --load-config-only

Save that and from there on in your resolution and whatever other settings you have entered in nvidia-settings will load during bootup
