### Post by asmoore82 on 2010-04-25
First of all let me say that I respect you and value your opinions.
If I didn't, I wouldn't say anything at all, I would just ignore you.

That having been said, I've seen you around and I couldn't help but notice
that your posts, while usually helpful, are always over simplified and not
100% correct. You usually miss the bigger picture and quickly move on.

You are obviously intelligent and it would be a shame if you continue on this way.

Absolutely no disrespect/insult intended.
I apologize in advance if you take any offense from this, but remember
:D **Open Source** is interested in your talent;
**Proprietary** is only interested in your money.
----------------

> **prodigy_ said:**
> ...
6. **Gnome network manager is garbage**

...

8. **FUSE/GVFS is implemented in the worst way imaginable**

...

15. **Directories clutter**. All those /bin, /sbin, /usr/bin, /usr/share and so on. We have a 40 years old obsolete and obviously wrong concept that is still here only because of some misplaced sentimentality.

#6 I myself have been pleased with network-manager's continual improvement over the last few years.
Of course there is always room for improvement. I am unsure as to whether
you are talking about network-manager in general or Gnome's specific integration and applet. If your going after network-manager itself
I would have to sarcastically say "Good Luck!" because Intel themselves
have spearheaded the Connection Manager "Connman" project: [http://connman.net/](http://connman.net/)
and it doesn't seem any better. I've been running 10.04 on my laptop since
Alpha 2 and haven't had any issues at all with network-manager including
suspend/resume, static IP profile at work, Wifi **with** static IP
at work, and mobile broadband usage.

#8 The design of gvfs *for its intended use cases* seems to be
a great improvement over the past. My only complaint about it would be it
definitely has some performance bottleneck issues.

Perhaps your #6 and #8 issues may stem from missing the **intended use cases**
in their design goals. Particularly gvfs - I would argue that it isn't
designed for the end user who has any need or even knows how to gain root.

#15 - this is where you are really totally and utterly off the mark.
The filesystem layout has most certainly _***[SIZE="5"]NOT[/SIZE]***_ survived 40 years due to "misplaced sentimentality"
**It has survived and endured because it is battle hardened and field tested.**
It is simply ignorance of cosmic proportions to assume otherwise.

There is absolutely nothing an alternate layout can do that UNIX cannot
*_**except**_* maybe provide a *false* sense of order and "safety."

Aside from functioning just dandy for desktops, laptops, servers,
upercomputers, cell phones, tablets, netbooks, mids;
There is one other trick up it's sleve that is non-trivial, very important
and not easily accomplished by other layouts...
netbooting "thick" client workstations. This is not the same thing as thin clients
or terminal services, these are much more powerful. Check out: [http://drbl.sourceforge.net/](http://drbl.sourceforge.net/)

The often complimented layout of Mac OS X is probably the worst of all.
#1 **it actually has the Unix layout hidden underneath!!!**

#2 click and drag to install, eh?? maybe ... but delete to uninstall, wtf?? no way.

#3 **it actually has the Unix layout hidden underneath!!!**

#4 Broken PATH:
open a terminal in Linux and ```
firefox -P
```^^what happens on a Mac?? [-X

#5 **it actually has the Unix layout hidden underneath!!!**

#6 There is no such thing as a *.app file!!! - They are folders with many more files underneath, the answer to #4 is, on a Mac you must:
```
/Applications/Firefox.app/Contents/MacOS/firefox -P
```

#7 did I mention that Mac **actually has the Unix layout hidden underneath!!!**
If the "newer" and "better" layout can't fully replace the old layout,
what is the point?

/rant :D

### Post by renkinjutsu on 2010-04-25
> **Jpenguin said:**
> [LIST]
[*]Many sound systems (We need one good one)
[*]Many file systems (We need one good one)
[/LIST]

Those are my main complaints with linux

I agree with the sound systems, but i disagree with the filesystems

Many people use linux for different reasons, and for different things, you need different filesystems. One wouldn't host a fileserver with Fat32, one would probably have a myth box with XFS.. some people prefer more stable filesystems like ext3 while others like the speed of ext4.. Those that make tons of changes in their filesystem (installing random packages/etc) can use btrfs (when it's ready) as it offers really amazing snapshotting capabilities..

It's like how nowadays all windows use NTFS (Desktop AND Servers)

The truth is, you can't have all of those features within ONE filesystem. Instead, one has to choose the filesystem to fit their needs.

Most programs are able to work the same regardless of the filesystem anyway, but performance and disk life varies for different tasks

### Post by murderslastcrow on 2010-04-25
I always hated that about the Mac- pretending the system doesn't exist. I mean, Linux is the same way- you have a home folder with hidden configuration files just in case for all of your settings, and the file system, which is actually neatly organized and only looks confusing to anyone who doesn't know what it is- which is exactly like every other OS.

You don't ever HAVE to go into the system files for anything substantial, and even if you do it's not that confusing. I don't see what people are complaining about there.

But yeah, if people say Ubuntu is hard to use, then OS X is certainly difficult to use in how different it is from the 'norm'. The way you control windows, minimize, organize, install, uninstall applications... it's all so against any prior paradigm. Being different just for the sake of being different, not to improve usability. Sorry, I sound very biased, but I've had a lot of experience with it, and seen a lot of people 'experiencing the magic of Apple'. There's a fair deal of frustration, and that's why having choice in interface like you have in Linux is so vitally important- there is no one size that fits everyone.

To be completely honest, I have no issues with Linux itself. My only issues are with some independent software vendors choosing not to support it, which is becoming less and less of a deal these days. Ubuntu in particular just keeps getting more and more usable as time goes on, so I don't have complaints, but I'm sure if I had to go back to using Lucid a year from now, I'd probably have a few gripes. I just don't know what they are, yet. XD

### Post by ptm on 2010-05-01
Documentation:  Here is the Static Network instruction list
Static Connections

If you don't use DHCP then you can configure a static connection.

   1.Right click the NetworkManager icon in the system notification area.
   2.Click Edit Connections.
   3.Click the Wired tab.
   4.Select the connection and click Edit.
   5.Click the IPv4 tab.
   6.Choose Manual from the Method drop down.
   7.Enter your details and click OK.
   8.Click Close.

When your documentation for the "details" of item number 7 doesn't agree with the words used by the Ubuntu design who do you ask?

     My data sheet gives me the following information:
IP address nnn.nnn.nnn.nnn
Sub Mask 255.255.255.224
Default nnn.nnn.nnn.nnn
Pref. DNS nnn.nnn.nnn.nnn
Alt DNS nnn.nnn.nnn.nnn

      What Ubuntu shows 
Address
Netmask
Gateway (which resets to all 0's when you exit the window)
DNS server
Search Domains

### Post by prodigy_ on 2010-05-01
> **asmoore82 said:**
> #6 I myself have been pleased with network-manager's continual improvement over the last few years.
/sigh

Do you always use Ubuntu and only Ubuntu? I suppose you do, or you'd know that NetworkManager is simply inconsistent. I'll give you a quick example: NM is supposes to run scripts in **/etc/NetworkManager/dispatcher.d** when network interfaces are brought up or down. Guess what? It works in Debian or Fedora. But in RHEL and OpenSUSE the contents of dispatcher.d directory are ignored by NM.

> #8 The design of gvfs *for its intended use cases* seems to be a great improvement over the past.
You gotta be kidding me. The purpose of GVFS is to allow users to mount and unmount filesystems w/o forcing them to use su/sudo or asking for password. Naturally, for rw mode a mount point is needed where a regular user would have write or full access. But this doesn't explain why **/home/<user_name>/.gvfs** directories are inaccessible for root. This behavior breaks scripts and causes other problems. That's poor implementation. Nor do we have any explanation of why any mount points should be in **/home** at all. (I could expect a symlink to be placed in my home directory, but not the mount point itself.) That's poor design.

> #15 - this is where you are really totally and utterly off the mark.
There is absolutely nothing an alternate layout can do that UNIX cannot except maybe provide a false sense of order and "safety."

Oh sure it can. At the very least it can try to be intuitive and helpful. **C:\Program Files** is intuitive. **/bin + /usr/bin + /usr/local/bin** is not. (Not to mention that that you can also find executables in various **sbin** and **lib** directories. And that's just executables.) Usually it's merely inconvenient. But on occasion, when things break and you need to clean them up manually, it becomes a pure nightmare.

> **Roasted said:**
> How exactly are you doing the upgrades? Although I don't recommend doing upgrades, I have never heard of this issue before, especially one that has repeated itself. Is there any more information you can give me in regard to what exactly you did during the upgrades?

I typed into terminal (from 9.10):
```
sudo apt-get dist upgrade
```
It then proceeded to delete a LOT of things, like it might replace them later. It didn't and the upgrade finished with an error.

Then, after CHROOTing into my disk from a livecd, I finished installing the updates with:
```
sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get install -f
sudo dpkg -configure -a

```
This all finished and checked out fine. Upon rebooting into the newest linux 10.04 kernel, it froze on the plymouth splash screen. I tried booting into the Karmic Kernel, which gave me this at the plymouth screen:
```
The disk drive / is not ready or does not exist. Press M to manually configure or wait.
```
If I try to press S, nothing happens. It's locked up. 
I have a bootlogscript thing saved. I can post it here if you like, if it helps.
EDIT: The disk is fine, it isn't failing or anything.

 - opened up computer, took card out. replaced with nvidia card. same problems.

> Ive spent the last 8 hours trying to boot up but all I get is:

the disc drive for / is not ready

at splash. What an awesome upgrade.


I feel for you... My attempt at 9.10 > 10.04 migration fell in a heap as well. It turned out it was trying to mount non-existing devices, then promptly crashed and lock the keyboard. 

I gave up and restored from a backup. 

I have now done a clean install and am currently restoring all my data. Seems to me that that is a better way. Leave the dubious upgrades alone, do a backup, then new install and restore your applications, configuration files and data. You'll rage less and will feel much better. :biggrin:

Cheers

### Post by Roasted on 2010-05-03
> **asuastrophysics said:**
> I typed into terminal (from 9.10):
```
sudo apt-get dist upgrade
```
It then proceeded to delete a LOT of things, like it might replace them later. It didn't and the upgrade finished with an error.

Then, after CHROOTing into my disk from a livecd, I finished installing the updates with:
```
sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get install -f
sudo dpkg -configure -a

```
This all finished and checked out fine. Upon rebooting into the newest linux 10.04 kernel, it froze on the plymouth splash screen. I tried booting into the Karmic Kernel, which gave me this at the plymouth screen:
```
The disk drive / is not ready or does not exist. Press M to manually configure or wait.
```
If I try to press S, nothing happens. It's locked up. 
I have a bootlogscript thing saved. I can post it here if you like, if it helps.
EDIT: The disk is fine, it isn't failing or anything.

I checked to see if my ATI card was the problem: it's not.
 - opened up computer, took card out. replaced with nvidia card. same problems.

Did you ever do an upgrade the standard way through Update Manager?

### Post by Another Monkey on 2010-05-04
There's a few (very few) things that I hate in Ubuntu, and some are general to Linux.
[LIST=1]
[*]_Poor printer support_
Sorry, but it's true.  I agree that it's the fault of the OEMs, but it is still a sod for the end-user.
[*]_Remote desktop_
Or the total lack thereof.  Vinagre simply does not cut it.  I am well aware of XDMCP, but the VPN freaks at that.  Ubuntu (Linux!) needs a remote desktop system on a par with MS.  Scratch that.  BETTER than MS's!
[*]_Network Manager_
This really is a pox on the face of Ubuntu.  You can't connect to hidden networks easily ("OK" button does not become enabled).  I sets every new connection as "Auto", even if you are just trying to tests stuff.  If you have two cards, the list of available networks is split between them, making it hard to connect to a network via card B as the network only appears in the list for card A.
[*]_Poor multi-head support_
I have tried repeatedly and failed to ever get this to work.  It should be as easy as it is on Windows (and be able to have screens at different resolutions, not this "square" virtual desktop garbage).
[*]_Poor graphics performance_
Again this may be down to OEMs, but it is still a sod of the end user.
[*]_Documentation_
Often out of date.  No real indication of what version it applies to or if the page has been superseded.
[*]_Ubuntu One (esp. the client)._
It's simply too unstable to be of any use.  I keep having to add computers to my account again and again, even though they are already there.  Each app (e.g. Tomboy) seems to want it's own account.  When there is a fault, there's never any information what went wrong.  It's just dreadful, which is a shame as this really could be a flag-ship product for Canonical.
[*]_Empathy_
Simply does not offer enough control, even simple things (custom statuses) are missing.  Stick with Pidgin!  Although they both lack SIPE support which is a major blocker.
[*]_Rhythmbox_
Again, overly simple.  Does not support easy syncing of podcasts etc with portable devices.  Crashes regularly.  Shame really.  If this thing could handle podcasts properly, it could be killer.  Instead one has to try various apps (Banshee, Miro...) which all fail to work correctly in their own little way and entail duplication of function.
[*]_Sound_
PulseAudio sometimes works.  It sometimes works well.  I like the way it lets me manage the volume of the different apps.  There endeth the honeymoon.  There is no system-wide equalizer.  The volume resets randomly (causing me to leap out of my seat).  The number of options are confusing.  ALSA?  PulseAudio?  OSS?  Something else?  I dunno.  I want the sound to come out of my speakers, I do not want to take a degree in audio-engineering just to figure out WTF has gone wrong this time!  Pick ONE and stick with it!  I am willing to learn, I am willing to read documentation but...see above.
[/LIST]
The list of what I like is far longer (as is the list of what I hate about Windows!) and I know I can change many things, I am thinking of how Ubuntu is out-of-the-box.  Of course this is all merely just my opinion and recent experience.  Yours may vary.
Flame on...

---

### Post by asbesto on 2010-05-04
> **Another Monkey said:**
> 
[*]_Sound_
PulseAudio sometimes works.  It sometimes works well.  I like the way it lets me manage the volume of the different apps.  There endeth the honeymoon.  There is no system-wide equalizer.  The volume resets randomly (causing me to leap out of my seat).  The number of options are confusing.  ALSA?  PulseAudio?  OSS?  Something else?  I dunno.  I want the sound to come out of my speakers, I do not want to take a degree in audio-engineering just to figure out WTF has gone wrong this time!  Pick ONE and stick with it!  I am willing to learn, I am willing to read documentation but...see above.


Same to me. First thing I do after an install: apt-get remove pulseaudio. Kill that CRAP. Totally unuseful. Totally confusing people. 

And, why the hell all those sound cards settings in my mixer? I only have one, ONE, *1* damn soundcard, not FIVE!! I don't give a hell about alsa, oss, pulse, HW stuff. I want sound OUT of my speakers, and I want to CAPTURE from my damn mic! What's all that CRAP in mixer settings??!?

I'm really becoming MAD creating a live stream from a webcam here using ustream.tv interface. I set up mixer, I turn on everything, and it work fine. Until something decide to SHUT OFF THE MIC. And really I can't find what the hell is doing this. And so my live stream become mute!!!

I had only a solution - a chmod a-w /dev/mixer, and now the livestream is working perfectly with audio capture on. 

Really, this bunch of audio crappiness is driving me mad.


Another thing I HATE in Ubuntu is all this stupid asking for a password for everything an user has to do. This is totally nonsense. A password for login, a password for connecting network, a pass to edit network, a pass to logout, a pass to install packages. DAMN, this is not windows, this is UNIX. I'm logged, I can use SU and do WHATEVER I WANT. STOP asking passwords! this is SO STUPID.

I wrote an howto on [http://lab.dyne.org](http://lab.dyne.org) about how to get rid of passwords in ubuntu, maybe can be useful for someone; just search there or on the web. I can't post it here because administrators blame me about some sort of stupid policy about circumventing security (but this is not about security, this is about stupidity)

:confused:

---

### Post by Rubi1200 on 2010-05-04
Excuse me for being somewhat ignorant, but if you don't like the way security is handled in Linux, why are you using it in the first place?

You have a choice: go back to Windows and all its major security flaws.

I am new to Linux, but even I understand why the model adopted by Ubuntu enhances system security.

If you want to mess your system up, it is your prerogative as a computer user. 

But I don't think you have the right to say something is stupid merely because you find it inconvenient for your particular needs.

I have read a fair bit already about Linux security, root, and sudo, and I think it is an excellent layer of protection.

I would rather pay the price of a little bit of inconvenience and in return get a whole load of security.

Just my opinion.
:)

---

I find that a sensible option.

### Post by asbesto on 2010-05-04
I hate Pulseaudio because every time pulseaudio, alsa, oss and other stuff I have not identified goes in conflict against each others. Also, I really can't see how pulseaudio can help me: everything works fine WITHOUT pulseaudio; also, many things have PROBLEMS using pulseaudio. So, I hate that crap. :)

About the security issue: every time I obtain the same answer: "If you dont like this way, go back to Windows". No. I go back to UNIX, to LINUX. I go back to DEBIAN, to SLACKWARE, and to every other LINUX os rather than Ubuntu. 

I don't have any such thing as "PolicyKit" on Debian or other distros. I don't have any STUPID requester asking for the SAME password every time I do something on my OWN LAPTOP, with no open doors and no other account than mine. And I don't work as root.

I'm used to give ONE password and to work without any kind of problem. Having the user asked for the same stupid password every time it has something to do, is the same behaviour of stupidness that WINDOWS is. 

It's ubuntu like Windows? In this way, definitively, YES. And this is what I hate.

This is UNIX, not Windows. Let's stop this way to "guide users thru security". If you can't deal with SU or fdisk or dangerous commands without having a password asked for everything, please use windows instead.

The correct way is to teach users to use UNIX/Linux, not to transform Ubuntu into the same crap as windows is.

IMHO. ;)

### Post by jerrylamos on 2010-05-05
Comparatively I prefer Ubuntu to other distro's or Windoze, which is why I'm using it here.

Ubuntu development does "spring" things on us like "KMS" with no clear explanation I'm aware of about what it is or what it will do for us.  For many many months the development work on "KMS" causes "black screen" hangs on two of my pc's.  With workarounds from the forum users I can get running again.  O.K., Alpha's and Beta's break, but the Release Code is still breaking!

What is "KMS" and what use is it for me since I have to keep finding out new ways to turn it off?

"Pulse Audio" is another one.  Suddenly it appeared months and months ago resulting in "no audio" and if there was an explanation of what it is and why it is needed I missed it.  All I know is that many many of us have had to use the forums to get audio back again.

What is "Pulse Audio" and why does it keep breaking sound??

Now I am a glutton for punishment so I do run Alpha and Beta Ubuntu's.  I'm just surprised that the Release is made when there are still widespread problems with "KMS" whatever that is and "Pulse Audio" whatever that is.

Anyway, when's the next Alpha code coming out?

Jerry

### Post by Cincinnatux on 2010-05-09
I hate that Ubuntu is still buggy enough that it is a hard sell to convince non-geeks to try it out.  For developers, this is probably a good thing, as an order of magnitude jump in the number of 'help me, I borked my system' requests would only slow down improvements to Linux apps in general.  But as a guy who loves using and talking about open source software, I really wish more of my friends used Ubuntu (or some other Linux distro).  Talking to my wife all the time about it is slowly driving her nuts.  :)  And she's not about to switch from XP to Ubuntu because she sees how routinely I lose basic functionality of things like wireless Internet, Flash, web browser stability, and the like.  I enjoy tinkering around with my computer, so that increases the risk of breaking functionality, but even if my goal were to have a 100% reliable and stable experience, it would be touch-and-go as the update from one software package inadvertently screws with the functionality of another software package.  Maybe Linux will forever be a niche OS.  But the key reason why I use Ubuntu is that I'm hoping it will achieve mainstream appeal (adoption rates above 15 or 20% of the personal computing 'market' would be awesome).  Yet every 6 months when I upgrade, the upgrade typically breaks bits of my system to the point where I have found that May and October of each year are my troubleshooting months, rather than productivity months.  I know, I should probably just stick with LTS upgrades; from that perspective, though, Canonical ought to make LTS-only the default Ubuntu installation and recommend the 6-month cycle only for geeks willing to be in a perpetual state of tweaking/troubleshooting.

In the end analysis, though, I love Ubuntu.  Every time I have to deal with a Windows computer I find the intrusiveness of that OS increasingly frustrating.

---

### Post by dtfinch on 2010-05-09
There's always something, and I can never remember them all. A few recent things:

The default IM program (Empathy) and/or the indicator applet are very bad at letting me know when someone's trying to message me, with no option to fix it apart from installing pidgin in its place. All because someone wrote this nifty new indicator applet, which emphasized silence over all else, and they wouldn't rest until everything and everyone used it and only it. They got their way, and yet another pre-alpha quality package was pushed in place of a mature and capable one.

Copying a large number of files in the background brings the system to a crawl. Firefox freezes for 5-10 seconds at a time, whenever it tries to access the disk for something. Apparently this is a known year and a half old kernel bug. Switching the elevator from CFQ to deadline reduces the delays, but doesn't fix it entirely. See [https://bugzilla.kernel.org/show_bug.cgi?id=12309](https://bugzilla.kernel.org/show_bug.cgi?id=12309) and [http://it.slashdot.org/article.pl?sid=09/01/15/049201](http://it.slashdot.org/article.pl?sid=09/01/15/049201)

The busy/wait cursor. ~90% of the time that it's showing, nothing is actually busy, like it's on a fixed timer whenever a new program is launched. Because it's round, with no tip, the system is harder to use whenever it's showing. On my own system, I got around it by making a new cursor theme based on DMZ-White and replacing the "watch" cursor with a copy of the "arrow" cursor. Busy cursors are helpful, but I'd rather have none at all than one appeared all the time for little or no reason.

---

### Post by alienprdkt on 2010-05-12
> **juliobahar said:**
> Well this is also interesting. But does Asterisk support SIP Voip servers?

I've downloaded it once, but wasn't able to figure out how to get it to work, for the lack of a GUI.

Very easy to install the gui ..

with a single command 

 yum groupinstall  &#8220;X Window System&#8221; &#8220;GNOME Desktop Environment&#8221; 

login and run the command 

startx 

will start up you up in a gnome environment.

It is a VOIP server... lol

you should never run a gui on a server.

Plenty of documentation is out there, thats how I got it up and going.

New version 1.5 has FreePBX integrated. Lots of help is out on the Freepbx.org forums

Did take me bout 3 weeks to get running well.

But well worth the hassles.

### Post by seeker5528 on 2010-05-14
> **alienprdkt said:**
> well it use to feel real responsive and was able to install more then one app. now I dont use it and instead just fall back to the cli, or create an unattended script because its much faster to install everything you want on a system all at once, instead of the find an app, install, hit back search another app install;)

If you have created an unattended install script or if you do the 'dpkg get-selections', 'dpkg set-selections', 'apt-get dselect-install' thing, that's always going to be faster if you are installing on multiple machines or want to duplicate your existing installation on a new machine. I've never done the unattended script thing, so I don't know what kind of time is involved in the software selection process, but for duplicating an existing install, it doesn't get much more efficient than 'get, set, install'. 

The 2.0.3 version of Software Center I have on my system now seems fairly responsive compared to what I remember and is more efficient to navigate and initiate installations as well.

As far as the more than one app at a time thing is concerned.....

In every version of Software Center I have used, going back to 1.x versions, installing multiple things was never an issue, selecting multiple things then choosing to install as a single job was never an option. It's always been.....

Find something to install, choose to install it, it starts installing. 

Find something else and choose to install, if the download/install of the first thing is not completed yet, any additional software chosen for installation gets put in the queue.

When the first thing has completed installing, the next thing in the queue starts installing, which repeats until there is nothing left in the cue.
 
Later, Seeker

---

### Post by madmax75 on 2010-05-14
Installing Sun Java in Ubuntu.

Just pain in the ***.

And don't say "just check out the Partner repository in the Software Center". I did, it is not there. There is only Adobe stuff.

I found this guide to add the repo from the UbuntuGeek website:

[http://www.ubuntugeek.com/how-install-sun-java-runtime-environment-jre-in-ubuntu-10-04-lucid-lynx.html](http://www.ubuntugeek.com/how-install-sun-java-runtime-environment-jre-in-ubuntu-10-04-lucid-lynx.html)

So far, no luck with this one either. I get to this part:

"you’ll get a screen that contains the Sun Operating System Distributor  License for Java and hit Enter to continue."

Except that "hitting Enter" or anything else for that matter does absolutely nothing. All that can be done there is to stare that license text for all eternity.

And the installation guide that Sun has at it's own web site. Just horrible crap that does not work. I have tried that as well, several times. Does not work.

Oh, did I mention that the open source IcedTea Java is buggy? It heats up my CPU to a boiling point and freezes the Firefox. So that won't work either, until it gets fixed.

### Post by aj_the_first on 2010-05-24
#1: Flash.
It will never work properly in Ubuntu.  There will always be a problem somewhere.  It may be youtube one day, megavideo the next day, flash games have never worked, and a few pieces of flash based software that I use never work.
Oh, and full screen flash.  This finally got fixed it looks like, but flash is an integral part of the internet nowdays, and without a fully functioning flash plug-in, the operating system will never make it out of a small group of dedicated users.
I've poured more hours of my life into fixing flash bugs in Ubuntu than I have spent playing all versions of final fantasy combined, and to any FF player, that should drop some jaws.
#2: Sound.
It will never work properly.  Maybe after a couple reboots and lots of tweaking, but I shouldn't have to fool my computer into playing sound.  And it won't auto-switch to a USB sound output device, you have to open sound options to change output to the newly pluged in device.
#3: Wireless.
Sometimes it works, sometimes it doesn't.  Depends on the mood of the computer.  Sometimes it will work, but there will be no icon, just a black square.
#4: Big buttons
The system (Nautilus) buttons and bars are too big, even with GTK+.  They seem to be about three to five pixels bigger than windows in dimensions.  It uses up way to much viewing space.
#5 Games
This doesn't really need to be said.  I think we all know that we have to give up most games in order to use ubuntu.
#6 Photo editing
I have spent HOURS pouring over every photo editing program that I can find for linux.  Not a single one is as easy and intuitive as MSphotomanager, and most don't have any of the usual tools.  All I want to do is crop and adjust lighting.  You can't do that with GIMP without hours and a how-to manual.  For people who just use a point-and-shoot camera and then want to do a little clean up to their photos: there are no Linux options out there.

I am a dedicated Ubuntu user, and I don't plan on going back.  I hate windows and Mac far more than ubuntu...

---

### Post by libssd on 2010-05-26
> **Zeven said:**
> What I dislike about Ubuntu is that there are not enough users, meaning that I have to wait quite a while for software developers to release their latest marvels for Ubuntu. Examples of this are Opera 10.5 and Skype, both being in their beta. Another issue is that I just can't figure out how to make VLC Media Player my default media player. Although Ubuntu is far superior to Windows Vista, my other operating system, it's little things like the aforementioned issues that send me back to Windows Vista every now and then.
I haven't read all the posts in this thread, but this one hits home. And, far too much under the hood command line tweaking is still required to attract a mass audience. If a given version of Ubuntu works out of the box on your hardware, it's great,  arguably superior to Windows, and nearly as good as Mac OS X. If it doesn't, you may be able to make it work after a lot of googling and command line tweaking, which is a turnoff to the "average" user who may have little or no interest in *how* computers work; he just wants his computer *to* work. As a result, I'm afraid Ubuntu (like all the other flavors of Linux) will always remain a niche OS compared to its commercial competitors.

With 9.04 on an Acer AA1 D150, I have none of the hardware problems others have reported. Flash, sound, mic, camera, suspend, memory cards all work. 

My one gripe (which I tolerated for a year before tweaking) is that the default "grab" area for resizing windows is too narrow, but this is configurable (with a bit of sleuthing). I would prefer to be able to make the actual window border a little wider, but increasing the width of the grab zone is good enough.

Many people have complained that they don't like the way the latest version does things (I couldn't get 10.04 to work at all on my hardware). So, why use it? If an earlier version works for you, stick with it.

### Post by roberthr on 2010-06-01
Mostly this:

1. Mozilla products does not save print settings (bug present for at least a year)
[https://support.mozilla.com/en-US/forum/1/547037](https://support.mozilla.com/en-US/forum/1/547037)
[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=869537&highlight=firefox+print+resolution](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=869537&highlight=firefox+print+resolution)

2. Webcam freezes aMSN (it never work the way it should)
[https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/linux/+bug/443278](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/linux/+bug/443278)

3. Bluetooth not working on MSI Wind (also present since MSI Wind came out)
[https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/kdebluetooth/+bug/290151](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/kdebluetooth/+bug/290151)

4. Connecting to 3G networks with USB or PCMCIA cards almost impossible 
[https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/modemmanager/+bug/417389](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/modemmanager/+bug/417389)

5. You don't have any control to change network or check signal strength using 3G modems

6. Evolution does not support IMAP Idle (after 10 years of Idle extension, Evolution still doesn't know what it is)

7. Evolution does not support Cyrus IMAP Public folders

8. Folder sharing is not straight forward

9. Network sharing is to difficult to achieve

10. Flash video and Skype still doesn't recognise V4L2 devices, so you have to use workaround

Well, I know those things are not really Ubuntu specific, but at least there could be some effort to fix them

### Post by rg4w on 2010-06-25
For all my love of Ubuntu, two things drive me crazy:

1. Notifications banner
Is this Windows?  If the OS had confidence in its ability to connect to a network, it would simply do it without splashing a large banner letting me know it did what I told it to do.  It should be expected to do what I told it to do, and only notify me when it doesn't.  Making a big notification when performing normal routine tasks without error just looks bad.

2. I've never had an upgrade go smoothly, and am now in the habit of doing fresh installs.  I'm okay with that, since I find a fresh install a little cleaner than upgrades (on OS X too, FWIW).  But I fear for my less tech-savvy friends who adopt Ubuntu expecting it to have the fit and finish of things like OS X when it comes to upgrades (and of course OS X isn't perfect, but in 20 years with that OS I've only had one upgrade go bad).

### Post by LoREZ on 2010-06-26
Let me first state that I love Linux.  But this is because I'm a person addicted to variety, and I like to tinker.  Linux satisfies both tendencies.

That being said, there *are* things done better in Windows.  You know, I like the CLI; I like GUIs too.  I like BOTH.  I don't want to *have* to leave one to go to the other for a non-recovery reason.  Windows, while it has an anemic CLI, generally sticks with the GUI, allowing you to do almost everything that way.  Linux straddles both interfaces, but is filled with apps that half-a$$ their GUI frontend.  I don't want to launch a frontend only to find that the one setting I need doesn't exist in the preferences menu, but that I might make it happen in a text file (or two) somewhere.  That is slow and annoying.

That leads to the second criticism: config file organization.  Why do I have to hunt these down?  Why can't they all be in the same place?  You know, Arch does a good job of correcting this madness, reducing the number of system configuration files dramatically, but Ubuntu will drive a man to madness with the huge number of places one can find obscure settings.  And it's getting worse -- a lot worse.  I realize they make a good show of it with Control Center, but not good enough -- and it still leaves me in a bind when I want/need the command line.  I'd really prefer not to have to memorize obscure file paths.  A menu-driven vi-style preferences editor would be nice, with an exposed command line in the same session, so I don't have to go around the world in eighty days to find, edit, and test a setting.  This is my primary frustration with Linux.

---

### Post by WinRiddance on 2010-06-28
Windows incorporated "Windows restore" ever since Win98 SE (command line back then). I must have fixed dozens of machines for people by using that, and later more standardized "click-restore" functions in later versions of Windows. That can be a life saver for a lot of people and I hate the fact that Ubuntu has not made that a standard function yet, more than 10 years later. You give people fish (Operating System) and they'll have food (awesome functionality) ..... but if you give someone a fishing pole (restore function) he or she may have food (a properly working OS) for life !!!


Hmmm, I also hate the fact that Ubuntu seems to be **losing it's focus on keeping things simple,** in particular for people who've never had a computer before or people with very little computer experience. Speaking strictly for Ubuntu and not some of the other Linux distros out there, that was always one of the main things about Ubuntu that made it different ... the simplicity with a smaller learning curve.
But now Ubuntu is _getting packed more and more stuff by default_ with settings under SYSTEM - PREFERENCES **and** SYSTEM - ADMINISTRATION that it can literally scare that new Ubuntu user to death. There's a lot of stuff between those two areas that can/should be disabled by default. Don't get me wrong, those items are definitely needed, but have someone get used to their system first, **then let them ask questions and be pointed** to SYSTEM - PREFERENCES - MAIN MENU if they haven't already figured out how to get there yet.

For me personally it's a bit of a pet peeve by now, to constantly have to answer questions by saying things like "You'll probably never use that" and "Don't worry, it's not meant for users of your caliber" or "Just stay away from that because you could mess something up inadvertently" and so on.

From my point of view as someone who installs, supports, and helps others with their Ubuntu, it can never be too easy to use. **The easier the better ....**
Keeping things streamlined more _also keeps things easier and less confusing_ for many people who get overpowered with the many available features & options.

Another example of this is the removal of Gimp in order to replace it with Pitivi. I deal primarily with average people, average computer users, generally people who are not specialized in one area or another on their machine. Oddly enough _just about everyone whom I know_ wants to be somehow involved in some type of image editing, be it for generic business purposes, photos for a photo album, a simple little personal website, whatever. Most of those average people would benefit far greater from Gimp than from Pitivi which is a much more specialized video editing application. In all honestly, aside from myself and a couple of other people I don't know anyone who does video editing on the computer. Yet I know dozens of people with computers who like to dabble a little here and there with images for whatever reason. It would have made more sense to install **Gnucash** by default since darn near everyone has a bank account, or software like **Backintime** with some simple help files to make anyone, even a computer newbie, at least somewhat comfortable about the prospect of backing up their important data. But no, let's give everyone Pitivi because it's cooler .... :confused:

---

### Post by GuiGuy on 2010-06-28
> **WinRiddance said:**
> Windows incorporated "Windows restore" ever since Win98 SE (command line back then).

### Post by quixote on 2010-07-12
Some of the braindead defaults in gnome:

F-spot: the world's most useless photo program.  Can't even save a file to a directory normally.  (Btw, Shotwell? Same thing.)  Gthumb is there, it does everything.  USE IT.  Why shove F-spot at us?

Pulseaudio: Maybe it's brilliant.  Fine.  But it messes up all sorts of common applications (hello? Skype?) which are badly behaved enough to insist on direct access to sound.  Sorry, pulseaudio, but some of us use Ubuntu because it "just works."  When stuff as basic as the microphone takes two days and a degree in computer engineering to get functional in a common program, you've broken something important. 

(Yeah, Pulseaudio is my current problem and I'm tearing my hair out.  That's why this whole rant is so intemperate.  I've been using Ubuntu since 2005 as my only OS, love it, and think the whole thing and the community are a truly amazing endeavor.  But right now I'm Really Frustrated.)

Make it easy to uninstall stuff like Pulseaudio.  Don't interdigitate it with the whole damn system.

Same goes for indicator applet.  I don't want the stupid messaging envelope taking up space in my panel.  I want easy access to volume control.  Those are two separate things!  Stop combining them!

The default buttons in Maverick.  Don't get me started.  Do not muck about with the user's desktop.  That belongs to the user, not the devs.  We all have our own work styles, and your style may not be mine.  **Make it easily configurable**.  Not as in open-a-terminal-and-use-gconf-editor configurable.  As in right-click configurable.  As in give people a choice during upgrade/install.

The totally braindead way media are referred to in the file manager and on the desktop.  By *size*?  Hello?  How many 8GB removable media do you think are connected to my computer right now?  The only way to tell them apart is to open each damn one and see what's in there.  D.U.M.B.  Also, no clear reference to the mount path which you're going to need for lots of things.  

And then, where they're mounted, in /media, they're referred to NOT by size.  There it's by UUID, which I'm sure is an even better way for normal humans to recognize partitions!  Honestly.  

Just a suggestion: how bloody hard would it be to let the user rename the drive with an alias, and then in the title bar or address bar actually provide the device and path.  Like so: /dev/sdb*2* mounted on /media/*my-alias*

...

Hrmph.  Well, I guess that's all for now... I'll go get back in my basket...

### Post by t.rei on 2010-07-15
> **quixote said:**
> 

Pulseaudio: Maybe it's brilliant.  Fine.  But it messes up all sorts of common applications (hello? Skype?) which are badly behaved enough to insist on direct access to sound.  Sorry, pulseaudio, but some of us use Ubuntu because it "just works."  When stuff as basic as the microphone takes two days and a degree in computer engineering to get functional in a common program, you've broken something important. 

(Yeah, Pulseaudio is my current problem and I'm tearing my hair out.  That's why this whole rant is so intemperate.  I've been using Ubuntu since 2005 as my only OS, love it, and think the whole thing and the community are a truly amazing endeavor.  But right now I'm Really Frustrated.)

Make it easy to uninstall stuff like Pulseaudio.  Don't interdigitate it with the whole damn system.


Hi,
I also had lots of big great fun with regards to pulseaudio. Not to go offtopic on the "hate" thread, but to let you know, how I 'solved' it:
```
 
 sudo chmod -x /usr/bin/pulseaudio
 killall pulseaudio

```Yes, this turns off pulseaudio, yes the sound-applet will not work anymore, BUT: alsa runs great, I can skype, ts3, play on wine and listen to musik with amarok all at the same time (seperate push-to-talk-buttons are sweet )

Also there is a way to make the volume control work for your multimedia-keys:
create a script in /usr/local/bin/ and make it executable:


```

#!/bin/sh

if [ $1 = 'up' ]; then
    amixer set Master 2+
elif [ $1 = 'down' ]; then
    amixer set Master 2-
elif [ $1 = 'mute' ]; then
    amixer set Master toggle
else
    echo "Unknown control command: $1" >&2
    exit 1
fi
VOLUME=$(amixer -c 0 get Master|grep %|sed -r 's/.*\[(.*)%\].*/\1/'|head -n 1)
STATUS=$(amixer -c 0 get Master|egrep  -e "Playback.*\[on|off\]"|sed -r 's/.*(on|off).*/\1/g')
if [ $STATUS = 'off' ]; then
    ICON=notification-audio-volume-muted
else
    echo $VOLUME
    if [ $VOLUME -eq 0 ]; then
        ICON=notification-audio-volume-off
    elif [ $VOLUME -lt 33 ]; then
        ICON=notification-audio-volume-low
    elif [ $VOLUME -lt 66 ]; then
        ICON=notification-audio-volume-medium
    else
        ICON=notification-audio-volume-high
    fi
    VOLUME="${VOLUME}%"
fi
notify-send "Volume" -i $ICON -h int:value:$VOLUME -h string:x-canonical-private-synchronous:

```
now you can go to your keyboard settings in gnome and create some commands:
"volume.sh up" will increase the volume
"volume.sh down" will...
"volume.sh mute" will toggle mute on and off
all the while only using alsa and no pulseaudio.

Works for me.

Greetings.

### Post by prodigy_ on 2010-07-17
> **betlog said:**
> [this includes knetworkmanager right?]

This includes any version of NetworkManager. There are three main problems with it:

1) The lack of documentation. Once you want to perform some non-trivial task, you're on your own and mostly you'll have to take the trial-and-error route.
2) The behavior is totally inconsistent from distro to distro. (I already wrote about this earlier in this thread.) Want a portable script that configures, let's say, a VPN connection and integrates with NM? Prepare to write 10 different scripts.
3) NM ignores other tools. It just forces its way if it can or breaks down. If it's supposed be a smart GUI tool then why is it so dumb?

There are also many minor issues (NM isn't modular so you can't easily disable just a part of it, the developers prefer binaries to shell scripts so you can't fix something without patching sources and recompiling, etc.)

### Post by asuastrophysics on 2010-07-18
[B]This is just my opinion. Everything I have mentioned below exists solely for the constructive benefit of the open-source writers. 

You may wish to tell me any of the following:
a) I'm wrong b) I'm wrong because "I don't know what I'm talking about". c) I'm wrong because "I'm just an end user, and I don't know anything about software programming" (True statement, but isn't the end user supposed to be the person who usually gives most feedback?)

That's okay. I wasn't writing the stuff below to please anybody. I only wish the best for my open-source community. Because of this, and because I wish to write my feedback in the most constructive manner possible, I may sound harsh, blunt, or whatever you want to call it. In the end, opinions are like assholes so take this as you wish:[/B] (No troll)

I don't like the fact that the whole thing is put together by random people who don't seem to communicate anywhere near enough IMO. Every small component making up the operating system is developed by some  other independent person or small group of writers. This may create a fantastic community project (GNU), but overall, the end resulting operating system doesn't seem to work as seamlessly as I think it should. Because of this, the OS as a whole, is like a deeply intricate jigsaw puzzle, and it's behavior seems very fragmented at times. This, consequentially (more often than not), makes problems buried deep within the OS as difficult to diagnose as an extraterrestrial alien with with hodgkin's lymphoma. Overall, I don't feel like the OS operates seamlessly enough.

I've been running Ubuntu over a year and a half now, and it seems that some (not all, I didn't say all) of the Ubuntu-community "Software Center"-endorsed programs are so unstable, they're almost unsuitable for everyday practical purposes. (Program versions 0.5 and earlier really aren't ready for the software centers of beginning linux users such as myself.)

On the subject of drivers:
For a while after switching from Windows to Ubuntu, I noticed a quite noticeable drop in graphics performance. I was told that the reason for the poor performance of my graphics was due to my ATI graphics card. I was recommended to go with nVidia, since ATI made crappy linux drivers. So here I am finally running with an nVidia graphics card, as recommended by the community.
The result: absolutely no change in my graphics drivers' performance at all. Rhythmbox visualizer still sucks while running with other programs open at the same time (with compiz running). Libflashplayer.so is still just as buggy, and I still sometimes have to disable compiz to see graphics animations work correctly. (<- The above has been a problem forever, existing even when my computer was brand new. 

Food for thought: I think there may be a reason why Mozilla just abruptly officially abandoned it's code development with Songbird for Linux, even though it's an open-source media player. I hypothesize that it was almost impossible (logistically and financially) for them to develop a simple compiz-window behavior for it, since it doesn't seem to obey compiz window settings (among MANY other things) It may have not cost-effective for them to work this stuff out.

So sure, you can simply just dismiss all the problems I've said above as being primarily caused by independent software writers who don't want to play nice...and we can all continue to go on and play the blame-game: "it's Acer's fault for not making graphics drivers", or "It's Adobe's fault for the last buggy version of flash not working well with your window manager".

You can point the finger at software writers, or the hardware driver developers, or the hardware itself, or anything you want. But in the end, this lack of uniformity detracts from the otherwise really attractive aspects of the operating system. 

So that's just what I think.

---

### Post by Mike_IronFist on 2010-07-19
Simply put, and this is really the fault of NVIDIA, but it still holds true to me, I HATE NVIDIA's graphics drivers for Ubuntu and Linux in general. It seems like every time they fix a serious bug, they find a way to create a new one. Performance with the current driver is usually fine, but it takes a nosedive when you put your machine on suspend and then wake it up. Back in the days of 8.10 Intrepid, I nearly gave up on Ubuntu altogether because the NVIDIA driver was so bad that it made YouTube videos run like a slideshow. The next machine I'm getting will have an ATI graphics card, no doubt about it.

The only other thing I really don't like is in Lucid, which has this stupid bug that doesn't initialize compiz properly. As a result, window borders are sometimes gone right when I start up my machine. I worked around this by adding "compiz --replace" to my startup apps, but that adds more to my startup time, which is obnoxious.

Other than NVIDIA being terrible and that minor compiz bug, I'm very happy with Ubuntu. It doesn't crash, freeze, slow-down, or anything in the way that Windows does. One of my favorite things, though, is the file structure. /home/mike has all my files. Meanwhile, Windows has C:\Users\Mike, and also the music libraries, photo libraries, etc, all in a brutal mess that doesn't make it easy to find your own damned files. So I am, by no means, considering switching back to Windows.

### Post by spamhog on 2010-07-21
What I hate is not exactly a problem with Ubuntu itself, more an old design error that could be ameliorated through an amendment in RFC793 or thereabouts.

The protocol should include a detector for strings like "ubuntu" and issue a challenge packet triggering a strong CAPTCHA on the host, drawn from a cooperatively managed cloud-caching system.

Successful solution would trigger a PKI signature (keys being obviously DNS-indexed) and a pass flag by the challenging router. All subsequent routers would only have to check the signature against keyservers before honoring the pass flag.

This would require a modest and inexpensive increase in computation at router level, while requiring a prohibitive effort by non-qualifying agencies at the edge.

In the course of time this challenge system would dramatically improve the S+N/N ratio in the results of preexisting-knowledge searches by hindering the presently excessive input dispensation (especially REPLY-type) by agencies presumably not qualifying as chordata: [http://is.gd/dAKu7](http://is.gd/dAKu7)

### Post by dtfinch on 2010-07-21
> **GrogTheDreamer said:**
> I hate how FF and Chrome are so much slower in Ubuntu than they are in Windows 7.  Pages load 2% of the time, the other 98% is either a timeout with no notification or a WSoD.  That and the non-standard backup/imaging methods.  Other than that, it's beautiful :)

Years ago, I had issues where several programs on Ubuntu assumed I had working IPv6 support when I didn't. So they'd sent dns IPv6 dns requests, and wait a couple minutes for them to timeout before trying IPv4. If that's your problem, a Firefox specific fix would be to go to the url "about:config" and change "network.dns.disableIPv6" to true. I forgot the steps for disabling it system wide, as it's been years since it did that to me.

As far as backups, I always found them to be hardest to perform on Windows, with its mandatory deny-read file locking preventing important files from being copied, and crippled unreliable built-in backup utility, creating a market for high priced backup software to work around their flawed design. Volume shadow copies made things a little easier, but it's still a pain. Rsync works well for most of my Linux backup needs, quickly synchronizing large directories to backup servers or drives with minimal copying, while preserving past revisions. I use it on several servers and at home.

### Post by Vimmander on 2010-08-01
> **dtfinch said:**
> Years ago, I had issues where several programs on Ubuntu assumed I had working IPv6 support when I didn't. So they'd sent dns IPv6 dns requests, and wait a couple minutes for them to timeout before trying IPv4. If that's your problem, a Firefox specific fix would be to go to the url "about:config" and change "network.dns.disableIPv6" to true. I forgot the steps for disabling it system wide, as it's been years since it did that to me.

As far as backups, I always found them to be hardest to perform on Windows, with its mandatory deny-read file locking preventing important files from being copied, and crippled unreliable built-in backup utility, creating a market for high priced backup software to work around their flawed design. Volume shadow copies made things a little easier, but it's still a pain. Rsync works well for most of my Linux backup needs, quickly synchronizing large directories to backup servers or drives with minimal copying, while preserving past revisions. I use it on several servers and at home.

It's not IPv6, and my internet just magically started working without an update or my intervention.  Af for Windows 7 backups, the built-in utility works just fine.  I've used to to restore my system after getting rid of Ubuntu, which I started using again a month ago.

### Post by Maverick_Meerkat on 2010-08-04
Greetings,

First off let me state: Ubuntu is a great OS! Any issues I may express are minor and superficial. All things considered the Ubuntu Team has done an outstanding job and Ubuntu has something truly special going for it. 'Nuff said!

Issue 1:
Changing or customizing the Plymouth splash screen, login screen, & shut down screen is a hassle. Even following procedural guides doesn't produce intended results. Modification of these things should be as simple as changing the wallpaper or choosing a theme.

Proposed Solution: 
Add Plymouth Options to Appearance Preferences GUI  

The same Appearances Preferences GUI that allows users to change the wallpaper and customize theme options in Ubuntu 10.04 should also include the ability to add, change, modify, and customize the Plymouth splash screen, login screen, & shut down screen. 

It really wouldn't be difficult to implement. Every other aspect of the environment is so easy to customize, it seems odd that Plymouth lacks the same support features. 

Because Plymouth is a recent addition, it is understandable that such considerations have not yet been made. Still, it is something I would like to see addressed. 

Issue 2:
It would be nice to have a built-in GUI that supports system restore. I realize there are third party apps that basically serve the same purpose and we can do it manually via terminal commands. Still, it just seems like a system restore wizard should be a standard package in any modern OS.

Proposed Solution: 
Either bundle a third party app as a standard package or ask the Development Team to create an Official Ubuntu Restore app. 

Like I said, minor and superficial.
;)

### Post by simosx on 2010-08-06
> **juliobahar said:**
> (1) Ubuntu not supporting THINKPAD ACPI until this date.

Laptops come and go with broken ACPI (also known as DSDT) information, which are important for Linux to enable power saving and detect your hardware.

Manufacturers tend to use old versions of these "DSDT" tools that produce bad ACPI information, hence the problems. Windows is full of undocumented workarounds that can bypass the errors. That's why we ask users to updated their BIOS, if an update is available.

For more, see [http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1036051](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1036051)

What you can do with this ThinkPad is use the tools at [http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1036051](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1036051) and recompile your DSDT tables. Check to see whether there are any errors in them on your ThinkPad.

### Post by jroa on 2010-08-06
Things I don't like about Ubuntu?  I can name a few.

1.  I can not easily uninstall programs.  For example, I loaded VirtualBox and everything was fine.  Some how it screwed up and I removed it and when I reinstalled, it was still screwed up.  After a few days of posting and searching, I found out that you can not just remove the program, you have to manually delete files that are attached to it.  I read conflicting directions on how to do this, so I finally just gave up because I was spending more time trying to figure out this little problem than I was spending on my studies.

2.  I have yet to get my wireless adapter to work.  I have two, and both are 802.11n, but neither works right with Ubuntu.  However, both work fine with Windows.  My NIC card works fine, but I have to carry my computer across my house to be able to connect to the Internet so that I can try to find a fix for the wireless adapters.  It would be nice if I could down load drivers in Windows and then save them in a file for Ubuntu, but apparently that is not the case.  It looks like I have to reverse engineer the drivers so that i can use them in Ubuntu.  That might be fun for most of us, but I work 50 or more hours per week and take 18 college credits per semester.  I don't have time to screw with the drivers so I just carry my computer back and forth so that I can use Linux when I have to.

3.  The live cd only works on 2 computers in my house.  I have two desktops and 1 laptop.  My kids have a desktop and 3 more laptops.  I can only get the live cd to work on two of them: one desktop and one laptop.  

4.  Maybe I am just a newbie, but I hate having to research commands for something that would be simple to do in Windows.  Most of the pages I find to fix a problem confilct with each other, and I don't understand why there is not just a point and click feature for most of this stuff like Windows has.  Most of you will probably say that I just need to read more about the OS.  But, I personally think that Windows and OSx are way more user friendly.  You don't have to type much code, if any, to get a Windows program to work or to get a driver to work.

5.  I may sound bitter, but when I started with Linux, I expected to have to learn new things.  I did not, however, expect to have to learn a bunch of codes just to run a program on the computer.  And, by the way, most of the codes that I have found that worked gave no explanation as to why the will work.  The just list a few lines of code that may or may not work in your application.

### Post by longtom on 2010-08-11
Ubuntu was my first Linux love and because of that it will always hold a special place in my Linux heart.

However, in the meantime and after being around the block I believe I found something better for me.  Running release without reinstalling every 6 months or having old and/or outdated  software, no networking problems, no video driver problems, videos and sound just work and the best out of the box hardware recognition I have encountered so far.

I am a simple user, not a developer or a fiddler.  I want my stuff to work so I am able to work with it.  Ubuntu, in the end, couldn't step up to that plate for me.

However - it's still my first love and every new distribution gets downloaded and driven in a virtual setup.

So what do I hate in Ubuntu?  Nothing.  It just doesn't work for me for every day use.  So I do not scream: "I go back to Windows and Apple ... be afraid!"  I just find myself something that works better for me.  Is it perfect?  No.  The perfect distro - doesn't exist.

So if you don't like this or that in Ubuntu - find something else.  Choice is at the very heart of Linux - exercise it.

### Post by tjktyler on 2010-08-21
> **mrbitterne said:**
> 
2)have never got the dvd player to work but does play some other media like music


You won't be able to play DVDs unless you have libdvdcss2 installed. It allows your media programs to decrypt encrypted DVDs (which is nearly all of them from major studios) so they can be played. Libdvdcss2, however, *technically* violates the DMCA in the US and possibly other laws in other countries, which is why Ubuntu doesn't distribute it. If you *need* to play DVDs, visit [http://medibuntu.org/]("http://medibuntu.org/") which hosts a repository and HOWTOs for installing the libdvdcss2 package (along with many others). Good luck, and I hope this helps!

---

### Post by quixote on 2010-08-21
> **mrbitterne said:**
> I have a few problems with it-poss you can direct me to another bit of the web site to sort this out
1)displays the correct date but not the time.i reset the time and then after i switch off the computer and restart a few days later it's the wrong time again
Setting time is critical to system functions, so only root (ie sudo) can do it.  If the system isn't asking you for a password to set the time, then you're only setting it for your current session.  When you click on the "Time Settings" button, it should ask for a password first.  If not, run it from a terminal.  The instructions are [here]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=2685546&postcount=3") but briefly it's:```
sudo date *nnddhhmmyyyy.ss*
```
    * nn: two digit month, 01 to 12
    * dd  two digit day, 01 to 31
    * hh  two digit hour, 00 to 23 
    * mm  two digit minute, 00 to 59
    * yyyy four-digit year
    * ss  two digit seconds. Notice the period ‘.’ before the ss.

Eg: May 19th, 2007, 6:00pm (and 0 seconds): date 051918002007.00

### Post by GuiGuy on 2010-08-24

Your missing the point; the learning curve on OSS is generally frustrating because of a lack of documentation. No search function. Sure there's a table of contents to drill through, but nothing to ease the frustration if one cannot find something of relevance. 

As with any software, it's easy to use when you know how. Quality documentation with good search facilities would help prospective get to that point instead of throwing their hands up in despair and going back to the devil they know.

### Post by murderslastcrow on 2010-08-24
GIMP may be missing CMYK support to the level present, as well as non-destructible layer effects. But layer grouping and inline text editing are here, now, and a single-window mode is just around the bend.

I don't see, other than what I mentioned (I am a graphics professional and use GIMP in my work, by the way), what makes GIMP suck compared to Photoshop? Perhaps it sucks at taking up the same amount of RAM, or looking inconsistent with the OS it runs on?

There are many tools available for GIMP that have more flexibility and more robust features than the Photoshop version currently does as well, such as the Scissors (magnetic lasso) tool.

So, while I respect your opinion, and I think it's good to be honest and just say something sucks when it sucks and could be better or more useful, you shouldn't discount the quality of a product just because it doesn't fit in perfectly with how you do things, or your UI expectations. We're not trying to be Windows here, although we strive to create the same functionality for users.

However, you can run Photoshop in Linux, and you can run Microsoft Office in Linux. So really, this argument is more against open source alternatives than Linux itself.

### Post by beew on 2010-08-28
It is not really a problem of Linux or Ubuntu, but software developers who make their products with only MS in mind. Since I don't use a lot of the multimedia stuffs(this is going to change when I upgrade my PC) my complaint is quite simple. How come there is no decent PDF reader in not just Ubuntu, but Linux in general?

What are the choices if you want to  view PDF with a native Linux app? Basically: Evince, Okular, Xpdf and they all suck. Evince is slow to load pages, uses a lot of resources and renders pictures horribly and it lacks basic functions. Okular has more functionality, but also slow in loading and doesn't render pictures well. Xpdf is fast but very basic and ugly. None of these have tab reading. 

There is also acroread, but I wouldn't use adobe even in windows. If you visit Linux forums, you can find many threads debating the relative merits of Evince and Acroread, so basically the choice is between a propietary bloatware and an open source crapware, that is not much of a choice. Whereas in Windows one can use either Foxit or Pdf-Xchange viewer,both free as beer, light weight and fast (foxit is very fast) and full of features. There are probably other options as well (Evince is available in Windows, though I have no idea why any windows user would want to use a piece of crap like Evince while there are so many much better alternatives for Adobe)

I am using as default Foxit's Pdf reader for Linux, it is fast and renders pages nicely (except for its own pdf manual which shows up as gibberish) but it is very functionless comparing to the windows version and hasn't been updated for a year so I am not sure if it is still under development. When I want to use advanced functions or tab reading I use Pdf-Xchange viewer in WINE. I try to avoid using WINE as much as possible because I think there should be native Linux solution for something as basic as reading PDFs!

Well too bad I am not a programmer, otherwise I would like to contribute to the open source projects. :)

---

### Post by Budoc on 2010-10-01
Ubuntu mostly fulfills my requirements for a useable, reliable operating system. However, 10.04 has been a poor release for me. Chiefly, the fact that I can't play music reliably off my external USB drives as they will [disconnect randomly]("https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/linux/+bug/349767"), the fact that I *still* cannot hibernate on my 4 year old laptop without getting a [graphics]("https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/xserver-xorg-video-intel/+bug/582057") [freeze]("https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/xorg-server/+bug/647521"), and the fact that there is frequently [visual corruption]("https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/gnome-panel/+bug/439448") of the panels making Ubuntu look like a far poorer operating system than it really is. The latter problem isn't big deal for me, as killall gnome-panel will resolve it, but it could easily put a new user off. I've been lead to understand that the last problem also affects Maverick. I'm principally looking to upgrade to 10.10 in the hope that the first two problems are fixed (especially the usb drive problem!).

Other than that, there are programs that I dislike such as Empathy and Gwibber, but providing that alternatives such as Pidgin are still in the repos, or still installable from elsewhere I can't describe my feelings as "hate".

---

### Post by dtfinch on 2010-10-01
Lately, since Lucid, things that call sync() sometimes hang for many seconds, minutes, or hours, even if it would only take a second or to flush all pending writes like it's supposed to.

[https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/linux/+bug/624877](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/linux/+bug/624877)

The calling process can't be killed either without rebooting or letting it finish naturally. So today before I could leave work I relearned how to background and disown a process from ssh+bash so that a simple "apt-get install" that should have taken 10 seconds can keep running over the weekend. Hopefully it's done by Monday.

The server was busy copying ~900gb of files from the server it's going to replace, but it was copying it to a raid on separate disks than the root filesytem, so there's absolutely no reason for dpkg to be sync()ing against it, but it does repeatedly, once for every single file it unpacks. I had also used "ionice -c3" to lower the copy to idle priority hoping to avoid this exact problem, but sync() cares not.

** insert rant about ext4 shifting the burden of protecting against data loss onto apps, in order to implement optimizations that are far outweighed by the performance loss that comes with all the naive app fixes that try to make it safe again **

---

### Post by aeronutt on 2010-10-01
> **quixote said:**
> Some of the braindead defaults in gnome:

F-spot: the world's most useless photo program.  Can't even save a file to a directory normally.  (Btw, Shotwell? Same thing.)  Gthumb is there, it does everything.  USE IT.  Why shove F-spot at us?

Pulseaudio: Maybe it's brilliant.  Fine.  But it messes up all sorts of common applications (hello? Skype?) which are badly behaved enough to insist on direct access to sound.  Sorry, pulseaudio, but some of us use Ubuntu because it "just works."  When stuff as basic as the microphone takes two days and a degree in computer engineering to get functional in a common program, you've broken something important. 

(Yeah, Pulseaudio is my current problem and I'm tearing my hair out.  That's why this whole rant is so intemperate.  I've been using Ubuntu since 2005 as my only OS, love it, and think the whole thing and the community are a truly amazing endeavor.  But right now I'm Really Frustrated.)

Make it easy to uninstall stuff like Pulseaudio.  Don't interdigitate it with the whole damn system.

Same goes for indicator applet.  I don't want the stupid messaging envelope taking up space in my panel.  I want easy access to volume control.  Those are two separate things!  Stop combining them!

The default buttons in Maverick.  Don't get me started.  Do not muck about with the user's desktop.  That belongs to the user, not the devs.  We all have our own work styles, and your style may not be mine.  **Make it easily configurable**.  Not as in open-a-terminal-and-use-gconf-editor configurable.  As in right-click configurable.  As in give people a choice during upgrade/install.

The totally braindead way media are referred to in the file manager and on the desktop.  By *size*?  Hello?  How many 8GB removable media do you think are connected to my computer right now?  The only way to tell them apart is to open each damn one and see what's in there.  D.U.M.B.  Also, no clear reference to the mount path which you're going to need for lots of things.  

And then, where they're mounted, in /media, they're referred to NOT by size.  There it's by UUID, which I'm sure is an even better way for normal humans to recognize partitions!  Honestly.  

Just a suggestion: how bloody hard would it be to let the user rename the drive with an alias, and then in the title bar or address bar actually provide the device and path.  Like so: /dev/sdb*2* mounted on /media/*my-alias*

...

Hrmph.  Well, I guess that's all for now... I'll go get back in my basket...

Ditto on everything you said about f-spot, users desktop, and mounted media.
