### Post by FiveSidedPoly on 2010-10-04
[COLOR=black][FONT=Verdana]I don't really hate anything in Ubuntu, it has made me use more and more Linux on a regular basis.  But I have some disappointments and they seem to be growing with each release.  This has led me to do a lot of highly tweaking my Ubuntu installs or just plain distro hopping as of late.  All these disappointments have also scared off or stopped me from getting other people to leave Windows or MAC.[/FONT][/COLOR]
[COLOR=black][FONT=Verdana] [/FONT][/COLOR]
[COLOR=black][FONT=Verdana]All of my disappointments seem to be based around the theory I have that the developers or the major people calling the shots with the direction of Ubuntu don't seem to be listening really to the majority of the user base.  Or listen to only what they want to hear.  That or their bug/trouble ticket system doesn't work properly and or they just don't really care.[/FONT][/COLOR]
[COLOR=black][FONT=Verdana] [/FONT][/COLOR]
[COLOR=black][FONT=Verdana]Examples:[/FONT][/COLOR]
[COLOR=black][FONT=Verdana] [/FONT][/COLOR]
[COLOR=black][FONT=Verdana]- moving the window buttons to the left, not too big a deal because you can move them, but seriously how many "humans" got asked about this before it needed done so they could incorporate some new feature for the right side of the window border?  Will the feature work if I decided to move the buttons back to the right after the feature is incorporated?  Will I have to uninstall ubuntu-core and linux-core if I decided I don't want that feature.  I know I'm being sarcastic, sorry.[/FONT][/COLOR]
[COLOR=black][FONT=Verdana] [/FONT][/COLOR]
[COLOR=black][FONT=Verdana]- 99% of the new features everyone on the forums is asking/dreaming for do NOT get incorporated into a new release.  These are the features that need added, most of them are standard in the big commercial OSes, and they are usually the ones that dozens of threads get opened for help because someone tries to get a needed program or feature working but can't.  I mean, there shouldn't be a way to buy music through Rythmbox, before a solid backup system is in place.[/FONT][/COLOR]
[COLOR=black][FONT=Verdana] [/FONT][/COLOR]
[COLOR=black][FONT=Verdana]- constant new features that only 1% of current or future users will actually use get incorporated hastily without working out the bugs from the last new features that were added.  And pounding out tons of updates that may or may not work doesn't help either, especially for an LTS.[/FONT][/COLOR]
[COLOR=black][FONT=Verdana] [/FONT][/COLOR]
[COLOR=black][FONT=Verdana]- these features that make Ubuntu easier or friendly to newcomers are so tightly knit into other features or the OS itself that you can't even move them from one side of the Gnome panel to the other.  Prime example- the Me Menu, great for social networking, but do you really need it attached to Indicator Applet Session.  Or the fact that you can't remove mail icon or any of the other forced upon icons from the Indicator Applet without getting rid of the sound preferences icon too, especially when there isn't a Applet to replace it with right out of the box.  Seriously, this doesn't seem a problem with Windows taskbar.[/FONT][/COLOR]
[COLOR=black][FONT=Verdana] [/FONT][/COLOR]
[COLOR=black][FONT=Verdana]- Ubuntu specific software/package dependency has gotten so overboard that either new users break their system trying to get something simple done or additional "optional" software is left to create bloat or steals resources to run in the background.  This is a Linux OS problem in general, but it’s worse on Ubuntu than say Debian, which Ubuntu is based off of so what gives?   I shouldn't keep anything from Evolution, etc, if I don't want to use them.  If MS started forcing Outlook or anything else on anyone and you couldn't remove them without breaking your system, everyone would be in an uproar, everyone here would have one more thing bad to say about MS.[/FONT][/COLOR]
[COLOR=black][FONT=Verdana] [/FONT][/COLOR]
[COLOR=black][FONT=Verdana]- Services should not be started on bootup, especially Ubuntu specific services, unless they are actually essential.  Example:  Bluetooth, everyone complains about this, and note I didn't say "isn't installed"... just not started on bootup especially if it’s on a desktop computer.  Ubuntu Bluetooth = Bluetooth for Humans.[/FONT][/COLOR]
[COLOR=black][FONT=Verdana] [/FONT][/COLOR]
[COLOR=black][FONT=Verdana]- Network file sharing or connectivity should be transparent and painless to the user, more so with Linux to Linux boxes, let alone Windows boxes.  This is long overdue, but I guess cloud computing and Ubuntu One needed the priority instead.  I think the forum may explode if another thread pops up about Network Manager, CIFS, NFS, or Samba.[/FONT][/COLOR]
[COLOR=black][FONT=Verdana][/FONT][/COLOR] 
[COLOR=black][FONT=Verdana]-  Remotes should work right out of the box, I can install XBMC and use my remote right away, but if I load Lirc it takes tweaking to get it the play button working with Totem.  Which isn't much of a surprise because XBMC also does video and music playback better too.[/FONT][/COLOR]
[COLOR=black][FONT=Verdana][/FONT][/COLOR] 
[COLOR=black][FONT=Verdana]-  The only language support that should be installed ever with any program are the ones I choose, not really a Ubuntu specific issue, but none the less.  Installing Localepurge should be a thing of the past.[/FONT][/COLOR]
[COLOR=black][FONT=Verdana][/FONT][/COLOR] 
[COLOR=black][FONT=Verdana]-  All the additional software that your everyday user doesn't use, shouldn't be included, especially if it's dependency heavy.  Even worse when its only a few clicks away in the Software Center, which is why it was created, so that everyday users could easily find and understand what they are looking for.  Even if it doesn't show me how much hard drive space I'll lose if I install something from there, like Synaptic does, which brings up another thing to add to Software Center.  Since most people still care about hard drive space and resource usage.  [/FONT][/COLOR]
[COLOR=black][FONT=Verdana] [/FONT][/COLOR]
[COLOR=black][FONT=Verdana] [/FONT][/COLOR]
[COLOR=black][FONT=Verdana]The more and more I use Ubuntu, the more I realize that it is just redone version of Debian testing, that has a lot of very good things incorporated into it, but also a whole bunch of "fluff" that rarely works for most people or your forced to keep it because it’s part of Ubuntu's big plan.  Which most of stuff people actually use just comes straight from Debian testing to begin with, but Ubuntu gets the credit for.[/FONT][/COLOR]
[COLOR=black][FONT=Verdana] [/FONT][/COLOR]
[COLOR=black][FONT=Verdana]I can honestly say that I never had a real issue loading or using it right away on any of the computers I have, either the ones I bought, or any of the many I have built.  Well except to small things, but I mentioned them above.[/FONT][/COLOR]
[COLOR=black][FONT=Verdana] [/FONT][/COLOR]
[COLOR=black][FONT=Verdana]But when I can tweak the heck out of a base Ubuntu install (with a whole lot of time on my hands) and it takes up 1.4GBs of hard drive space and uses 89MBs of ram on boot up; while still being able to load it on just about any computer and it works out of the gate, provides mostly everything anyone would ever need from a OS.  And it’s still using Gnome, Metacity compositing, and a bunch of things most people call "fluff".  I got this idea from comparing what Ubuntu had on a fresh install compared to Debian.  This just shows there is something wrong![/FONT][/COLOR]
[COLOR=black][FONT=Verdana] [/FONT][/COLOR]
[COLOR=black][FONT=Verdana]I have put this custom Ubuntu install on tons of PCs.  Best example was a crappy old Dell Inspiron using 1.6GHz CPU with 512MBs of ram for someone, showed them how it ran, what it had, then showed the user the Software Center, explained how to use it.  And you know what?  They loved it, even after I showed them a fresh install, and after they told me to put Windows XP back on it.[/FONT][/COLOR]
[COLOR=black][FONT=Verdana] [/FONT][/COLOR]
[COLOR=black][FONT=Verdana] [/FONT][/COLOR]
[COLOR=black][FONT=Verdana]Now, I know that everyone just complaining about it all doesn't really solve the problem, in fact it will get worse before it gets better, if you're not part of the solution, then you're part of the problem.[/FONT][/COLOR]
[COLOR=black][FONT=Verdana] [/FONT][/COLOR]
[COLOR=black][FONT=Verdana]But I think the real solution is to just take the whole thing back a notch, fix the big problems, and add the "essential" software needed that 99% of the forum asks for.  Get the basics right and stop using the "shotgun good idea fairy approach"  Then make all this extra stuff "optional" no strings attached (dependencies included) through the Software Center as "Featured Items" since that was already started.  And then you can get these companies to start making the paid for software for Ubuntu.[/FONT][/COLOR]
 
 
You can flame all you want.  But I am just saying what most people on the forums are saying on a regular basis.  Which is why the post was so long.

---

### Post by BrokenKingpin on 2010-10-04
I like Ubuntu a lot, but there are a few things that I personally do not like. Most of this stuff I really do not care about as I can easily change it, but I still wish they would change it by default.

- The theme/wallpaper. Much better than the brown, but now it has too much purple. And the default wallpaper of 10.04 was just hideous.

- Some of the default apps... like shotwell replacing fspot. Why would they do this? Yeah maybe down the road shotwell will be better, but it currently has less features, and is far uglier than fspot, so why change it.

- The 6 month release cycle is too short, resulting is a few overly buggy release. Go to an 8 month or annual release, but maybe just have a few repo updates here and there.

- Too much focus on things like social media, instead of things like configuration tools that make setting up the OS easier (samba config, etc.)

- The name "Ubuntu"... I just hate the sound of it lol

I hate having to login. I hate having to type in passwords to get to everything. The one thing I like about windows is I can choose not to have to use a password while booting up.
 
I'm a noob at Ubuntu so there may be a way to bypass having to log-on booting up. I just haven't found out how yet.
 
I'm starting to get the hang of using the terminal screen to get things running but it would be a lot easier to get more people to use Ubuntu/Linux the more point and click stuff you have installing programs. I hate Windows and Mac but you can't argue about their ease of use.

---

### Post by moorebounce on 2010-10-09
I think a lot of people would be lost w/o Youtube. I now have two less things a hate about Ubuntu.

---

### Post by 0N3 on 2010-10-09
No Veetle support for 64bit Ubuntu

---

### Post by MisterGaribaldi on 2010-10-09
I actually like it very much.

Only things I would change aren't really Ubuntu-centric. I just think more attention and focus needs to be given to certain kinds of software used by pros and in the commercial sector (accounting software, web and graphic design software, office productivity, rendering software, video editors, etc.) to make it more competitive with commercial titles. Also, I think there probably are a few things about each DE which could use more love.

Again, none of this really is Ubuntu's issue. If anything, Ubuntu is as polished as it is thanks to Mr. Shuttleworth's efforts and money. I think we need more people out there like him who either have the money or can get the money focused on lots more "pet issues".

---

### Post by 0N3 on 2010-10-09
@ moorebounce go to user Users and groups and change it there

System > Admin > Users and Groups

---

### Post by MisterGaribaldi on 2010-10-09
There's admittedly not very much you *need* to do in a terminal window these days.

In fact, the only thing I do for installation purposes at a terminal prompt is to [finish getting Ubuntu set up to play DVDs]("http://lifehacker.com/350015/enable-dvd-playback-in-ubuntu-in-two-commands"). Everything else I do via System > Administration > Synaptic Package Manager.

However, one thing that is COMPLETELY non-obvious without doing a search for it on Google is knowing to install the Restricted Extras packages so you get all the codecs and standard Windows fonts and all that jazz. I understand Ubuntu doesn't ship that stuff by default for licensing and other reasons. However, to make it available at all isn't much different than having it come up on first log-in to one's account (after install) with a dialog saying something like "click here to install non-free codecs, etc., pending the other updates to your present install".

But then again, what would *I* know? :)

---

### Post by yanom on 2010-10-09
> **pbpersson said:**
> The reboots and lockups.  I am using Kubuntu Gutsy Gibbon and they are few and far between but they are still annoying.

On the lockups when the mouse is still working I guess I could restart XServer but I can never remember the key combination.

However, the last time this happened I was in Firefox typing a post in this forum, I clicked on "preview post" and the machine locked up so hard even the mouse was dead.

On the plus side, when Kubuntu restarted all my data was saved, including the post I was working on - that was very impressive.  :)

That's cause you're on KDE :) (I think)

Try IceWM or LXDE they rock!!

### Post by LifeTheHound on 2010-10-10
> That's cause you're on KDE
KDE SC 4.5 series is pretty slick. It has more visual appeal than Windows 7, a lot more compositing candy, and actually has a huge number of neat features like tabbed multi-app windows, multiple distinct desktops (different wallpaper and useful sets widgets for each), etc.

### Post by windfix on 2010-10-13
First, I love Ubuntu and have been promoting it in academia for three years.  However, on the topic, two "papercut" issues that will not leave me alone:

1. The clock in my panel frequently sticks on the time when I boot the computer.  With each Ubuntu release, I hope for fix and it never happens.  Yes, I know I can do "killall gnome-panel" to reset it, and the clock starts working; but by the time I realize this I am already PO'ed

2. Photos Screensaver. Why must the only option be your entire /Pictures folder?  It'd be nice to have some control there.

---

### Post by MrNatewood on 2010-10-14
Default application selection is poor.

I spend the same amount of time it takes me to install ubuntu as to change all the applications afterwards.

Thunderbird instead of Evolution, Pidgin for Empathy, leafpad for gedit, banshee for rythmbox, vlc for totem, Qalculate instead of gnome calculator, qBittorrent for transmission.

Moreover, most of these apps are neatly integrated to the Me Menu etc. and for it to be compatible with the replacements some tweaking needs to be made.

This seems to be getting worse from release to release.

Heard Mint has a better software selection, will give it a shot once the next version comes out(as I need the new maverick kernel to handle suspend on my laptop)

---

### Post by Scunnered on 2010-10-14
Their ability to mess up that which worked perfectly in for example 10.04 and wont work in 10.10. Such a simple thing as screen resolution should not be corrupted between a 6 month update.

Is it any wonder that folk get totally peed off with Linux when what worked perfectly yesterday will not work today. KISS must be the first rule to ensure that updates just work

---

### Post by waspinator on 2010-10-14
- inconsistant 'places' menu
	- network drives do not show up in many places
		- cannot search network folders
		- cannot use network iso for virtualbox

This is fixed by mounting network drives manually instead of going through Places->Network. This should be the default behavior when using Places->Network

### Post by DouglasAdams on 2010-10-16
> **Machiavelli said:**
> I'v never been able to burn a CD in Ubuntu.
here's one i think i can help ... yippee!!!

launch your cd burning software, probably:
applications > sound & vision > brasero
use the "burn image" option
and direct it to the file you downloaded.
tell it where your blank cd is.
make a cup of your favourite beverage ... job done.

### Post by wheels666 on 2010-10-22
*OK - i felt compelled to blog on this whole thing and copy part of it here...*

Sometimes Linux looks to me like the way forward, the holy grail, a cool new computing system that could free me from the horrible clutches of Microsoft.

But before going any further, I want to make it clear that this is not because I don't like paying for Microsoft. In fact I can say happily that I've never paid a cent for any Microsoft OS or Windows programme. Unlike corporations, government departments, and some righteous Americans, I couldn't give a toss about copyright laws and wouldn't pay for software if hell froze over. 

It's also not because I'm looking for "security" or have been losing sleep over viruses - despite going to nearly every dodgy site on the internet and downloading some of almost everything that can be downloaded, by taking only a few simple precautions, security has never been an issue on my XP computers.

And it's not because I need to keep up with the latest gizmos - I use Pentium 4 computers circa 2003 because they are cheap, reliable, easy to fix, and I have a mountain of spares for them. And I use XP because it's my favourite OS, not because I wouldn't know how to go onto warez-bb and find a free copy of windows 7 if I had such a desire.

Finally and most emphatically, it's not because I want to "write my own command line in the terminal" - to tell the truth I'd rather never see a computers horrible back end, and command line geeks totally freak me out.

It's because Bill Gates is a *eugenisist* with ties to Monsanto and the Bilderburg group, and that freaks me out more than command line geeks!

### Post by murderslastcrow on 2010-10-22
It sounds like most of the problems mentioned here have nothing to do with Linux itself and Linux developers, but people wanting Linux to be more like you-know-what.

Since we have so few complaints outside of Windows compatibility, I think the developers in our communities deserve a round of applause. While Linux can't satisfy everyone from the Windows world, anyone who isn't invested in an OS beforehand finds Linux refreshing, fun, cool, and very easy to work with.

Now we just need to fix the sound issues, get some open drivers finished for NVIDIA, and polish up our DEs and programs. I think if we work on making our killer applications more murderous, and we get some more force behind Wine development, Linux could be ready to debut on a larger scale than anyone anticipates. Of course, it's already making quite the impact even with these "problems."

The thing I hate most about Ubuntu is how it keeps getting better. XD My mind has no time to catch up with the updates.

### Post by dtfinch on 2010-10-25
That mdadm bug (or maybe a feature since it's over a year old) where on random boots, it'll steal away one of your raid disks to create a new but inactive "md_d0". Because of that, the real raid fails to auto-assemble. And if you do "mdadm --assemble --scan" without first "decactivating" md_d0 (though it's already labeled as "inactive"), it'll fail to use the disk that was stolen, causing your real raid to become active in a non-redundant state and forcing a full resync when you finally get it worked out.

I know adding your array to mdadm.conf prevents that bug from happening, but it's still pretty gut-wrenching to encounter this on an important system.

---

### Post by Shanazon on 2010-10-25
Honestly, I can't find anything that I just downright don't like.  For the past 2 weeks I have been test-driving many different Linux distributions, and even a few flavors of BSD.  I have to say that having reviewed the rest of them just makes me like Ubuntu that much more.  It's perfectly suited to either command-lining it all the way, or getting things done with the gui if you're doing something general or media-oriented.

Many of the other distros that I tried didn't have the driver/codec support I was looking for.  Ubuntu really has it all.  No more jumping from dist to dist for me!

My one peeve (and this is a Linux general thing) is that the Xorg ATI drivers are...well...kinda crappy.  AMD hasn't been as forthcoming with code as the driver writers would like, I'm sure.  Hopefully this will improve in time.  

10.04 ;-)

### Post by gregzeng on 2010-10-26
> **dalvan said:**
> I hate not being able to install any other programs;  getting error messages when I try to open synaptic.  I get this message:

E: dpkg was interrupted, you must manually run 'dpkg --configure -a' to correct the problem. 
E: _cache->open() failed, please report.

Then I try to run dpkg - configure - a and nothing happens;  I try it with sudo in front of it, get a screen that asks for my password,  then the problem is still there.  Can't install anything and can't run synaptic.  So this OS sucks for now.  Dal in Medford MA USA
Sorry about the repeat of your problem as your answer to the problem.

Ubuntu is supposted to hide us from the CLI - command line interpreter.  Of course, being 99% unsuccessful (M$ Windows is 99% successful), UBUNTU & its derivatives (SUPER-OS, PINGUY, MINT, ... ) DONT HIDE the CLI from use.

You need to drop what Microsoft calls the "DOS BOX".  That's the same as Ubuntu.

It is very HIDDEN in UBUNTU.  Go to the Windows equivalent of the START button.  Click-and-hold-down on that, and move the cursor to "Accessories".  Still Click-and-hold-down on that, and find "TERMINAL" near the bottom of a long list.

If like me, you can't get "TERMINAL" to start with the mouse, then you must use the "UP", "DOWN", "ENTER" keys on your keyboard to try to start it.

When it starts, THEN you finally enter the commands that Ubuntu tries to tell you to enter.  Ubuntu does not COPY-PASTE from the silly Ubuntu message directly into the Terminal.  So you must write the silly codes EXACTLY (all the exact spaces, exact "--") that it tells you.  If you don't, Ubuntu is guaranteed to displease 99% of the generasl public, as it does now.

Unlike M$ Windows, Ubuntu is very stupid.  There is no quicker way to get to their "DOS BOX" - no "SUPER-KEY R" nor "Windows-key R".

One day Ubuntu will discover why they are so hostile to the general public.  At the moment, they don't care about being popular - but trying to stay gracefully hated  (the subject of this Forum title).

Retired (medical) IT Consultant
Australian Capital Territory.

PLEASE STAY ON TOPIC.  UBUNTU IS HATEFUL.  But it uses less disk space, boots quicker, crashes less ... than Windows.

### Post by wheels666 on 2010-10-26
Slow performance

After trying out Puppy Linux, I expected Ubuntu to be faster than Windows XP. It certainly boots up faster than XP. Surprisingly, I found Ubuntu 10.04 to be massively inefficient, hogging down system recourses and slowing my computers down horribly. Yes I'm using old (2003) P4 computers, but they are reasonably high spec ones with 2.8 processors and 3 gigs of RAM. Running in XP they work fine, even though I use them for image editing, web design, and managing over 200,000 files on over 20 hard drives 

Some programmes just run like pigs in Ubuntu. For me the worst one was my favorite programme in XP - Firefox. Surprisingly, Firefox was absolutely appalling in Ubuntu (and it works fine on the same computer running XP), to the point of being unusable for some uses, such as google image searches. I ended up using Chromium quite a bit because it works so much faster than Firefox on Ubuntu. (And I even spent a lot of time tweaking Firefox following some intense geek instructions, and improved it a bit, but never really got it working properly) 

Many other programmes were slower than their Windows equivalents - eg. Evolution vs.Outlook 2003, (Evolution seemed to run slow motion), Gimp 2.6 vs Photoshop 7, Nautilus vs Windows Explorer Classic, Writer vs Word - in each case I found the Windows programme faster, as well as easier to use, having better features, and more intuitive tools. In fact I never got through a day without Evolution having at least one lock up, and hot swapping a bunch of external drives would lock up Nautilus every time 

Part of the slow performance is comparative, and it's because having used Windows for more than 10 years, I'm reasonably fast on it - if i spent 10 years using an unchanging version of Ubuntu I'd get a lot faster on that too. But for all it's good points, even mainstream Ubuntu never gave me the impression of being designed for speedy, easy use by people who are not primarily computer enthusiasts, and all that time I spent looking for answers on the forums, is time I could have spent actually doing things in XP... 

Although it's harsh to call a Linux OS bloatware, this is a critisism that's constantly leveled at Microsoft. Ubuntu ran OK on my 2005 dual core computer, but it knocked the stuffing out of my 2003 P4's. Without offering any additional features, it was a major step back from the performance I'm getting from 2003 Microsoft XP SP2. While Puppy Linux transformed a 1998 P2 with 64 megs of RAM into a warpseed rocketship, Ubuntu turned my favorite tricked out P4 into a flogged out donkey...

---

### Post by wheels666 on 2010-10-26

After eight years of XP I was suffering from "grass is greener on the other side of the fence" syndrome. But after two months of Ubuntu, I have come to really appreciate XP. For all it's faults, I now see that some of it's decade old features are way ahead of Linux. Certainly, for me, no Linux file browser I've tested comes close to the old Windows 2000 style classic view file browser that I use on XP.

It takes less than 45 minutes for me to install Ubuntu, install its updates, then install my needed programs and drivers, along with tweaking the settings to make ubuntu look how I want it to look.

(There is one old Dell that I am trouble shooting right now, it had XP on it, I removed it and installed Ubuntu 10.10 and it sucks,--not enough ram and very hungry graphic card which is probably malfunctioning,-- but even then it sucks less than when XP was installed. :)) I can probably just install Lubuntu or Xubuntu to solve the problem, which is one download of an iso image away. ### Post by masgeeks on 2010-11-02
I was a Linux hobbyist for YEARS before finally going entirely Linux this past year.  The ERP software I sell and support only runs under Windoze (used to be a Unix version but the dumb asses DISCONTINUED IT back in 2005), so I always kept up with M$ crap just for the sake of my livelihood.

Finally said SCREW IT, and for myself and my business went wholly Linux this year, primarily Ubuntu.  And you know what?  MY BLOOD PRESSURE WENT DOWN, DRAMATICALLY.

Ahhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhh...

There is nothing I do not like about Ubuntu, and any sore points I may find are NOT the OS's fault, but rather other entities whose products they don't want playing in the same sandbox with Linux, like Netflix. 

Sure, I have some Windoze VM's I use when I need to do work for clients, and I have one evil M$ server here for my own ERP system, but that's it.  They are just little adjuncts of my world now, and are no longer MY WORLD.

Ubuntu is like a natural happy-pill...!!!  :)

People noticed the change - say they haven't seen me smile this much in AGES.

Of course, friends and family are the ones now frowning - when they try to suck me dry for tech support, I merrily tell them I no longer use Windoze and so don't have a clue how to fix whatever is their issue... lol...

Ubuntu = Peace
OpenSource = Peace

---

However both Maverick and Lucid take a long time to boot from an external hard disk.It may get better if I actually install Ubuntu (it seems that a reason for slow boot is that the bios is looking for some "Dell signature" but can't find it, must be because I made the install from a Dell machine and somehow it got written into grub or something) 

I bought this machine before I started playing with Ubuntu. Why doesn't ubuntu realize that one of its components isn't working, and file the bug report automatically?

### Post by dtfinch on 2010-11-09
There's a fsck for fat32.
[http://manpages.ubuntu.com/manpages/lucid/man8/dosfsck.8.html](http://manpages.ubuntu.com/manpages/lucid/man8/dosfsck.8.html)

If they're ntfs formatted, there's also ntfsfix for some issues.
[http://manpages.ubuntu.com/manpages/lucid/man8/ntfsfix.8.html](http://manpages.ubuntu.com/manpages/lucid/man8/ntfsfix.8.html)

I haven't had corruption problems with usb in years, and it's usually been my own fault when I did, with the exception of it choking on an international filename and automatically remounting read only in the middle of a long write once. Though I do manually "sync" after copying files to it out of paranoia, and then unmount the usb before removing it. Copying appears to finish long before it's actually been written, and removing the usb early can result in empty files and other corruption.

---

### Post by Foxuvius on 2010-11-09
I don't really h8 anthing in Ubuntu 10.10.  But since you asked.
[LIST]
[*]When you launch a program, nothing happens.  Then, after a few seconds, the program launches.  I try to be Zen about it.  I tell myself that the only difference between Win and Linux is that the program developers didn't write a include in the script that shows me the load status.  That it's a developer thing.  (If I know I clicked the icon properly, I should know a program will show up eventually.) But then nothing still happens.  So I wait, and double click again.  Then maybe it'll work.  Or not.
[*]I do not like the text editor load time.  How can it take SO LONG for a freakin' text editor to load?
[*]That the numeric keypad doesn't work (on my config, anyway)
[*]GIMP
[*]That it doesn't see my Hauppage video card (the only Win App I still run in Win)
[*]That the line in audio in 10.10 is attenuated about 30 decibels.
[*]That my Home Folder icon never stays on the desktop.
[*]That you will spend the rest of your life typing SUDO.  REALLY? "S" was unavailable??
[/LIST]

I consider these all nuisance.  U is awesome.  Screw Microsoft.  

Other than that, I heart Ubuntu.   It makes me free.

### Post by asuastrophysics on 2010-11-10
Ubuntu simply does not talk to the hardware. Period. In windows, if I have a piece of hardware that's about to go out, I'll get a message saying "so and so driver in device manager has stopped working". Linux simply ignores all warning signs of imminent hardware failure, and continues to run your computer into the ground until the hardware abruptly fails. 
(Ok fine, ubuntu monitors SMART and you can scan your RAM. That's it)

How is that considered a **stable** solution? If I own a server farm, I want to know when my hardware is going to fail. I don't want to be caught off guard during high traffic with a broken server. With linux, that's exactly what you get. Then you have to spend hours figuring out whether your problem is hardware or software related. 

Completely retarded. And this will never change, because linux buries hardware drivers into the kernel, because "it's better that way". Sure it is.

---

### Post by asuastrophysics on 2010-11-10
> **Damiox said:**
> example-
 musicians = USE JACK AND ARDOUR !!!  TADA !!


I'm a musician, and I run UbuntuStudio. I completely gave up on Jack. It's unstable. If you have ANY program currently open that is using pulseaudio, jack won't start. Even if you closed all the programs using audio, jack still won't start. Then you have to restart GDM, log in, launch jack, and pray it doesn't completely lock up the computer, which it does sometimes with no explanation.
Sometimes it will conveniently lock up while you're recording your tracks. Then you loose all the audio you were recording, as you restart the computer. 

The only solution:
If you want to use jack, drop everything else you're doing and restart the computer. When you log in, the **first** thing you do is launch jack. This is the only guaranteed way that it works. And it's time consuming and inconvenient. And it will still randomly lock up as you're recording tracks. 
(To recreate this, launch audacity, record a track, loop the track over and over, and record another track on top of it. Garaunteed way to crash everything)

So no. If you are a musician, ubuntu studio is definitely not the way to go. Save up some money, and buy an $800 multitrack recorder. Worth every penny.

### Post by vanquishedangel on 2010-11-12
Although I have used Ubuntu for awhile now, one of the things i hate is how easy it is to "break" the system, A bad video driver update, or wine issue even just as examples. Also after awhile when stressed the gnome icons seem to disappear and I get a warning asking me to delete them, I accidentally click yes sometimes and that is very annoying.

Over all it is by far better than windows, windows keeps you stupid about your computer, that way when something is wrong you need to get a tech for it, Ubuntu helps you to learn to do it yourself and learn about it.

If I may say to the users of this forum Ubuntu is not Linux, Linux is a kernel only, Ubuntu is an operating system made from collected programs assembled together one of them happens to be that kernel. Most of which are gnu programs and the gnu project was commissioned in 1984, 7 years before Linus wrote the Linux kernel.

In short Ubuntu is a distribution of GNU/Linux, and a good one to. Great job to the developers, you have done a wonderful job as even though I have a copy of windows xp, I refuse to use it anymore.

### Post by markfaine on 2010-11-18
Sound - plain and simple.

I admit this may not be specific to Ubuntu but I do know that with Ubuntu sound is always an issue. Linux has several different sound systems and none of them are a drop in replacement for the others with full functionality.  I think I just consider sound so essential that I don't have a problem with a single sound solution being mandated or at least a standard - some things are just too critical to over-complicate.   

It should just work, every time, without any interaction on my part beyond that which is expected of any computer user (mixer, volume, etc).  I hate to say it but even with driver issues with strange sound cards I never had as much sound problems in Windows as I do in Ubuntu.  The thing I hate more than how it inconveniences me is that I have to admit that.

---

I think I just consider sound so essential that I don't have a problem with a single sound solution being mandated or at least a standard - some things are just too critical to over-complicate.

### Post by markfaine on 2010-11-18
Here is another one.  I have been using and working professionally with Linux/Unix for 15 years so I've done quite a bit of work with Linux and while a lot of the distributions are different in some ways none are quite so different as Ubuntu.  Don't get me wrong, in some ways it is great but when you need to do something you've done a half-dozen times already you will probably discover that "in Ubuntu we do it in a totally different way".  

Take my sound issue for example, it should be a simple matter for me to rebuild the kernel and put OSS support right back in - that is one of the great things about Linux - no one can force you to do it their way - except they can - I have no idea how to compile a kernel the "Ubuntu way" and I bet even if I figure it out there will be several other hoops (blacklisted modules, kernel upgrades,etc) I'll have to jump through just to get it to work. In the end it usually isn't worth it and you just go along with what you are given - reminds me of another operating system.

So to sum it up, I hate that what makes Ubuntu great: widespread support, general ease of use, and a large user community, also makes is not so great.

### Post by Mike_tn on 2010-11-23
> **glossywhite said:**
> This graphics card is an NVIDIA which is over eight years old... poor show, Ubuntu - there really IS no excuse for this not being a point-and-click resolution to my problem. Epic fail x10.

I think it's smart to obtain hardware for the OS
My Dell laptop has an Intel chipset which loves Linux any Linux...it gets Ubuntu
My 4 year old hp Desktop with those same nVIDIAs as yours does not like much Linux at all.  However, Mandriva Linux works beautifully out of the box. And it's a huge long standing distribution like Ubuntu with lots of packages in their repos.

!!Edit: I have to recant my complaint. I discovered that the nVIDIA (nvidia-current) drivers in the repository do indeed work very well on my offline hp desktop. I got them running tonight. Previous problem for me was only trying to install them on a LiveUSBwithPersistence system. Discovered if you install all the way to the HDD then it works well, at least for that machine and Maverick 64bit.  Pretty cool too because that machine never had a 64bit OS install before. It was quite easy to install too. Even offline.  Just got the driver, looked online at the depends list. Found I needed two or three more by comparing it to the list made from running dpkg --get-selections > ~/my-packages.   So all is well. Got Ubuntu Mav on both my machines now. Quite happy, no more hate.

### Post by Stigmata13 on 2010-12-01
My biggest complaints aren't exactly Ubuntu related, more GNU/Linux and hardware related mostly.
One would be the lack of a proprietary driver. Sure, the Open Source one is nice, but it's not nearly on par with the windows proprietary driver, or the legacy Linux one (which is unavailable since the 8.10 release).
Second would have to be bad implementation of Non-Open Source plugins, such as adobe's flash plugin and Moonlight. (Moonlight lacks DRM support and Silverlight isn't available for Linux... no Netflix for me :( )
Aside from those and a few other things, which I can't name off the top of my head right now, Linux has been great for me. I'm too far in... I don't think I could ever manage to switch back to Windows.

### Post by Niva on 2010-12-02
> **andrew732 said:**
> Started using Linux again after about 10 years.  Open source will always lag behind MS and Apple in the user-friendliness department, but Ubuntu 10 is very impressive; light years from Red Hat 7. Anyhoo, I just have a small gripe about the top panel: why is the volume button A) called the "Indicator applet" with no indication as to its function and B) why is it permanently linked to the button for checking email?  :confused:  It didn't occur to anyone to just have a "volume button" that you can add to the panel?

Agreed, the indicator applet is a horrid thing I must get rid of immediately since they implemented it.  I guess it works if you use evolution and the default messaging program (that requires a separate account to be created) but I don't like either of those.  Delete indicator applet, install the gnome-volume control, install thunderbird and pidgin and I'm good to go.

As far as the topic goes I very much don't like the default theme in Ubuntu, never have.  This is a matter of personal taste but the colors don't even appeal to me.  So I must immediately customize.  

The buttons on the left side by default?  Really?  Frankly I despise the OSX interface and why they decided to go that route bothers me a bit.

Unity?  I'll be awaiting the first release before I make a judgment on it, but as it is right now it sucks.  Why not just wait for Gnome shell and use it?  We'll see how that pans out.

My microphones will not work under linux on my laptop.  The system sees them, but they don't pick up any sound at all.  Haven't really bothered trying to fix it and researching yet because the rest of the system works great, but the wife has to log into windows every time she wants to use skype (which happens almost every day.)

ATI graphics drivers?  Yup, not Ubuntu's fault but it's so bad it makes me hate Ubuntu.

For all my gripes I still stay with Ubuntu.  If for no other reason but these forums are the best out there and I've always been able to get quick help resolving my issues when I looked around.

### Post by Hur Dur on 2010-12-23
I only have two complaints about Ubuntu:

1.) This may sound a little weird, but how user-friendly it is. I personally started with Linux to learn more about computers. And, as a beginner in Linux, I used Ubuntu. I then switched to Tiny Core, and learned more about Linux in a day on it than I did in a month on Ubuntu.

2.) How heavy it is. I understand that nowadays we have better hardware, but I think that Ubuntu just goes overboard with how many processes it has, and how many packages it comes with default. A default Ubuntu install uses about 200mb memory. That can be trimmed down a bit by using IceWM and no desktop environment, but it's still one of the heaviest distros out there.

### Post by asuastrophysics on 2011-01-02
(Partially in response to uRock's post, and with all due respect):

I hate how whenever anyone has hardware and software qualms about ubuntu, they get dismissed as "not being ubuntu's fault. It's the manufacturer's fault." That kind of attitude is the exact reason why hardware and software compatibility is still down the toilet on linux. Blame it on someone else, it's not our problem. Issues will never get solved if we keep blaming them on other people. 
If they won't help us make software and hardware compatible with the OS, then we need to work WITH them to do it. They won't write software compatible with the OS because they believe that there is no monetary incentive for doing so. Therefore, we need to prove to them that there is monetary gain for doing so. 

Another thing people need to realize is that not every piece of software can be "free and open source". If this were the case, there wouldn't be a single software company in existence. Don't get me wrong, there's plenty of decent free software, but if you want a really nice program like Final Cut Pro, be prepared to pay for it, it's that simple. I really want some nice media editing programs for my computer, but nobody sells them because ubuntu seems to have the attitude that "everything must be free". This attitude is destructive if we want more compatible programs on our OS. 

Additionally, if software companies completely refuse to help us, then it is our responsibility to take on the project ourselves. We already reverse-engineer hardware drivers (such as printers), so why don't we reverse-engineer software as well? Reverse engineer the software, and then SELL it at a reasonable price to the consumer. Pass along most of the profit to the original developer of the software, and keep a little bit of profit for those who reverse engineer it. Why can't we just do that? Then I could run some nicer stuff like Final Cut. I also would like to have adobe photoshop, but it seems I'm stuck with GIMP forever. :neutral:

In conclusion, I have two points to make:
1. If I want to spend some money on a decent, powerful media editing application (such as garageband, finalcut, cakewalk), I should be able to do it. 
2. We need to be more accountable for our operating system's shortcomings and come up with real solutions (as described above) instead of playing the blame game.

---

### Post by gimfred on 2011-01-10
> **asuastrophysics said:**
> (Partially in response to uRock's post, and with all due respect):

I hate how whenever anyone has hardware and software qualms about ubuntu, they get dismissed as "not being ubuntu's fault. It's the manufacturer's fault." That kind of attitude is the exact reason why hardware and software compatibility is still down the toilet on linux. Blame it on someone else, it's not our problem. Issues will never get solved if we keep blaming them on other people. 


If someone volunteers to sweep the streets and manages to keep 1/8 of the streets pleasant, do you blame them because the rest of the streets are gunked up?

Considering how easy and cheap it is for programmers to recompile their source on a free system, blaming *nix/open source/free software is beyond stupid. 

> If they won't help us make software and hardware compatible with the OS, then we need to work WITH them to do it. They won't write software compatible with the OS because they believe that there is no monetary incentive for doing so. Therefore, we need to prove to them that there is monetary gain for doing so.


Yes, I've written to numerous places offering to test their software on linux and pay for it; I get back a response they have no intentions of developing for linux. As ars technica's Humble bundle shows Linux fans are more than willing to pay for software to run on their system; linux people paid more per package on average and more total for the software. You blame linux users for not being able to purchase a non-existent product. ](*,) There was even a bloke who offered to develop drivers and software for the unix systems for free and not one manufacturer took him up. This deficiency is not the result of the *nixes trying to fix it. It has been deliberately hampered by apathy and destain from manufacturers and software developers. Their choice, not ours.

> Another thing people need to realize is that not every piece of software can be "free and open source". If this were the case, there wouldn't be a single software company in existence. Don't get me wrong, there's plenty of decent free software, but if you want a really nice program like Final Cut Pro, be prepared to pay for it, it's that simple. I really want some nice media editing programs for my computer, but nobody sells them because ubuntu seems to have the attitude that "everything must be free". This attitude is destructive if we want more compatible programs on our OS. 
 
You can't purchase non-existent products.

> Additionally, if software companies completely refuse to help us, then it is our responsibility to take on the project ourselves. We already reverse-engineer hardware drivers (such as printers), so why don't we reverse-engineer software as well? Reverse engineer the software, and then SELL it at a reasonable price to the consumer. Pass along most of the profit to the original developer of the software, and keep a little bit of profit for those who reverse engineer it. Why can't we just do that? Then I could run some nicer stuff like Final Cut. I also would like to have adobe photoshop, but it seems I'm stuck with GIMP forever. :neutral:

Yay! 'cept for the difficulty in doing so.

Oh and this: 
> it is our responsibility to take on the project ourselves 
conflicts with this:

> Then I could run some nicer stuff like Final Cut. I also would like to have adobe photoshop, but it seems I'm stuck with GIMP forever


'Our responsibility' morphs into a moan others not delivering YOU with what you want. Besides, do you really need the features PhotoShop has that Gimp doesn't? Depending on your career, not likely. 

> In conclusion, I have two points to make:
1. If I want to spend some money on a decent, powerful media editing application (such as garageband, finalcut, cakewalk), I should be able to do it.
 You can't purchase non-existent products.

> 2. We need to be more accountable for our operating system's shortcomings and come up with real solutions (as described above) instead of playing the blame game.
Well, I look forward to being able to purchase laniF tuC or pohSotohP from you shortly.

Linux does have downfalls. Ubuntu has problems. Mostly they are the same as any operating systems development; the expectation of feature development precedes perfection. Otherwise we would all be running *bsd or similar. I could be changing soon.

But you can not seriously blame any operating systems for lack of support by third parties. Heck, *wine* and others has been developed to 'simplify' this problem and you still blame *nix. (Sounds like you need Cygwin on windows rather than wine on Ubuntu.)

As for paying the development, there is [flattr]("http://flattr.com/") or similar. If you care so much, start running fundraisers to do what you want. 

Please think through your accusations and who is 'to blame'. I'd love to think that most problems aren't anyone's fault but lets be realistic. Sometimes someone is to blame. Lack of software for third party products for significant  systems* can only be the fact that the manufacturer has chosen (probably for economic reasons --- though there is *some* evidence for it being political) not to provide the desired product. Which is their prerogative.

* Except when the system deliberately excludes any method of running available products, i.e., iOS/Apple refusing particular programs because the owner doesn't like what the product does.

### Post by asuastrophysics on 2011-01-11
Gimfred, just because I said that we need to take on more of the responsibility ourselves does not mean that I was in any way trying to blame our own software developers. I just meant we as a community.

Now, I don't know about anyone else on here, but **I'd be willing to pay a decent amount of cash to see programs like final cut, photoshop, winamp, etc run flawlessly on linux.** And I know that the original makers of the software have no intentions of helping us, but where there's a pool of money to make something happen, there's almost always a way forward. I know that goes against the Ubuntu philosophy of "free and open source", but I think it would make for a more seemless OS if we could do it.

I wasn't trying to point the finger by saying that we shouldn't play the blame-game. That's exactly the opposite of what I was trying to say. I may not be able to write software, but I can contribute money to see things like this happen. I'm not trying to yell at developers to make this stuff magically appear in front of my eyes. I will do anything it takes to help out and contribute financially to making this stuff happen.

### Post by XEtedBear on 2011-01-12
1. That a technically non-skilled user of Ubuntu can break it so comprehensively and so easily by inadvertently requesting an update to the video driver and that Ubuntu will install a driver that does not work with the current hardware configuration.

Recovery takes some days of work.


2. That the Software Centre will install some programs but not tell you where they are or who to invoke them. They are for ever lost - shown as installed but unusable.


3. That so much information is entered into this, and other help sources, which is undated. It is frequently advice or guidance which in fact does not apply to the software/hardware configuration I have, but this cannot be determined except by trying it. Some times the incompatibility is obvious (command does not exist, or menu item does not exist). Sometimes the effects are undisclosed but fatal, without warning. Some of the guidance is just plain factually incorrect, but there is no vetting - aside from peer review - of what is entered into the forums.

---

### Post by asuastrophysics on 2011-01-12
> **Chronon said:**
> And your intent is what?  To bribe proprietary software vendors to port their software to Ubuntu?
It's not a bribe. You pay for the software license to use their software on your platform. That's how commercial software works. When I spend $60 on an introductory version of Photoshop, I'm paying for the license to use that software. 

> **Chronon said:**
> Find a free software project you want to support and donate some money to them.
You are missing my point entirely. Additionally, it is highly unrealistic and naive to assume that every piece of software ever written can be free of charge. You get what you pay for, or, in this case, you don't get what you don't pay for.

EDIT: This brings me to another thing I do not like about Ubuntu, err I mean the Ubuntu Forums. You are automatically singled-out as some sort of devilish monster or troll if you show any sort of respect or liking in any way to commercialware. So in order for the community to ever really help me with any sort of actual computer problems that I may have, I must renounce all of my own opinions, assimilate into the dominant opinions held by the majority of the forum, and proclaim to the world about how much commercialware sucks. Not everyone has the same views, and not everyone thinks alike. In a forum dedicated with helping the community, it is stupid to assume that everyone loves (and only loves) free, open-source software. I hate to break it to you, but without commercial software, millions of people would be without jobs. Without commercialware, Microsoft wouldn't exist, Apple wouldn't exist, UNIX would've never been conceived by AT&T, and therefore Linux would not be here today. Linux owes its very existence to the development of UNIX, which, oh yeah, was (and is) commercial and proprietary. If you are to say that commercialware sucks, then you are saying that UNIX sucks, and therefore that Linux sucks. It's an extremely ignorant and backwards statement that no one seems to think twice about making.

I'm not saying that I *love* commercialware, I just think that it's extremely ignorant for people to proclaim that they hate it as much as they do. Linux owes a lot from commercial development.

---

### Post by dtfinch on 2011-02-28
> **asuastrophysics said:**
> ... Without commercialware, Microsoft wouldn't exist, Apple wouldn't exist, UNIX would've never been conceived by AT&T, and therefore Linux would not be here today. Linux owes its very existence to the development of UNIX, which, oh yeah, was (and is) commercial and proprietary. ...

I agree with most of that, but technically, AT&T was forbidden from commercializing UNIX due to a 1958 consent decree, and had to license it to anyone that asked until 1983 when they were broken up. Because of that a lot of UNIX development was academic work outside of AT&T. Even Winsock on Windows was based on the Berkeley socket API. [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Unix#History](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Unix#History)

### Post by LinuxFox on 2011-03-06
I don't hate Ubuntu, I love using it.  Though there are a few things that bug me about Ubuntu...

Digital Audio Player compatibility - I'm able to use my mass storage digital audio player just fine.  Loads up as a flash disk, I just drag and drop audio files and I'm set.  My problem is jukebox software, Rhythmbox won't detect it.  I did submit my player to "media-player-info" and it was added, but it hasn't been updated yet so I can't use it. :(

Video formats and DVD playback - I refuse to install them due to their legal issues.  I'm sorry I never used illegal software on any computer I ever had, and I don't plan to.  Besides I mostly watch my DVDs on my TV anyway, not to mention most online video uses flash, which is legally availible for Ubuntu since it's on Adobe's website.

Those are two things I don't like, otherwise I love Ubuntu and use it for most things.  I dual-boot so I also have Windows for things I can't do on Ubuntu.

---

Amen to that!!!

I backed up my 10.10 machine, upgraded to 11.04 last night, and am struggling beyond compare with Unity.  I can't find where the System menu is without typing in the search to find it, amongst many of my applications / settings now.  It wiped out my Mint Menu and Compiz Fusion.  (to the developers): Don't even bother explaining why because it's not worth your time typing;  I already know why.  What I don't know though, is why you install Unity and wipe Gnome when you can detect when a user account has a heavy configuration tied to Gnome?  Why?  And don't tell me it's for support reasons because I can rip Unity right out of 11.04 and put Gnome right back in.

For the life of me I cannot figure out why Shuttleworth did not branch Ubuntu for netbooks, touchscreen, etc..  I don't care how much you know about Ubuntu, what level of development you do, QA, etc., you have to listen to your user base.  If you wanted to get a consensus on how people feel about Unity, branch Ubuntu and see if it takes off.  Don't take what possibly has made Ubuntu so popular and trash it... and don't take offense to this this because I know you guys are smart, but that is dumb what you guys are doing.

It's ok, I'm a really smart guy too, but sometimes I mess up and do dumb things.  99% of the time it takes someone telling me what I've done is dumb for me to figure it out.  Dumb is ok, but retarded is not ok.  Retarded is when we do something dumb, are told over and over it's dumb, and continue doing it anyway... that's retarded.

Why do I say retarded?  Because Ubuntu has a good thing going for itself, why screw it up?  Trust me, forcing Unity down people's throats is screwing it up.  It's way too much of an interface change for people to jump on and say, "Yea I love it, hated Gnome."  Troll the internet, find out for yourselves... people hate Unity.

I don't hate Unity, but I hate that 11.04 wiped Gnome off my system and has forced me to bust tail wiping Unity and re-installing / configuring Gnome (again).  I had a sweet setup (just the way I like it), and it's gone now.  I didn't say changed or different, I said gone.  And you're cool with that, right?  If you are, that's just wrong.

---

### Post by Lucradia on 2011-03-21
> **GuiGuy said:**
> Well, maybe now it's time for "gnubuntu", much like we have kubuntu etc.. :twisted:

I'm perfectly fine with Xubuntu for my netbook:

1. Install Xubuntu
2. Remove the bottom panel.
3. Add the workspace switcher to the top panel.
4. Add the icon box to the top panel.
5. Switch the places and applications menus to be icon only.
6. Install Openoffice or LibreOffice. Doesn't matter really, spellcheck won't work on RTFs.
7. Remove all unecessary programs: ristretto, gnomine, mahjongg, aisleriot, vinagre, thunderbird, etc.
8. Replace all the bulky apps: transmission-gtk to deluge; xfce4-terminal to sakura; clipman to parcellite.
9. Install the Mozilla daily build Firefox-4.0; it goes vastly faster than 3.6. Switch to HTML5 on YouTube after.
10. Put the trash applet on the top panel if needed. (it takes up more memory, as it's a separate binary.)

Basically all you need right there. I still take up like 300~420 MB of RAM though out of the 1016 MB I have physically. (Office alone takes upt 100~200 MB.)

### Post by Copper Bezel on 2011-03-21
> Well, I tried that and it didn't work.

Didn't work how? You can't have Unity and not have that option. 

> Compiz was disabled, so I went in to enable it and my whole desktop went kaplooyee. I tried everything to get 11.04 operational after that and gave up and restored from backup my 10.10 environment. Life is good again.

Sounds like you were running Unity 2D, then, as the standard Unity interface uses Compiz. In 10.10, Unity is actually implemented through Mutter, and running Compiz kills it (leaving you with a naked desktop.)

### Post by Lucradia on 2011-04-21
> **MBybee said:**
> Really odd package choices. Empathy over Pidgin, Evolution over Thunderbird, etc. 
Drivers that work in one version being seriously broken in the next.

Empathy replaced Pidgin due to the envelope icon, this decision was made back a release or two. However, Pidgin now integrates with the newer envelope, so there's no reason to keep empathy around.

That, and empathy encrypts passwords, supposedly. But, Windows Live Messenger doesn't actually encrypt your password well: [http://developer.pidgin.im/wiki/PlainTextPasswords](http://developer.pidgin.im/wiki/PlainTextPasswords)

No doubt, Empathy does any better.

### Post by nomko on 2011-04-22
It's not something I hate, but I think is very disturbing. The many updates over the past week I've received gives me the impression that there is not a good and stable product delivered to the end-users. Compared with the origin of Ubuntu, Debian GNU, Ubuntu feels very unstable. I&#8217;m now using version 10.04.2, but I have the strong feeling that it is not very stable and reliable. The many daily updates from the past week increase that feeling with me very much.
 
What I also find a big criticism is that there is a new release every half year. All these releases are often too unstable and full of childhood diseases. Instead of struggling to put out a new (half yearly) release, Canonical can better choose to put their efforts in releasing a good, stable and reliable version once in every 2 years for example.
 
By updating the kernel, the reliability and stability won&#8217;t benefit either. By updating the kernel, the users will experience a high risk the new kernel will mess up a stable and functioning system due to the reason that one does not even know if the new kernel will function properly on their system.
 
In that respect, Debian has their business much better together. In Debian there is still stability and reliability. The robustness of Debian is, thanks to Canonical, killed unfortunately.

### Post by peyre on 2011-05-07
Mounting.

I'm running Xubuntu, which is a little different--I gather there are some bugs in Xubuntu re. mounting.  But hell, that part of it sucks, not being able to rely on your system to mount removable media reliably.

To sum up, zip disks work flawlessly on my system.  Flash drives worked almost as well, but CDs/DVDs didn't always mount properly.  Or it would mount but my various media players still played dumb and pretended they couldn't see the DVD I had inserted (and could browse in the file manager!).  Floppies are hopeless (in my installation, anyway).

Until recently, flash drives were working very well--short of flawless, but still, very well.  But for some reason, all of a sudden the system insists on mounting them as root--so I have to run my file manager as root in order to copy to them, delete from them, or even dismount them.  chmod doesn't help, MountManager doesn't help, changing permissions in Properties doesn't do the job.

So yes, mounting removable media is something I hate in *buntu.  IMO this is a real falling down on Linux's part (or at least Ubuntu's), that it fails to handle a basic operating system function like mounting removable media reliably.  Windows does it right every time--so what's Xubuntu's excuse?

Ok, I realize that came to a bit of a rant there.  I really like Xubuntu.  I'm just frustrated with it on that point, and it's a fairly serious issue that several version upgrades have so far failed to address.

---

### Post by Retlol on 2011-05-08
Not Ubuntu, but more Linux stuff in general.

I just like my shizzle to work with ease. Can still be an issue in Linux.

Most hardware and software works, but not "Windows work".

Drivers and software often are lower quality. Getting stuff to work could mean editing text files or cli code *(mostly copy, paste, but there's always something you'll have to change, always. When you don't know why random error x appears and you have to start googling to make it work or worse, troubleshoot yourself)*.

I really don't mind it when I have the time, but when I'm busy or am chillaxing I really cba and can get a bit ticked off.

[I]I'm sure some dude will reply with, installing software is easier (if it's in the repo or has a deb, sure. Not that downloading and double-clicking is hard but still). Or system updates are easier (not when yum crashes and hangs the system). Or Windows easy, haha --> blue screen of lol. Well, put random joe behind Ubuntu and see how fast he effs up the system.
[/I]

---

### Post by peyre on 2011-05-08
You're right, Retlol.  "Just works" is an issue.  Sometimes Linux (or at least the distro you're using) has it; other times you have to fight for it.  Some things just work out of the box, maybe even easier than in Windows.  Others you have to wrestle with to get working (mostly) right.  A few others you probably can't get working at all, or very well anyway.  And at the end you have this feeling of accomplishment, of "Wow, I got it working and everything! DIY FTW!"...and then it strikes you that "...I could have gotten everything put together, without the extra work, three days ago."

And yes, installations are a mixed bag.  If what you want is in your repository, installation is actually easier than in Windows(!).  If the app you want has a package available (.deb, .rpm, or whatever your distro uses), it's basically the same as Windows.  But if all you can find is a tarball, you're looking at compiling from source, which *never* works right the first time.  IMO, installations are another real issue for Linux--not that it can't do it, but Linux is all over the place with different package management systems, and that shouldn't be.

### Post by Lucradia on 2011-05-09
> **beew said:**
> I don't get it, why is it bad? I seem to hear this complaint from time to time but it works very well for me and I multiboot several Linux. But then I haven't  used legacy grub so may be I don't know what I am missing. Another thing I hear complaint about from time to time is pulseaudio, again I have no idea why, it works well on all machines I install Ubuntu on.

GRUB2 has an issue on some Harddrives when installed freshly, side-by-side on a Windows Machine, you will need to install grub removing grub2, or install grub2, and use update grub from within ubuntu's install (the latter method doesn't always work. grub2 often is installed halfway side-by-side on my windows machine.)

However, installing Fedora works perfectly (but you have to make unallocated space first, Fedora can't resize NTFS like Ubuntu could.)

### Post by maddbaron on 2011-05-12

### Post by peyre on 2011-05-12
I'd like to add that I find it inconvenient that I can't rename files by doing the click-pause-click thing, like you can in Windows.  Sure you can right-click and select Rename, or hit F2.  But those both interrupt my workflow, especially since I normally use a mouse left-handed.

That's just an annoyance, though.  What I can say I hate about Unbuntu on this subject is that, when people have suggested it on Ubuntu Brainstorm, the developers have shut down the discussion with comments amounting to "No! That's bad design--and no, I won't explain why."

The click-pause-click thing is how things work in both Windows and OSX, which means anyone coming to Ubuntu from another OS (which is most Ubuntu users) has to make yet another adjustment.  I can understand that it might not be a best practice, from a design/programming point of view, but if it's a feature that a significant number of people would like to see, why not make it an option that users can turn on?  Don't even make it the default behavior--but let people turn it on if they want to.

For an OS that claims to be all about choice--and usually is--it seems jarringly out of character to refuse to consider implementing it as a selectable option.

---

### Post by Lucradia on 2011-05-13
> **demilord said:**
> I have that with synaptics package manager... :mad:

Did you have something selected and change your mind and closed it? Sometimes apps will "freeze" and stay in memory until the system decides to force quit it. This usually happens with Firefox when you close it with a flash thing open, or a page with a lot of DOM / AJAX content.

DOM doesn't only have an effect on the web page's scroll-ability, it can affect the performance of the entire browser, a-like so: [https://bugzilla.mozilla.org/show_bug.cgi?id=633765](https://bugzilla.mozilla.org/show_bug.cgi?id=633765)

### Post by JHBrewer on 2011-06-04
Well, I hate to inject a sour note into this chorus of ecstatic true believers, but I definitely *hate* that the "Do you want to upgrade to 11.04?" message (that automatically appeared last time I did an update) didn't mention that the entire look & feel of Ubuntu was completely changed with this version and includes "cool" features (like automatically maximizing a window when I move it to a corner) that appear to be impossible to turn off.  

I have chosen the auto-upgrade option in the past and never had a problem, but suddenly it's like I was running *Windows* instead of Linux.  I totally *hate* 11.04, and am trying to decide which will be easier: to downgrade back to 10.10 or to pull out my Mandriva DVD and go back to that old familiar clunker.  

News flash, Ubuntu: some of us are more interested in **working** with our OS than with having it "look cool".  And most of us are not really interested in *your* opinion of how we should set up *our* workspaces.  Shades of *NeXT*, for goodness' sake!

### Post by piper1 on 2011-06-04
Cool thread, and great replies i've seen here :D

Now my personal rant (just to inform you, it's 2:23 late at night here):

So i downloaded the latest release of WUBI on my netbook and installed it.

Which worked quite fine, until it rebooted and tried to configure itself.

Crashed 3 times in a row.

For some unknown reason, after the 4th reboot, it brought me to the log on screen, where i was able to log on.

And then it scared me to death with that new look&feel.

Who the heck got the thought, that it is a good idea to write such a piece of software and release it? Not asking about to integrate it into WUBI and not even give the user a chance to choose something differrent?

I want a GUI which does what i want, and not something wich starts to blink and flicker and sends me through an adventure, when i just want to find stuff like a simple terminal window or an application which lets me change the the appearance and such!

And at lest nothing like that, which maximizes windows out of the blue, or a "task bar" at the left of the screen, which hides, when i move the mouse towards it!

Ok, starting firefox was quite easy, i must confess.

Crashing the whole thing and causing a kernel panic by searching something in firefox was easy too.

How the hell can an application cause a kernel panic?

Well, all that downloading and installing took me this evening like 5 hours and the result is, friendly said, not so nice.

My hardware is a simple netbook, 1.5 years old, and the WUBI release from 1.5 years ago was working fine, until one of the automatic windows 7 updates destroyed the boot loader and i couldnt log into Ubunto anymore.

Which brings me to another thing:

It would be nice, if WUBI would not just offer the option to de-install an installed version of Ubuntu, but even have the feature to recover that old version.

Because obviously in my case only the boot loader was destroyed, while the Ubuntu installation was still there.

Okay i'll stop my rant now, but seriously, this latest release of WUBI is worth to be kicked in the bucket ASAP.

---

### Post by JHBrewer on 2011-06-04
OK, I *searched* for an explanation for why **seamonkey** crashes in 11.04, and I found the following in _[]("https://answers.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/seamonkey/+question/156671")[https://answers.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/seamonkey/+question/156671](https://answers.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/seamonkey/+question/156671)_ 

"After 3 days work...Here's the catch seamonkey 2.0.13 works fine with  ubuntu 10.10.  If you update to ubuntu 11.04 seamonkey quits working.   If you install ubuntu 11.04 for the first time and install seamonkey  from the ubuntu software center you get seamonkey 2.0.13 which goes not  work.  You MUSt MANUALLY install seamonkey 2.0.14 Then all is good  again.  My mailbox, address book  and bookmarks stayed intact.  But back  them up anyway before upgrading just in case."

Would it be too much to ask that 11.04 install a version of seamonkey that *works* with 11.04?  Has the "rush to release" bug infected Ubuntu?  

I don't want my desktop to look cooler.  I want to be able to keep working without having to spend days learning my way around a completely new (and much more intricate) labyrinth of configuration tools, some of which (like "slideshow" desktop backgrounds)  have had to be provided by third parties with their own repositories -- something any security-conscious user tries to avoid.  I'm as dedicated to Open Source as anyone, but I don't think "Trust everyone!" is a safe strategy even for Linux.

Is the title of this thread intended as a "honeypot" trap for people you want to prevent from complaining?  If I get blackballed for these complaints, that will be my conclusion.

### Post by peyre on 2011-06-04
> **JHBrewer said:**
> Well, I hate to inject a sour note into this chorus of ecstatic true believers, but I definitely *hate* that the "Do you want to upgrade to 11.04?" message (that automatically appeared last time I did an update) didn't mention that the entire look & feel of Ubuntu was completely changed with this version and includes "cool" features (like automatically maximizing a window when I move it to a corner) that appear to be impossible to turn off.  

I have chosen the auto-upgrade option in the past and never had a problem, but suddenly it's like I was running *Windows* instead of Linux.  I totally *hate* 11.04, and am trying to decide which will be easier: to downgrade back to 10.10 or to pull out my Mandriva DVD and go back to that old familiar clunker.  

News flash, Ubuntu: some of us are more interested in **working** with our OS than with having it "look cool".  And most of us are not really interested in *your* opinion of how we should set up *our* workspaces.  Shades of *NeXT*, for goodness' sake!

Not to get on your case, but Ubuntu is hardly alone in messing with the user experience from one version to the next.  Ever compare Vista/7 to XP?  Granted Windows has a slower upgrade cycle, but still, if you think Ubuntu is bad about letting you go back to your old settings, it's on good behavior compared to MS.

I can understand the frustration that "Hey, they f*cked up my computing experience!", but I think it's a stretch expecting them to advertise in the upgrade prompt that a bunch of changes will by made by it.  (I agree that it would be a Good Thing to include such a warning, but I think it's going a bit far to demand it.)

Then again, I'm looking at this whole thing a bit from the outside, since I'm a Xubuntu user, and it's only mainstream Ubuntu that went to Unity.  By the way, you *can* turn Unity off--email me offline if you want instructions, and I can find them again.

but this thread is named "Things that you hate in Ubuntu", which doesnt look like a place, where i would ask for help, just a place where i drop my rant.

My complain is, and as you can see in this thread, the complain of some others too, that that new cool looking interface is the default, and you don't have any chance to change that at install time.

I just want an operating system i could install and use and don't have to learn how to use a cool and flashy looking interface, most simple point, i want to open a terminal window without doing research for hours.

If you want my concern in simple words and not a rant, here it is:

Make a distro, which has a GUI everybody knows and can use, and don't come up with a *cool* GUI.

If someone wants a *cool looking* GUI, there should be an option to install it, but it shouldnt be the default.

Forcing users to use something new, but not giving an option to use it the regular, known way is always a bad idea IMO.

### Post by tortugo23 on 2011-06-08
I hate that I want to use it so badly, and that to do that I have to do double work in order to collaborate with my colleagues. I work with people who use Microsoft Office, and I have been switching over to Libre Office only to find its not as seamless as its cracked up to be. 

What is driving me back to Windows are drivers. Ubuntu doesn't print without streaking color. I hate that.

### Post by diacad on 2011-06-08
Speaking as a beginner, nearly everything, except the fact that it is free.  And that the virus trolls have so far not bothered it.

But there is not enough complaining.  Perhaps everyone accessing the forums are Ubuntu experts by birth.  I am not.

I just posted a message to the wrong place here (the administrator), but I will repeat my latest gripe with Ubuntu:  I am running 10.04 with a decent but older graphics card (nVidia M200 32Mb), The nVidia driver is loaded as recommended.  I can only get 640x480 resolution, and there is no obvious way to get more. The card works fine in Windows at 1024x768.  I do not need high end graphics, why throw this card away?  How you change the resolution (or if that is impossible) is not immediately obvious, and it is hard to find documentation for what is and what is not possible.  Sure, there are endless blogs, and somewhere there may be that needle in the haystack that will mend the tattered sleeve of care.  But this repeats all the time, with different subjects, and it is getting to be a bore.

I can only get 640x480 resolution, and there is no obvious way to get more. The card works fine in Windows at 1024x768.  I do not need high end graphics, why throw this card away?  How you change the resolution (or if that is impossible) is not immediately obvious, and it is hard to find documentation for what is and what is not possible.  Sure, there are endless blogs, and somewhere there may be that needle in the haystack that will mend the tattered sleeve of care.  But this repeats all the time, with different subjects, and it is getting to be a bore.

Ubuntu expert by birth?  Not even close, in my case at least.  Windows and its command-line environment feels intuitive to me, and Linux feels counterintuitive.  Doing stuff in Linux feels just like getting by in a second language to me.  I just think it's important for me to learn and use it--partly for work reasons, partly out of a sense of wanting to grow as a computer professional*, and partly out of admiration for the open source community and its ideals.

* which I fancy myself to be, on a good day

I agree with ivanovnegro--it's probably a driver issue.  Most likely you need to install the proprietary NVidia driver.  If you're lucky it'll appear automatically when you open System, Hardware Drivers (if I remember right--I don't use regular Ubuntu, but a variant).  If you're unlucky, you'll have to research a little and install the driver manually, which is a Royal F*cking Hassle--you have to close out of the GUI and install from the command line, hope you got it right, then restart the GUI or maybe even the computer.

---

Not being able to access more than 4 workspaces in 11.04
