### Post by GuiGuy on 2011-06-10
**Abysmal Power Management**

In this era of ever increasing power (electricity) costs I find it frustrating that not more attention is being given to having intuitive and reliable power management (auto suspend/ shutdown) on linux generally and ubuntu in particular. 

Apple and M$ have this sorted nicely. Networked computers can be made to power up without stuffing about by having to send magic packets manually. Both of their OSs are smart enough to know not to shut down when the computer is doing something or when it is being accessed by another networked PC.  

Not in the linux world. It's only smart enough to pay attention if the mouse cursor is moving about or if the keyboard is working. Other than that, it will blissfully go to suspend in the middle of a batch job such as a back up process or whilst watching a video or if the PC is being accessed by another over the network. Essentially, it means that if you intend to do prolonged jobs that don't require user input, the power management has to be turned off.

The reason I have a "[bee in my bonnet]("http://www.phrases.org.uk/meanings/bee-in-your-bonnet.html")" about this now is that last night I set a large back job in motion, The backup was important because I am going away for a few days. The backup was going to take about two hours. Unfortunately I forgot to turn power management off.... You know the rest :( 

It's all very tedious and not at all green. 

As always, of course, happy to be proved wrong. 

Cheers

---

### Post by ZombiePriest on 2011-06-10
I'm new to Ubuntu. I'm used to Fedora Core. First thing I absolutely detest and cannot work around: Window buttons on the top left. Why? It makes absolutely no sense. It would not be so bad if I had the option to configure it, but I can't find it.

Second thing is Unity in general. My eyes ache. Make it stop. Make it go away. Stop letting it do things to my monitor space that I did not want to be done. At least let it do the same thing after the same sequence of mouse clicks.

Third thing is Broadcast Accounts. How do I disable this? Remove it? Kill it? Delete it off the internet entirely? There doesn't seem to be a way for me to start an application without waking up a keyring password manager and all my broadcast accounts. 

Orthogonality is one of the brilliant features that set Linux apart from Windows. When I fire up one application, I do not wish to tumble down a rabbit hole. I expect one application to fire up, perform its magic and then disappear in a puff of smoke. Nice and clean, nobody needs to get hurt.

I do not care for eye candy, I want things to work. If I wanted a one size fits all operating system, I'd purchase Windows 7.

### Post by Copper Bezel on 2011-06-10

sudo apt-get remove notify-osd indicator-messages ubuntuone-client
```

### Post by jhonan on 2011-06-14
This isn't Ubuntu-specific, but a general linux distro gripe.


[LIST]
[*]CTRL-C in a terminal window breaks the running process by default.
[*]CTRL-C in Windows copies the selected text by default.
[/LIST]

Okay, I know Linux got there first with the 'CTRL-C as break' convention. But I find nothing more infuriating than forgetting about CTRL-C in a terminal window and stopping a process that's been running for an hour when really all I wanted to do was copy some output to the clipboard.

inb4 "change the default CTRL-C mapping in Ubuntu" - Great, if you remember to do that on every linux terminal you use in the course of a project.

---

Okay, I know Linux got there first with the 'CTRL-C as break' convention. But I find nothing more infuriating than forgetting about CTRL-C in a terminal window and stopping a process that's been running for an hour when really all I wanted to do was copy some output to the clipboard.

### Post by ed bear on 2011-06-17
I am all bent out of shape that built-in dial-up support was ripped from network support after 8.04!  Configuring gnome-ppp is a pain...

### Post by peyre on 2011-06-17
> **ed bear said:**
> I am all bent out of shape that built-in dial-up support was ripped from network support after 8.04!  Configuring gnome-ppp is a pain...

If I was closer to a current edition, I'd probably be upset about the removal of Gnome, too...
ED BEAR

Wow, I came on board with 8.10, so I didn't know dial-up was ever installed, integrated, by default.  I agree, it was (probably) a mistake to take it out.  Yes, I've had trouble with GnomePPP too, so I've had to use KPPP instead, which is much more heavyweight, takes longer to load, and doesn't go look for your modem and tell you what port it's on, like GnomePPP will.  It doesn't include TTYSM0 for Martian (for Agere winmodems), and doesn't let you type in the port--you can only select from the list.

*For those wondering why I would possibly want to use dial-up, I always like to keep it on hand on my main computer as a fallback for those times when our DSL connection dies, and on my laptop for when I'm visiting relatives who don't have high-speed Internet.*

---

### Post by peyre on 2011-06-20

Sometimes the latest version just wouldn't install successfully, so I had to go back to an earlier version, like you.  But just now I'm finding that my flavor of choice (Xubuntu) keeps locking up--over and over.  I'm installing Lubuntu instead, which might be a prudent idea anyway since it's a pretty old machine, and so far it seems to be working better under Lubuntu than it did under Xubuntu.

### Post by dtfinch on 2011-06-21
> **GuiGuy said:**
> It's my observation that the machine isn't "freezing" up, rather some process takes control at the expense of everything  else. SimpleBackup is a good case in point. So is Firefox, especially FF4. 

So, either Linux isn't very good at multitasking or applications have been poorly implemented. Or maybe a bit of both.
I've run into that a lot. In Firefox, you can go to about:config and add an integer "toolkit.storage.synchronous" with the value 0 which fixes most of the pauses.

As I see it, Ext4 brought with it delayed allocation (similar to XFS's), which in theory improves performance and reduces fragmentation, but to try and make it more effective they also lengthened the auto commit delay from 5 seconds as in ext3 to up to 120 seconds, encouraging applications to call fsync() explicitly after every write to avoid data loss, and making data and metadata updates out of sync with each other (rather than ext3's ordered mode) so that data loss often meant you lost the entire file, rather than getting a previous state. And all this extra syncing exposed a seemingly recent [kernel bug](https://bugzilla.kernel.org/show_bug.cgi?id=18632) that causes sync/fsync() calls to wait for all writing to finish, rather than only those writes preceding the call. Firefox itself became fsync-heavy after its switch to sqlite, though you usually don't care if you lose a history entry or two in a power outage enough to warrant the extra slowness.

### Post by FormatSeize on 2011-06-22
I don't like Unity. I don't like that when Unity is installed, my monitor all of a sudden becomes incompatible with the Grub screen, so I have to guess at what I'm booting into (pushing the down arrow three times is what I have to do to boot into Unity). 
 
I don't like the .deb packaging system. Well, I guess I don't like any packaging system, for that matter. Sure, there's Alien, but soon enough the list grows long, then I have to write a script, and then something still seems to go wrong. For people that don't want to compile tarballs, each system should have an optional GUI that extracts and compiles tarballs. 
 
Why can't the page in a repository for each package also include a link to a text file that shows the dependency tree? I know, I know (they even tell you on the repository page) that there's the whole "apt-get" thing. But why is it automatically assumed that every computer in one's home is connected to the internet?
 
Too, I should never have to download a .deb file, save it to a USB, take it to another computer, actually go through Synaptic package manager (which I hardly ever do) by double clicking the package, only to have that package try to access the internet to look for another package. So then I have to go into the script, look for what it's trying to access the internet to get, compile it, then go back to installing the original package.
 
A package with no listed dependencies should not contain "wget" anywhere in it's installation scripts, in my opinion. Why not try to look for the package on my machine if there is no internet connection? (That's probably something that I could have it do, I suppose. I'll give that a try). 
 
Surprisingly, though, I don't have a problem with the updating process. I guess I don't do the things that others do, and I don't notice that something is different or breaking. I don't lose printers during an update because I don't print things. I don't lose Samba, or if I do I don't notice, because I'm not even sure exactly what Samba is. For me, an update is mostly a slightly altered system that I'm told is up to date, and unnoticeable changes to gedit, if any.

Why can't the page in a repository for each package also include a link to a text file that shows the dependency tree? apt-cache depends <package name>

### Post by seeker5528 on 2011-06-24
> **FormatSeize said:**
> I don't like Unity. I don't like that when Unity is installed, my monitor all of a sudden becomes incompatible with the Grub screen, so I have to guess at what I'm booting into (pushing the down arrow three times is what I have to do to boot into Unity).

Hmm, nothing Unity related after comes up on my machine until after I have selected which OS to boot and left the Grub screen. 
 
> Why can't the page in a repository for each package also include a link to a text file that shows the dependency tree? I know, I know (they even tell you on the repository page) that there's the whole "apt-get" thing. But why is it automatically assumed that every computer in one's home is connected to the internet?

If you want to directly browse the repositories, that is your choice, but there is an alternative that was actually designed to allow searching and will show you the dependencies. 

[http://packages.ubuntu.com/natty/gnome](http://packages.ubuntu.com/natty/gnome)

Of course that only tells you the dependencies of a package, not the dependencies of it's dependencies.

On the machine you are downloading the packages on, assuming it is the same release, or on the machine you want to install the packages on assuming it's list of packages is relatively up to date and apt-rdepends is installed, doing ```
apt-rdepends gnome > gnome.txt
```

Would create a gnome.txt text file with dependencies for the Gnome metapackage along with the dependencies of it's dependencies. 
 
> Too, I should never have to download a .deb file, save it to a USB, take it to another computer, actually go through Synaptic package manager (which I hardly ever do) by double clicking the package, only to have that package try to access the internet to look for another package. So then I have to go into the script, look for what it's trying to access the internet to get, compile it, then go back to installing the original package.

In other words, it should create the things it depends on out of thin air?
 
> A package with no listed dependencies should not contain "wget" anywhere in it's installation scripts, in my opinion. Why not try to look for the package on my machine if there is no internet connection? (That's probably something that I could have it do, I suppose. I'll give that a try).

If you put the packages in '/var/cache/apt/archives/' they will be found when something depends on them.

If there was a package that satisfied the dependency, the installation script would not "wget" anything, installation scripts only use "wget" to download things that can't be included in the repositories because of some licensing or other concern. Like with the proprietary Flash plug-in for example.

Later, Seeker

### Post by darkhelmetchris on 2011-07-09
> **Dyzphagia said:**
> I hate that my hard drive died with ubuntu on it. I had that baby for five good years.

Tee hee.  True, we've all been through something similar.  But, I suppose it's hardly Ubuntu's fault that modern mechanical hard drives are no longer the quality that they used to be a few years ago.  So, I have adopted a rule:  change my hard drive(s) every 3 years whether it's in good shape or not.  $50 for a drive over 3 years that cost me less than $20 a year, that's cheap safety.  In fact, I am probably going to do it every 2 years now, $25 per year, that's easy to swallow.  Data crash, not so easy.  (yes, don't choke there, I *DO* back up.  I just don't LIKE to restore if I don't have to).  I might even go so far as to put in 2 drives in a RAID1 mirror just because it's a lovely way of getting a little extra jolt of read-performance and data redundancy as a side benefit.  Blah blah blah... lol.  I have changed, quite literally, hundreds of hard disks for customers and a few for myself.  You go out and buy a nice new 500GB or 2TB drive and poof... 6 months it's toast and you find yourself filling out an RMA for a warranty claim.  Sheesh.  I still have my original 60MB WD Tidbit from 1992.  Yes, 60 MEG!  And it works flawlessly STILL.  Mind you, so far, touch wood, my SSD in my laptop has hit 2 years and it's perfect.  I think I'll just let it die so I can get a feel for how long the SSDs actually last.  Bloody engineers. hehe.  But, I digress, not Ubuntu's fault there...

So .. what I hate about Ubuntu?  I hate that any minimized application that is "flashing attention" fades in/out SLOWLY and does NOT attract my attention, and that I can't find any information ANYWHERE about how to make that actually flash instead of fade in and out gracefully hiding from my attention.  7 versions of Ubuntu later... it's STILL a slow, barely noticeable fade.  So, I will continue to "ignore" my friends yelling at me on Pidgin, my update manager that wants to tell me something it thinks is important, and the two application windows that just opened behind the rest and are "flashing" to tell me they are ready for me, behind all my other things.  *sigh

### Post by GuiGuy on 2011-08-06
VENTING: - I just wanted to quickly play a few songs...

Why isn't there a simple, effective music player, one I can simply point at a folder and tell it to play what's there, randomly or otherwise.

Windows and the MacBook does it, but the linux players, after waiting for several minutes for the frigging thing to load, at the expense of every other task, will first want to 'scan' and build a library. An hour or so later we're finally ready to play some music. 

Prove me wrong, but I have only come across VLC that is reasonably efficient in use; Juk, Amarok (why did they have to "fix" it), Banshee etc all IMO are overkill. 

While I am at it, are those who write application for linux aware that it is supposed to be a multi tasking operating system?

---

So I don't think it is a Linux problem .

### Post by Copper Bezel on 2011-08-06
"Hate" isn't the word, but as I'm tripping over the bits I've left out on a recent fresh install, I find it weird that gparted, baobab, and gnome-device-manager, among other basic system utilities, aren't installed by default from the LiveCD installer. There are other things that could be trimmed, I should think, and GParted is already available *in* the LiveCD and used by Ubiquity.

Edit: But I have a lot of respect for what Canonical brings to the table, regardless, and my new install is reminding me of *that*, too. Lots of cruddy workarounds I had to muddle through on 10.10 that aren't necessary on 11.04.

### Post by arizonagirl11 on 2011-08-07
Things I hate about Ubuntu...

1--Things are 10 times harder then they are in Windows. This computer used to run Ubuntu. I did an update to 11.something, and the PC crashed, and I couldn't get it back up. The reason I updated is because my PC continually rebooted itself. Finally I put windows back into it.

2--The sound is HORRIBLE. I love a game called Second Life, and half the time, sound didn't work. I can't hear the media abilities of second life. Using the voice features were also iffy. I was also told my voice was always crackly. Now that I have Windows in this PC, I can hear all media, and I am told my voice is crystal clear.

3--Everything in Ubuntu is HARD! I just upgraded an older PC, and have a new video card, etc... Do you think I can find the correct video card driver? I did! but it is a .run file, and Ubuntu will not run it! I even tried to follow the directions, no go. I did it on 8.04, and just updated to I think 10.04. Still, won't work.

4--impossible to format! I cannot format my hard drive on the other PC. I have finally had enough of Ubuntu, got real tired of fighting to find drivers, etc..., so decided to go back to windows, totally. but I can't install Windows, cause I can't reformat the drive. So now, I"m stuck with a PC that has no video drivers, or anything else for that matter.

5--The limitations are GREAT. I can't use my web cam, among other things in Ubuntu.

---

### Post by NightwishFan on 2011-08-07
Nvm.. Basically buy supported hardware because products without vendor support for linux can perform poorly. Also there is no way that you are 'unable to format the drive'. Windows should automatically wipe it when you try to install, if not there are ways to wipe it like the gparted live cd.

Most webcams work, you can buy a usb one at almost any store.

---

### Post by el_koraco on 2011-08-07
@arizonagirl
I believe you can't format the drive because you're trying to do it while ubuntu is running. Boot a LIVE CD, open up Gparted, right click on the swap partition and select swapoff, then you can format the drive to NTFS, and install XP.

### Post by rpaskudniak on 2011-08-07
Wow! A chance to air y petty grievances with 11.04!

OK, for starters, the upgrade from 10.10 to 11.04 destroyed my boot sector.  I had to recover from the Windows installation disc.  And the 10.04 install did the same when I installed from a live disk.  So Ubuntu has issues with dual boot installations.  Adding insult to the injury when I did the upgrade to 11.04:  It gave me a choice whether to keep the old grub or install a new one.  No clue as to the consequences of either choice.  I simply chose to keep the old, which is apparently how my boot sector got destroyed.  So now it's my own fault.

Do you want the cheese omelet or the mushroom omelet? .. Excellent choice, sir.  Oh, the omelet you just chose and ate was poisoned.  Well, it WAS your choice!  Not my fault.

OK, another peeve: I can't get my microphone pickup to work in Ubuntu.  In windows, this worked without an ounce of extra effort - plug it in and it works! Hence, I can't use Skype under Ubuntu.  And looking at the posts related to this issue, the solutions read like [[COLOR="Blue"]_this technobabble I found on YouTube_[/COLOR]]("http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Sn4WF7z8__4").

And while we're on the subject of sound: In windows, when I plug in the earphone jack, the OS mutes the speakers.  Expected behavior, yes?  But not in Ubuntu!  No, again there seems to be a bunch of technobabble solutions but, thus far, no simple fix.

Come on, folks!  Ease of use is a major stumbling block to wider acceptance of Linux.  If the supposedly easiest Linux installation (Ubuntu) blows stuff like this, what can the target audience (the entire computing world) expect from the Linux world in general.

I have not abandoned Linux or Ubuntu.  But I find less convenient to use it lately due to these little annoyances.

---

### Post by peyre on 2011-08-08
> **arizonagirl11 said:**
> Things I hate about Ubuntu...

1--Things are 10 times harder then they are in Windows. This computer used to run Ubuntu. I did an update to 11.something, and the PC crashed, and I couldn't get it back up. The reason I updated is because my PC continually rebooted itself. Finally I put windows back into it.

2--The sound is HORRIBLE. I love a game called Second Life, and half the time, sound didn't work. I can't hear the media abilities of second life. Using the voice features were also iffy. I was also told my voice was always crackly. Now that I have Windows in this PC, I can hear all media, and I am told my voice is crystal clear.

3--Everything in Ubuntu is HARD! I just upgraded an older PC, and have a new video card, etc... Do you think I can find the correct video card driver? I did! but it is a .run file, and Ubuntu will not run it! I even tried to follow the directions, no go. I did it on 8.04, and just updated to I think 10.04. Still, won't work.

4--impossible to format! I cannot format my hard drive on the other PC. I have finally had enough of Ubuntu, got real tired of fighting to find drivers, etc..., so decided to go back to windows, totally. but I can't install Windows, cause I can't reformat the drive. So now, I"m stuck with a PC that has no video drivers, or anything else for that matter.

5--The limitations are GREAT. I can't use my web cam, among other things in Ubuntu.

I feel your pain, arizonagirl.  I just spent most of a week wrestling with my system, trying to get the video driver installed correctly.  But I'm back up and running now, and I'm glad I stuck with it.  To get the .run file to--um--run, I had to execute "chmod +x *filename*.run".  And to run it, I had to do a Ctrl-F11 and another key combination to get out of X Window so it could do its job.  You'd think that all wouldn't be necessary in a modern operating system, but maybe they'll fix that someday.

I'd venture that the difficulty of Ubuntu vs. Windows is not so much a one-way street as you think--on average, at least.  You may have just experienced the a** end of the average.

My observation has been that between Ubuntu and Windows, some things are easier in Windows and some things are easier in Ubuntu.  I do think it works out to something like 2/3 are easier in Windows, but still, it *does* work both ways.

I had trouble with sound too--one of the reasons I reinstalled Ubuntu was that my sound got scratchy (crackly?) when I turned the volume up above quiet.  It may be a driver issue, but I think the reinstall cleared it up.

My Web cam works just fine, and it's no high-end model.  Again, you're probably experiencing a driver issue.  I have another piece of hardware (a Plextor PX-AV100U) that doesn't work in Linux because no drivers are available...but such is life, really.  Knowing this doesn't help much when you're just trying to get something to work, but it really isn't Linux's fault that drivers don't exist for a given device.

### Post by peyre on 2011-08-08
> **rpaskudniak said:**
> And while we're on the subject of sound: In windows, when I plug in the earphone jack, the OS mutes the speakers.  Expected behavior, yes?  But not in Ubuntu!  No, again there seems to be a bunch of technobabble solutions but, thus far, no simple fix.

Yes, that's the way it is in Xubuntu as well--it doesn't mute the speakers when you plug in a set of headphones.  (That's an advantage for me; I can just leave the headphones plugged in all the time and simply push the power button on my speakers.)  But Lubuntu works like Windows--it mutes the speakers when you plug in headphones.  I remember remarking on that back when I had 10.04 installed on my laptop, and I found there was a setting I could use to toggle that on and off, so I could get it to work either way if I wanted to.  I don't see that option in Xubuntu, so it may not be offered in regular Ubuntu either.  I could look to see if it's still in Lubuntu 11.04 if you like--right now I'm too tired to fire up the laptop.

### Post by Copper Bezel on 2011-08-08
That's a bug, not a feature, and almost certainly results from your audio device not being fully supported. Both of you, I mean. That shouldn't happen under any DE running on Ubuntu on any system. The switching is handled at device level. I've had errors with my audio before, specifically a day where the internal speakers stopped switching on, but it was a temporary problem.

I'm actually quite impressed with 11.04. The default kernel tried to kill my computer, but after getting that fixed, everything has worked extraordinarily smoothly. Gnome now automatically detects external monitors when connected instead of only detecting them when the Monitors dialog is launched, my Wifi connects absurdly quickly when I resume from sleep, I have an external optical drive that took a workaround to get running (in Windows or Ubuntu) but worked the moment I plugged it in after the upgrade, I get less video jitter and no audio hiccups and latency, and in general, all the little irritations I'd become used to have gone away.

I loved 10.10, and the new Compiz certainly has its quirks, but 11.04 really brought back the new-device smell.

---

### Post by Alwimo on 2011-08-09
> **nec207 said:**

If you log in under **Ubuntu Classic**, which is what I wrote, you would see that I am correct. You will find that it does not have the dock (launcher), does have the taskbar (panel with the window selectors) and does have the start menu (applications menu).
 
The option to change to Ubuntu Classic is on the bottom of the screen when inputting your password after turning the computer on, where there is a box that by default will say "Ubuntu".

### Post by rockney on 2011-09-02
Things I hate ... ok ... there are several in no particular order ...

This is oriented around Ubuntu 11.04 Natty on a box-on-the-floor quad-core system (that I make my living on).

 1. Geoclue is an invasion of my privacy. It serves no purpose for a desktop system and yet there are system services that depend on it.

 2. Zeitgeist is also an invasion of privacy and a total joke. All this unified "user experience" stuff is a bunch of B.S. that really does nothing useful, wanted, or relevant (unless you are in marketing).

 3. Unity - again, for a desktop system (with 54" of flat panels) it's totally worthless.  Worse - you're talking about dropping what is now called "Ubuntu Classic" at 11.10. If so you WILL LOSE many users, including me, my company, and all my customers.  GET A CLUE - most Linux users are NOT starry-eyed 20-somethings with the latest (useless) mobile i-whatever toy thingy.

 4. Overall I've never liked KDE ... particularly given the way it takes over everything if you ever install and run it even once. Talk about arrogant egos  All this talk about switching between desktops is a joke. But given the imminent death of a real desktop for Ubuntu thought I'd give it another try. KDE changes so many things I reformatted my HD to get rid of it.

 5. Rather non-technical ... the whole "get used to it" no-options attitude simply doesn't cut it. That's The Microsoft Way - which is exactly why I run Linux.

All the dependencies on these things - and given no installation options for whether you want this stuff - it seems Ubuntu has lost its way.

As a 35-year software engineer, 25 years on UNIX and 18 on Linux, my strong recommendations are:

 A. Branch Ubuntu and make a new fork for this mobile stuff separate from a solid desktop for people who actually work in Linux. The Unity user paradigm simply doesn't work for that use case. (( I would be VERY interested in seeing installation statistics. ))

 B. Give people options for things like Geoclue and Zeitgeist.  Not everyone wants or cares about trendy stuff like that.  And there are those of us who are seriously concerned about how those data might be used by the less scrupulous members of society and government.

 C. Remember your roots!  Linux has made huge inroads into classic IT and development shops. Don't make enemies of them.

Hope this doesn't fall on deaf ears.

---

### Post by seeker5528 on 2011-09-02
> **rockney said:**
> Things I hate ... ok ... there are several in no particular order ...

 2. Zeitgeist is also an invasion of privacy and a total joke. All this unified "user experience" stuff is a bunch of B.S. that really does nothing useful, wanted, or relevant (unless you are in marketing).

If by invasion of privacy you mean allows people *you* allow to log in to *your* user account or in some other way given access to your system *while logged into your user account* to see some history of activities displayed by things running *in your user account*, then I guess you would be correct.

But it looks like [help is on the way](http://www.omgubuntu.co.uk/2011/04/privacy-settings-are-coming-to-zeitgeist/).

In the mean time you could always create a user account for doing the stuff you *really* want to stay private and a separate one to use when other eyes are present, unless you have an OCD about dumping history of recent documents and other histories applications keep, that's probably the suggested route anyway.

Later, Seeker

---

### Post by rockney on 2011-09-02
I've read several pieces about Zeitgeist, such as "The current user interfaces for Zeitgeist do not give users a hint of the real power which lies dormant under the surface. Our current goal is to index as much information from as many different sources as fast as we can. Once that&#8217;s done, it&#8217;ll be easy to build exciting new user interfaces which pull information out of Zeitgeist&#8217;s database and display it to users in all sorts of innovative ways."

Meh - who cares! 

"Real power" - I just don't see the point. Seems like it might be used on a mobile thingy with severely limited screen space, maybe. 

"Index as much as information..." is just scary. What I have on my system is NOT to be indexed, shared, or even touched without my explicit permission. I'm a professional engineer and much of the information is confidential and proprietary.

"All sorts of innovative ways" - sounds like foo-foo yuppie marketing (Bronx cheer!).

As with GeoClue - this thing should be handled as a Optional Feature and NOT shoved down everyone's throats. Unscrupulous people, employers, government, wives, etc. could use this information against you. And when it's a "feature" that people do not want then there should be a way to disable or uninstall it without a hassle or breaking your system.

One fundamental question ... how can anyone be sure this information is not being sent or accessed off your computer without going through the code?  It seems with just a little effort all that data could be pulled out without your knowledge or consent.

---

### Post by rockney on 2011-09-02
And given Zeitgeist and Geoclue are built into the operating system, folks are not given any options, and removing them may break a system - I have to seriously question the motives and agenda of the whole idea.

---

### Post by Copper Bezel on 2011-09-02
It really isn't anything to be upset or worried about.

You can, as seeker pointed out, blacklist folders that might contain confidential information.

Zeitgeist is nothing more than the outgrowth of the old "recent documents" list with a touch of desktop indexing. It's only "forced" on you insofar as it's a dependency for several popular software packages. Event logging does, in fact, offer quite a few advantages; I'm quite dependent on Synapse myself, for instance, and it's heavily dependent on Zeitgeist.

And as a developer yourself, you know quite well that malicious logging behaviors could not possibly exist in a popular open-source software package for long. There's no reason to suspect Zeitgeist any more than any other random application. They all have access to the same things.

---

### Post by rockney on 2011-09-02
No argument with most of your reply. You're probably right about the Open Source aspect in particular.

However my statement stands - that information can be used against you.  I don't like or use Recent Documents either. Largely because, as a developer, I touch so many things that recent files lists aren't very useful except on rare occasions.

Event logging has few real uses beyond program or security problems and is a waste of time, disk space and CPU load. That information could also conceivably be used against you. Anything from criminal to civil, like divorce.

They ought to be using soft dependencies to allow folks to remove the packages if they wish. There's no particular reason to make them hard dependencies.

Another concern with Geoclue is the data sources. It's entirely likely they record and track requests - therefore being able to track where you are. That is no one's business and a direct violation of privacy.

A tool that would be especially useful for those of us concerned with privacy would be a configuration and cleaner tool for all the lists, databases, caches, etc. that are in use and be able to turn some or all of it OFF.

---

### Post by seeker5528 on 2011-09-02

> However my statement stands - that information can be used against you.  I don't like or use Recent Documents either. Largely because, as a developer, I touch so many things that recent files lists aren't very useful except on rare occasions.

Event logging has few real uses beyond program or security problems and is a waste of time, disk space and CPU load. That information could also conceivably be used against you. Anything from criminal to civil, like divorce.


In those cases, the forensic investigator investigating your system probably isn't going to need zeitgeist indexes to find your email, documents, porn, etc... and could probably find some evidence of places you have been on the net as well.

If you have warning or legitimate reasons for extra levels of paranoia there is always DBAN and Bleachbit.....

Looks like there is a PPA for the Activity Log Manager.....

Later, Seeker

### Post by rockney on 2011-09-02
Very cool - thanks!

I have found other ways to deal with Geoclue and Zeitgeist on my own - albeit brute force.  You need to be 'root' for these procedures.

For Geoclue --

Goto /usr/lib/geoclue and rename geoclue-master to something else, I used geoclue-master-DISABLED just in case it needs to be re-enabled at some point.

Doing this causes a problem for the "Indicator Applet Complete" where is won't display the calendar but that is easy to fix. Remove that applet from the panel by the right-click options menu. First unlock it, then remove it from the panel.

Then right-click on the panel and select "Add to panel". Add to your panel the Indicator Applet, Indicator Applet Session, and Clock.  Works great.


For Zeitgeist --

In your home directory change directories to .local/share. Then run: chmod -R 600 zeitgeist  and then run: chown -R root:root zeitgeist


When you reboot Geoclue won't start because the main program has been renamed. And Zeitgeist won't start because it can't access it's data directories.  So far I have not seen any problems.

Note that I do not know what might be changed during an update or upgrade of software. You may need to repeat these procedures if anything reverts back to its original state.

### Post by Copper Bezel on 2011-09-03
There were occasional applications that actually let you jump to a particular position on the scrollbar by clicking it, but most just incremented as if you'd hit the arrow button, anyway, so I never formed a habit of using that (because the behavior was unpredictable.) Partly because I'm more likely to use two-finger scroll in any case, I've found scrollbars to be unnecessary UI clutter (I've removed them in my browser and later the terminal since before overlay scrollbars became a thing.) Overlay scrollbars work for me for that reason - but if I'm honest, I still don't actually use them all that much.

[QUOTE=rockney]Event logging has few real uses beyond program or security problems and is a waste of time, disk space and CPU load.[/QUOTE]
I don't know, it's not that much of a resource drain, and once it grows up to be decently functional, the Activity Journal seems like a nice way to keep track of things, no different from a browser's history. I like that my dock and launcher get to know my habits, because it saves me some steps, particularly if I remember a bit of the content but not the filename of a recent file or something like that, and really, in exactly the way that you don't, I just like the fact that my machine keeps track of what it's been up to lately.

In fact, in general, it's just no different from features that are already commonplace in modern browsers, and I like having those sorts of tools available on the desktop.

I can see the point on soft dependencies, though for many apps, that might mean falling back on another less complete duplicate service if Zeitgeist isn't present. (Yuck.)

### Post by docbop on 2011-09-03

### Post by apollothethird on 2011-09-03
> **docbop said:**
> Doesn't have Install option for barebones installs.   I want to start using ubuntu at work for desktop users, but only want it to have just the app's they need for work.  I'd rather thing never be installed versus removing things and hoping all residue is gone. 

Back in the day Linux installers had options for server,  server with X, developer, and so on various installs that only puts on the necessary things for that task.  Lean and mean installs for a purpose, then just add the extra you need.

I'm sure you can find barebone Linux distros.  I believe Ubutu is for users who are not looking for barebones but full featured desktops that they can start using with minimum technical savvy.  While some appear to think that Linux should be reserved for Gurus and others who have lots of time on their hands for programming, developing, and constantly tweaking, it's good to have a choice of an alternate for people who just wants to turn on their computer and it works.

A lot of the things people are describing about hating concerning Ubuntu, they might find in the Ubuntu server edition.

But for now, I believe it's nice to have an OS option that can easily be shipped on a computer out of the box and a new computer user can start to use it.

### Post by beew on 2011-09-05
Putting in crippled or broken software in the repos.

For example, I have been trying xvidcap and found that it has no audio, after some search it turns out that for some reasons Ubuntu has disabled sound recording on it. The work around is to install an old .deb package from "jaunty" (that is way before my time with Ubuntu) Now why on earth would they do that?

It seems that Ubuntu's repo would need some quality control, not just for stability, but the actual functionality of the software instead of a single minded obsession with "stability". Who gives a damn about stability if the apps don't even work?!

---

### Post by Copper Bezel on 2011-09-05
I agree. It's infuriating that the Emerald decorator remained in the repos for Natty without being patched to work under the new Compiz, for instance, while a community PPA existed for the fixed version. I know they can't possibly test every package in the repositories, and that setting up such a system would doubtless exclude many valid packages for logistical reasons, but there has to be a middle path on that.

[QUOTE=mk4]"The scroll hasn't either. To some degree it's even more convenient." One convenience I can see is that it releases precious real estate on the screen. Definitely a great idea. Maybe they could have made scroll bar appear together with that thingy.[/QUOTE]
Scroll bars in Natty can be reverted - [see here]("http://www.tuxgarage.com/2011/04/disable-overlay-scrollbars-in-ubuntu.html"). I don't know about Oneiric, if you're doing the beta thing.

### Post by oscarivera9 on 2011-09-05
Hate is a strong word.  I don't necessarily 'hate' anything in Ubuntu, heavens NO!  Especially since it helped me out big time when my Windows XP install wouldn't start a while back, that's how I got turned on to Ubuntu as a matter of fact.  I have loved it ever since.  
However, there is one thing I don't like about Ubuntu 11.04 (prior to this release, everything was great!).  I don't like the new Unity desktop environment.  I am actually not affected by it, because I have been using Ubuntu Studio since Maverick Meerkat because I am a musician and I really like the package selection that Ubuntu Studio provides.          :guitar:
 However, the question was what I 'hate' about Ubuntu and the fact still remains that I don't like the Unity desktop environment.  At least not as much as good old Gnome.  KDE is also great but if I liked it that much I suppose I'd be using Kubuntu, right?

### Post by StoshByGosh on 2011-09-07
I'm am (was) a new user wannabe.  I hate everything about it.  Most of all I hate that it has wasted many hours of my life trying to get a usable operating system.  I hate that it let me install 10.06 (or whatever) on top of my Vista, slowed my computer down, and forced me to spend months (yes, I said months) to get rid of it.  Had to reformat my hardrive, reload everything, and it still seems to be causing some unknown interference.
 
I detest 11.04.  I have no normal destop, just wallpaper.  I detest the arrogance of the entire Ubuntu community.  I think the developers have lost touch with reality and are more concerned with the appearance of being techie experts than they are with providing usable software codes.  They are the epitome of "Specialists"; those people that learn more & more about less & less, until they know absolutely everything about nothing at all.
 
I hate the "help" that is totally worthless to "newbies".  I hate the rapid changes and updating of Ubuntu versions.  They make Microsoft shine in comparison.  I hate the boasting and touting found everywhere that tells me how great their programs are.  It is akin to getting bombarded with their flatulence before they deliver the real product.
 
I hate the forums.  They run so off track that it is like following rabbit trails.  The posters seem to mostly come from residents of mental institutions and the moderators are worthless.  Even this thread is filled with unbelievable spam, from people that should be sent packing.  This is a thread about what you hate for crying out loud!  If you love Ubuntu go find a thread about "What I love about Ubuntu".  Good luck on that one though.
 
I hate the commercialism, the lost focus of sharing with your fellow man, and the gimme, gimee requests for donations before you can even try out the (mostly garbage) products.

### Post by MarjaE on 2011-09-12
A bit frustrated right now ...

I can turn the touchpad off, but it won't stay off for long, and it will go haywire and mess with my typing once it turns on again.

I find it annoying the way that, if I try to move two windows out of each others' way, to compare both, it's all to easy to maximize one, not to mention the way that windows change size if I brush against the edge, but don't change size if I try to change them back. (And the new style scroll bars - before going back to the old - those were terrible!)

I find it annoying that LibreOffice files keep opening to 104% or 132% or some such, when I'd like to be able to set a default size like 100% or 125%. It's just a few extra clicks, but every time I open anything...

I hate the fact that if I'm typing I have to use both Character Map and KCharSelect to handle certain characters. Character Map has good language support, but it's only needed because keyboard preferences have terrible coverage, even for English, let alone the languages the keyboard preferences don't even include. KCharSelect covers symbols.

### Post by Jay Car on 2011-09-14
Ok, since this thread is down in the recurring discussions basement where few will bother to read it, and since I've been feeling unusually grumpy today, I shall make my first ever negative comment about Unity.

I don't hate Ubuntu, it's much too useful to me, but I'll admit that I hated the experiences I've had with Unity. 

Also I hate that, according to some folks here/there/and yonder, any dislike of Unity must mean we're just "afraid of change" or that we "haven't given it a fair chance" or (I'm paraphrasing here) "The new way of doing things in Unity is more convenient, and if it doesn't feel convenient to you, you must have been doing it wrong before"...

I installed Natty on my new computer in late June, it runs fine, but Unity only lasted around 2 months before I changed to the classic desktop. 

It had nothing to do with being afraid of change. After all, if all of us ex-Windows users were so bloomin' scared of changing, we wouldn't be HERE now. Right?

Not giving Unity a fair chance?  I don't know, maybe someone needs to define "fair chance", because I used it daily on my main work computer, making an honest effort to learn how to work with it, and still couldn't get past the frustrations.

So, maybe you'd assume that all my old work habits were poor and I'm just too old and too damned stubborn to learn "better ways", but that's simply not true.  Learning new things is fun and I've never shied away from changes. Change is the only constant, and the older I get, the clearer that becomes.

I think the ones who are "too stubborn to change" are those who've got such dazzling visions of future Unity glory in their heads that they can't see anything else. They're disappointed in those of us who just can't see the same dazzle.

I actually really like Natty with the classic desktop, once I learned how to remove the (very clever, but infinitely annoying) invisible sliders, Natty has turned out to be a fairly nice, stable, speedy system. 

And that's quite enough Unity bashing from me, it's all been said before by plenty of others, and so far it's not done anyone any good (except that it feels good to finally vent a little)...but if I ever get a chance to meet Mark Shuttleworth face-to-face, then I'll likely have a thing or two more to say (it will be the Granny Inquisition. Our chief weapon is fear. Fear and surprise). 

Jay Car (who rarely complains, but felt the urge to do so today. It probably won't happen again)

---

### Post by robert shearer on 2011-09-15
@ Jay Car.... well put, with you all the way.

If you like classic on Natty then I would suggest you try Oneiric beta and install the gnome-session-fallback package.

Then, when you reboot you can log-in to the classic desktop (from the ldm greeter) and experience gnome 3 performance with a very familiar interface.
(Oh, one thing to note is that customizing panels by right clicking as per gnome2 does not do anything, you have to press **alt** and right click.)

I have been using it for several days and find it mostly stable. Looking forward to the final release and installing it as my main work system.

Cheers, Bob.

### Post by Jay Car on 2011-09-16
> **cariboo907 said:**
> If Unity doesn't work for you, use what does, the other members of the Ubuntu family work quite well, although may have to do a little work to get the same functionality as Ubuntu. Keep in mind, this is a Linux distribution, you don't have to use the defaults.

cariboo907, I know what you are saying is mostly true, and I hope you know that I'm not usually a complainer. I'm generally pretty appreciative of the beautiful software that's available to all of us.

However, it took a long time for me to get comfortable and confident enough to use Ubuntu full time, for my work and everything else. It actually took several years to find the right combination of hardware and OS, where everything just worked, and Ubuntu was it. For a very long time every upgrade just seemed to get better and better.   

I had finally reached the point where I could stop getting used to the OS itself and concentrate on becoming more proficient with the programs I use for my work. I've never been afraid of trying or learning new things, and apply a dogged determination to most everything I do.

With Unity it all changed, it couldn't be customised to fit my work habits, my work habits had to change to fit Unity. 

If all I was doing was email and chat and swapping knitting patterns with my old auntie, a little change wouldn't bother me. But when I'm working, everything that gets in my way (and can't be moved) costs me time.

I'm well aware that it's my own problem to deal with. But yes, it's all made me rather cranky. (Hey, it happens. I'm only human.) 

And yes, I know I don't have to use the defaults, and I'm glad for that.

I also know that you spend a great deal of time helping people here, and you deserve gratitude for that. But tell me honestly, do you truly not see why so many people are unhappy with Unity?  

Jay Car

---

### Post by cariboo on 2011-09-18
@Jay Car, I probably don't realize how many problems people have adapting to Unity, I've been using Unity since it was pre-alpha at the start of the Maverick development cycle, and have developed the muscle memory to where using the old style 2 panel interface just doesn't feel right to me anymore.

The good thing about the continuing development of Unity/Gnome 3 is that as more people start using it and find itches that need scratching, tools become available to customize the interface. What we have today in Oneiric is a huge improvement over the Natty version.

I do know how you feel about having to change the way you work though. When Gnome 2 was first released, I hated the interface, and changed to KDE, I didn't try Gnome again until I started using Ubuntu in 2006, the changes that were made in the intervening 5 years were just amazing to me.

There is a bright spot on the horizon though, just as there is a fallback mode in Unity, Gnome-shell has one too, it is an updated version of the 2 panel interface, it will be available for download when Oneiric is released. The only drawback I've seen so far, aside from developers taking there time updating applets to work with GTK-3, is the name, some people have complained that they don't want to use an interface called gnome-session-fallback, as it makes them feel that it isn't as good as the main desktop environment.

Because most people are prone to complain about change.  The developers encouraged people to test the change, but provided a resource so that they didn't have to.  For some reason, that still doesn't stop the complaining.

I truly tested it starting with the release date.  After a month or so I tested Gnome again.  I find it harder to drop back from Unity that it was to move up to Unity.

I also understand the interface is in it's infancy with Ubuntu.  I expect improvement due to feedback from the users.  I'm patience for it, and again, enjoying the interim.

### Post by Copper Bezel on 2011-09-18

Don't oversimplify. Jay said outright that she doesn't like having to get used to a new interface, but that's far from the only reason someone might not like or use Unity. More importantly, for those like Jay who *do* feel that way, why do you feel that you have any right to be judgmental toward them for it?

### Post by varunendra on 2011-09-18
> **apollothethird said:**
> Because most people are prone to complain about change.[]("http://www.apollo3.com/%7Eljames") Not entirely correct. Particularly, if you are talking about Ubuntu users in general (not ubuntu 'testers' - who fail to figure out their way around, and fall back to win or mac), I completely disagree.

The idea of Unity may be a good one, but at its current state, it has its flaws which are worth complaining and are a big hurdle in making people switch to it (of course unless you force them to).

I have personally witnessed at least two such users (one of whom is a forum member, and the other one a friend at my workplace) who until a couple of months ago, didn't even seen someone using ubuntu or any linux distro. They never heard or imagined of anything like a "LiveOS" and couldn't believe there exist many with everything free! (long time MS victims of course, not even mac users). But when they got hands over 11.04, they liked everything except Unity (and I wasn't the one to 'bias' them or guide them). It didn't take long when they figured out the way to fall back to the classic interface and feel satisfied afterwards (ok, I did tell one of them that there is another one he can try, but didn't favor any).

So my point is - none of them was familiar to linux or any of its interfaces, so both classic gnome or unity were a 'change' for them. They preferred the 'classic' one. As for older users endorsing or rejecting unity, you can browse the forums to see the variety. Even among windows users, I've seen a 'craze' for the mac theme or mac-type docking panels on windows (like cairo-dock in ubuntu). Once they taste it, almost none of them falls back to the default 'Start Menu' in windows. Now that is a change!

It is not a bad idea to try introducing a better interface, but Unity definitely needs a lot of improvement before making it permanent.

Actually Unity is a big change.  Gnome resembles the other computers more.  Gnome resembles Windows more than Unity.  The people I introduce Linux to always look for the Windows way for doing things.  If there are options to resemble Windows and they can be shown those options, they would choose those options.  This is because many people don't like a lot of change.

### Post by apollothethird on 2011-09-18
> **Copper Bezel said:**
> Don't oversimplify. Jay said outright that she doesn't like having to get used to a new interface, but that's far from the only reason someone might not like or use Unity. More importantly, for those like Jay who *do* feel that way, why do you feel that you have any right to be judgmental toward them for it?

Change is an idea. Unity is a thing. Don't confuse the two. = )

For my part, I'd hate to "drop back" to Unity myself.

I understand that Jay already mentioned that she doesn't like having to get use to a new interface.  So, yes, Jay mentioned what I consider a most important component.  Then she asked the question.  I believe the answer is very simple and stated the answer.  If Jay was being rhetoric I'm sorry.  I didn't mean to be funny.  I was just stating my opinion based on my experience with many things.

I've been providing people with computers and tech support since the early 80's.  Almost all of my clients complained about every version of Microsoft upgrade.  However, they eventually became used to each version and would feel a lost if they had to drop back to a previous version.  I understand there are some that say they decided to skip Vista and go straight to Windows 7.  But to me, those that skipped Vista didn't give it a good enough chance.  Also, those were not among my customers.  I upgraded my customers computers and they immediately tried to do things the old way.  In each case after a month or so were very conformable with the upgrade.

Unity is about the most radical change I've seen in Linux.

As I mentioned, the developers did provide a fall back.  But again, Unity itself made the interface so different, it was almost like a differ OS.  It was almost like one day a person is using Windows and the next day they are using a Mac.  Many people would complain about the difference.  The difference would be too radical.  That goes the same if the change was immediately forced from Mac to Windows.

-- L. James

### Post by Phil Binner on 2011-09-18
What I hate about Ubuntu is that it's not seriously trying to replace Windows. This is a serious hate, and since many/most of you out there will disagree with me, I'm going to try to explain.

I want to have serious adult relationships with other people and organisations. My definition of adult relationships is ones where power is shared; when children have relationships with each other they try to grab all the power, but as we grow up we learn that we need to share in order to get the best out of anything. In the modern world many organisations try to grab all the power when they deal with their customers, and this often makes many of our necessary commercial ventures far more frustrating and unpleasant than they need to be. Consequently, many of us spend lots of time being angry, when we really don't want to be. One theory is that we have to be responsible for own anger, it's no-body else's fault but our own. That is true, but it's also true that if the buggers out there treated us like adult human beings, rather than trying to control us in every transaction, life would be a lot less aggravating and there would be a lot less anger out there.

Which brings me to the next thread of my argument. One of the major corporations trying to control the modern world, including all their customers, is Microsoft. By my definition a childish organisation, but a childish organisation with immense power. Almost everybody in the world owns a computer, and almost all computers run Windows. Microsoft use this domination to try to control your access to media, to generate extra income by distributing cut down versions of their software, by building in weaknesses which you have to upgrade to get out of, even by trying to control when you are able to replace your motherboard.

So far I suspect most of you are with me.  Where many will disagree is when I claim that Ubuntu is not seriously trying to replace Windows. Let me tell you about myself.

I am 64 years old and retired, and I have worked all my life in IT. I started as a PL1 programmer, but soon moved into systems analysis. I have been the MD of a small software company, and I have been project manager in a major software company responsible for multi million £ projects. Consequently, although I have never been what would be regarded as a tekki, I would claim to be reasonably computer literate. Nowhere near as computer literate as most of you guy's reading this (I hope someone is still reading), but more so than 95% of the people you pass on the street. 

And that is the point. I have been trying to get a Ubuntu system working the way I want it for about 3 years now, and I've only just about succeeded. This is not because Ubuntu couldn't do what I want, but because many of the solutions to make this happen rely on some level of technical understanding and technical language that I just don't have. And if I don't have it then you can bet the other 95% of ordinary people mentioned above don't have it either.  Most people just look at Ubuntu and turn off, because they think, quite correctly as it happens, that it's a private domain for tekki's to play in.

And it shouldn't be.  Ubuntu is a fantastic system, and it should be setting people free.  After a lot of effort it has done that for me. Most people will not put in the effort.

So finishing with a note for Ubuntu developers. 

First, make sure that Ubuntu does all the things that Windows does out of the box. I'm thinking of things like connecting to wifi, where Windows is completely reliable but Ubuntu is notoriously temperamental. There are more.

Second, get rid of the terminal. That is to say, don't get rid of it, but make sure that, as for Windows, whenever a beginner has a problem the solution can be achieved through the GUI. Save the terminal for the more technically oriented. It must be absolutely possible to set up and run Ubuntu without it, and if any of you want to say that this is already the case, then I would say try it.

Third, make sure there is better documentation on all the common issues, like connecting to wifi or sharing files over a network. Do this by including a set of simply written documents for all the common topics within the Ubuntu implementation. Make sure these do not contain any technical language.

Fourth (and I'm aware that this is contentious), move away from the six month turnaround time for new issues. Make it easier on us poor non-tekkies.

I hope none of you think I don't like Ubuntu because of this response. I love it. It won't really set us free until more of the third party suppliers out there have to take it into account, by writing driver software, and making all commercial software available, etc. This will not happen until the user base starts to challenge Apple and Microsoft.

So please, try to seriously replace Windows. That's a world I want to live in.

---

I want to have serious adult relationships with other people and organisations. My definition of adult relationships is ones where power is shared; when children have relationships with each other they try to grab all the power, but as we grow up we learn that we need to share in order to get the best out of anything. In the modern world many organisations try to grab all the power when they deal with their customers, and this often makes many of our necessary commercial ventures far more frustrating and unpleasant than they need to be. Consequently, many of us spend lots of time being angry, when we really don't want to be. One theory is that we have to be responsible for own anger, it's no-body else's fault but our own. That is true, but it's also true that if the buggers out there treated us like adult human beings, rather than trying to control us in every transaction, life would be a lot less aggravating and there would be a lot less anger out there.

Which brings me to the next thread of my argument. One of the major corporations trying to control the modern world, including all their customers, is Microsoft. By my definition a childish organisation, but a childish organisation with immense power. Almost everybody in the world owns a computer, and almost all computers run Windows. Microsoft use this domination to try to control your access to media, to generate extra income by distributing cut down versions of their software, by building in weaknesses which you have to upgrade to get out of, even by trying to control when you are able to replace your motherboard.

So far I suspect most of you are with me.  Where many will disagree is when I claim that Ubuntu is not seriously trying to replace Windows. Let me tell you about myself.

I am 64 years old and retired, and I have worked all my life in IT. I started as a PL1 programmer, but soon moved into systems analysis. I have been the MD of a small software company, and I have been project manager in a major software company responsible for multi million £ projects. Consequently, although I have never been what would be regarded as a tekki, I would claim to be reasonably computer literate. Nowhere near as computer literate as most of you guy's reading this (I hope someone is still reading), but more so than 95% of the people you pass on the street. 

And that is the point. I have been trying to get a Ubuntu system working the way I want it for about 3 years now, and I've only just about succeeded. This is not because Ubuntu couldn't do what I want, but because many of the solutions to make this happen rely on some level of technical understanding and technical language that I just don't have. And if I don't have it then you can bet the other 95% of ordinary people mentioned above don't have it either.  Most people just look at Ubuntu and turn off, because they think, quite correctly as it happens, that it's a private domain for tekki's to play in.

And it shouldn't be.  Ubuntu is a fantastic system, and it should be setting people free.  After a lot of effort it has done that for me. Most people will not put in the effort.

So finishing with a note for Ubuntu developers. 

First, make sure that Ubuntu does all the things that Windows does out of the box. I'm thinking of things like connecting to wifi, where Windows is completely reliable but Ubuntu is notoriously temperamental. There are more.

Second, get rid of the terminal. That is to say, don't get rid of it, but make sure that, as for Windows, whenever a beginner has a problem the solution can be achieved through the GUI. Save the terminal for the more technically oriented. It must be absolutely possible to set up and run Ubuntu without it, and if any of you want to say that this is already the case, then I would say try it.

Third, make sure there is better documentation on all the common issues, like connecting to wifi or sharing files over a network. Do this by including a set of simply written documents for all the common topics within the Ubuntu implementation. Make sure these do not contain any technical language.

Fourth (and I'm aware that this is contentious), move away from the six month turnaround time for new issues. Make it easier on us poor non-tekkies.

I hope none of you think I don't like Ubuntu because of this response. I love it. It won't really set us free until more of the third party suppliers out there have to take it into account, by writing driver software, and making all commercial software available, etc. This will not happen until the user base starts to challenge Apple and Microsoft.

So please, try to seriously replace Windows. That's a world I want to live in.

I find it incredible that it took you three years to get a grip on the system.  Everyone I'm familiar with it can do that in a few minutes.  As far as I can see, it actually works out of the box.

I have more problems with Windows wifi than Ubuntu.  Almost all the adapters I have works right out of the box with Ubuntu.  Almost none of the works without searching and finding the associated drivers for the Windows machines I work with.

As far as the terminal, I believe that's a problem with OS' like Windows and the MAC.  Everything is totally hidden in the GUI and when it comes to fixing something that's missing in the gui or just doesn't exist, you're just out of luck for a long time, or unless you get a support person to fix it for you.

With Linux, if something is broken since the terminal isn't hidden, a person can easily go into the terminal and fix it.  Sometimes its just as simple as "mv .config-example .config-example.old", then restart the program.  You can't use that fix while the gui program is running because the files in the ".config-example" area are inuse... locked.

As far as I can see Ubuntu is already where you say it needs to go to.  Right out of the box a new user can use it.  He can load Libre Office and write his documents.  He can print them.  He can click on the Mail Icon, fill out his credentials and sent the documents over the email.  He can browse the Internet.  He can play his music.  He can do whatever he wants to do.

I believe the problem isn't the OS, its the people.  Many people believe you get what you pay for.  Many people won't give Linux a fair chance because it's free.  They feel that since they pay $400.00 for their Windows OS (Windows 7 and Microsoft Office) they have something better.  Then there's all the advertising and marketing.  Advertising and marketing have a very strong effect on the population.  You can use it to convince people to plug billions of dollars into killing themselves with cancel.  Look at the cigarette industry.

Replace the amount of advertising Windows and the MAC with Ubuntu and you'd see a mass exodus over to Linux.

I believe the Linux industry is on the right tract.  With the economy, people will start to wakeup and catch on.  Also, I'm sure eventually the Windows and MAC industry will wake up also and become more economically reasonable.

-- L. James

-- 
L. D. James
[email]ljames@apollo3.com[/email]
[www.apollo3.com/~ljames](www.apollo3.com/~ljames)

---

### Post by Phil Binner on 2011-09-18
Hi

When I started with Ubuntu this time I couldn't connect to wifi. I still have windows machines which connect, but Ubuntu wouldn't. After a lot of help from this forum (which I don't think I could have got for windows) it was fixed by assigning fixed IP addresses in Ubuntu. Personally, I have never had this problem with a Windows machine, but reading the forums it's more than obvious that a huge number of people do with Ubuntu, hence my claim that Ubunbtu is temperamental in this respect. Without a Windows machine to connect to the Ubuntu forum this would have been a killer. 

The next thing I wanted to do was share files over the network. All the documentation I could find pointed me to using Samba, suggesting I download and configure it. I did this, and consequently screwed up what, it turned out, was already up for the task. I could have sorted that through the GUI, but the available documentation pointed me to a technical solution when it was not necessary. Sharing files is a common task. There should be a basic setup in the implementation and there should be good documentation assuming that most newbies will want it.

The next problem is with firewall and anti-virus. I have a sneaking suspicion that these will already be there, but the obvious documentation assumes that I already know this. Again, this is something everybody will want, make it obvious.

The other current problem is with setting up software to read my encrypted data store on my NAS. I use a common piece of freeware for this, and this is available in Linux.  I'm expected to install from a tarball, however. I would say "tarball, what's that", but I do actually have a good idea.  So far i have failed to get it working, however.  This may not be something that every computer user will want to do, but it's hardly uncommon.

Every one of these problems involved me delving into the system's innards. None of them did so for Windows.

So please, get real. I love Ubuntu, and I want everyone else to be able to love it too.

---

### Post by apollothethird on 2011-09-18
> **Phil Binner said:**
> Hi

When I started with Ubuntu this time I couldn't connect to wifi. I still have windows machines which connect, but Ubuntu wouldn't. After a lot of help from this forum (which I don't think I could have got for windows) it was fixed by assigning fixed IP addresses in Ubuntu. Personally, I have never had this problem with a Windows machine, but reading the forums it's more than obvious that a huge number of people do with Ubuntu, hence my claim that Ubunbtu is temperamental in this respect. Without a Windows machine to connect to the Ubuntu forum this would have been a killer. 

The next thing I wanted to do was share files over the network. All the documentation I could find pointed me to using Samba, suggesting I download and configure it. I did this, and consequently screwed up what, it turned out, was already up for the task. I could have sorted that through the GUI, but the available documentation pointed me to a technical solution when it was not necessary. Sharing files is a common task. There should be a basic setup in the implementation and there should be good documentation assuming that most newbies will want it.

The next problem is with firewall and anti-virus. I have a sneaking suspicion that these will already be there, but the obvious documentation assumes that I already know this. Again, this is something everybody will want, make it obvious.

The other current problem is with setting up software to read my encrypted data store on my NAS. I use a common piece of freeware for this, and this is available in Linux.  I'm expected to install from a tarball, however. I would say "tarball, what's that", but I do actually have a good idea.  So far i have failed to get it working, however.  This may not be something that every computer user will want to do, but it's hardly uncommon.

Every one of these problems involved me delving into the system's innards. None of them did so for Windows.

So please, get real. I love Ubuntu, and I want everyone else to be able to love it too.

Everything you mentioned is things I do for a living and get paid for doing it.  I do it for Windows.

This is even though all the hardware and drivers have been designed for Windows.  The market for Windows IT support people is still very high.

At present the hardware developers are not specifically designing their hardware for Linux, and yet I find it much easier to give support for.

I'm real.  As I mentioned most people will Rent the Windows OS for $400.00 every couple of years and barely complain.  Then you find those who grabs the free OS and could complain heavily for a device they are having problem with under Linux.  They won't go out and spend $50.00 to replace that hard to support device.  They will complain about about how hard it is to get it working, even though they eventually get it working with great support for the community.

I kept having problems with one of my Windows customers.  The last time I took his computer in, I converted in to Ubuntu.  When I went to deliver it I was a little alarmed that his Lexmark paperweight, I mean, printer didn't work.  I'm glad he didn't mind going out to spend a few bucks for a supported printer (as far as I know all the HP printers fit the bill) and haven't had a problem since.

I could have spent the 3 years you spent trying to make that Lexmark work.  But again, I believe I went the best way.  I purchased supported hardware and now don't have to spend money the next on the next Windows bi-annual upgrade.

If I were your IT person you certainly wouldn't not have had to spend 3 years downtime.  I would have suggested that you replace the non-supported hardware with a fraction of the money you would have spent staying current with Windows and have been all set within the first day or so.

When the masses wake up to this, the hardware manufacturers will start to build Linux standards or provide linux drivers for their hardware and everybody benefits.  But the consumer looses when they blame Linux for not easily piercing the secret for driving hardware built for Windows, and the manufacturer won't provide operation codes and specs to the Linux driver developers.

I'm just glad the Linux developers work so hard against those odds.  I also try to provide to the community where the problem lies.  Your description in this capacity appears to be a reason to love Ubuntu, not the opposite.


-- L. James
