### Post by varunendra on 2011-09-18
> **Phil Binner said:**
> What I hate about Ubuntu is that it's not  seriously trying to replace Windows. This is a serious hate, and since  many/most of you out there will disagree with me, I'm going to try to  explain. With all due respect sir, I would like to share my  opinion on some of your views, which is not meant to argue or disregard  your concerns.

> **Phil Binner said:**
> Most people just look at Ubuntu and turn  off, because they think, quite correctly as it happens, that it's a  private domain for tekki's to play in.
 ..at least with  Ubuntu, I think this is changing. In fact, I've seen quite some cases  where the real 'non-tekkies' are much more satisfied with Ubuntu than  windows once someone like me tuned up their system and turned off  unnecessary updates. This approach of mine was based on a popular saying  in BSD community that "If it ain't broken, don't fix it!". So once they  got it up and running the way they wished and with everything they  needed, they got happier with better speeds, freedom from viruses and  other win-specific headaches. I think this is almost same as what apple  is doing - spoon-feed everything, don't let them do it by themselves or  make a non-recommended choice.

On the other hand, it were those so called 'tekkies' who couldn't live without experimenting, screwed up, and gave up on it.

> **Phil Binner said:**
> And it shouldn't be.  Ubuntu is a fantastic  system, and it should be setting people free.  After a lot of effort it  has done that for me. Most people will not put in the effort. I  think this is something ubuntu is trying very hard to do. But if we take  the meaning of 'free' as it is interpreted in Linux community, along  with example of Mac os, then it is very difficult (if not impossible) to  make people both comfortable and free. What I mean is, if, like macs,  you want to give them a guaranteed stability and ease of use together,  then you'll have to impose strict restrictions so that they don't screw  up things and be able to do thing only the way you wish. This is radical  to our basic philosophy of freedom.

> **Phil Binner said:**
> 
First, make sure that Ubuntu does all the things that Windows does out  of the box. I'm thinking of things like connecting to wifi, where  Windows is completely reliable but Ubuntu is notoriously temperamental.  There are more. Not more, I see a lot more...., but I don't see  any reason to credit MS or Windows for those reliabilities.

Windows  does nothing, it simply can't. It is those applications and drivers  which are mostly developed by third parties - not by MS. As soon as they  get serious about creating linux version of all those apps/drivers,  we'll take over MS by a huge margin. So this way it seems like a  catch-22 situation (although it may not necessarily be) - we are not as  popular as we deserve because they don't create for us - thus we don't  have the popular ones so we are not popular. I don't think "our"  developers can do much about it. All we can do is to somehow widespread  it, increase its popularity so that those commercial companies and  hardware providers start taking us seriously, and I think the Ubuntu  developers and community are making honest efforts for that.

> **Phil Binner said:**
> Second, get rid of the terminal. That is to  say, don't get rid of it, but make sure that, as for Windows, whenever a  beginner has a problem the solution can be achieved through the GUI.  Save the terminal for the more technically oriented. It must be  absolutely possible to set up and run Ubuntu without it, and if any of  you want to say that this is already the case, then I would say try  it. From installation to using officially recommended software, I  think almost everything has a GUI to use it. This is also true for  configuration operations. For troubleshooting, I think this is the real  power of Linux that despite having so much variety in looks and feel, it  has a common way of doing things - via short and fast and capable  commands. The only thing where it seems odd is compiling and installing  external apps. But again, the reason for that lies in the fact that no  mature version of those apps exist and that's not "our developers'"  fault. That is definitely gonna change once we get enough focus.

> **Phil Binner said:**
> Third, make sure there is better  documentation on all the common issues, like connecting to wifi or  sharing files over a network. Do this by including a set of simply  written documents for all the common topics within the Ubuntu  implementation. Make sure these do not contain any technical  language. That is a good point. However, I do not favor creating  documentation with 'no technical language'. Much of our strength comes  from the fact that we get better understanding of those terms once we  get used to linux. But yes, a parallel, simpler version may be created  and distributed with distros and selected places on net.

> **Phil Binner said:**
> Fourth (and I'm aware that this is  contentious), move away from the six month turnaround time for new  issues. Make it easier on us poor non-tekkies. No comments on  this :)
I think this is related with the way open source works.

> **Phil Binner said:**
> I hope none of you think I don't like Ubuntu  because of this response. I love it. It won't really set us free until  more of the third party suppliers out there have to take it into  account, by writing driver software, and making all commercial software  available, etc. This will not happen until the user base starts to  challenge Apple and Microsoft.

So please, try to seriously replace Windows. That's a world I want to live in.
I definitely don't think you hate Ubuntu. You took so much time to share  your thoughts and advice, how can one think so? "The best friend is one  who tells you your faults, and still stands with you".

Also, I think you yourself came to the main reason of all our shortcomings - low user-base, less 3rd party suppliers.


That  was all from me. I also want the situation to change (and I believe it  will), but I do not want Ubuntu or Linux to change its fundamental ways.  If it does, probably it will become another Windows and then I'll have  to look for something else.

### Post by Phil Binner on 2011-09-18
Hi to all, particularly Apollothethird and Varunendra. I don't think we're too far apart. Appollothethird certainly has a point about me not wanting to spend money on supported hardware, even though I'm saving money on software. Most of my problems have come from the fact that I was mistrustful of the unknown, so I tried to experiment on the left over bits of computers after I'd upgraded the windows machines.

On the other hand, Apollothethird, you are an IT support guy.  Things that I (and the other 95% aforementioned) find impenetrable will seem obvious to you. You need to understand that they are not obvious to us. I suspect that many of your friends who find implementing Ubuntu easy also have your technical leanings. Reading the forums will bear out what I'm saying. And just because we don't understand doesn't mean we're stupid.

On the other hand, many people who would be considered to be not too bright manage to operate Windows.  Most of these would just as easily operate an Ubuntu system if they had someone to set it up for them. I suppose the question is, could they set up the Windows system.  I think that in the main they could, but I could be wrong there.

The one problem I always had through many years working in the industry in moderately senior positions, was language.  Every specialist branch of knowledge develops it's own arcane language set. It has to, because that language set is designed to support specialist concepts that don't exist outside. This is particularly true of IT where the concepts are very specialised. The difficult thing is recognising when you're doing it. Throughout my working life (at least after I reached senior analyst), I waged a continual campaign for good communication. Communication is the single most difficult thing that any of us has to do.  When I was MD of a small software company I always wrote the documentation myself, but then we were very small. Even as project manager for a very large company, I still managed to write some of the documentation and most of the bids I was responsible for.  It's a matter of putting yourself in the shoes of the guy who's trying to make sense of it, and trying not to use arcane language.

Varunendra, I think I agree with almost everything you say. The Ubuntu developers certainly can't force third party developers to work with them; this will only come through getting more of the market share.  A few basics might make this more likely to happen though.  Perhaps a few articles distributed with the distro to sit on the desktop for easy access. Topics covered would include, for me, anti virus/firewall, file sharing, setting up regular backups, and connecting to a network.

One other thing which could be dramatically improved would be a standard for installing third party software. The software centre is immensely better that it was a couple of years ago, and way beyond anything Windows has, but it only goes as far as it goes. Try logging onto Google and installing Google Chrome to see how it should be done for all the others. I'm sure developers who do support Linux, and there are many, could be persuaded to go the extra mile and provide that kind of implementation path.

I truly hope I am making friends rather than enemies here. Whatever my criticisms I do agree that things are improving, and I am very appreciative of the people who give their time to make this happen. In fact, if any official Ubuntu bods are reading this, then if a non tekky with a more macro than micro understanding could be useful then don't be shy.

Phil Binner

---

### Post by varunendra on 2011-09-18
> **azurehi said:**
> If Shuttleworth wants Ubuntu to truly compete with operating systems produced by Microsoft and Apple, why not include [COLOR=Red]**Everything necessary**[/COLOR] for Ubuntu to "just work"?  Canonical could CHARGE a nominal fee and compete.  A Modest Proposal...:confused: ?
This "everything" is the most dangerous thing when you consider "anything" available in the market - hardware or software :)

The 'everything' in mac (I'm repeating its example because it is both a unix derivative, and widely popular) is actually not 'everything' available freely. Its stability and ease of use depends upon its restrictions on hardware and software you can use.

For Win, that 'everything' comes from outside, MS doesn't create or maintain them.

In Linux, almost 'everything' is developed independently and made available freely. But unless the third parties join, this 'everything' proves to be very little thing since we don't put restrictions like macs.

Thing is - we don't want to be Win or Mac, we want to be what we are proud of - Linux!

Binner's idea of proper documentation and its effective presentation sounds good though. I remember I got scared of the interface of DSL when I first saw it. It was the first ever Linux distro I had seen or used. But then on top of its desktop - there was that html document open waiting to be read. As soon as I had a glance at it, everything seemed less alien and even friendly after a few minutes of reading.

I detest 11.04.  I have no normal destop, just wallpaper.  I detest the arrogance of the entire Ubuntu community.  I think the developers have lost touch with reality and are more concerned with the appearance of being techie experts than they are with providing usable software codes. ### Post by Copper Bezel on 2011-09-18
The new scrollbars are meant to save screen space and reduce clutter, and they're still quite functional if you use them, but the old scrollbars are far too much a presence in the UI when almost everyone has some kind of scrolling feature in the mouse or trackpad. However, you can do a quick Google search for "remove overlay scrollbars" to revert them.

Incidentally, Unity is not written for tablets. It's rubbish as a touch interface in its present form. The aesthetic is tablet-like, but the functionality is entirely centered on the desktop.

---

### Post by apollothethird on 2011-09-18
> **Phil Binner said:**
> 
…
 
On the other hand, Apollothethird, you are an IT support guy. Things that I (and the other 95% aforementioned) find impenetrable will seem obvious to you. You need to understand that they are not obvious to us. I suspect that many of your friends who find implementing Ubuntu easy also have your technical leanings. Reading the forums will bear out what I'm saying. And just because we don't understand doesn't mean we're stupid.

You’re right that my experience in giving support will tend to make adapting and understanding the various platforms easier.  But it also has given me experience with what causes problem problems and what is needed to resolve those problems.  In my professional opinion Ubuntu goes that direction better than Windows.

If I have 20 people who had never used a computer before and had to start them off.  If I started out 10 of them with Windows and 10 of them with Ubuntu, it would be easier with the 10 I started out with Ubuntu.  It’d be much easier to support also.

Of course if all twenty had started out with Windows, I’d have to take a minute to get the Windows users to consider something different.  I don’t always try that.  But I do start out completely new computer users with what I consider easier and better, Ubuntu.

>  
Varunendra, I think I agree with almost everything you say. The Ubuntu developers certainly can't force third party developers to work with them; this will only come through getting more of the market share. A few basics might make this more likely to happen though. Perhaps a few articles distributed with the distro to sit on the desktop for easy access. Topics covered would include, for me, anti virus/firewall, file sharing, setting up regular backups, and connecting to a network.
Phil, I don’t mean to argue.  I’m sharing this information with goodwill.  I believe most of the things you look for is already done.

If you’re running Ubuntu 11.04 with Unity, type “Help” in the program launch search field.  Click on the Help lifesaver.  I believe most of what you are looking for is there.  You might not see an anti virus/firewall because it’s not as immediately a thread as it is with Windows.  But support, installation, and configuration is easily available.  I find it substantially easier than Windows.

Then there is: [http://help.ubuntu.com](http://help.ubuntu.com) .  This is the official Ubuntu documentation.  It’s very well maintained and very much up-to-date.  You have a search field at the top of the page and each page where you can search the documentation.  You might consider testing, typing firewall or antivirus in that field.

That particular link might not come already on the desktop, but if a person asks for the documentation, someone on this forum almost always points to it.

I really believe this is the provision of what you’re thought was missing.  I want to remind you that I’m sharing information.  I’m not trying to argue or change your mind about any complaints or dislikes you have about Ubuntu.  But there is a chance you might have missed some of this availability.

> 
One other thing which could be dramatically improved would be a standard for installing third party software. The software centre is immensely better that it was a couple of years ago, and way beyond anything Windows has, but it only goes as far as it goes.
I can’t identify with the problems you have installing software.  All the software I’ve attempted to install, even “third party” software has been a matter of a few clicks… just as easy as I have found it on any other OS.  But of course, I don’t know what software you’ve been looking to install.  If it isn’t made for the OS then I can imagine that you might have some problems trying to get it working with the OS.  A company like MAC solves this by preventing the installation of software that isn’t made for it.  They also prevent their OS from being installed on non expensive (overpriced) proprietary hardware.  Microsoft tries to do this to some degree, but is substantially more flexible than the Mac people.

> 
Try logging onto Google and installing Google Chrome to see how it should be done for all the others. 
Which version of Ubuntu are you running?  I don’t use and don’t like Google Chrome because it doesn’t allow the user to disable tabs.  I can’t tolerate tabs… but as far as the installation, I wanted to check out chrome and simply typed chrome in Unity’s launch/search field and click on install… a few minute later it was fully installed.  I can’t imagine it being easier.

> 
I truly hope I am making friends rather than enemies here. Whatever my criticisms I do agree that things are improving, and I am very appreciative of the people who give their time to make this happen. In fact, if any official Ubuntu bods are reading this, then if a non tekky with a more macro than micro understanding could be useful then don't be shy.

Phil BinnerYou’re making friends!  at least you are in me.  I hope I’m doing the same.  I believe you missed some obvious features of Ubuntu/Unity which can make your life with the OS extremely easy.

-- L. James

### Post by apollothethird on 2011-09-18

Hi, Liketoscroll.  At one time I shared your annoyance with the modified scroll feature.  However, after using the current feature for a while I began to like the functionality.  I don't find anything missing.  I find it more convenient.

It took me a while to notice that page up and page down feature that I had always used in the past.  I never liked grabbing the scroll bar and sliding.  I would read the visible text then click on the top or bottom of the scroll bar (in the slide) to page up or down.  With the new "widget" you have the slide feature or the page up and page down just by clicking on the top or bottom of the "widget".  This is convenient because you don't have to move the mouse to search for a blank spot in the scroll slide to click as before.

### Post by Jay Car on 2011-09-18
> **cariboo907 said:**
> @Jay Car, I probably don't realize how many problems people have adapting to Unity, I've been using Unity since it was pre-alpha at the start of the Maverick development cycle, and have developed the muscle memory to where using the old style 2 panel interface just doesn't feel right to me anymore.

The good thing about the continuing development of Unity/Gnome 3 is that as more people start using it and find itches that need scratching, tools become available to customize the interface. What we have today in Oneiric is a huge improvement over the Natty version.

I do know how you feel about having to change the way you work though. When Gnome 2 was first released, I hated the interface, and changed to KDE, I didn't try Gnome again until I started using Ubuntu in 2006, the changes that were made in the intervening 5 years were just amazing to me.

There is a bright spot on the horizon though, just as there is a fallback mode in Unity, Gnome-shell has one too, it is an updated version of the 2 panel interface, it will be available for download when Oneiric is released. The only drawback I've seen so far, aside from developers taking there time updating applets to work with GTK-3, is the name, some people have complained that they don't want to use an interface called gnome-session-fallback, as it makes them feel that it isn't as good as the main desktop environment.

cariboo907, thank you for your thoughtful answer. There are a lot of great people on Ubuntu Forums, but there are a few who stand out from the crowd and you are one of them. 

I'll probably give Unity another try in a few months. Not sure if I'll ever really like it, I've never been fond of having things I can't change if they don't work for me.

One of the things that gives me hope for Gnome 3 is that people are creating extensions for it, so it can be made to fit the user's needs. I guess we'll have to wait and see if that happens.

For now, I'm getting along just fine with Natty Classic. 

Thanks again,
:)

### Post by peyre on 2011-09-19
> **apollothethird said:**
> I have more problems with Windows wifi than Ubuntu.  Almost all the adapters I have works right out of the box with Ubuntu.  Almost none of the works without searching and finding the associated drivers for the Windows machines I work with.

I think the issue is: if you're running Windows, you have to go find the d*mn driver and download it, then double-click on setup.exe, jump through the hoops, and you're up and running.  Sure it's a hassle.  By contrast, when Ubuntu has support for a WiFi card, it Just Works.  You don't get that in Windows too often.  OTOH, if Ubuntu doesn't have support for that card, you're in trouble.  You might be able to download Linux divers from the manufacturer, if you're very lucky, or you might be able to get the Windows drivers running using ndiswrapper--but that's way beyond the sophistication of a nontechnical user, which leaves many of them with a "that's not supported in Linux!" response from whatever forum they've gone to looking for help.  (Not a knock on Linux forums--it's all volunteer work, and it's unreasonable to expect volunteers to go to unreasonable lengths {unless you're Habitat for Humanity, apparently, but I digress}.)

> **apollothethird said:**
> As far as the terminal, I believe that's a problem with OS' like Windows and the MAC.

Yes I admit this is a nitpicky thing, but still, letters etc. have meaning: apostrophes aren't used for plurals!  The plural of OS is OSs or OSes, not OS'.  Just like I chafe when I see someone write Linux' instead of Linux's.  Not cool.

> **apollothethird said:**
> With Linux, if something is broken since the terminal isn't hidden, a person can easily go into the terminal and fix it.  Sometimes its just as simple as "mv .config-example .config-example.old", then restart the program.

This is a good example.  Why should someone have to type "mv .config-example .config-example.old" (making sure to get the syntax right!), when they never had to do such nonsense in their old OS?  (I realize that stuff isn't really nonsense itself--but it is nonsense that it should be *required* in a new, modern operating system.)

> **apollothethird said:**
> As far as I can see Ubuntu is already where you say it needs to go to.  Right out of the box a new user can use it.  He can load Libre Office and write his documents.  He can print them.  He can click on the Mail Icon, fill out his credentials and sent the documents over the email.  He can browse the Internet.  He can play his music.  He can do whatever he wants to do.

This is good stuff.  It's one of the areas where Ubuntu has done very well, and I would offer it a handshake in this area.

> **apollothethird said:**
> I believe the problem isn't the OS, its the people.  Many people believe you get what you pay for.  Many people won't give Linux a fair chance because it's free.

I'm not sure that's quite the problem.  It's part of the problem with Linux adoption in the business sector, but for consumers, I think the problem is: 1) Windows penetration (everyone knows/expects Windows), and 2) OEM penetration (MS has worked out this sweetheart deal with OEMs: you get a copy of Windows with the purchase of a new computer. That license is limited to that physical box and you can't upgrade for free, but no one notices that at purchase, and most people don't notice at all, since they buy a new computer when they want a new version of Windows. I think that if it weren't for the OEM deal, Windows would have a lot more trouble holding onto its spot as King of the Mountain.)

---

> [COLOR=DarkRed]if you're running Windows, you have to go find the d*mn driver and  download it, then double-click on setup.exe, jump through the hoops, and  you're up and running.  Sure it's a hassle.  By contrast, when Ubuntu  has support for a WiFi card, it Just Works.  You don't get that in  Windows too often.[COLOR=Blue] [/COLOR][COLOR=Blue] [/COLOR][/COLOR][COLOR=Blue]OTOH, if Ubuntu doesn't have support for that card, you're in trouble.[/COLOR]  You might be able to download Linux divers from the manufacturer, if you're very lucky, or you might be able to get the Windows drivers running using ndiswrapper--but that's way beyond the sophistication of a nontechnical user, which leaves many of them with a "that's not supported in Linux!" response from whatever forum they've gone to looking for help.That's a very small price you pay for the variety you have in Linux, of-course, unless you consider that a drawback too! Besides, many of those software providers who are serious about linux, separately provide their software in suitable packages which you can just double-click to install. As for drivers, once it gets tested and verified, it is either embedded in the kernel or made available via "Additional Drivers" option. In your own statement above, the first part is what Linux does, while the latter part is what the providers should have taken care of but they didn't. _So it takes time to get fixed by Linux developers themselves, but ultimately it does get done_. Hence it is always a 'temporary' (w.r.t a particular piece of hardware) issue of slower development which we can't blame linux or its developers for. As pointed out thousands of times before, once we get focus, anything 'not' working out-of-box or with a simple update should become a rare thing.

> **peyre said:**
> The plural of OS is OSs or OSes, not OS'.  Just like I chafe when I see someone write Linux' instead of Linux's.  Not cool.come on buddy, this is a technical support forum, not a literature class! As long as you can understand, who cares!

> **peyre said:**
> Why should someone have to type "mv .config-example .config-example.old" (making sure to get the syntax right!), when they never had to do such nonsense in their old OS?  (I realize that stuff isn't really nonsense itself--but it is nonsense that it should be *required* in a new, modern operating system.)So how do you do it in Win/mac? Copy>Paste>Rename? Or ctrl+drag?? Go try that in your ubuntu desktop and report back if it doesn't allow you to do so. I hope you understand you are not allowed to do so everywhere unless you have root privileges. Now don't say that's bad!


> **peyre said:**
> 1) Windows penetration (**everyone knows/[COLOR=Red]expects [/COLOR]Windows**), and 2) [COLOR=Red]**OEM penetration **[/COLOR](MS has worked out this sweetheart deal with OEMs..There you are! I am both amused and annoyed to see that most of us know and understand these issues, and yet, in the next split of second, blame it all on Linux for things not working out in an "easy way".

I don't know/understand the facts and trends good enough to predict whether or not Linux is ever going to dominate the Desktop market. But I do know that there are solid reasons why I prefer Linux over Windows, and it will be a huge disappointment if those reasons are lost in blind attempts of domination.

### Post by Phil Binner on 2011-09-19
Quote from Varun

"I don't know/understand the facts and trends good enough to predict whether or not Linux is ever going to dominate the Desktop market. But I do know that there are solid reasons why I prefer Linux over Windows, and it will be a huge disappointment if those reasons are lost in blind attempts of domination."

This is where I came into this thread. I don't have anything like such great preferences between Windows and Ubuntu as some of you. I like the speed of Ubuntu, but I also like the way that when I download software from a website no-one ever gives me a tarball.

What I really like, passionately, about Ubuntu is freedom, and it provides that by getting me out from under the control of a vicious, greedy child - to whit Microsoft.  What I don't like about Ubuntu is that it has frequent glitches, which are normally sorted out on this forum, but which are largely caused by the various bits of hardware and software we use not having proper Ubuntu support from the manufacturers. And the only way to address this is by having, if not domination, then at least a far greater market share than at present.  As you said earlier, Varun, this is almost but not quite a catch 22.  I believe Ubuntu can go some way towards this by having the guys who manage the developers spending much more effort trying to get into the heads of the ordinary Users, and particularly new users, rather than by creating more fancy bells and whistles. Anyway, I said all that before.

By the way, can anyone tell me how to do that quote thing.

Phil Binner

### Post by liketoscroll on 2011-09-19
> **apollothethird said:**
> Hi, Liketoscroll.This is convenient because you don't have to move the mouse to search for a blank spot in the scroll slide to click as before.


I'm glad you like it. I don't like it, it makes it difficult for me to scroll. There isn't any significant adjustment period needed because its not a complicated change, I just can't scroll without several attempts and some labor. Its not fatal, there are other desktops available, and even for now there is the ability to remove it, but I'm pretty sure that won't last. Its just a very comically bad idea, but pretty typical for Gnome.

Gnome (and now Ubuntu) have been trending toward change for changes sake, I think because there is now Mac envy and some perceived need for "redesign" without regard for the userbase. Part of this is because Ubuntu does not hurt from a drop in userbase numbers due to the volunteer nature of the project, lower numbers of users actually helps Ubuntu, and so it becomes to a lesser degree about the users, and more the "designers" resume. Whether the resulting designs work or not just has a lesser impact on any perceived metric of success due to the nature of volunteer software organizations.

The positive to take here is that there are choices, as has been posted  by Gnome / Unity refugees all over the web, there are plenty of desktops  and windowing environments out there, and it would also help Ubuntu to lower the userbase numbers some.

### Post by varunendra on 2011-09-19
> **Phil Binner said:**
> Excuse the jollity, but I've just been informed that it's international talk like a pirate day.

One thing more though Varun, where did you get the number to link back to the original post.
Every post has a 'Quote' button at its bottom-right corner. Click on the one that you want to quote. It will automatically include everything you saw in my example. To quote multiple posts, use the 'Multi-Quote' button beside it, and 'Quote' button at the last post to quote.

By the way, that number is the serial no of the post you are quoting which you can also see in browser's status bar if you hover the pointer over the post count # (at the top right corner of a post).

While doing these things or simply browsing the forums, just keep an eye on the address bar. You'll learn a lot of tricks like:

[LIST]
[*]Linking to a single post ('showpost=')([example]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=11265256&postcount=2033"))
[*]Linking to a post so the whole page appears, but opens at that post ('showthread=')([example]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?p=11265256&postcount=2033"))
[/LIST]

### Post by MarjaE on 2011-09-19
> **Copper Bezel said:**
> The new scrollbars are meant to save screen space and reduce clutter, and they're still quite functional if you use them, but the old scrollbars are far too much a presence in the UI when almost everyone has some kind of scrolling feature in the mouse or trackpad. However, you can do a quick Google search for "remove overlay scrollbars" to revert them.

Incidentally, Unity is not written for tablets. It's rubbish as a touch interface in its present form. The aesthetic is tablet-like, but the functionality is entirely centered on the desktop.

First of all this machine came with an Alps touchpad or haywire device. So it has no scrolling functionality, and it reads pressured variations as button1 clicks. And the damn thing keeps turning back on.

Second, even with an external mouse, it's easier to have clearly-visible spaces for scrolling than mostly-hidden ones. I find the mostly hidden ones popped up when I was trying to click the window behind, and disappeared when I was trying to scroll. It made the areas when the scroll-bar MIGHT pop up just as unusable.

### Post by apollothethird on 2011-09-19
> **peyre said:**
> This is a good example.  Why should someone have to type "mv .config-example .config-example.old" (making sure to get the syntax right!), when they never had to do such nonsense in their old OS?  (I realize that stuff isn't really nonsense itself--but it is nonsense that it should be *required* in a new, modern operating system.)


I don't consider it nonsense for a new user or anybody.  A person doesn't have to type "mv config-example".  They can browse around with GUI tools such as nautilus (such as what you have to do with Windows and the MAC).

You see, what's good about Linux, is that you don't have to, but you can!

Since you can, its very easy to write scripts to do those things.  Since you can it makes the OS very easy for an IT person to support it.  I really hate trying to describe to a user what an Icon is, the desktop is, where to find "My Computer", click on Next... click on Next (I want him to do it twice, but can't tell how many times he's done it).

For me since a person can do things from the terminal, I can easily just send a script (with a list of commands such mv config-example) and have it done with one click.

I hope Linux will never hide the terminal like the other OS' do.

### Post by liketoscroll on 2011-09-20
> **MarjaE said:**
> First of all this machine came with an Alps touchpad or haywire device. So it has no scrolling functionality, and it reads pressured variations as button1 clicks. And the damn thing keeps turning back on.

Second, even with an external mouse, it's easier to have clearly-visible spaces for scrolling than mostly-hidden ones. I find the mostly hidden ones popped up when I was trying to click the window behind, and disappeared when I was trying to scroll. It made the areas when the scroll-bar MIGHT pop up just as unusable.

I'm actually putting the new scroll controls through their paces, and they're worse than I initially thought. The mouse targets are clearly much smaller and more labor intensive, and if I pause my mouse on the scroll widget (a common activity when browsing heavily list oriented applications), it disappears and I end up bringing an entirely different window to the foreground.

This new design is not subjectively worse as a matter of taste, its actually objectively worse by concrete experiences that we as the userbase should be relaying to the designers. Given the really bad job in this particular instance, it would be a disservice to future Ubuntu users not to highlight design mistakes. Sure I could revert, but instead I can work to help the Ubuntu designers realize their mistakes. 

Obviously alternative mechanisms that need NO scrollbar activity should be outside the scope of evaluation of the new broken scroll controls. If there were no scrollbars needed, then even the little malfunctioning one wouldn't have been included. Clearly people still scroll with a mouse cursor, and its best that the designers go back to the drawing board on this one.

---

### Post by peyre on 2011-09-20
Varunendra, I must have come across wrong.  I'm actually very fond of Ubuntu--I've switched over to it on both my main home system and my personal laptop.  Any OS has room for improvement, though, and I was trying to point out some inconveniences on the Linux side vs. the Windows experience.

> **varunendra said:**
> That's a very small price you pay for the variety you have in Linux, of-course... _So it takes time to get fixed by Linux developers themselves, but ultimately it does get done_...

I've pointed out myself that driver issues in Linux aren't the fault of the OS, but of the manufacturers not providing driver support.  Linux (esp. distros like Ubuntu) actually does a remarkable job of providing support for a wide and unpredictable variety of hardware.  But still, though it's not the fault of Linux, the issue of drivers does affect Linux users.

> **varunendra said:**
> So how do you do it in Win/mac? Copy>Paste>Rename? Or ctrl+drag?? Go try that in your ubuntu desktop and report back if it doesn't allow you to do so. I hope you understand you are not allowed to do so everywhere unless you have root privileges. Now don't say that's bad!

I'm very much aware of the root-privilege dimension.  What I was getting at here is that installation and configuration in Linux has a much greater tendency to be overly technical, which can make an unpleasant experience for a new person who's been told how wonderful and easy Linux is to use.  I'm not really thinking of this simple rename command specifically, but: instructions for installation/configuration in Linux often get very complex and technical, and difficult to follow if you're not a fairly advanced user.  

As a better example, ever download and install a video driver from nVidia?  It's not pretty: it starts with killing X Window, then navigating to the download location, entering a command to run the shell script, then (usually) going back and rereading the readme file to figure out why the installation failed, then making adjustments and trying again, sometimes to see it still not install properly.  When you finally have it finished, you restart X or reboot, and if you're moderately lucky, it actually installed properly and you can move on.  This is a far cry from double-clicking on Setup.exe and clicking Next a couple times, followed by a reboot that you can be confident the computer will come back from with the driver properly installed.

> **varunendra said:**
> I don't know/understand the facts and trends good enough to predict whether or not Linux is ever going to dominate the Desktop market. But I do know that there are solid reasons why I prefer Linux over Windows, and it will be a huge disappointment if those reasons are lost in blind attempts of domination.

I'm right there with you.  I just try to bear in mind that there are inconveniences to the Linux experience that new users will find frustrating, and want to keep people's expectations realistic that switching to Linux does require some adjustments and shouldn't be undertaken lightly, and that the OS does have some drawbacks, as well as substantial advantages.

---

### Post by liketoscroll on 2011-09-21
> **varunendra said:**
> I don't know/understand the facts and trends good enough to predict whether or not Linux is ever going to dominate the Desktop market. But I do know that there are solid reasons why I prefer Linux over Windows, and it will be a huge disappointment if those reasons are lost in blind attempts of domination.

I do! Linux's best chance at competitiveness looks to be with Android and only if one of those devices morph into a laptop would Linux make any significant inroads anywhere close to desktop style computing. Ubuntu was really the best shot given its previous successes, and the recent Gnome 3 and Unity problems will probably kill Ubuntu's shot there. Other Ubuntu derivatives lack the backing of the main Ubuntu site and support system. 

People really have associated their OS purchase with the desktop purchase, and even when Linux worked this way for the brief but interesting period of Lindows / Linspire, it didn't do that well. Dell tried it, and people were clearly confused why their installation of Windows was different from their friends.

And even now people are forecasting the end of the desktop computing era. Possibly if the desktop computer declines significantly, the remaining holdouts might run an increasing share of Linux installations. Thats probably the only way Linux can significantly increase their share of desktop installations.

### Post by varunendra on 2011-09-21
> **liketoscroll said:**
> I do! Linux's best chance at competitiveness looks to be with Android and only if one of those devices morph into a laptop would Linux make any significant inroads anywhere close to desktop style computing. Ubuntu was really the best shot given its previous successes, and the recent Gnome 3 and Unity problems will probably kill Ubuntu's shot there. Other Ubuntu derivatives lack the backing of the main Ubuntu site and support system. 

People really have associated their OS purchase with the desktop purchase, and even when Linux worked this way for the brief but interesting period of Lindows / Linspire, it didn't do that well. Dell tried it, and people were clearly confused why their installation of Windows was different from their friends.

And even now people are forecasting the end of the desktop computing era. Possibly if the desktop computer declines significantly, the remaining holdouts might run an increasing share of Linux installations. Thats probably the only way Linux can significantly increase their share of desktop installations.

Buddy, I'd say you are over reacting :)
Perhaps you have studied a lot about recent trends (while I haven't), but let me very humbly point out this :-
"None of Unity, Gnome or even Ubuntu itself represent **Linux** in general! Other Linux flavours were there before Ubuntu, and they are still there after Ubuntu and are developing."

Ubuntu has grown faster doesn't mean others are dying :). Certain distros are preferred for certain tasks. Besides, if Ubuntu or its derivative now seem everywhere in those areas, I don't see it as a fallback anyway! On the contrary, I see it as a growth.

Oh, but you were saying about Unity and Gnome3. I remember the time when even [KiwiNZ]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1470633") criticized 10.04 too bitterly when it first came out. But we all know it is now the most compatible version of Ubuntu. No software comes out totally mature. In fact as per theory, no software can ever become 100% perfect! Give it time, and maybe it'll become your most favourite on a later date. Your doubts about Linux or Ubuntu's future are based on fresh experiments, not a mature trend!

### Post by Phil Binner on 2011-09-21
> **apollothethird said:**
> I don't consider it nonsense for a new user or anybody.  A person doesn't have to type "mv config-example".  They can browse around with GUI tools such as nautilus (such as what you have to do with Windows and the MAC).

You see, what's good about Linux, is that you don't have to, but you can!

Since you can, its very easy to write scripts to do those things.  Since you can it makes the OS very easy for an IT person to support it.  I really hate trying to describe to a user what an Icon is, the desktop is, where to find "My Computer", click on Next... click on Next (I want him to do it twice, but can't tell how many times he's done it).

For me since a person can do things from the terminal, I can easily just send a script (with a list of commands such mv config-example) and have it done with one click.

> **peyre said:**
> What I was getting at here is that installation and configuration in Linux has a much greater tendency to be overly technical, which can make an unpleasant experience for a new person who's been told how wonderful and easy Linux is to use.

We are more or less following three trends in this post, things that dont work how we like them; things that don't work; and the experience of new Ubuntu users. From my point of view, "things that don't work how we like them" is a red herring. Developers change things; they presumably get paid to do this (???) but it's not you and me that pay them. Usually I like the changes they make, and sometimes I may not.  At least in Ubuntu I have a voice, even if that voice does not always carry the day. I don't have a voice for either Mac or Windows.

I see "things that don't work" and "the experience of new users" as being critically linked, in that Ubuntu needs the kind of support Windows gets from third party suppliers if it's ever going to be remotely close to error free on the diverse platform it supports. The user base has to be expanded.

Apollothethird has convinced me that much of what I need is already there, but I still believe he's missing the point in not recognising that new users are put off by the overly technical nature of Ubuntu. I will not campaign for removal of the terminal, it's obviously an immensely valuable tool for the experienced user or the IT support guy's, but it has to be easy for a new user to configure a new system, in all the obvious ways, without it. 

First wifi. At the moment, if a new user puts his disk in an empty computer, he usually gets a simple installation.  The instructions for connecting to wifi during that installation are minimal, though, and if there is any problem with the connection for that or any other reason, without another computer to gain access to support he's dead in the water at that point. If he does have this he will get directed straight to the terminal (which is not at all obvious in Unity until you've found it in apps and added it to the launch bar) for diagnostics, and at this point he'll more than probably put the XP disk back in.  So, _FIRST THING_, put a document on the desktop explaining how to connect to the internet. Include in that document how to use a fixed IP address, because Ubuntu is much more fragile than Windows when it comes to connecting to wifi with a server assigned IP adddress. For my network none of the 4 Ubuntu machines have been able to do it, as android phones and iPones can't either, but Windows has no problem. I don't believe my network is unique. Incidentally, when I was directed to documentation for fixed IP addresses, the standard Ubuntu documentation, with screen shots, turned out to be wrong. It had me filling in a field that had to be auto filled.

Second, file sharing. There is a GUI to publish files from Ubuntu over a network. It is very simple to use, though more complex routines are difficult. It is not at all obvious, however. When I looked at the documentation it had me go into a terminal to download Samba with sudo apt get, and edit the config file in a terminal with sudo gedit. The upshot was that I messed up Samba, which as it turned out was already configured on my machine. So _SECOND THING_, include a file on the desktop which explains in simple terms how to configure filesharing and workgroups, using the GUI. Anything more complicated about domains and permissions can wait for later.

Third, programmes.  At this point the new user has a machine which is as configured as a new Windows machine would be. It will usually work fine because nothing complicated has been undertaken yet. The next thing the new user requires is more programmes, probably some of the same programmes they used in Windows, but where to get them.  Because my recent successful implementation of Ubuntu was not my first try, I already new about the repositary, though I still don't fully understand the universe, the multiverse, et-al. I also didn't know about search bar at the top of the apps window untill Appollothethird told me about it. So _THIRD THING_, include a document on the desktop explaining in simple terms how to install new software.

Fourth thing, still programmes.  It's very likely that the new user will not find the software they want in the repository. For example, I needed a progam to check PDP signatures, and the Ubuntu documentation suggests one. Is it in the repository though, is it .... (well no). I guess they want me to use sudo apt get again.  So the next thing the new user does is goes onto the internet and finds the software they want, and lo-and-behold there is a Linux (probably even Ubuntu Linux) version available, so they download it. What they get is a tarball, and what they say is "@*% what's a tarball".  They might try to use the tarball, and they might not, but being new users, if they do try to use it they will most probably fail.  I have tried a few times, and I haven't succeeded yet. So _FOURTH THING_ talk to the many suppliers who are supportive of Linux and get them to provide Ubuntu versions, and make sure the document about loading new programmes which is left on the desktop explains the difference between programmes written for Ubuntu and ones written for Red Hat. In general, make tarballs unnecessary for all but the most experienced users.

Fifth documentation.  Please forgive my profanity, but all of the official documentation for Ubuntu is written by tekkies for tekkies. This will be difficult for you to understand. That is because you are tekkies.  I have had technical support guy's working with me and for me for all my working life (starting in 1970), and they have always been very bright guy's, they have to be to do their job. They are usually great to get on with,too, but like initiates of any arcane branch of knowledge they have their own language with it's own grammar. This is not how people communicate. Please do not say that Technical terms are required because they lead to greater understanding. This is garbage. Technical terms are just shortcuts for people who already understand the concepts. You have to explain the concepts first, in ordinary language, then you can introduce technical terms. You have to understand that there is more required than "technical documentation", and you have to understand that wherever there is a way of doing things which does not require a terminal, (and ultimately this should be almost everything) you should show that first. The people who are happy with terminals will be adept enough to access the more technical branch of the documentation anyway, when they need it. At this point I will mention the joke about TLA's. Many of you will not know what I'm talking about, the others should feel suitably admonished.

When I started this I had no intention of going on so long. I must be feeling passionate. I don't believe anything I've suggested here in any way threatens the established Ubuntu users, though, and I do believe that the established users do need me, and more to the point people with even less expertise than me, to expand the userbase and interest third party providers to work with them. 

Switching to Ubuntu is a daunting experience for most people, and no amount of saying "No it isn't" will help. People who say that,however well intentioned, have their heads in the sand. Until they understand that they will not do anything about it, and Ubunto will continue to not work when it comes to third party provisions.

That's all folks.

Phil Binner

---

### Post by Phil Binner on 2011-09-21
I should have put another item in the experience of a new user. Almost as soon as they start they are going to want an AntiVirus/Firewall.  I have googled that for Ubuntu, but as yet nothing has seemed obvious. I've not tried too hard though.  I'm willing to bet that when I do find one, it'll come in a tarball.

I'm aware, because Linux users keep telling me, that Linux is intrinsically more secure than Windows. Be that as it may, no computer is complete these days without a full security suite.  If it's already included within the infrastructure of Ubuntu then that needs to be made obvious, and there needs to be a full and complete explanation as to how it operates.  If not, then selection of a partner for this should come as a higher priority than Libre Office and Evolution mail. Unfortunately, this is the world we live in.  If you can't sort this in Ubuntu, then unfortunately it is not a competitor for Mac and Windows.  I'm sure you can though.

### Post by varunendra on 2011-09-21
> **Phil Binner said:**
> We are more or less following three trends in this post, things that dont work how we like them; things that don't work; and the experience of new Ubuntu users.........
I vote this post as a valuable one for pointing out some important and useful recommendations.

**_Summary_:**
Primarily - Create and Provide on-desktop, easy to read, easy to understand, easy to follow "howto manual(s)", targeted towards willing, but absolute beginner, non-tekky innocent users.

Secondly - try to get more support from third parties somehow.

However, as far as application search is concerned, I think the "Software Center" is already over-simplified. I prefer Synaptic, which I find more friendly than even a google search. I don't have to type the name of a package to search it. I just type a keyword (like mp3, hp printer, avi, video editor, pdf,.... etc-etc) - and get a bunch of results. Selecting any of the results, I get a complete description of what it is meant for, where it comes from, and whether or not it is supported or recommended. Oh, sometimes also a note of comparison with others of its kind! All this with just a matter of clicking or scrolling with arrow keys. I can't think of a better and faster way to search for an unknown application from within an OS.

Hope I am not exaggerating.

### Post by varunendra on 2011-09-21
> **Phil Binner said:**
> I should have put another item in the experience of a new user. Almost as soon as they start they are going to want an AntiVirus/Firewall.  I have googled that for Ubuntu, but as yet nothing has seemed obvious. I've not tried too hard though.  I'm willing to bet that when I do find one, it'll come in a tarball....
ClamAV and ClamTK (GUI frontEnd to ClamAV) are in official repositories for a long time. And Untangle Labs - in a public demonstration - proved that it is the second best antivirus in world in terms of both speed and detection rate (First was Kaspersky, but it was much heavier than ClamAV or most of other competitors). Although that report has now perhaps been more than a year old, but should still be sufficient to assure people of its efficiency :)

Other involved competitors included Symantec, AVG, Norton, McAfee.... you name it ;) Norton was worst among recognized ones (I know I don't need to state that!).


**_EDIT_:**
Just wanted to add, AFAIK, no antivirus in Linux is a "Live Scanner", all of them are "On-Demand Scanners". Means - they will scan for virus etc. only when you command them to do so, not all the time in background. I think it is in line with the security model of Linux - can't explain how.

### Post by cariboo on 2011-09-21
I know this isn't supposed to be a support thread, but I can't stand it when someone doesn't know enough about the operating they are using, that almost everything in a Linux distribution is changeable. For liketoscroll, all you have to do is remove the overlay-scrollbar package, and you get the old-style scroll bars back.

Most of the problems I see in this thread are more due to lack of knowledge than anything else. Keep in mind that most users have used a windows version most of their lives, and have forgotten what it was like when they first started using it.

My suggestion is to install an LTS version, and take your time learning the ins and outs of the system. Once you have a fairly good understanding of how your system works, then install one of the interim releases, but always keep in mind that the versions between LTS release are largely experimental in nature, and aren't expected to be stable on all hardware.

### Post by liketoscroll on 2011-09-21

> Your doubts about Linux or Ubuntu's future are based on fresh experiments, not a mature trend!

Actually I'm just drawing fairly obvious conclusions. Linux has been out there going pretty strong since 1994 (thats when in my opinion it became easy enough for casual users like me to install), and during all this time it hasn't cracked a significant portion of the desktop market, especially for the rather obvious reasons I've presented in my previous post, your admirable but futile denial not withstanding of course.

Now as we see the market trending away from the traditional desktop, I don't think its that hard to draw some conclusions about the trends, and I'd have to see some fairly compelling evidence to the contrary about Linux not trending toward domination on the desktop except as a residual install on older more poorly supported hardware.

### Post by diacad on 2011-09-25
Several months later I have revisited this problem.  I have nearly concluded that Ubuntu is less satisfactory than Windows XP for graphics on older computers.  Other nVidia cards also seem to have the problem of 640x480 resolution when loading the recommended drivers.  In fact, the default Ubuntu drivers give higher resolutions than the nVidia drivers!  Another problem is jerky youtube viewing.  The Flash driver that Ubuntu uses seems to be defective.  There are many grandmother's remedies suggested on the internet, but none of them have worked for me (including minitube).  Life is too short to horse around with a toy operating system, or one which requires such a depth of arcane knowledge to even predict what can be done with it.
 
For me, the promise of Ubuntu was to rescue XP-class systems from oblivion.  For use by the average user, not only the operating-system enthusiast.  So far, I doubt this promise can be kept.  Perhaps Ubuntu works better on newer systems, but then if you are going to spend money on hardware, why not spend it on software as well and get something that is at least somewhat supported?

### Post by diacad on 2011-09-25
> **Copper Bezel said:**
> I think there are Linux distros that cater to what you're looking for there, diacad, and you might look into lighter-weight variations even within Ubuntu, like Lubuntu. The goal, or the focus at least, for Ubuntu is to be a full-fledged, modern desktop OS run on modern hardware. ....
 
The systems that I am currently wrestling with are back numbers, but well within Ubuntu requirements. 2.4 Ghz Celeron, 1 Gb DDR RAM, 40 Gb disk, 32 Mb nVidia AGP. I have not had great luck with Lubuntu or other distros, and doubt that the graphic support would be any better. In googling on the problems of (1) poor official nVidia driver support with 640x480 max resolution and (2) poor youtube (flash) performance, I find that I am not alone, and that no one has a cure that works. I tried gnash and other things some have suggested without success.
 
A system with the specs I gave may not be the latest and greatest, but it will (and does) run like a top with XP. Unless I can find solutions under Ubuntu, I would have to reluctantly conclude that this distribution is not as efficient as XP. At least where graphics are concerned - this may be the Ubuntu Achilles Heel. Sad, due to XP's many problems and periodically threatened lack of support and availability.
 
It is interesting that you can't seem to find a decent diagnostic or benchmark for graphic performance under Ubuntu. Glxgears is a joke. Others even seriously suggest running a game like Nexuiz! Surely the Ubuntu community can do better that this.
 
Thanks for answering, I am not trying to be a wise guy here, I want Ubuntu to work - and not just with state-of-the-art machines! That is why I am spending some time on it, and probably why others are too.

### Post by Alwimo on 2011-09-27
Yeah, that is unfortunate. The usability studies point to it being a good idea to put it there for beginners (they would click on the home folder icon, expecting to open applications that way). So it's probably good for beginners. But not having something on the screen all the time to click on to get the dash is a step back for some people, and wouldn't make much sense to a lot of non-beginners who are familiar with the BFB.  
 
I just use the super key, so it's pretty much aesthetic for me. Pushing things more to the left side of the screen up the top doesn't look good to my eyes. But I do understand that putting it there fits in better with Unity's whole "let's hide things unless your mouse movements suggest you want it" thing.
 
I like both Unity and Gnome Shell, but I'll probably prefer Gnome Shell over Ubuntu 11.10's Unity.

---

### Post by MarjaE on 2011-09-29
Can't read or write to web archive files. So if I need to save something I've found online, I need to print it out, but print-to-file is really frakking buggy.

### Post by linfidel on 2011-09-29
> **MarjaE said:**
> Can't read or write to web archive files. So if I need to save something I've found online, I need to print it out, but print-to-file is really frakking buggy.


I really don't understand this - Are you talking about the format Internet Explorer uses to save web pages to disk?  Why would you need to open one online?  They are for local viewing, but there are many ways to save web pages, even entire sites if needed, from linux without needing to save it as MSIE-specific format.

### Post by apollothethird on 2011-10-02
Hi, Phil.  First I’ll mention I see the message I’m replying to is a week old.  I’m reading all the messages, so there’s a chance someone has already addressed these issues you’re mentioning.

Second, for the people who may want to remind me that I can’t change someone’s opinion of what they like, I’m mentioning that this isn’t my intentions.  However, I’m responding with respect to what a user might miss the same way I’ll hope if I had a gripe, maybe someone would point to me that the gripe might not actually be there, I might be missing a valuable feature that is actually in place.

> **Phil Binner said:**
> 
Apollothethird has convinced me that much of what I need is already there, but I still believe he's missing the point in not recognising that new users are put off by the overly technical nature of Ubuntu. I will not campaign for removal of the terminal, it's obviously an immensely valuable tool for the experienced user or the IT support guy's, but it has to be easy for a new user to configure a new system, in all the obvious ways, without it.


I believe it’s very easy for a new user to work in the environment without the terminal.  They can with Ubuntu, just as they can with Windows.  It just happen that it’s very easy for me to tell someone to type in the following commands from a terminal (as an example):

```

cd
mv .config .config.old

```Than to say:  Go to your root directory.  Turn on the option for your file browser to show hidden files.  Find a directory named “.config”.  Rename it to a different directory.

Many times I might get a responses asking: What is a folder?  How do I rename a folder? And a host of other questions.

A person doesn’t have to use the terminal, as you have already agreed.  But it’s nice to have the convenience that doesn’t exist as readily in Windows and on the Mac.  Must about anything that can be done in Ubuntu, Windows, or the Mac  in gui can be done very easy from the commandline in Linux.  However, many things will not work from the commandline in other OS’.  This make for a need of lots of pages of dialogue and pictures to walk the user through.  Many times they get lost with the pictures.  Many times they have changed something about their default view that even when you’re trying to help them on the phone they can’t find the pictures you’re trying to get them to click on.

> 
First wifi. At the moment, if a new user puts his disk in an empty computer, he usually gets a simple installation. The instructions for connecting to wifi during that installation are minimal, though, and if there is any problem with the connection for that or any other reason, without another computer to gain access to support he's dead in the water at that point.
Yes, I agree.  Just as with any other OS that has unsupported hardware.  However, this is something that I have experienced a lot in my years of setting up computers and giving text support.  This happens much less frequently with Linux than Windows.  It doesn’t happen with the Mac because people would have already spent an enormous amount of money for overpriced hardware to ensure compatibility.

If you have hardware that isn’t immediately supported and you have to find support for, then of course, you’re very right.  You’ll have to find alternate ways of getting support for that hardware.  But let a new user install Ubuntu on a new machine with supported hardware and I assure you, he’d have less problems than a new user trying to start out with any other OS.  The Ubuntu environment is definitely friendly and GUI supported in this regard.  I find it to be substantially easier to configure than trying to navigate Windows.  I did this recently when I bought a Cricket Broadband Modem to use my computer while on Vacation.  I never saw a need to go into the terminal.  It was easier to install and use than with Windows.  I can’t speak for the Mac.  But I can’t imagine it being easier than Ubuntu if the modem was supported since I can’t imagine more ease than plugging in a device, clicking on the desired connection provider, then browsing the Internet.

I guess you can’t help by hate the fact that every piece of cheap hardware your find might not be supported by Linux and might require some tweaking to get it working.  But I find that the brand names that I use are usually supported.  If I find something that isn’t supported I’d be more likely to blame the hardware developer over blaming Linux.

Today almost any component hardware item you might get cost less than the price of Windows, so I’d still win if I put that component on the shelf and bought a supported hardware component.

I’m not sure if I mentioned it in this thread, but that is what I had one of my clients recently do with a Lexmark paperweight.  I recommended any HP printer brand.  He replaced the hardware and was immediately in business.  All GUI.  All automatic.  All, easier than spending a half hour loading a lot of unnecessary, unneeded, unwanted software bogging down his computer.

> 
Second, file sharing. There is a GUI to publish files from Ubuntu over a network. It is very simple to use, though more complex routines are difficult. It is not at all obvious, however. When I looked at the documentation it had me go into a terminal to download Samba with sudo apt get, and edit the config file in a terminal with sudo gedit. [COLOR=red]The upshot was that I messed up Samba, which as it turned out was already configured on my machine.[/COLOR] So SECOND THING, include a file on the desktop which explains in simple terms how to configure filesharing and workgroups, using the GUI. Anything more complicated about domains and permissions can wait for later.
Yes.  A lot of what is needed is already installed.  On one hand you want to understand and have access to the details of managing things.  On the other hand you feel things can wait until later.

The quick documentation is available.  Just type: help in the launch search option.  It really appears to start simple enough for anyone needing to do normal computer operations.  If someone wants to do something more complex, it appears the documentation is appropriately there.  I believe some of the things you describe that you want to do is a bit above the normal user who buys a new computer and doesn’t know anything.

You appear to become very accustom to doing certain things you’ve done in Windows, and try to describe yourself as a new computer user, not knowing where to start, but going there with a very specific IT chore.

Of course, the chore is available.  It’s just different than what you’ve done with Windows.  You want to speak about networking.  That whole concept is very technically orientated to a new computer user, who won’t immediately need it.  This new computer user will probably have one computer.  It’ll be networked to the Internet, and also, it’ll be a GUI click away from accessing any network resources that anyone has configured in the Network neighborhood.  If you click on the file browser (Nautilus), you’ll see that you can easily click and browse around all the files in your local network, without needing the read complete documentation or look at the terminal.

> 
Third, programmes. At this point the new user has a machine which is as configured as a new Windows machine would be. It will usually work fine because nothing complicated has been undertaken yet. The next thing the new user requires is more programmes, probably some of the same programmes they used in Windows, but where to get them. Because my recent successful implementation of Ubuntu was not my first try, I already new about the repositary, though I still don't fully understand the universe, the multiverse, et-al. I also didn't know about search bar at the top of the apps window untill Appollothethird told me about it. So THIRD THING, include a document on the desktop explaining in simple terms how to install new software.Actually the documentation is there.  But personally, I believe it’s easier done than said.  On Ubuntu, you just type in the chore in the launch bar and click on it.  If it’s on the computer it’d start up.  If it’s not already installed the new software manage will show up with a description of the piece of software with the option to install it right from the GUI.

I might be missing something.  But I can’t understand this section as to “what you hate about ubuntu”?  Again, I understand that you’ve said in other messages that you actually like the OS.  I’m probably missing something here if there’s something bad about a new user typing about what he wants to do and seeing a list of applications that he can easily click on to install, then do what he wants to do.

> 
Fourth thing, still programmes. It's very likely that the new user will not find the software they want in the repository. For example, I needed a progam to check PDP signatures, and the Ubuntu documentation suggests one. Is it in the repository though, is it .... (well no). I guess they want me to use sudo apt get again. So the next thing the new user does is goes onto the internet and finds the software they want, and lo-and-behold there is a Linux (probably even Ubuntu Linux) version available, so they download it. What they get is a tarball, and what they say is "@*% what's a tarball". They might try to use the tarball, and they might not, but being new users, if they do try to use it they will most probably fail. I have tried a few times, and I haven't succeeded yet. So FOURTH THING talk to the many suppliers who are supportive of Linux and get them to provide Ubuntu versions, and make sure the document about loading new programmes which is left on the desktop explains the difference between programmes written for Ubuntu and ones written for Red Hat. In general, make tarballs unnecessary for all but the most experienced users.I believe you’re having problems with that because you’re searching for something by the wrong name.  Your search is bringing hits that are not the best for you to decipher.  If you go to the synaptic package manager (the many repository resource) and type in “pgp signature” you should be able to do all this from gui.  You won’t see any reference to tar balls or the terminal.

> 
Fifth documentation. Please forgive my profanity, but all of the official documentation for Ubuntu is written by tekkies for tekkies. This will be difficult for you to understand. That is because you are tekkies. I have had technical support guy's working with me and for me for all my working life (starting in 1970), and they have always been very bright guy's, they have to be to do their job. They are usually great to get on with,too, but like initiates of any arcane branch of knowledge they have their own language with it's own grammar. This is not how people communicate. Please do not say that Technical terms are required because they lead to greater understanding. This is garbage. Technical terms are just shortcuts for people who already understand the concepts. You have to explain the concepts first, in ordinary language, then you can introduce technical terms. You have to understand that there is more required than "technical documentation", and you have to understand that wherever there is a way of doing things which does not require a terminal, (and ultimately this should be almost everything) you should show that first. The people who are happy with terminals will be adept enough to access the more technical branch of the documentation anyway, when they need it. At this point I will mention the joke about TLA's. Many of you will not know what I'm talking about, the others should feel suitably admonished.I’ve been admonished many times for using descriptive words.  I tolerate it and use them anyway.  I prefer for a person to ask me what an icon is, when I first use it rather than constantly use the word small picture.  Some of my paying clients speak very angrily when I say Icon, desktop, application, file browser.  There’s hardly a word in my vocabulary that I haven’t been admonished about.  But they soon understand the menu bar, cursor and all the other words I need to use to give support over the phone.

I really commend the community supported effort for what they have done with Ubuntu’s documentation.  I find it hard to imagine it better and easier to navigate.  From your message it appears that you’re reading something different from what I’m seeing.

I find it easier to navigate [http://help.ubuntu.com](http://help.ubuntu.com)  than [http://help.microsoft.com](http://help.microsoft.com) (or wherever you find their documentation which I’ve been using for years to give support.

There’s a chance that your complaint isn’t so much what you hate about Ubuntu, but what you hate about the computer community.  Most of what you describe, appears to be something about the computer community that just happens to be easier on Ubuntu than Windows.

> 
When I started this I had no intention of going on so long. I must be feeling passionate. I don't believe anything I've suggested here in any way threatens the established Ubuntu users, though, and I do believe that the established users do need me, and more to the point people with even less expertise than me, to expand the userbase and interest third party providers to work with them.

Switching to Ubuntu is a daunting experience for most people, and no amount of saying "No it isn't" will help. People who say that,however well intentioned, have their heads in the sand. Until they understand that they will not do anything about it, and Ubunto will continue to not work when it comes to third party provisions.

That's all folks.

Phil BinnerMost of the people hear understand that there is hardware (as software) that isn’t designed to work generically, but priority wise.  As long as the general public use this as a reason to hate Ubuntu, they will continue to pour their bucks into Microsoft, and the hardware and software will develop for where the masses are.

If the public start to place the blame where it exists, many will join HP’s bandwagon and provide support for Linux.  I hope I can convince the masses to hate Lexmart because they won’t provide drivers for Linux, or hate their favorite software developer because they don’t port their software over to Linux.

I’m not trying to make the people in this thread stop hating Ubuntu.  But I hope to help address some of their issues which might be resolved with a little education.

I don’t know what the OP’s full intention for the thread, but I hope some good might come in that some users who are banging their heads against the wall about something they think is broke, can learn some direction, and the components that really needs changed can be filtered out some that someone can present the changes to the powers that be.

-- L. James

--

### Post by apollothethird on 2011-10-02

Otherwise Ubuntu 11.04 is great (even Unity).

Interesting peeve... dislike of free software.

I could understand a peeve specifying a specific software that is needed.  But I don't understand a peeve at it being stamped as free rather than pay.

I understand how much more appreciation most people have for something they throw money at.  My system was a manager at a clothes manufacturer.  They sell their same clothes to various chains.  The retail price is extremely different.  However, the only difference in the clothes is the label sewn on them.

A lot of people would probably appreciate Ubuntu even more if they had to pour money into it.  But I'm glad there are enough of us who appreciates the operation whether it's commercial or free.

I hope most of Google's features will remain free also, though I wouldn't be surprised if they don't start charging a fee so that certain people will like it better.

### Post by Mikeb85 on 2011-10-03
> **apollothethird said:**
> Interesting peeve... dislike of free software.


I like free software.  But I also like well-made commercial software.  I use whatever suits my needs, free or not.  For instance, Libre Office is fantastic.  Works for me.  

But some free software (games for instance) simply doesn't have the polish of commercial software, because at some point, if you dedicate your career to doing just one thing, you need to make money.  Which is why Canonical, Red Hat, etc..., all sell services.  

For me the strength of open source isn't that it's free (gratuit), but that it leads to collaboration, resulting in a better end product.  I don't use Linux/Ubuntu because it's free, I use it because it works better than Windows.  

But anyhow, the lack of some commercial software still doesn't keep me from using Ubuntu, and ONLY Ubuntu... (I don't dual boot anything)

### Post by peyre on 2011-10-03
> **MarjaE said:**
> I opened the link, I clicked the install button, and then the Software Center started up. I thought I was pretty clear about that. Anyway, here's the thread where I've been asking for help:

[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1853650](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1853650)

Oh!  I've run into that myself.  The problem is that your system has associated .deb files with the Software Center.  You want to switch that to gdebi, but you'll probably have to install that first; fortunately that's easy enough in Synaptic or the Software Center.

Then once gdebi is installed, right-click on your .deb file, and select Open with other application.  Then Browse, select gdebi (in /usr/bin), make sure the box is checked next to "Use as default for this kind of file", and click OK.  That should do it.

(At least, that's the step-by-step in Xubuntu--YMMV in standard Ubuntu.)

### Post by Copper Bezel on 2011-10-04
I actually do hate this: [_Three-finger tap in Synaptics is read in 11.10 as a utouch gesture_,]("https://bugs.launchpad.net/unity/+bug/839769") for the new window move and resize handles, bypassing middle click.

My Elantech trackpad apparently isn't supported by the new kernel anyway - yay - so I'm not directly affected. But this means that touch pads don't get middle-click, crippling browsers and removing features in Nautilus and the Unity dock.

This despite the fact that the same thing could have been accomplished by simply setting middle click to activate the handle plugin through Compiz.

Full disclosure: I'm comment #9.

### Post by Tutoka on 2011-10-05
Ubuntu should be more empathical towards dummies like me.
