### Post by Copper Bezel on 2011-10-05
Which browser are you using? The tab bar should be visible as ever; if it's not, start a thread in one of the support forums. The Launcher's autohide can be set to "Never" in compizconfig-settings-manager, which makes it always visible. If you don't have compizconfig installed, grab it from the Software Center.


[QUOTE=el_koraco]This is pure, unadulterated moronism.[/QUOTE]
Thanks for saying so - I was beginning to wonder if I was just crazy.

[quote=azurehi]what do you mean, please?[/quote]
I can't speak for el_koraco and I don't really mean to call anyone a moron, but to my perspective, it really is equivalent to remapping a random key on the keyboard to a shell feature and not offering any way to switch it back.

Edit: And it only applies to touch devices, so middle-click on mice or trackpad buttons still works, if the device includes a middle button. So you either get middle-click (on a mouse) or the shell feature (on multitouch trackpads) but never both (except for a few laptops with both a click button and a gesture for middle click.)

### Post by apollothethird on 2011-10-08
> **MarjaE said:**
> It's more like every application within this OS... And my home folder is my home folder. It's where Ubuntu put the damn thing. I hardly ever use my home folder because I keep my work on the other partition, so, if I ever need to use Windows, I can access the files from either system; I don't know how applications within that OS save things though.

I don't think the OS controls where the applications save their data.  At least as far as I can see it doesn't.  Every application that I use has a configuration option where you can actually set a preferred save area.  By default if the program is sane it'll consider the home folder, but they all allow you to configuration them differently if that's the way you want them to be configured.

Libreoffice (for example) just like Microsoft Office has a "Path" configuration under options that allows you to configure the save path.  Mine by default is "[my home]/Documents".  If I wanted to override that default, I'd just change it there for that application.

I can't blame the OS for putting not putting something random in every application.  I would consider, that even the thought of this, would result in lots of confusion and chaos.

You already notice that the OS uses your home folder.  Don't you think that makes sense?  How else can the OS expect to operate proper for each user and load their configuration?  I would also expect for the developers of the applications to start out with similar logic and start out in a place where the users can find their data.

Windows doesn't totally respect this and places some of your defaults to some random space that every user shares.  But the developers of most of the Linux applications respect a logic of starting out with the user's home folder.  This way every user can have his own space.  Every user will be able to easily find where his data is (by default).  Every user can configure space for his various applications to his preference if he doesn't like the default.

By the way, if you don't want your home folder to be in the reasonable space where it is by default you can change it.  Then all other applications would start out with that area.  But it would seem reasonable that the applications would have to start somewhere.

The logic of having a default start place makes such obvious sense to me, that I can't understand what you expect different.  I'm trying to, but I can't see it.  If I'm missing something I hope you'll help me with it.

I don't mean to try to make you stop hating the OS, but I hope to clarify something that you appear to be missing, at least in this feature.

By the way, if you mention an application that you’re having specific problems with, I’m sure I can help you with it.  If we can’t fix that application, it’s really not the OS’ fault.  We should go an report the flaw to the application developer.

-- L. James

### Post by apollothethird on 2011-10-09
> **MarjaE said:**
> Firefox?

Thanks in advance if you will tell me the results you get from:

Firefox -> File -> Print ->  (Click on [home foldername] -> Other -> [Click on any area including File System] -> (Browse to where you have a particular area or browse to where you want to create a new frequent area] -> [Click on Create Folder] -> [Type in]Example -> [Press ENTER and go to Example] -> (Click) Add (Now Example is in Favourites a single click away for the next print time) -> Open -> Print

Your file will name be printed in the Example folder.  The Example folder will be a single click away the next time.

Again, thanks in advance for tell me how this works.  By the way, the steps are easier done than said.  While typing them and doing them for the first time might seem like a lot.  I went this long route as an example to try to make it clear.  But its really as simple as:

...clicking on the home folder button and browsing to where you want to print.

-- L. James

### Post by Flymo on 2011-10-10
**+1 to new123**

Used to be a happy clappy Ubuntu user (and guerilla installer) with no serious complaints that I could not easily fix - like grabbing the CODECs and stuff for DVDs and other media.  I've installed a **lot** of Ubuntu since Breezy. Oh yes!

But all of our posse is on Lucid.  We've tried Natty.  And I've tried Oneiric Beta 2 on live CD.  Won't Suspend properly.  And despite trying for six months, I cannot get on with Unity. I've really tried to like it.

But nor can anybody else around here.   

Do feel sorry for all the hard work wasted on it, and recognise that mistakes have to be made in order to innovate successfully, but Unity is unfinished in the same way as KDE 4.0 was.  

Wish I could help, but cannot think of a way of fixing something this far from a useful GUI.

Love Lucid, hate Unity.  <sigh>

### Post by apollothethird on 2011-10-10
> **Flymo said:**
> **+1 to new123**

Used to be a happy clappy Ubuntu user (and guerilla installer) with no serious complaints that I could not easily fix - like grabbing the CODECs and stuff for DVDs and other media.  I've installed a **lot** of Ubuntu since Breezy. Oh yes!

But all of our posse is on Lucid.  We've tried Natty.  And I've tried Oneiric Beta 2 on live CD.  Won't Suspend properly.  And despite trying for six months, I cannot get on with Unity. I've really tried to like it.

But nor can anybody else around here.   

Do feel sorry for all the hard work wasted on it, and recognise that mistakes have to be made in order to innovate successfully, but Unity is unfinished in the same way as KDE 4.0 was.  

Wish I could help, but cannot think of a way of fixing something this far from a useful GUI.

Love Lucid, hate Unity.  <sigh>

Hi, Flymo.  Since it appears that the marriage is here and most likely her for the long run (to stay), there might be a chance that the Unity haters might consider it a bit more productive to give suggestions for the interface rather than trying to banish it (which your message appears to suggest as an unlikelihood).

Personally I think Unity is a great addition.  I probably shouldn't mention this so early in my message, because it might turn many off from considering my followup.  Many people hated unity because of the size of the icons.  An addition came where the size can be adjusted.  Some said they hated it because it was there and didn't have a feature for hiding it.  There are numerous features to hide it (autohide, dodge windows, dodge active window).

Some people said they missed the category feature of Gnome's ability to browse: Accessories, Games, System, Education, etc.  Those users missed this feature is available with a click on the Filter option.

It appears that you can do just about everything with Unity that you can do with Gnome and more.  I believe it might be more productive and have more impact on the developers if the haters would focus on what's missing rather than throw the baby out with the bathwater.

I hate the fact that you can't right click on the running icons and individually pick out a single one to bring to foreground.  It's problematic to have to clutter my screen with 30 Firefox browsers just to look for a recent inactive window.  For me just about the only thing missing is the ability to right click and pick from the running applications in that group to bring to forward.

You'll probably say you hate Unity and everything about it, but I'd be curious about what specifically you hate about Unity.  If the message basic can't contain the large list, can you consider picking out two or three of the worse components to start with?  This will give the developers something to work with.  But for most people to just say they hate Unity without any specifics, to me, will appears to suggest they can't say anything about it because they haven't used it.  The developers probably feel if they actually gave it a chance, they would fall in love with it, and have the mentality to wait for the masses to actually use it.

-- L. James

### Post by GuiGuy on 2011-10-12
> **MarjaE said:**
> Thanks. It looks like Gnome 3 will impose the same workflow problems that Unity does. I'm deciding whether to stick with 11.04 or to reinstall 10.10 right now...

Actually, I don't quite understand why KDE is so maligned; for the Windows user coming across KDE is probably the best experience. I run it now because I do not like unity and there are features that gnome is supposed to have and I cannot get working. The default file manager is also lacking features I want. 

So, other than the mythbuntu media center in our house, our notebooks and PCs run KDE. Not so much by choice but that it is the best of a poor lot.

NB: what I do like about KDE is that when running multiple desktops ALL of the running applications are listed on the task bar.  Gnome etc, as best as I have been able to work out, shows only the applications running on the current desktop. And you have to install a task bar plug in even for that. The inexperienced user will therefore frequently have multiple instances of the same application running. My wife found this when she used gnome. It was one of the main reasons for moving to KDE.

### Post by Copper Bezel on 2011-10-12
[QUOTE=GuiGuy]NB: what I do like about KDE is that when running multiple desktops ALL of the running applications are listed on the task bar. Gnome etc, as best as I have been able to work out, shows only the applications running on the current desktop. And you have to install a task bar plug in even for that. The inexperienced user will therefore frequently have multiple instances of the same application running. My wife found this when she used gnome. It was one of the main reasons for moving to KDE.[/QUOTE]

This depends entirely on the task switcher you're using and is often configurable. It was in Gnome 2's panel, at least. In Unity, apps from all workspaces are displayed.

I frequently have multiple instances of the same application running on separate workspaces, because I'm using them for different tasks. If I wasn't doing that, I'm not quite certain why I'd have workspaces, and I don't think I'd much appreciate a task bar that didn't reflect that and let me ignore things I'm actively deeming irrelevant to the moment. I understand that it works for other people, though. = )

KWin, Mutter, and Compiz all have their perks, and Compiz is particularly weak in workspace management, but this isn't that - just which windows show up in which taskbar at what times is controlled by whatever taskbar you're using.

### Post by GuiGuy on 2011-10-13
Here's what I really dislike about Ubuntu and Linux. The obfuscation!

This morning I followed a link to give feedback on Kubuntu. This is what I see

> Introduction
    Add your feedback like this w/o the bold, and then add @SIG@ at the end of your line - trust us, it doesn't show up in the preview, only when you save your comment.

There is, of course, no where to give feedback, and clicking the claydo link takes the user to another totally confusing, cryptic, obfuscated page.

Kubuntu is not alone. You see it everywhere in the linux world. 

It is, IMO, the single, dominant reason linux will always be on the side line. 

Get away from the language of experts, start using [plain English]("www.plainenglish.co.uk/") and we might get somewhere.

### Post by MarjaE on 2011-10-13
I'd like something which is compatible with my current Wubi installation.

I currently use Classic in 11.04 in Wubi which keeps running out of memory and disk space, so it ought to use less memory and disk space.

I'd like something that enables my kind of workflow. So it needs to make window-switching as easy as Gnome 2 and Classic, and application switching is no substitute for window switching. A good search system, better than Nautilus and less memory-intensive than Tracker, would also help, which can distinguish similarly-named files from different folders.

I'd like something that doesn't rely on animation, visible-on-mouseover devices, or other gimmicks. One reason is aesthetic. Another reason is that these are unreliable, and something which isn't visible but might pop up makes more screen-space unusable than something which is just there.

I'd like something which isn't prone to maximizing windows willy-nilly.

I have more than enough screen-width, but not enough depth for Gnome 2 and Classic where windows frequently stretch off the bottom of the screen, and maximize when I try to move them onto the screen.

I seem to have worse hand-eye coordination than certain features in Gnome 2, Classic, and Unity take for granted. For example, I end up resizing windows by accident, but can't restore them to their original size because the resizing handles are visible-on-mouseover devices [see above] and too small and too finicky for me to grip except by accident.

---

### Post by seeker5528 on 2011-10-13
> **MarjaE said:**
> I would like to find a more suitable desktop environment than Gnome 2, Unity, or what I've read about Gnome 3. But how difficult is it to try KDE without reinstalling let alone repartitioning?

```
sudo apt-get install kubuntu-desktop
```
or
```
sudo apt-get install kubuntu-full
```

Or using Synaptic or other package manager of your choice select and install kubuntu-desktop or kubuntu-full.

Then log out, click on your user name, select the desktop session you want to log in to, type in your password and log in.

This assumes your installation is on a partition big enough to hold all your existing stuff and the additional KDE/Kubuntu stuff that will be pulled in.

Later, Seeker

---

### Post by apollothethird on 2011-10-14
I mentioned once before that I don't know specifically the OP's immediate reason and intention of this topic, but for me it had done a lot.  It had really helped me to appreciate what a great facility I have in the availability of Ubuntu.

Looking at all the complaints helped me to see how insignificant my gripes have been.  This is in light of how easy it is to see resolutions for the other gripes that have been presented here.  It is very apparent in my observation that most of the haters would rather to hold on to their hatred than use a resolution to resolve their issue.

As for me, Ubuntu has provided for the excitement of Christmas to come an additional two times a year.  Reading the messages if the various forums shows that I'm not alone to have May and October marked on my calendar for special events.

This message isn't directed at anyone specific.  I understand there are some who are very genuine with their gripe who wants to see things different about the OS.  But I hope some of those with their very legitimate gripes can see some merit in my post to have appreciation for the variety of the Linux community that someone can have the freedom to develop and build on a specific Linux flavor according to what they feel is great for the community.  The fanfare I've watched over the past few days certainly (to me) is an indication that they are doing something right.

Some of the features might be hated (which a user can usually easily customize and circumvent).  But the OS appears to be very well embraced.

-- L. James

--

### Post by quixote on 2011-10-14
> **MarjaE said:**
> I can't find clear info on:

- What options, if any, I'd have if I upgrade to 11.10.

- Exactly what has changed in Unity between 11.04 and 11.10. I know I can't do any work in Unity in 11.04.

- Exactly what has changed between Gnome 2.0/Classic and Gnome 3.0.

I look up GNOME in Wikipedia, and find complaints about 3.0 but not specifics about the differences between 2.0 and 3.0.

I am concerned that installing 11.10 might be a mistake and reinstalling 10.10 or 11.04 might be my only option. And reinstalling can be a pain.
Well, I just tried it in VirtualBox because I was afraid of the same things as you. It took forever on my connection to install the Gnome desktop, but, okay, that's tangential.

Gnome3 (the one labelled just "Gnome" in the list of desktop choices at login) is basically like Unity. Applications are buried instead of being accessible with one click off an expanding Applications menu. Also, you can't easily move the panel to wherever you want.

(I don't mind learning new things. I've spent my life at universities.  It's my job. But after I've learned them I expect BETTER capabilities. Not worse.)

If you select Gnome Classic, you get the Applications menu back.  You still can't easily move the panel. If you like it in the default top position, then that wouldn't be a problem.

For me, the lack of control over my own desktop is a showstopper. I've moved to [LinuxMint Debian]("http://www.linuxmint.com/download_lmde.php").

Edited to add: Just to be clear, I know about gconf-editor and editing config files in nano or whatever. That's not my point. My point is that it has to be a simple right-click to change what I don't like. I don't want to waste hours just fighting with my desktop!

Gnome3 (the one labelled just "Gnome" in the list of desktop choices at login) is basically like Unity. Applications are buried instead of being accessible with one click off an expanding Applications menu. Also, you can't easily move the panel to wherever you want.

(I don't mind learning new things. I've spent my life at universities.  It's my job. But after I've learned them I expect BETTER capabilities. Not worse.)

If you select Gnome Classic, you get the Applications menu back.  You still can't easily move the panel. If you like it in the default top position, then that wouldn't be a problem.

For me, the lack of control over my own desktop is a showstopper. I've moved to [LinuxMint Debian]("http://www.linuxmint.com/download_lmde.php").


Edited to add: Just to be clear, I know about gconf-editor and editing config files in nano or whatever. That's not my point. My point is that it has to be a simple right-click to change what I don't like. I don't want to waste hours just fighting with my desktop!

I agree 100%.  I've been selling the virtues of Ubuntu to Windows zombies for years.  But with Unity as the default, I'm no longer comfortable telling people that Ubuntu is just as easy to use. 

 I tried Unity in 11.04 and gave up because I couldn't easy find the basic tools that I uses almost every day.  I also found it very difficult to setup my desktop the way I like it.

 Now I'm hearing that Synaptic is gone??  If that's true I'll be moving on too. Ubuntu Software Center is ok for installing apps, but do they really believe it can replace a solid package manager?? 

 I'm happy with 11.04 with classic for now but with these new changes it sounds like Ubuntu has deficiently made a step backwards.

### Post by An Sanct on 2011-10-16
> **GuiGuy said:**
> Maybe the "experts" who sit around the round tables deciding what we out here in the real world want and need (without asking us, of course!), think it's the way forward as we move to touch screen devices. 

Maybe.

But as many have pointed out, it generally takes two or three actions or gestures in Unity to achieve what most other GUI desktops will do in one. I'm not convinced that is a move forward...

I agree, the move was toward the fact, that it is estimated, that in 5 years, noone will have a computer sitting home any more (cloud computing) and that everything will be touch based (touch screens)

Now, speaking from my perspective, I *will* have a computer, I want it to do my "dirty" work right in front of me (and away from the net), I do not want it to be all over the net if I write a job application or watch an "adult movie" or in fact, if I do anything. I refuse to have a drone-terminal, with a monitor, keyboard and mouse, attached to internet, relying 100% on some other peoples service. But thats is just me, I still keep my C64, thats who I am.

Touch screens ... If I had a dime for every time I had to wipe the screen of my phone in the summer, right after a conversation. The screen made me feel I am holding some part of a public toilet. Yuck! My smart-phone (the brand does not matter) has a full qwerty keyboard and I love it for that ... wouldn't change it in a lifetime! My personal opinion is, that touch technology may be good for phones and tablets, but not for fully blown personal computers. They should stop thinking in our name and ask us what we want, but I also want a million dollars - the wish its quite the same...

Here, living in a free and open eco-system, that is Linux, I have the choice to switch and probably will... Think about those poor souls, that use Windows ... I'm sorry for them, Win 8 is coming and they cannot dodge...

### Post by doom3d on 2011-10-16
Why anybody wants to move from the good old windows series?

**1. stability** - under win7 You can change the background color of blue death, but most of the users just don't want crashes
**2. Speed, responsiveness** Some of us just want to use older/cheaper/greener hardware without compromising speed.
**3. viruses** Since windows is the most frequently used desktop OS, virus makers generally don't care about Linux distro's.
**4. Price, TCO, legality** You can get win7 pro + ms office pro + antivirus stuff for free, but this is not recommended. Linux is legally free. 

Newbies coming from windows also want to run their beloved applications (including games), so
**5. wine and virtualbox** is also necessary.
**6. eye candy** as an option- not everybody needs them

Now 10.04 LTS comes with a beta version of grub bootloader, they have forget to implement wine and virtualblox propely. Also ,every new version runs slower then the previous. The new distro's are released in beta state. So the project is going in the wrong direction. A fast, clean and reliable system would attract more users.

I hate the release cycle. The Ubuntu team is going to sacrifice stability and speed for eye candies and version numbers. I have to spend a weekend to fix most of the problems coming with each update.

I hate release policy. My proposal is to offer new packages as an option, and don't replace applications without a question - except if the old application isn't avalaible in the new repositories and flagged as auto installed. 
   Actually It was a mistake to force the installation of Unity with 11.04 & 11.10. It should be offered as an option- if it becomes the nr.1 preferred desktop environment according to usage stats, then it can be the default for new installations. 

BTW, I strongly dislike Unity. That's why I'm running 10.10, and downloading live CDs. Linux Mint Debian edition and Debian itself looks promising. I will give a last chance to 11.10 also.
Actually I have a 32" monitor and 1000+ apps, & dislike the idea to use a touch screen while eating my hotdog. Cleaning the mouse is easier. :)
Unity is ok for a small touch screen, and a few apps, but not for me.

### Post by MarjaE on 2011-10-17
I did a clean install [of Xubuntu] using the live cd. The regular Live CD hung while it demanded a wireless connection [and wasn't satisfied with the one I did have]. The alternate Live CD worked properly _once it was burned properly_ - by that time, I'd deleted my old installation and was using another machine with another OS which wanted to burn an .iso as a file on a cd-rom instead of as a true disk image.

I managed to get out of the wireless connection dialogue, and I skipped it and the clock settings dialogue, and the whole thing has installed. Now the clock settings are messed up, and not responding to my resetting them... ### Post by beew on 2011-12-05
> **JayKay3OOO said:**
> Software centre is nice, but is slow & the fact that you can't uninstall a bunch of apps in one go makes it a real slow process on first boot to get rid of all those apps you never use.

Thankfully i've copied these apps into a txt file so I simply run the terminal command and... boom baby!

Then I run the install command and paste the stuff I always use.

Two commands and the default install is smashed into what I like.

so yeh. I hate the software centre if I'm honest. At least synaptic exists still so I can go around ticking everything I need.

Agree. First thing I do after installing Oneric

```
sudo apt-get install synaptic gdebi
```Don't have to deal with the Software center ever, too bad many new users who don't know that will get frustrated by the slowness and bloatness of the USC. I think this brings out a general point, Canonical seems to be pushing things that looks good but completely impractical like the overlay scrollbar and the global menu.

The overlay scrollbar in natty is completely unusable, it jumps in descrete steps and I can't read a simple pdf document with it. In Oneric it is improved somewhat but the improved version is not even backported to Natty so if you want a more usable version you have to install from ppa (or you can remove it of course, or "upgrade" to a bug ridden 11.10)

Fixes don't get backported is another problem I have with Ubuntu.

---

### Post by Copper Bezel on 2011-12-05
USC is painfully slow and glitchy. I really do hope it improves for 12.04. It's a great idea, and as of 11.10, it looks and works in a very sensible way in design terms, but the performance is really not what I'd expect. I don't know what it's doing that Synaptic isn't that makes it so unwieldy.

The overlay scrollbars really are perfect in 11.10; they're pretty, they're consistent, and they reduce the overall UI clutter. I think they're fairly intuitive, too - I was showing my Windows-user step-dad the new interface on my netbook, and he picked them up and was using them without having to ask any questions. The inconsistencies in 11.04 really did make them quite unusable for a lot of folks (and ugly, for that matter, because of the inconsistencies regarding which apps would get them.) 

I don't think the designs are impractical or too focused on flair at all - it's just that they're rolled in as default in the stable releases at a state where they're not much better than functional mock-ups.

---

I couldn't really "scroll" as it jumped in discrete steps of something like half a page,  and if I try dragging the thing continuously I would simply land on some random page (I was trying to read a pdf ebook)

There will always be "inconsistencies" in terms of which apps get the overlay scrollbar. Firefox doesn't have it and I think all kde apps don't (e.g Okular) Of course if you remove it all together there would be consistency. :)

I agree that it is much improved in 11.10, but pity those using 11.04 and try to read a pdf (without knowing about the options of removing the overlay scrollbar, installing it from a ppa or use Okular. It is not unthinkable for new users)

### Post by Copper Bezel on 2011-12-05
[QUOTE=beew]Inconsistency is just a design term and it is not where my problem is with 11.04's overlay scrollbar.[/QUOTE]
Yeah, that was the wrong term for what I meant. I meant that in addition to the inconsistency in where they appeared, there was inconsistency in how they worked. In gedit, for instance, you don't get those discrete steps so far as I remember. What's worse is that in some settings windows, they just *didn't work* - they'd disappear when clicked. (!) Since they appear in all GTK apps now and function the same way across the board, I don't see any problem with the 11.10 implementation. LibreOffice and Firefox have their own weird, nonstandard ways of doing things, and even Chrome uses, I think, its own HTML renderer to draw the scrollbars (which is at least forgivable in that it leads to better *internal* consistency) and there's nothing the DE's toolkit can do about that. If Firefox rendered mauve polka-dot scrollbars that worked backwards, that would be the case across DEs, too.

And I agree, for the users who end up working with those incomplete implementations instead of fixing them, even if they're the least likely to fuss, they're representing a big part of the userbase getting a rather junk experience. I don't understand the logic, either.

---

### Post by MarjaE on 2011-12-07
> **Copper Bezel said:**
> Yeah, that was the wrong term for what I meant. I meant that in addition to the inconsistency in where they appeared, there was inconsistency in how they worked. In gedit, for instance, you don't get those discrete steps so far as I remember. What's worse is that in some settings windows, they just *didn't work* - they'd disappear when clicked. (!) Since they appear in all GTK apps now and function the same way across the board, I don't see any problem with the 11.10 implementation. LibreOffice and Firefox have their own weird, nonstandard ways of doing things, and even Chrome uses, I think, its own HTML renderer to draw the scrollbars (which is at least forgivable in that it leads to better *internal* consistency) and there's nothing the DE's toolkit can do about that. If Firefox rendered mauve polka-dot scrollbars that worked backwards, that would be the case across DEs, too.

And I agree, for the users who end up working with those incomplete implementations instead of fixing them, even if they're the least likely to fuss, they're representing a big part of the userbase getting a rather junk experience. I don't understand the logic, either.

Overlay scrollbars also create latency and accessibility problems, which aggravate each other. I think they have enough inherent problems that, however they get refined, they should be an option and not the only default type.

That's why I'm against making visible-on-mouseover features the only default anywhere. A user can't always be sure where and when the visible-on-mouseover features will pop up and mess with her work, and can't always be sure if she's too close to where they'll pop up. And a user can't always be sure those features won't retract or disappear just as she's using them.

That's also why I'd prefer reasonably-sized buttons and conventional scrollbars. I've used some trick mentioned here to widen the scrollbars on my machine, but it would be nice to have the option in Universal Access. The corner buttons and resize grips in Gnome 2 are also small enough to be an issue. That said, I find Gnome 2 much easier to use than Unity or Xfce.

---

### Post by Copper Bezel on 2011-12-07
I agree that reverting the scrollbars would work well as an accessibility option. I don't understand the complaint regarding visible-on-mouseover, at least regarding the scrollbars. The overlay controls are really too small to interfere with anything, and they appear wherever your cursor hits the edge, so there's not the problem of "missing," either. I also haven't seen the latency problems - they're quite smooth on my install, and they don't come with the lighter Ubuntu spins line Xubuntu and Lubuntu, which are largely *meant* for the kind of hardware that *has* latency problems with basic desktop effects.

The resize grip has been improved quite a bit in 11.10. It appears on all windows and is of a reasonable size without interfering with anything (basically a triangular overlay itself.) Like many things, they were awful in 11.04, and I ended up disabling them fairly soon on.

In what way do you mean "reasonably sized butons"? The buttons that don't come directly from the Unity shell are defined by the toolkit, the application, and the application font size. They probably could a bit bigger, particularly for touch implementations, but for Ubuntu to change them would probably take some doing, lead to conflicts, etc. at least as much as the resize handles and overlay scrollbars did.

---

### Post by Copper Bezel on 2011-12-15
Bug #11107734 in gnome-shell (Ubuntu): "Gnome 3's network manager quietly despises you."

**Bug Description**

Gnome 3's network manager quietly despises you.  If you ever make the network manager unhappy, it passive-aggressively ensures that you will never be happy again.

Expected behavior: More or less *anything* other than the

Actual behavior: Connecting to the wrong wireless network causes the network manager to subtly but consistently punish you for eternity for tempting it with the possibility.

Steps to reproduce: 

1. Visit your local coffee shop and accidentally select the private, behind-the-bar wireless network.

2. Go ahead and click Cancel. It's already too late. 

3. Suspend and resume your notebook. Watch as the network manager feigns a lengthy attempt to connect to the protected network, then coyly prompts you for a password. 

4. Click Cancel, give the network manager a flick on the nose, and select the unsecured, public network.

5. Repeat from step 3 until you find a new coffee shop.

Workaround: Open Network Settings. Select the offending network from the dropdown list in the Settings window and find that its settings in the dropdown menus cannot be changed unless you successfully connect with it, which you can't. Select it from the panel's network menu again anyway.

When the password prompt comes up this time, click Connect, instead of Cancel. You're a clever sort; you'll trick it yet!

While it's still trying to connect, click Configure. Think about disabling "Connect Automatically" at the upper left. See this.

[img]http://dl.dropbox.com/u/17749392/Screenshots/nm-beastlet/umkay.png[/img]

Become dejected, then impatient. Allow your impatience to build until you accidentally click Configure in the main network settings window *twice*. Be astonished at the miraculous appearance of the *old* Edit Connections screen. Select the new connection at the very, very bottom of the list and find that you still can't change its settings, but can delete it from the list. Do so.

Never do it again.

Alternately, try typing the wrong password for any secured network, anywhere.

You can't beat the convenience of dragging components of the application to a different window/monitor while I work on a particular component such as the editing on my monitor.

More and more the developers are opening up to this convenience by making the windows undockable from the work space.  There isn't much I hate worst than having to drag a work space between multiple monitors in order to put a tool bar or other component temporarily out of the way.

### Post by apollothethird on 2011-12-20
> **Garthhh said:**
> nah 
the devs never make to these parts

Some of the consensus does trickle through via various connections.  I've started to communicate on launchpad and some of the other forums for reporting bugs.  I bring some of the legitimate complaints to the attention of people who has influence.

Of course some of my pleas falls on death ears also, but not all of it.

I'm glad to see that some of my complaints are recognized by some of the other users, such as Unity's or Ubuntu's lack of respecting size and windows positions such as ubiquitin.jf's complaint about Firefox.  There doesn't seem to be a way to stop Unity from opening applications to full screen.  It's such a lost in efficiency when you have to perform so many clicks to get the applications under manageable sizes.

I never run applications in full screen.  But I'm constantly performing clicks to get programs out of full screen and reasonably positioned.  It would appear that a fix for that would be so easy.  Just have an application recall the way the user last used it.  I believe it was that way before Unity.  It's also that way in Windows.

Many of the complaints here are very legitimate and really should be addressed.  This thread has helped me to see, it's not just me or a glitch in my configuration.

### Post by coffeecat on 2011-12-20
> **apollothethird said:**
>  There doesn't seem to be a way to stop Unity from opening applications to full screen.  It's such a lost in efficiency when you have to perform so many clicks to get the applications under manageable sizes.

If an unmaximised window is sized to less than 75%, it will re-open unmaximised. If 75% or more, it will re-open maximised. You cannot change this threshold in 11.04, but you can in 11.10. Install compizconfig-settings-manager if not already installed > Desktop > Ubuntu Unity plugin > Experimental tab > Automaximise value. I find 85% to be a workable threshold for my particular laptop, but see which value suits your needs.

---

I second the bit about Firefox going full screen it even ditches the menu on my system, it seems like something to do with page encoding as yahoo and google pages switch the browser to full screen over and over, but other pages it stays windowed.

I think this one mite not be a Ubuntu only issue, but more of the browser and Ubuntu.

I have noticed on the windows versions of Firefox that Mozilla is going the route of microsoft and putting in add push "Features" that are requiring more and more addons to fix the new advertising problems added into web pages.

HTML5 has a whole new set of crap to overcomeall the addons that weed the advertising crap off of pages, and a whole new set of tracking BS.



I agree with you that Linux developers need to listen to what people say even if they are sure that the complaint is a user issue.


Some of the things I'd like to see in Ubuntu.

A better default hard drive setup in the Ubuntu installer, this having to look on the net and then choose a custom hard drive partitioning scheme is over the heads of most non-Linux Guru's.

Another thing is more/better settings in system settings, simple things like changing system colors shouldn't require loading an extra app or doing some cryptic terminal commands.

Another bug from a general user prospective is that there is no true unified install formats that work in all Linux versions.

Truth is over all Ubuntu is pretty impressive for free software but some simple things mare it from being ready for the majority of the computing illiterate.

Its a shame too as with microsofts rip off pricing on real versions of  windows 7 and all the spyware built in and microsofts business practices Ubuntu and Linux in general could be making major inroads into the market.

If Linux had a higher market share then major software company's would have to put out Linux versions off their software, and all hardware manufacturers would have to make Linux drivers.

Anyway keep up the good fight and get the developers to listen to the people.

### Post by jcoles on 2011-12-21
While there's much I don't like about Unity, I'm getting used to it. The worst part about Unity is the developers' attitude that we should just accept it because it's change and change is good. Not all change is good. 

Choice is good too. And there are other choices, even within Ubuntu. If Unity disappoints, looking at alternatives like Xubuntu and Lubuntu can provide some insights as to what you really want and need in a desktop environment. 

After playing with Xubuntu and Lubuntu, combining them with Docky and Cairo dock, I realize that Unity has one thing I can't find elsewhere -- a dock or panel that can be accessed entirely by keyboard. The touchpad on a laptop or netbook really sucks as a mouse, the less you need to use it the better. 

In my better-than-Unity search, the leader is Xubuntu with xfce4-appfinder and catfish to provide search capabilities. Gnome-Do is supposed to be good, but it's flaky and sometimes doesn't respond to its keyboard shortcut.

Docky is pretty and MacOSX-like, but doesn't offer much functionality beyond regular Xfce panels, which are more configurable. Keyboard navigation would be a major improvement for either Xfce panels or Docky.

Lubuntu is less configurable than Xubuntu, including its panels, in which you can create a launcher only from a menu item. This is like Docky, where you can only pin currently-running applications to the bar. In Xfce Launcher panels, you can create an empty item and specify the actual name, command line and icon that you want.

### Post by apollothethird on 2011-12-25
> **coffeecat said:**
> If an unmaximised window is sized to less than 75%, it will re-open unmaximised. If 75% or more, it will re-open maximised. You cannot change this threshold in 11.04, but you can in 11.10. Install compizconfig-settings-manager if not already installed > Desktop > Ubuntu Unity plugin > Experimental tab > Automaximise value. I find 85% to be a workable threshold for my particular laptop, but see which value suits your needs.

Thanks.  I read your answer immediately after you posted it.  I tried your resolution.  I took time in responding because I wanted to make sure it worked before I reported the results.

It works!

This will save me a lot of keystrokes and aggravation in the future.  I had been looking for ways to fix this forever.  I see now why it only happened some of the time.  It was because of the size of my previous window.  I uninstalled and reinstalled Firefox many times.  I reinstalled Ubuntu 11.10 a number of times.  Some of the times I thought iit was fixed for a while then went bad again.

I posted a number of messages in various forums.  I'll go back to those threads and post the resolution and mark the questions as solved.

This fix makes the thousand messages of this thread well worth the space.  I'm sure many people have found similar relief of their frustration in this thread for various issues also.

About the only other thing that I hate about Ubuntu is that in 11.10 they added clicks to bringing up applications where you could bring them up in two clicks in 11.04 and other versions.

In 11.04 you could click on More Apps and you'd likely see your preferred application in the short recent list.  Click on that and you'd have your application up and running.

They removed the More Apps button from 11.10.  Now you have to click on Dash Home -> More Apps -> (click the x to clear the recent search field) -> (then Click on your application).

That added two clicks to bringing up an application.  If those two extra clicks could be removed the efficient of Gnome 2 and Ubuntu 11.04 would be back.  Adding clicks to do frequent things is a bad thing.  Every time you sit at the computer you have to bring up applications.  Long as you're working you'll continue to have to bring up applications.  I can't understand the logic of adding clicks to that.  After the course of a day, for me, that's hundreds of extra clicks.

-- L. James

There are some people on here that really try and do help, they are about 1 in 100.

To get your question answered to your satisfaction, provide as much information as possible, including links to the program you are attempting to install.

### Post by quixote on 2012-01-12
(You've started something, haven't you? :D)

The ubuntu forums are actually models of politeness and helpfulness. You should have seen some of the old linux forums before Shuttleworth laid down the law for ubuntu! That said, the skipping-essential-steps is a real problem. It's very hard to avoid when something is second nature, so it's not necessarily due to arrogance (although it can be).

Re *.tar.gz: (you probably know all this by now, but what the hell) it's a unix/linux zip format. It can be used on anything: text files, programs, images. The file manager, eg Nautilus, will open an Archive manager if you double-click on a tar.gz file and offer to extract it for you. Alternatively, you can do it command line by opening a terminal and typing:```
tar -xvzf *filename* 
``` -x: extract,  
-v: verbose, so you can see what it's doing, 
-z: expect "gz" packing format (as opposed to "bz" which requires a "j" instead, e.g. -xvjf)
-f: here comes the filename

The command line way will unpack the file within whichever directory you run the command.

Once it's unpacked, what you do next depends on what it is. If it was just a way of compressing images, text, or backups, you're done. If it was a program, ideally it'll have a good README file that tells you what to do. As often as not, they skip steps :P. That's when you need the info about how to use ./configure, make, and make install. So, as the moderator said, help for your specific .tar.gz depends on knowing what you're trying to do. Also, some programs have to be dealt with as "sudo" or they won't run.

And I've probably skipped a few steps... ;)

### Post by peyre on 2012-01-12
> **ben44b said:**
> A company like Microsoft cannot charge hundreds of dollars and compete against an alternative that costs $0.

Sadly, you're almost certainly mistaken on that one.  Even though it costs more, it dominates the market, and will continue to do so for the foreseeable future, for several reasons.  
[LIST]
[*] It's what everyone knows (most people more or less equate Windows with having a computer).
[*] It's the de facto standard out there, and almost all software runs in Windows.
[*] It's much easier to find someone to help you with Windows than with Linux.
[*] Installs for Windows are always easy: download and double-click on Setup.exe, answer some questions, and you're done--and you'll always find it in your Start menu.
[*] Last but most important, Microsoft has sweetheart deals with the OEMs, so when you order a computer from most vendors it has Windows preinstalled by default, and you have to go out of your way to get one *without* it.  This means that people get Windows without even really thinking about it, and the way it's done, it's perceived as being free--the cost of Windows is included in the cost of the unit and not mentioned separately, and also the added cost of Windows is offset by the advertising money the OEMs get from third-party vendors that pay them to install crapware on their systems.
[/LIST]

---

### Post by varunendra on 2012-01-15
> **foska said:**
> Sometimes it seems that no one cares about your Ubuntu issues.
I'm pretty sure we all do. But we also care about the fact that **it is better to not make any comment than making a non-helpful one**. Because then your thread will show up in a general search as if is being replied (implying - 'is being helped'), causing the other experts, who otherwise could actually help, to move on ignoring that one.

If you post a problem which doesn't get answered for long, you may either bump your thread every 24 hours or more, or may try contacting experts personally (via pm or email) who you think are capable of solving your problem. Most often they will either try to help you, or will refer you to the right person for the job.

### Post by Simian Man on 2012-01-16
> **ewaynec said:**
> My most recent waste of time was trying to install xxx.tar.gz file. (BTW I have been a user since 8.04, but I have always found a way around using a tar file) I found about 100 different instructors with 100 different sets of instructions, none of them worked.

This is because there is no possible set of instructions on how to install any tarball.  A tarball is just an archive of files.  It could be source that needs to be compiled.  In that case you could use configure/make/make install.  Unless the author used cmake, or scons, or some other build system or even their own build system.  In any event in this case you may have to install dependencies yourself.  A tarball could also include pre-compiled programs in which case you could copy them to /usr/local/ or somewhere in your home directory.  In this case you may need to install dependencies or - even worse - different versions of dependencies.  You also may need to set your PATH or your ldconfig.

The tarball almost certainly came with instructions.  You should have read those and followed them, asking questions as needed.  If there was some process where any tarball could be installed correctly, it would be automated as it is for .deb files.  Basically the failure is your own.

### Post by Copper Bezel on 2012-01-16
You're not quite understanding what he's saying. "Installing a .tar.gz file" doesn't mean anything, because a .tar.gz is an archive format. It's the same thing as saying "installing a .zip file." A .zip file, or a .tar.gz file, isn't something you install. In Windows, you install .exe files. In Ubuntu, you install .deb files. 

If you're installing software in Linux that's distributed in a .tar.gz archive, that doesn't tell us anything about what's inside. What we're guessing you're talking about is something that's distributed as source code. That's well off the beaten path even for ordinary Linux users, the same as it is in Windows.

But again, the archive usually explains its contents; look for the readme file.

When you extract it you get CJZ600LE-CUPS-1.0-1.TAR, there is no read-me file, no instructions. So you go to the forums to get some instructions as to "What do I do next", now you get the 100 different ways it doesn't work.

Well you're right that installing from tarball isn't for beginners...or intermediate users either.  In my 2+ years in Linux, there's only one program I've ever been able to compile from source, and that was with help from one of its developers.  In my estimation, the lack of a unified package manager is a substantial weakness to Linux.  You have the main division between DEB and RPM based distros, plus oddballs like Gentoo's package manager, and the BSDs have their own (I know that's Unix not Linux, but come on), and then there are distros that install only from source.  It's crazy.  Honestly, you shouldn't *need* programmer chops to install a simple program, unless it's in alpha or something.

### Post by mutha88 on 2012-01-18
There is only one thing. Simple thing. It is called 5.1 surround. It is called 24 bit 96Hz sound. It is also known as a graphical interface or a "control panel" to manage your audio streams, channels, outputs, sample rates and a volume meter for each speaker.

Why isn't there anything for that sort of thing? Why can't I control the audio fast and efficiently in Linux? Why there isn't any graphical panel or menu or program, which gives me if not all then the most important things I need in order to control my sound?

This is why I am using still Windows. I hate it. I don't like the slow hard drives, the stupid viruses and processes in it, but there is one simple thing, called ASUS Xonar DX Audio Center. I open it and voala - little screen pops out. There I can control everything I need for my sound card, speakers and such. 

I like Kubuntu, KDE... Ubuntu and GNOME... I like to play with Linux, even though I don't use Terminal and I can't make complicated stuff with Ubuntu/Kubuntu, I like to use them! 

But please... please! Make a decent working audio player, which is like AIMP/Winamp/foobar2000! Please make a decent control panel for the audio settings! 

Stop the work with the interface - the interface is fine, we need Multimedia. That's all - we have everything else, just work on the Multimedia features, Canonical!

Thank you! Please excuse my hot temper and my bad English. 



I don't mean to be rude, I'm just sad and disappointed, that's all...

### Post by peyre on 2012-01-20
> **Garthhh said:**

"Perfect for organizations"

I don't think most people see that.  I think most people get Ubuntu recommended to them, they go and download it without doing a bunch of extra reading on the Web page (I probably wouldn't either), and then when they install it they find it's not what they expected.  A surprising amount of what you're just used to in Windows simply isn't there, or works very differently, in Linux.  That's not Linux's fault; it's because Windows is so standardized--but it's enough to land some folks in confusion & frustrationville.  I remember being similarly taken aback when I first tried Red Hat 6.2 & 7 back in the day, but I came back around eventually.  ('Course, it took Ubuntu, and Vista as its alternative at the time, to push me to do it.)

And you know what?  What if I did want to use Dreamweaver as a WYSIWYG editor?  Rather than trying to put me in my place (or the place you think I belong) why not tell me I'm wrong because Linux has XYZ WYSIWYG editor that's equally as good?  Just sayin...

I never meant to offend you personally, I have a way of doing that, sorry. I do both design and dev, I only use a text editor, yes even inside of a "woohoo" Wordpress/Drupal install (which i do all the time), I've never needed DreamWeaver, just syntax highlighting and an ftp program. I made the silly mistake of taking this thread and your post any other way than a personal opinion thread. If you like Dreamweaver then I have no right to criticize you for your wanting it. BUT GIMP > PHOTOSHOP all day long (lol).

### Post by Ashtray2 on 2012-01-21
> **|{urse said:**
> I never meant to offend you personally, I have a way of doing that, sorry. I do both design and dev, I only use a text editor, yes even inside of a "woohoo" Wordpress/Drupal install (which i do all the time), I've never needed DreamWeaver, just syntax highlighting and an ftp program. I made the silly mistake of taking this thread and your post any other way than a personal opinion thread. If you like Dreamweaver then I have no right to criticize you for your wanting it. BUT GIMP > PHOTOSHOP all day long (lol).

Thats cool that ur a real designer/developer.  I guess maybe if your only alternative is Aptana or Eclipse then a text editor might not seem so bad :biggrin:

I've heard stories of some people who used a text editor for X amount of years and never thought they needed anything more.  But they give a REAL IDE a shot and they don't look back.

If you're making edits to multiple files at once, do you really want constantly have to look in your unorganized taskbar to find the file you want to work on?  So I dont know how you deal with that hassle but that's on you.

Dreamweaver can save, upload, and test your website on a remote server with the press of a button.  So maybe it only saves you 10 seconds.  But if you're making constant edits and need to constantly check your work, that time adds up fast.  And its not just about beating time, its beating the monotonous process of hitting save and copying over the files manually.

I could probably list a million other ways Dreamweaver rules but I haven't used it in about a year.

Edit:  Have you actually ever used Dreamweaver?  I think I got back on the DW bus with either CS3 or CS4 and its very improved from MX.  I'll be honest, I would install DW on wine just for the aesthetics alone.  Making web pages in an text editor or Eclipse is depressing to even think about having to stare at those "unpolished" interfaces.  Maybe I'm just sensitive to my environment.  Its just more enjoyable to be working within something that looks nice.  And I think no matter how many boastable features DW has, thats one thing the everyman can appreciate.

### Post by GuiGuy on 2012-01-22

### Post by ayenack on 2012-01-22
I've bee using Ubuntu since 2005 (switched from redhat) and enjoyed the progression that it made right up until the Unity GUI and maybe just a little beforehand (basically when they changed the GUI scheme to black things began to go down hill for me).

Unity was made the default GUI and I just could not live with it so I started building from minimal install to Openbox GUI and carried on like that right up until 11.10 when I decided that I'd had enough of bloated Ubuntu and would build my own Distro based on Ubuntu with an Openbox GUI.

Before I `bit the bullet` I decided to have a look around to see if there was anything out there that met my requirements light, fast, and not trying to be fasionable.

I came across [CrunchBang]("http://crunchbanglinux.org/") Linux which is basicall Debian Squeeze with Openbox.

I downloaded and installed it and have not looked back.

I had forgotten what it's like to have such a quick and responsive system more or less like Ubuntu used to be before the bloat.

If you'd like to get back to a rational system again then take a look at [CrunchBang]("http://crunchbanglinux.org/"). Try it in a Virtual Machine and see how you get along with it.

I'm loving it.

I'll take a look at Ubuntu 12.04 just to see how things are going but I think from now on I'll be using [CrunchBang]("http://crunchbanglinux.org/").

### Post by WinRiddance on 2012-02-08
I hate the anti-ubuntu and anti-linux efforts of destroying pure ubuntu user freedom by replacing that with short sighted Unity. What made Gnome2 truly special was the fact that anyone could easily customize it in just about any manner to fit that person's desires. That's what freedom is all about, and that's also part of the meaning of Ubuntu, the humanity aspect, the freedom.

The fact that Unity could even be born in the first place is disturbing to me, so very much like Windoze ... you can do this, but you can't do that, you may go here, but you shouldn't edit that ... freedom went right out of the "window" with Unity. The rapid development of Cinnamon & Mate are further proof of this.

Instead of creating a new desktop environment and forcing that on people completely (if it was entirely up to Mark Shuttleworth), it's actually Unity that should have been a new optional desktop version with apps that could be "made to fit" for Unity if or when said developers of such apps chose to do so. I know, that topic has been beaten to death, but it offends me personally since the meaning of the word "UBUNTU" is what got me into Linux/Ubuntu in the first place.

.

---

### Post by rg4w on 2012-02-08
> **WinRiddance said:**
> I hate the anti-ubuntu and anti-linux efforts of destroying pure ubuntu user freedom by replacing that with short sighted Unity. What made Gnome2 truly special was the fact that anyone could easily customize it in just about any manner to fit that person's desires. That's what freedom is all about, and that's also part of the meaning of Ubuntu, the humanity aspect, the freedom.
Gnome2 was not killed by Canonical, but by the Gnome Project itself.

As for Ubuntu, the source code remains available, the GPL licensing remains intact; you have the freedom to make whatever changes you want to it, fully in accord with The Four Freedoms.

If instead you require that others implement your preferences for you, would we be talking about your own freedom or a developer's slavery?

Consider the middle path:  Gnome Shell is just a click away in the USC.  And then there's Mint, and a hundred other worthy distros freely available to suit every taste.

Many options are available.  All freedom remains, including the freedom of every developer to focus their own efforts as they choose.

---

### Post by peyre on 2012-02-09

### Post by Andrew_P on 2012-02-16
I hate the Ubuntu Software Center.  It's cobbled together from a collection of Python scripts and it's fragile.

Last week I uninstalled OpenOffice.org on Lucid Lynx (10.04.1) and installed LibreOffice 3.4.  Uninstallation of OpenOffice.org via Ubuntu Software Center was a mess, requiring removal module-by-module, instead of the entire suite with one command.  Installation of LibreOffice was a mess, requiring a multi-step installation from the command line in a terminal window, very unlike the typical .deb package installation in Ubuntu.  In the end it caused problems for the Update Manager.  While clearing up the problems, Update Manager deleted LibreOffice, cups-pdf, and several other things, without being asked to do so.  I re-installed LibreOffice.  Now Update Manager is happy, but Software Center is damaged, and after nearly a week of searching through forums and trying every suggested fix, I still can't get it to work again. It just *sits *there with the spinner cursor going 'round and 'round.  The Software Center was supposed to make software installation *easy*, especially for the novice, but it's mostly a source of frustration. ](*,)

After wasting a week, this morning I discovered the **[Ubuntu Apps Directory]("https://apps.ubuntu.com/cat/")**, an on-line version of Ubuntu Software Center.  It will do for now, and maybe will turn out to be a better solution than the one that gets installed on one's hard drive.

overlay-scrollbar
liboverlay-scrollbar-0.2-0

### Post by GuiGuy on 2012-03-23
> **cariboo907 said:**
> Does the problem show up in other distributions? Or is it an Ubuntu only problem?

I only know that someone using hardware that I gave them, hardware that was running natty and had the USB issue, works OK for him with fedora. 

The problem only exists with USB flash memory drives in my case, and has done so since Karmic. USB HDD work reasonably well. 

I suspect it is some sort of caching or buffering issue, because files are transferred in bursts at fast speed, then the transfer stops for a minute or two and the cycle starts over. This is why the problem only becomes noticable when transferring larger files (600MB > ). No matter, I have posted these and many more observations in launchpad, as have others, yet nothing gets done about it. I repeat, shame on them.

### Post by Ryan Csh on 2012-03-24

### Post by Copper Bezel on 2012-04-09
[QUOTE=peyre]So I wasn't just missing something! When I close my laptop lid, the computer goes into what would better be called a coma--I can't get it to resume with the power button or anything else, and the only thing to do is power it off and reboot.[/QUOTE]
I don't have any real problems with suspend and resume on my machine, but it's a major trouble spot for bugs, along with graphics cards and WiFi chips. Your problem can probably be solved, but there are doubtless some machines for which there aren't fixes.

That's separate from issues with the power manager's intended behaviors and features. (Which are not really a strength of the Gnome desktop.)

### Post by peyre on 2012-04-09
> **chenliyong said:**
> I am a new user of ubuntu and linux, and what I "hate" about ubuntu is that it's not like what people advertised.

It takes **so hard** to do simple things in linux, even in ubuntu, like installing application, getting wireless running, and listening music. And almost every problems has to resort to command line hacks at some point, with "alien" language that's a little bit "frightening". I just don't really know, how the people can have an ubuntu that "configured just the way I like". But, after all, I can't work with an OS that I have to hardly configured first before I can use it properly for daily task. I spent time 5 times more on fixing drivers problem or installing some programs, than writing some useful proposal or creating some presentation for work.

but I still want to give ubuntu a chance. I'm curious, and I want to see and experience, how long does it takes, before ubuntu becomes "configured just the way I like", like what people all over the internet boasts.

Some of what you describe sounds like just getting used to different applications to do what you've always done in Windows--and getting used to the extra options available (e.g., instead of Win Media Player you have Banshee, Rhythmbox, Parole, etc.).  That's an adjustment that would come with switching to any new operating system--if you bought a Mac, for instance (though with a Mac you wouldn't be faced with such a confusing range of options).

I can commiserate with you on some things here though.  I sometimes grumble to myself that "Everything's harder to do in Linux", but I have to remind myself that some things are actually easier.  Installing software can actually be easier than in Windows, for instance.  It sounds like you've been installing from source, and that certainly is doing it the hard way.  If what you want is in the Ubuntu Software Center, you can just select it and tell it to install, then wait while Ubuntu installs it for you automatically.  I'd say maybe a third of things are easier in Linux, and two-thirds are harder.  I do have to pay more attention when I'm doing something new or tricky in Linux.

I think the need to dink around at the command line has several causes.  One is that Linux has always been centered around the needs of programmers, who are at home with a command line.  Also it's partly because Linux is much more of a DIY (do-it-yourself) environment.  And third, because it's a small minority of the install base, it doesn't get nearly the driver support from manufacturers that Windows does.  Since the manufacturers don't always write Linux drivers, the Linux community has to create its own drivers and workarounds to get it working--and home-grown solutions are usually less polished than something done by the manufacturer.

---

### Post by Copper Bezel on 2012-04-10
As a Shell fanboy, I'd generally agree, but I think it's good for Ubuntu to have a visual identity and good for consumers to have options. (I. e., so long as *I* can use Gnome, I don't really care what ships, so long as that thing shipping is still a quality product.)

[QUOTE=Garthhh]that's an interesting view
in my world having a little money is better than having none[/QUOTE]
It wasn't money, though, it was developer time. That meant that Canonical had to sign off on the product (that is, include it in the core rather than the Universe repos.) I think the claim is a bit dubious and sour-grapes-y, but it's not *completely* baseless.

### Post by cariboo on 2012-04-10
> **chenliyong said:**
> I am a new user of ubuntu and linux, and what I "hate" about ubuntu is that it's not like what people advertised.

It takes **so hard** to do simple things in linux, even in ubuntu, like installing application, getting wireless running, and listening music. And almost every problems has to resort to command line hacks at some point, with "alien" language that's a little bit "frightening". I just don't really know, how the people can have an ubuntu that "configured just the way I like". But, after all, I can't work with an OS that I have to hardly configured first before I can use it properly for daily task. I spent time 5 times more on fixing drivers problem or installing some programs, than writing some useful proposal or creating some presentation for work.

but I still want to give ubuntu a chance. I'm curious, and I want to see and experience, how long does it takes, before ubuntu becomes "configured just the way I like", like what people all over the internet boasts.

If you have to use the terminal to do most daily tasks, then you are doing something wrong. Unless you are using bleeding edge hardware, almost all drivers are included in the instillation, the only exception is closed source video drivers, and some closed source wireless drivers. Installing software is as easy as starting the Software Center searching for what you need, marking it for installation, and clicking apply. If the Software Center doesn't work well for you, install Synaptic Package Manager, to see if you like that better.

The one thing to keep in mind, you have probably spent most of your computing life using and learning Windows. How many times have you had a perfect Windows installation right out of the box, without having to add any extra software. If you have a brand name computer system, you are lucky as all the work is done for you, but if you custom build your own systems and use a whitebox OEM windows disk, nothing is included in the install, you need to get the drivers from the hardware manufacturers, DVD's and MP3's won't play out of the box either, and the finished isntall is basically useless, as you can only get to the desktop, you have to download and install everything needed to do simple daily tasks.

### Post by Flymo on 2012-04-10
Really hate?  
That's a bit harsh.   
I do get pi**ed off when things don't work right (or not the way I expect) - Unity is (unfortunately) a good example of that for our family.<sigh>
I might even post some gruesome rant whilst in the heat of disappointment.
And then regret it.](*,)
Fortunately there is Xubuntu and Lubuntu too. 
Plus stunning 'buntu respins like Bodhi with its light but glossy E17 goodness and all-new stability.
.
Kubuntu? ...well, since KDE4 our hardware doesn't enjoy Kubuntu so much.  It does work reliably now and it looks pretty whizzy, but it just does not snap and crackle like the lighter desktops do on an Atom (eg).  If one of our n00bs wanted to try KDE4 on a decently powerful  machine, I'd cheer them on. Used to love KDE3.  Guess I'm generally with Linus when it comes to desktop choice.
The Ubuntu family has been good to us since we first had a go with Breezy, years ago. 
So it's really hard to sustain lasting hate even for small parts of it.
.
Things that *do* arouse my ire on a regular basis are the wannabe/successful monopolies who lock too-trusting people into their closed platforms and milk them until they're dry. 

I'm no fan of born-again Bill Gates, who [famously lied]("http://www.freeinfosociety.com/article.php?id=74") in order to get his first big deal with MITS, nor am I fan of the late Steve Jobs who [lied about money to his buddy Woz]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Steve_Jobs#Early_career") so early on.
That Woz forgave him says more about his own generosity than about Jobs.

The companies these people built reflect their own shallow values, and that I do hate!:mad:
It turns out that the things I hate are not in Ubuntu at all.
Ubuntu is pretty good, folks, especially compared with proprietary software monopolies (or wannabes).
Ben

### Post by Penguinnerd on 2012-04-17
> **craig10x said:**
>  because it's set up different, they instantly reject it...

I've definitely given it a fair chance. My complaint is not that it looks different. My complaint is in how slow it is to use compared to the traditional desktop metaphor. I need to get things done. I like things that save me time.

Really, I made my point quite well when I invited you to compare the number of clicks and key presses required to do anything that isn't on your dock in Unity.

In Gnome 2, I can currently open almost any application in two clicks. I can move panels to suit my personal preferences. I can delete, resize, move and add panels as I please. It gives the user some freedom. 

Unity is for people who can't handle options.

If Gnome 2 is for people who don't like change, then Unity is for people who only want to be entertained by their OS without getting anything done.

### Post by rg4w on 2012-04-17
> **Penguinnerd said:**
> I've definitely given it a fair chance. My complaint is not that it looks different. My complaint is in how slow it is to use compared to the traditional desktop metaphor.
The desktop metaphor is still there.  All that's happened is the addition of a Launcher.  You still have Nautilus, you still have Terminal, and you can even go back to the hierarchical menus that drove Gnome 2 by selecting Classic at login.

But before you do, consider the reams of usability research over the last few decades showing just how physically difficult hierarchical menus are for most people to use.

Also consider that most people use only a dozen or fewer apps most days, which happens to be about as many as can fit comfortably in the Launcher.

Dash is still a bit of a mess, but it's getting better.  But really, most folks don't need to use it often because the Launcher keeps the apps they use most often just one click away - far simpler than digging through hierarchical menus to find them.

I can appreciate the challenge of getting used to Unity.  I skipped 11.04 because it was so poorly implemented in that version, but it's been much improved in 11.10 and far more so in 12.04.

I won't try to dissuade you from using anything else.  Use what works for you; Classic is still an option in 12.04.

Just please keep in mind that all the changes in Unity were done with careful thought about the implications for most users, while retaining the flexibility to support those who prefer a different way of working.

Unity's far from perfect, but aside from concealing menus by default in all other respects I would imagine it scores much higher in overall usability than any previous Ubuntu version.

### Post by jshepherd on 2012-04-17
Printing... Well sort of.
If I could print anywhere near the speed and quality of Windows I wouldn't dual boot. 
Even XP makes printing pictures easy with Windows picture and Fax viewer.
Why can't we have something like that? I've tried photprint and that's OK but really? Is it that difficult to have an application as simple to use as windows picture viewer? Just browse your files - in my case photos taken on my mobile phone, choose print and the majority of common sizes ar there ready to go. No resizing images and guessing when the printer will spit out a low quality print - eventually. 
Other than that, Ubuntu suits me perfectly. Even my kids like it for their schoolwork except when their teacher has encouraged them to download some application which is Windows only and WINE won't even run it. 
BTW, text printing is fine just photo's I have issue with.

Just browse your files - in my case photos taken on my mobile phone, choose print and the majority of common sizes ar there ready to go. No resizing images and guessing when the printer will spit out a low quality print - eventually. 
Other than that, Ubuntu suits me perfectly. Even my kids like it for their schoolwork except when their teacher has encouraged them to download some application which is Windows only and WINE won't even run it. 
BTW, text printing is fine just photo's I have issue with.

Yes, I've found a good one.  In Windows I use IrfanView, an excellent free picture viewer with some basic editing capabilities, and it's fast.  In Linux, I've found a pretty close drop-in replacement: Image Viewer (gpicview).  It's in the Ubuntu repository and it's free; honestly I don't know why it's not the default in Ubuntu.

### Post by shieldw0lf on 2012-04-17
> **Penguinnerd said:**
> I've definitely given it a fair chance. My complaint is not that it looks different. My complaint is in how slow it is to use compared to the traditional desktop metaphor. I need to get things done. I like things that save me time.

Really, I made my point quite well when I invited you to compare the number of clicks and key presses required to do anything that isn't on your dock in Unity.

In Gnome 2, I can currently open almost any application in two clicks. I can move panels to suit my personal preferences. I can delete, resize, move and add panels as I please. It gives the user some freedom. 

Unity is for people who can't handle options.

If Gnome 2 is for people who don't like change, then Unity is for people who only want to be entertained by their OS without getting anything done.

Amen.  

I'm reminded of conflicts I used to have with a former Project Manager I worked for... he was from the camp who believes an ideal piece of software is so easy to use that someone who has never used it before can figure out what's going on.  For him, if the software confused the executive he was going to be showing it to before he collected the cheque, that was the ultimate design flaw.

Myself, when I design something, I take the attitude "The user has spent the last month becoming familiar with this interface and learning their way around.  They are invested in mastering this tool.  What will maximize the users effectiveness."

Executives, who will never actually use the tool to do any work, they love the first approach.  Until the inefficiencies introduced into their company start damaging the bottom line, which is what they do.

The people who use the tools to do their jobs... they LOVE my designs.  I need to convince the executive that they are too far removed from the day to day operations of their business to tell me what they need and do proper requirements gathering interviews to deliver such designs, and be trusted to know better than my employer what their system should look like.  

It takes a humble man to admit that he's not equipped to tell you what he needs.  Most execs can't do it.  But the ones that can, they don't get something shiny and easy to learn.  They get something powerful and efficient to use, and they make a lot more money.

As for consumers looking for an entertainment device... <snip>.  Who cares what they want.  There's nothing I can deliver to them that will be better for them than going outside and getting some fresh air anyway.

What do I hate about Unity?  I hate the philosophy they bring to design.  

My drill press does not have sparkles on it.  Neither should my computer.

### Post by cariboo on 2012-04-18
I guess google is broken again, the reasons were pretty well documented. Briefly, when Gnome made the switch from Gnone 2 to Gnome 3, gnome-shell was due to be released about the same time as Oneiric, not allowing enough time to properly integrate and test it before Oneiric was released. Unity was already available as a netbook interface, and it was decided to upgrade Unity instead of using the untested and unfinished gnome-shell.

Check Mark Shuttleworth's [blog]("http://www.markshuttleworth.com/") for more history.

### Post by Copper Bezel on 2012-04-19
[QUOTE=peyre]As a Xubuntu user, my default file manager is Thunar, which IMO kick a great deal of a**. It's fast, simple, and works well. The only drawbacks to it that I know of are that it won't manage shares (though it can connect to shares), and (like other Linux file managers) its developer refuses to consider adding the option to rename files via the click-pause-click that Windows and Mac users are accustomed to.[/QUOTE]
I used Thunar for quite some time under Gnome. With 11.10, it seemed redundant, since Nautilus now resembles Thunar a lot more. Thunar's custom actions are still better than Nautilus's scripts, but it's not worth the trouble to me of using an FM other than the default and dealing with slightly weaker integration with the DE. The custom actions I actually used in Thunar were largely features available by default in Nautilus anyway, like the "Make Link" command and Dropbox integration.

I would give a kidney for search-as-you-type in the search box, though - and for search results to come up in a new tab, and for moving files from a search to actually move them, instead of copying. Um.
