### Post by peyre on 2013-06-02
> **apollothethird said:**
> Something very complex to access in Windows.  You can't just normally bring it up in Windows just like any other program.  You have to do something special and hidden.

The Ubuntu equivalence is to just bring up the system monitor just like you bring up any program...  you can get to is just by hitting Ubuntu Launcher -> "M", then click on it.

Very complex??  Ctrl-Alt-Delete, which you use often (at least in the business version) brings up several options, one of which is Task Manager.  The other way is to right-click on the Taskbar, and again it offers that as an option.  It's not hard to bring up Task Manager; it just isn't offered in the Start menu.
 
This sounds dangerously close to disliking that Windows doesn't act like Ubuntu.

---

### Post by Ubun2to on 2013-06-03
> **peyre said:**
> Very complex??  Ctrl-Alt-Delete, which you use often (at least in the business version) brings up several options, one of which is Task Manager.  The other way is to right-click on the Taskbar, and again it offers that as an option.  It's not hard to bring up Task Manager; it just isn't offered in the Start menu.
 
This sounds dangerously close to disliking that Windows doesn't act like Ubuntu.
In XP, if you had the old login menu (where you have a Windows 98 style login screen), you had to do the key combo and then click on task manager, if you kept the new login screen, the key combo brought the task manager right up.
In Vista and 7, the key combo brings up a menu with other functions, such as shutdown and log off, and you have to manually click on the task manager (in XP with the old login screen, the functions were the same, with a different UI).
In 8, I'm not sure what the combo does-I do not have a key currently. But, I do know you have to be on the desktop to right click on the taskbar and open it from there (or you could use something like Start8).

### Post by vadimkolchev on 2013-06-07
So finally I leave Ubuntu and want to say goodbye to everyone. Reasons are simple. Ubuntu becomes less and less Linux. It is just ... Ubuntu. Hiding everything Linux is good for and loved for under the hood as deep as it is possible, and deeper with every release. User friendly, you say? Not at all. Some people using Ubuntu for a year do not know how to navigate file system in cli, and are helpless without gui. Unity, you say? Piece of bloatware ythat is made mandatory. Linux has always been about choice. This is not the case anymore. And they plan to make a single click installation with no dependancies... Each program in its own folder. With a bunch of embedded libs, that will lead to many duplicate files, just located in different dirs. And don't say that Ubuntu is not for everyone. It is intended to be, if you remember. I demand choice. Today I found out that I can instal one piece of software via software center, but it is not accessible via apt-get install. Different packaging, different way of distribution of software. Ubuntu is forgetting that it is Linux first of all. I don't want to point and click, I want to use cli, and now I cannot. Take away your free 5Gb cloud and bring me freedom back. This is a windows way. And Linux does not need it. Soon because of Ubuntu people will forget what power is hidden under the hood, because the money canobical spends on it makes people think that this is an example ofwhat Linux is. I refuse to take Ubuntu as Linux from now. It has been great 5 years ago, but now it is a real windows in linux world. That is why it gets all frsh faces into this world. It just becomes more and more similar to windows, and thats why newbies cvhoose it. But it will end up the same as windows. Bloatware and crap, nothing more, and user friendliness, that will make people stop thinking at all and willnmake them into the forums asking what cd command is and why their directory was not found. so, goodbye everyone, thanks for good company and friendliness,  it is not about you, people, it's about canonical decisions. You are great and I hate to leave you but I have no choice. I have no intention to give my freedom away and to stop thinkang and begin clocking. I knowbwhat distro I go too, but I think it is a little wrong to discuss it here. Don't want to advertise.

---

### Post by monkeybrain2012 on 2013-06-07
> **Flos Headford said:**
> I would like to get rid of rhythmbox.
But when I try to uninstall it from Synaptic, it threatens me with uninstalling Gnome!
I like Gnome. I dislike rhythmbox. If somebody can help in the next few days, I just might to decide not install Mint. Just...

You installed Ubuntu Gnome? I always get rid of rthythmbox with no problem. But then I use Unity.

Did a google search that appears to be a Debian packaging problem, but it says gnome is a meta package so it can be safety removed.

[http://www.linuxquestions.org/questions/debian-26/apt-wants-to-remove-gnome-660324/](http://www.linuxquestions.org/questions/debian-26/apt-wants-to-remove-gnome-660324/)
[http://forums.debian.net/viewtopic.php?f=10&t=81055](http://forums.debian.net/viewtopic.php?f=10&t=81055)

But be careful, I also saw this
[http://unix.stackexchange.com/questions/32793/how-to-remove-gnome-games-package-without-removing-other-packages](http://unix.stackexchange.com/questions/32793/how-to-remove-gnome-games-package-without-removing-other-packages)

So make sure that it doesn't remove anything else other than "gnome" and rthythmbox

Likewise, the people who hate Unity are very vocal about it, while those who enjoy using it stay out of the way to avoid the witch hunt that always seems to happen whenever someone says "I like Unity" on a website not primarily about Ubuntu (in a good light, of course-anti-Ubuntu websites have nothing but hate for Unity). This leads people to a false conclusion that Unity sucks, as they only hear about how it is bad, rarely about how it is good.

### Post by varunendra on 2013-06-07
> **cdoebbler said:**
> The moderators on Ubuntuforums, who close forums because they criticize Ubuntu 13.XX shortcomings.
Thank God I'm not a moderator :D

As far as I have noticed, any discussion is kept open as long as it is fresh and relevant. As soon as it starts going round and round in circles, discussing things that have already been discussed hundreds or thousands of times before, it is MOVED to "Recurring Discussions" section which is the right place for such threads.

Then, quite reasonably, there is a rule to close threads in Recurring Discussions after a week. Sometimes these threads may stay open longer because it has to be closed manually, so it is done when mods have the time to pay attention to them. I don't see anything 'oppressive' in this whole procedure. Everything is done as should be on a forum that is meant to provide technical support, not to clutter search results with 'going-nowhere' discussion threads.

The only threads that are closed premature are those ones that start getting ugly due to various reasons (trolling, flamewar, politics, etc.). Again, perfectly according to the Code of Conduct, and in the interest of everyone.

If you know of a thread that you think was closed wrongfully, you can always report it, or dispute it in the Resolution Center, and if it was indeed wrongfully closed, I'm sure whoever of us notices that thread will happily echo your voice there, including mods (they are just people from among us, able and willing to do some extra work). And if there were justifiable reasons for its closing, you will most probably get friendly and detailed justification there as to why it was closed (provided you don't start ranting there ;)).

Oh, and yes, mods and admins CAN BE Wrong! After all, they are humans.

Hi, Ubun2to.

The person you're referring to about formating paragraphs wrote a very hard to read message.  I tried to read the whole message so that I could reply to it in it's full content.  The lack of formating, as you pointed out made it very hard to read.  I got dizzy trying to read it.

Your message is just as hard.  Maybe you encrypted it with a lack of formating to make a point to Vadimkolchev.  It was a strong point.  I tried, but I couldn't read your whole message.  Without white spaces (especially between thoughts or paragraphs), it was so run-on, but I couldn't take it in.

What space doesn't take any room on the hard drive or the data band width.  So try to be kind to the reader and put more white space in your messages.

A person will grab a chunk of info, and process it, then grab another chunk.

However without white space, it's hard to tell where to pause for processing the information.  If you pause at the wrong time, you might not get the intended point.  If you don't pause while reading, you could get lost.

Be kind to the reader.  Provide white spaces between gulps of information.

-- L. James

-- 
L. D. James

### Post by Ubun2to on 2013-06-08
> **vadimkolchev said:**

Confirmed for failing at trolling.
> **vadimkolchev said:**

I think the developers of Elementary got that one down.
> **vadimkolchev said:**
> And I think windows to be worse than ubuntu, but they will vry soon be on the same level.
I do not think Canonical will sink to the likes of M$, trying to stop competing OSs by doing things such as Secure (Restricted) Boot and charging ridiculous prices.
> **vadimkolchev said:**
> And ok, kid, let developers decide for ypu, like in ms.
It's not letting devs decide for you if you switch distros in the same way that it is not letting politicians decide for you by voting for the other party and/or becoming a politician. You can modify the source, submit bug fixes, and get involved without becoming the boss of the devs. M$ does not decide for you, either. The way you tell the devs at M$ what you want is with your money, by not buying licenses (and likewise, by not pirating it or using it at all). The way you tell the devs in the Linux world is by participating in the community and (if need be) switching distros. Everyone is different, and to tailor an Ubuntu install to every single person's wants would be stupid. Not everyone's ideas can be included.

### Post by GuiGuy on 2013-06-09
.... that every day there is yet another mind numbingly, infuriating gem of nonsense this crazy OS is capable off:

1) I have a server (HP/Proliant)  running Ubuntu 12.04 LTS server. 
2) There is a printer connected and shared enabled
3) Connecting and using the printer from Windows clients, no problem.
4) Connecting and using the printer from Ubuntu clients does not work:

"FirewallD is not running. Network printer detection needs services mdns, ipp, ipp-client and samba-client enabled on firewall."

Oh the irony; to use a shared printer on Ubuntu one needs a Windows client PC.

Footnote: It goes without saying that I have spent countless hours trying to overcome this. I have given up now.

### Post by coffeecat on 2013-06-09
> **GuiGuy said:**
> 
Oh the irony; to use a shared printer on Ubuntu one needs a Windows client PC.

You don't say what printer. It's not a [GDI printer]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Winprinter#GDI_printers") (winprinter) by any chance?

> a GDI printer requires a PC running Windows, and is not compatible with other hardware and operating systems. In general GDI printers are not compatible with hardware print servers, although some servers have built-in processing capability making them compatible with specified GDI printers. GDI printers can be made available to computers on a network if they are connected as shared printers on a computer which is on and running Windows.

### Post by Swappiness on 2013-06-10
When uninstalling something in Ubuntu Software Centre only the programs are deleted and leaves behind orphaned packages and then the user has to delete them manually with Deborphan or Terminal. I know I can use Synaptic to conpletely remove a program and it's packages but for a noob like me it's hard to find a program when you don't know it's file name e.g. unity-tweak-tool or the Files (the file manager app that is install with Ubuntu).

I know I can just go into USC and find the file name by searching but this takes too long and I just want to have the option to un install something with 1 app instead of 3 or 4 apps. I am getting used to Ubuntu and all the apps and not a day goes by without learning something new.

They're about the only things I can think of at the moment that could do with improving. Of course I'll point out anything else I find when something annoys me lol.

By far out of all the Linux distros I've tried Ubuntu is the best. It feels as natural to me as Windows and maybe even more so and I like that it's a lot easier than Windows to apply themes and learning how the OS works is also easy. I've learned more about Ubuntu and Linux in past month or so than I ever have with any Version of Windows since actually owning my own pc. Ubuntu will definitely be the main OS that I'll use between now and whenever I decide I've have had enough of computers (which probably will never happen).

### Post by Copper Bezel on 2013-06-10
[Click packages]("http://www.webupd8.org/2013/05/ubuntu-might-get-new-simplified.html") might do a little bit to alleviate this problem. It's a system being developed for the phone and tablet, but usable on the desktop, too, that uses a sort of OSX-type application folder method - like, each app is supposed to be dependency-free in some sense. It wouldn't apply to all new apps or any system components, but it might become a more common format for distributing Ubuntu end-user applications.

### Post by monkeybrain2012 on 2013-06-10
> **Swappiness said:**
> When uninstalling something in Ubuntu Software Centre only the programs are deleted and leaves behind orphaned packages and then the user has to delete them manually with Deborphan or Terminal. I know I can use Synaptic to conpletely remove a program and it's packages but for a noob like me it's hard to find a program when you don't know it's file name e.g. unity-tweak-tool or the Files (the file manager app that is install with Ubuntu).
.

In synaptic there is a category called autoremovable. Those are things that were installed as dependencies and no longer needed. You can also do in the terminal "sudo apt-get autoremove" 
or "sudo apt-get autoremove programname" if you want to remove a specific program along with its dependencies (you can also use aptitude)

BTW, deborphan is dangerous and it removes things that are even not "orphaned" (just becuase there is nothing dependent on x doesn't mean x is orphaned, you may just want x rather than installing it as a dependency!) So don't use it,

### Post by Copper Bezel on 2013-06-16
Most of that is seriously unusual, and I don't know whether it's a bad install or just unusual hardware conditions. There are certainly a few problems on a typical Ubuntu install that a typical Windows install doesn't have, but I've not had WiFi card problems in Ubuntu for several years, and I certainly haven't had the session crash on changing settings (what were you trying to change?) Looking at your support thread, you obviously had a bad experience here to begin with, and it's very unusual (and extremely unfortunate to say the least) that the installer somehow messed up one of your existing partitions - it's supposed to, and generally does, create a new partition for the new system, and I haven't seen what you're reporting happen when that option is selected. (You could get there by doing manual partitioning in the installer, but I'm assuming you didn't do that.) Bad day, in any case, and I think that if everyone's first experience with Ubuntu or Linux was like that, you'd see a lot fewer people here at the Ubuntu forums.

>  [COLOR=#000000][FONT=Ubuntubeta]it throws an exception and asks if I'd like to send an error report every few minutes,[/FONT][/COLOR]

This bit *is* annoying, though. I get a hell of a lot of these, seemingly unrelated to anything the desktop is actually doing, and always have to disable Apport (the service that produces them.) Very irritating and not at all comforting, these messages.

### Post by DrewT on 2013-06-29
I can't express how disappointed I am with 13.04. I actually started having problems (mainly with removable disks) in 12.04; those problems seem to have gone away, but I now have really lousy video playback, the fix for which (I guess) is to replace my nVidia drivers with something else. Why should I have to be fixing something that worked fine for years? And what was done to Nautilus is unforgivable. It used to be the best file manager I used; now it's the worst. (Yes, worse than Windows Explorer in XP, and way worse than Finder.) Why? I guess the developers may have some rationalization for this stuff, but breaking stuff that worked very well -- and telling people they've got to manually customize their systems just to restore previous functionality -- is not a prescription for success. 

I've thought very seriously about reverting to 11.10, but everything I've read on that subject makes it look like I'd just be buying another bushel of headaches. The one thing I have learned is to stop updating just because an update is available. If I ever get another installation that I like -- and that works -- I'm sticking with it until there's a damn good reason to change.

---

I was also disappointed by 13.04--similar problems on my main computer (I couldn't get it to install the nVidia driver properly and it would reset my display to 1024x768 60MHz on every reboot no matter what).  I reverted to 12.10 on it and it's running just fine.  My laptop is still running 12.04 because the latest versions don't support the old AMD processor on this ze4125.  Nautilus hasn't been much of an issue for me because I'm a Xubuntu user and Xubuntu uses Thunar as its default file manager.  Thunar is awesome--it's lightweight but still fully functional and includes a fully functional Send To in the right-click menu just like Windows Explorer.  The only thing it doesn't do is let you manage shares--that is the one thing I still have to use Nautilus for.

This has always been the case with any version of Ubuntu that I have installed. ### Post by coffeecat on 2013-08-05
Ubuntu does not hijack the BIOS. After you power on the computer, the BIOS first performs the POST (power on self test), then hands over to the grub bootloader, which in turn boots you into Ubuntu. If you are having problems getting into the BIOS or if BIOS settings are not sticking, then this is a problem with your BIOS.

---

### Post by craig10x on 2013-08-05
That is very true...i have been running ubuntu or an alternate linux distro exclusively on every one of the toshiba laptops i have purchased over the last few years and always wipe out windows...i never have a problem resetting my bios to boot off of the cd/dvd drive first...i always do that because i often like to test live sessions of new versions of linux distros...As coffeecat said, it must be a problem with your computer's bios...it is not linux or ubuntu related at all...

### Post by buzzingrobot on 2013-08-05
> **stankopp said:**
> I hate how it high jacks the BIOS.  I find it very difficult to get into BIOS after installing Ubuntu.

It isn't hijacking the BIOS.  It can't.  If you cannot get into your BIOS setup menu (in an Award BIOS, for example, tapping the delete key during POST will open the setup menu) it could well be a keyboard issue.  My system will not respond to any USB keyboard I've tried during POST.  It only recognizes the keyboard after POST.  So, I keep a PS/2 keyboard around for the occasions when I need to change my BIOS configuration.

### Post by Boosh007 on 2013-08-20
I had the opportunity to upgrade from 10.04LTS to 12.04LTS, when my system crashed due to no fault but my own. I had always had problems upgrading, when the system would encounter one error or another. I gave up after the 20th file fix and there were still a laundry list more. So when I had to swap out hard drives, I jumped at the chance to upgrade. After a week or so, I ended up erasing that new hard drive and downgrading back to 10.04LTS. Here's why;

Unlike a lot of people, I use the four separate window stations. In 10.04LTS, you can open four separate firefox pages, each brand new. In 12.04LTS, it wasn't possible, because each click on a different page that resulted in opening another firefox window,cause the computer to revert back to the previous window that housed it in the first place. This is counter-productive for me. To counter act this in 12.04LTS, I had to create four independent and separate working windows login pages. So there I sat, four new icons on the side of my screen; Firefox, Chrome, Comodo Dragon, and Lunascape. For what I use it for, I first had to open one in each window. I do a lot of job searching these days, and it's nice to be able jump from one page to another in filling out an application without losing your place and having to start over again. To open the first through the last window in 12.04LTS you must first click on the station button. Four windows appear on your main screen, something that doesn't happen on 10.04LTS. You choose one by clicking on it, causing a red ring to appear around the window you want. Now you can either double click it or return to the windows button and single click it watching the twitchy window open slowly, growing larger in front of you. Now you need to open a separate and new opener window for each of the four screens. Repeat this process for each time you need to open another window. 

In 10.04LTS it's a little less cumbersome. There are four separate boxes at the bottom right corner of the main screen. To open one, simply click on it. Unlike 12.04LTS, you don't need to open three new starter pages, creating a couple hundred megabytes of extra storage space to maneuver around the internet with. You only need one starter window, my defaulted Firefox works fine. Couple that with instant open windows and not having to be bogged-down with animated opening windows that add a dozen or so seconds every time you need to open one, makes for a better, less stressful, experience altogether surfing the web. 

There were a few other issues, mostly minor in nature, but they only added to my frustration in downgrading back to my older system. I will never look a gift horse in the mouth again, and wish for a better system than the one I have now...

---

### Post by cariboo on 2013-09-21
> **M Star said:**
> Unfriendly installation system for slow internet connection.

Package management. Especially the fact that, it is very hard to save the .deb files for offline installation on other computers.

You can use:

```
apt-get -d <option>
```

Synaptic packae manager also allows you to download packages only without installing them.

During regular usage when installing or upgrading, all packages are stored in /var/cache/apt/archives. I'm running Saucy, which is still in development, and I normally do two updates a day, my /var/cache/apt/archives directory currently contains about 780MiB of packages.

---

### Post by varunendra on 2013-09-21
> **cariboo907 said:**
> You can use:

```
apt-get -d <option>
```

Synaptic packae manager also allows you to download packages only without installing them.

....and something similar is the "--print-uris" option with apt-get :
```
apt-get install --print-uris *<whatever you have to install>*
```

Instead of downloading and installing, it prints the download URIs of all the packages (target(s) + dependencies) which can be used on another computer to manually download the packages, then install them with "dpkg -i *". Very useful for target computers which have no or too slow connection.

For using packages already downloaded with apt or synaptic or USC, [AptOnCD]("https://help.ubuntu.com/community/APTonCD") can be used.

There are many ways to get it done, but these are probably the easiest ones.

### Post by ubu2008 on 2013-09-24
In 2011 Ubuntu/Gnome2.6 nearly conquered the desktop and Torvalds dream nearly came true. Then Unity happened. Unity is unique in the way it is trying to guess what the user wants and doing it nearly 98% of the time wrong.
Take opening the desired workspace and starting new Firefox in it.
In G2.6: You are in WS1. You have a cookie in your left hand. Eyeball the named workspace, say called Programming, click on it, then click Firefox, and you are done. (takes 2 clicks).

In Unity: You are in WS1. Your workspace is not named. Click on on the collection of workspaces, it will show all 4. Then eyeball the one you need (say 3rd from the left). Then click on Firefox. Got you (says Unity). The brilliant Unity will open Firefox but not on this workspace WS3. It will take you back to the space WS1 and open the Firefox there. Unity thinks this: You user, do not know what you need. I am Unity (the goddess of all desktops) I KNOW that you actually wanted to be in WS1. To which I say, dear goddess, go away, click again on all workspaces. Open all 4. Select the one I need WS3. Carefully put down the cookie in my left hand. Use both hands on the single Firefox button. Open the Firefox. Problems solved (takes 11 clicks/actions).

This is not a single example of unusability of Unity. I have 100(base 10) but just to write them down takes great deal of time.

When somebody asked, “Is Ubuntu turning into Windows?”, they probably meant, is the decision making process of what is good for the user adopting the decision making process old Microsoft used to use. Old Microsoft used to believe that they know what user wants. However they apparently changed their ways. If you try Windows8, you will find out that configuring your environment is now very usable. Dare I say, it is now nearly as elegant as Gnome 2.6.

---

### Post by buzzingrobot on 2013-09-24
The last Gnome 2 release was 2.32, not 2.6. 

Unity didn't just happen. The Gnome project announced they would abandon work on Gnome 2 and move on to Gnome 3. Canonical eventually made a decision that Gnome 3 was not moving in a direction appropriate for Ubuntu, and began the Unity effort in response.

### Post by buzzingrobot on 2013-09-24
> **mike_smith2 said:**
>  

I think Canonical should invest in financially promoting a Buntu variant, one with a traditional desktop environment. Maybe split there investment 60/40. At least do some tests on which users prefer.
 



An interesting idea, but I suspect it would be less an investment and more just an expenditure.

I imagine the result of tests to see what users prefer would mirror the current ratio of users of each of the Ubuntu spins. Every major DE is available in a ready-made Ubuntu spin, and Mate (not a major player at present) is dead easy to install. People who prefer a "traditional" DE have plenty of options.

Actually, all these DE's other than Gnome Shell *are* traditional.  They're all panel based, they all use menus, they all are premised on the notion of manually minimizing windows, and they all assume users will want to put icons on the desktop.

### Post by Sinixstar on 2013-10-12
I hate that I find myself needing to drop down the terminal to do things all the time. 
I hate that I have to sudo and enter my password all the freakin time. There's security, and then there's enough already.
I'm not a big fan of the launcher. I find it doesn't always pop consistently - i seem to have to bounce my mouse over to the left repeatedly from time to time. I hate that I can't move it to the right or to the bottom. Alt+Tab support is kind of sketchy. If I have for example multiple windows of the same app open, it seems like only the most recent appears in the window chooser. 
The battery meter seems to be pretty useless. I've run ubuntu on a few laptops - mostly slightly older asus laptops, and a macbook air, and they've all suffered the same horrible battery metering. 
The notification popups such as when you connect to a wireless network get on my nerves. Is there any way to close those things or do you just have to wait the 20 minutes for them to go away?
Along the same lines of the terminal thing - i hate that i have to configure things through the file system/gedit fairly often. Seriously - nobody's figured out a simple gui for this stuff? The potential to royall screw yourself because of typos is HUGE. As a developer I can write a scaffolding app that reads a dataset, and based on the data-type displays a different type of editor control - on a freakin webpage. Yet in an operating system I have to open up a text editor and edit everything in plain text? come on.
Did I mention entering passwords seems to be my new hobby? I know, i know "you're coming from a windows mentality and ubuntu is secure!" But you can impliment security that doesn't require constantly having to type your password. A simple "X is attempting to install an application - do you want to allow this?" would suffice for blocking unwanted applications. Same goes for when i'm trying to edit certain files. I shouldn't have to open the terminal and sudo gedit to be able to save something. Give me something like run click "open as root" or better yet prompt me when I try to save if I don't have permissions. You achieve the same affect but with a cleaner/easier user experience.

Overall ubuntu is decent, but there's a lot of little things like this that annoy me. I'm sure i could go on with a ton more, but what's the point? Again, you can achieve the same end result with a much better user-experience with even just some minor modifications to the process of using the system.

---

### Post by Sinixstar on 2013-10-12
> **Ubun2to said:**
> 
I do not think Canonical will sink to the likes of M$, trying to stop competing OSs by doing things such as Secure (Restricted) Boot and charging ridiculous prices.

It's not letting devs decide for you if you switch distros in the same way that it is not letting politicians decide for you by voting for the other party and/or becoming a politician. You can modify the source, submit bug fixes, and get involved without becoming the boss of the devs. M$ does not decide for you, either. The way you tell the devs at M$ what you want is with your money, by not buying licenses (and likewise, by not pirating it or using it at all). The way you tell the devs in the Linux world is by participating in the community and (if need be) switching distros. Everyone is different, and to tailor an Ubuntu install to every single person's wants would be stupid. Not everyone's ideas can be included.

I'm not really sure what you're getting at with the secure/restricted boot thing. I've had absolutely no problems booting into linux from a windows machine using a live USb or a CD. Macs on the otherhand are an absolute NIGHTMARE to get to run. If you want to talk about restrictive/locked down software vendors - I think Apple takes the cake there. MS was pretty crappy a decade ago, but I think a lot of their reputation is based on the past these days. 

I'm also glad you touched on that "everyone is different, we can't tailor systems to be all things to all people". That is in essence the very root of a lot of the problems MS has. Like it or not - they still command upwards of 80% of the market. They DO have to cater to a wide variety of users, and do have to add in a lot of crap that you or I may not need, but a lot of people do. That's why the different editions with different base capabilities. The average home user simply doesn't need enterprise level features like domain logins and romaining profiles. They've gotten a LOT better at customization/personalization. Again though - if you look at Apple, I think they're the ultimate example of "we know what's best for you better than you do - and we're going to charge you a premium for it and you're going to like it". Some of their software is nice, and does work pretty well - but in terms of cost and having a company dictate to you, Apple is easily the biggest bully on the block.

### Post by buzzingrobot on 2013-10-12
> **Adam_R_M said:**
> I hate that I cant just simply install a driver for something as basic as a wifi card (usb). Or any other device as far as I can tell. I cant drop the drivers into the folder either."[COLOR=#000000][FONT=Arial] You then place the file "isl3886pci" into /lib/firmware and then..."[/FONT][/COLOR] [SIZE=3]*_"Denied"_*[/SIZE] And the community assumes persistently that everyone who wants to use linux will automatically know where to type  [SIZE=3][COLOR=#000000][FONT=Arial]-r p54pci or rmmod p54pc.(I do not) The UI is awesome. I just can't do anything with it. I just built a computer that I have to Hack in order to mabee use it. A tutorial would be nice. A wizard would be super. what is the point of the UI at all? It seems as though every action begins with "open a terminal and type..."[/FONT]
[FONT=Arial]Where do I even get a "terminal"[/FONT][/COLOR][/SIZE]:confused: I'm certain this "terminal" has a more specific name.

The "terminal" is an application called "Terminal".  

The net is full of Linux tutorials.  Ubuntu has an extensive wiki. Without question, more online guidance is available for Ubuntu than any other distribution, much of it aimed at complete newbies, which would seem to apply to you.

You cannot install a driver simply by dropping it into a directory.   You get a "denied" message because the account you are using lacks the rights to alter that directory.  This concept is a fundamental part of Linux, as it is OS X and Windows.

Programs and drivers available for Ubuntu are maintained on Ubuntu repositories and installed via a variety of programs.  Chief among those programs is the Ubuntu Software Center, which is specifically geared to the needs of newbies.

You cannot expect to make random guesses about how to use an OS and be lucky enough to see them work.

---

### Post by varunendra on 2013-10-12
> **Adam_R_M said:**
> I hate that I cant just simply install a driver for something as basic as a wifi card (usb).
I understand your pain, and can't discard the concerns altogether, just trying to explain a few things although none of us needs to (hope you already understand that :)).

Most often you don't even need to install a driver, unless you bought an unsupported one. Some drivers can be buggy sometimes but they keep improvising provided the developers have enough resources and 'testers' (people like us who test and report back at right places, in right manner).

No software in the world is perfect and the hardware vendors most often don't cooperate with Linux at all, so all the hard work has to be done by the open source developers alone, thus making the progress in that area a bit slow, but definite nonetheless.

> I cant drop the drivers into the folder either."[COLOR=#000000][FONT=Arial] You then place the file "isl3886pci" into /lib/firmware and then..."[/FONT][/COLOR] [SIZE=3]*_"Denied"_*[/SIZE]
That's for your own security.

Complaining about that clearly indicates you don't have enough experience or knowledge about Linux filesystem and that's one very good reason why you should be kept from modifying 'system areas' to prevent you from sabotaging your own system.

Modifying these directories/files requires root access which you can gain by prefixing commands with "sudo" or "gksu" in Ubuntu. There are many distributions that offer the root login by default, but they are meant to serve different purposes.

> And the community assumes persistently that everyone who wants to use linux will automatically know where to type  [SIZE=3][COLOR=#000000][FONT=Arial, Lucida Grande, sans-serif]-r p54pci or rmmod p54pc.(I do not)
I agree we shouldn't, and most we don't. But troubleshooting requires us to make some assumptions, and unless it is made clear that one is 'Absolutely' new to Linux, or is clearly understandable by their post, sometimes we *may* make that wrong assumption in order to avoid a couple of extra sentences which we *assume* to be unnecessary (click that... type that... to open a terminal... then type bla-bla-bla 'In' it).

That being said, it is a part of our Code of Conduct that unless made clear, we should assume everyone a fresher and should treat them likewise. So yeah, if you faced that problem (doesn't look like by your bean count, which is zero yet), we are guilty as accused. :)

> It seems as though every action begins with "open a terminal and type..."
Only because terminal commands are quicker and uniform across different desktop environments.

It is a lot easier to just tell the user to post output of command "ifconfig" then to first ask them what is their desktop environment (probably another confusion requiring another explanation post about "what an environment is"), then accordingly tell them which 'Icon' to click/right-click (or how to locate it first) > then click 'that' menu item > 'that' tab > 'that' button > take screenshot > also one of 'that' screen > post the 'screenshots' here, and "*oh, this is how you attach the screenshot here..*"...., well, you get the idea.

There are hundreds of GUI variables and thus will require hundred of ways to remember if we approach each problem with GUI ways. We still do that whenever we have a clear idea of which environment the user is in, and we have experience with it too.

> Where do I even get a "terminal"
Now that should have been your first question (when asking for help), and probably the only one to save you all of the above frustration and me the explanation :P

In Ubuntu
the shortcut key for opening the terminal is **Ctrl+Alt+T**, or
press **Alt+F2** > type "**terminal**" > press "Enter", or
(If using Unity) **Dash** > type "**terminal**" > Enter, or
**Dash** (**right-click > applications**) > scroll down to "**Terminal**" > click it.

So there are multiple ways just in Unity as you can see, the Ctrl-Alt-T being the quickest.

As you gain experience in Ubuntu (or any GUI distro), you'll find that almost everything can be done via GUI, it just varies a lot across various environments and is more than often slower than the CLI methods.

---

### Post by Sinixstar on 2013-10-12
> 
That's for your own security.

Complaining about that clearly indicates you don't have enough  experience or knowledge about Linux filesystem and that's one very good  reason why you should be kept from modifying 'system areas' to prevent  you from sabotaging your own system.

Modifying these directories/files requires root access which you can  gain by prefixing commands with "sudo" or "gksu" in Ubuntu. There are  many distributions that offer the root login by default, but they are  meant to serve different purposes.


I would say that if you're going to take the "we know better than you, we're going to protect you from yourself" route - then the system needs to be more capable of dealing with these issues in a way that's transparent to the user. This is basically the same road apple travels, and they only get away with it because by and large their stuff does "just work". Linux on the otherhand is nowhere near that level of plug-and-play capability. If the system is going to require lots of little manual tweaks from the user to function correctly, then the process to do so should be easier. If it's going to lock you out for your own good, then it should handle more of those tweaks automatically. 
Right now you're getting the worst of both worlds (from an end user point of view).

You can complain about "windows users" or "uneducated users" - but i'll let you in on a secret. That's the vast majority of the market. You and I may understand the reasons for it - but most people will simply get frustrated and move on to something else.

---

### Post by varunendra on 2013-10-12
> **Sinixstar said:**
> I would say that if you're going to take the "we know better than you, we're going to protect you from yourself" route - then the system needs to be more capable of dealing with these issues in a way that's transparent to the user.
It does, as long as you stick to things that are compatible and recommended, and in that condition, it works better than apple.

When you move from the standards, defaults or recommendations, which is a matter of freedom to choose which apple doesn't offer, you are on your own and the system is not responsible for what you may have to go through.

> This is basically the same road apple travels, and they only get away with it because by and large their stuff does "just work".
..because they don't allow you to deviate from what is 'recommended' and created/made available 'in-house'.

> Linux on the otherhand is nowhere near that level of plug-and-play capability.
As a matter of fact, Linux as a 'kernel' supports the largest set of hardware on the planet, and no other kernel or software comes close to the level of plug and play support it offers **without the support from the manufacturers who created the hardware**.

> If the system is going to require lots of little manual tweaks from the user to function correctly, then the process to do so should be easier. If it's going to lock you out for your own good, then it should handle more of those tweaks automatically.
Tweaks are rarely required if you use something that is already compatible. And are required mostly when you happen to have something that is already broken (like acpi standards in BIOS, improper protocol or standards in device firmwares etc.) or not yet properly supported (things for which the support is in 'Beta' stage).

As for automation, almost everything in Desktop OSes like Ubuntu is a result of the 'make-easy-and-automatic' efforts. Those understand this better who have seen the "Text-only, compile-everything" days of Linux (I haven't, but can guess what it might have been like).

The GUI in Linux has come a long way in making things easier, and background processes transparent to the user, and the work is going on *(Boot-Repair being the most recent example IMHO, with WUBI, Sakis-3g, Jocky, Ubuntu-Tweak, the Software Center in Ubuntu being few other examples)*.

Why "Tweaks" are required more often than other OSes, has already been answered probably thousands of times on this forum alone. And some things (like the repository system) work already so much better than those 'Others' that they too are following the same suit now.

> Right now you're getting the worst of both worlds (from an end user point of view).
The growing number of Linux users doesn't corroborate that notion.

> ..i'll let you in on a secret. That's the vast majority of the market. You and I may understand the reasons for it - but most people will simply get frustrated and move on to something else.
Who cares? Those who actually develop the kernel and software 'FOR' Linux, (thankfully) didn't, don't and hopefully will never care about that. A quote from the old [**Linux Is Not Windows**]("http://linux.oneandoneis2.org/LNW.htm") page -
> Linux is not interested in market share. Linux does not have customers. Linux does not have shareholders, or a responsibility to the bottom line. Linux was not created to make money. Linux does not have the goal of being the most popular and widespread OS on the planet.

All the Linux community wants is to create a really good, fully-featured, free operating system. If that results in Linux becoming a hugely popular OS, then that's great. If that results in Linux having the most intuitive, user-friendly interface ever created, then that's great. If that results in Linux becoming the basis of a multi-billion dollar industry, then that's great.

It's great, but it's not the point. The point is to make Linux the best OS that the community is capable of making. Not for other people: For itself. The oh-so-common threats of "Linux will never take over the desktop unless it does such-and-such" are simply irrelevant: The Linux community isn't trying to take over the desktop. They really don't care if it gets good enough to make it onto your desktop, so long as it stays good enough to remain on theirs. The highly-vocal MS-haters, pro-Linux zealots, and money-making FOSS purveyors might be loud, but they're still minorities.

If you are talking about Ubuntu, and not Linux in general, then everybody knows how hard they are trying to fill these gaps, and complaining or threatening (please don't take it as offense) is not going to help. Testing, reporting and contributing in other ways is.

Complaining is different than reporting and feedback, and only the latter can be of help, and that too if done in the correct place, correct manner. I am posting this not because Canonical or Community pays me, or it is some kind of religion to me, or for any other benefit/fear of loss. I'm posting because **1)** I like the software and want to make it better, and **2)** I've got no better means to kill my time right now. :P

---

### Post by Blackice504 on 2013-10-12

Personal Preferences: Well this is just my opinion I find open office by apache to be better then Libra, but this is not a hate thing as I can just uninstall it. << not a big deal at all as far as I have seen there both the same I think Open Office might have more Toys. but keep in mind I been using Open Office for a long time so I could be just to used to it.

Firefox many people are shocked when I say I don't use Firefox and this is only because I do a lot of I.T Stuff some things just don't work on Firefox and just in case no one believes me here is one thing as an example. Some KVM Cards like Lights On Controllers get really upset when they are used on Firefox I have no idea why, I am not a web guy, there is also some Layer 3 Switch's ect that do not like it, thus I don't use it and I am pedantic about having programmes that do the same things as another installed so I just use 1 that works for all cases.

Solution: for programmes such as Libra or Firefox "Bundled Programmes" a choice to install or not install when configuring the OS you could make this option at the start of Ubuntu Deployment.

The Java issue: Many people use Java I believe that Java usage is getting higher and higher each day its almost a need on any Desktop, but many people don't know that Open Java is not 100% compatible with Sun Java Especially in Minecraft "modified" I have personally tested this, Sun Java Fixed problems after uninstalling Open Java Conclusion Open Java does not do all, Also some developers would use Sun Java over anything else as Sun Java is a Standard.

Solution: I would like Ubuntu to ask Sun Java if they could add a repository for Sun Java << keep in mind this is not a big problem as many people even newbies to Ubuntu have been able to solve it by installing it. Thanks to this Forum.

Improvements: Ubuntu Desktop could use some more Stability when using the Ubuntu Software Centre I get random crashes every so often. 100% sure it is not hardware as I have tested this to make sure and I get the same symptoms on other machines with Desktop. "but this is rare thing"

Promotional: I would love to give my Clients the option for Ubuntu OS on my website but from what I understand we are not allowed to do this because of the Copyright on logo's ect I hope Ubuntu changes this so we can Promote the use of Ubuntu Installed on Computers, Keep in mind the only charge I would do if I was allowed to do this is the time for installing and not profit on the software I think that is fare.

Solution: Ubuntu makes Promotional pack, Includes Logo that can be used on a non Ubuntu Website, Logo of Powered by Ubuntu << to show Clients that the backend of our Servers use Ubuntu, Ubuntu OEM Logo.
Possibilities: The more people use Ubuntu as there Linux OS the more it would become a standard and that also means Developers would follow and use Ubuntu as there O.S as there work is more likely to be used and seen. For us users this means we get an OS that could be a Replacement for windows. << This is what I would love to see because there is no way in hell I will use Windows 8 I seen it I don't like it as it is I had to put up with more steps to do things in Windows 7. << Ms has lost the Plot.

But in all Fairness I love Ubuntu, Its easy to use, Light weight OS that I do so many things with and over the next few months I am converting all my Business Servers from Windows to Ubuntu that is how happy and confident I am with the Server OS.

I have used Red Hat / Fedora / Gentoo.

Why I use Ubuntu over any other Linux, easy to use, simple to deploy, Ubuntu Server is Light weight and does not have a GUI for me this is the point of a server and I am glad Ubuntu Agree also simple to use like its Desktop Cousin. The possibilities in Desktop and Server with Ubuntu are endless.

I have been Using Ubuntu Server for the past 4 years never had any problems thus I will convert my Business to run on Ubuntu. "over the next few months So far so good"
My Laptop has been running Ubuntu for 3 years with no problems in fact I would say I have got more use out of my laptop compared to when I had windows on it.

Ubuntu4NAS: yes there are lot's of NAS O.S out there but I would love to see a package that Ubuntu development Team puts together as like most of you confidence is very high with the Ubuntu Development Team. PS if any dev's are reading your welcome to use the name ;)

Conclusion I taken the time to write this as I want more people to use Ubuntu and to see that its really hard to find something that people can hate about Ubuntu as there is really nothing but small things that can easily be changed / updated / fixed, Besides you will be hard pressed to find any OS that works Flawlessly out of the box but Ubuntu is very close.

Keep it up Ubuntu Development Team.:D

### Post by Sinixstar on 2013-10-12
I get what you're saying re "Linux is not windows" - nor should it be. 
There are basic usability concepts that apply regardless of platform however - that I think Linux - or really in the context of this conversation Ubuntu - should still try to live up to. Just as apple does with OSX, and Microsoft does with Windows (to varying degrees of success for both). It's not necessarily about "oh - if we do this, we can gain X market share" - but if you're being clobbered by alternatives, it's worth asking the question - what are they doing that people are so drawn to? What are the differences between the two - and is there something we could do to make the user experience better? I think Ubuntu is close, probably 85-90% of the way there, but it's that last 10-15% that really causes an annoyance. We can sit there and say that in theory linux is the most open and compatible platform out there - but all it takes is a minute or two of browsing the support forums even here on this very site - to see endless questions of "how do I get X hardware to work?". Maybe the capabilities are there, but the practical implementation of those capabilities needs work - and that's what i'm getting at. 
I also find it odd - that in one breath people say "we don't care about market share" but in the same post talk about the number of users picking up linux and it's growing popularity. You either care about popularity or you do not - you can't have both at the same time.

---

### Post by buzzingrobot on 2013-10-13
> **varunendra said:**
> It does, as long as you stick to things that are compatible and recommended, and in that condition, it works better than apple.

When you move from the standards, defaults or recommendations, which is a matter of freedom to choose which apple doesn't offer, you are on your own and the system is not responsible for what you may have to go through.


..because they don't allow you to deviate from what is 'recommended' and created/made available 'in-house'. 

That's not actually the case. While iOS is locked down, OS X is, in reality, quite amenable to being played around with.  You can run X, run a Linux DE or window manager, install Linux programs using the same kind of packagers/dependency resolving tools used in Linux, etc.  

Will Apple support any of that?  Of course not.  But, they do support OS X and Mac owners can call a support line and get help, or go to an Apple store and get help.  No Linux distribution offers anything comparable in terms of support.

The "freedom" at the heart of FOSS and Linux is the freedom developers have to examine and repurpose source code, and to design and build as they see fit. The "freedom" Linux users have to tinker and play is secondary, and not really a consequence of FOSS principles.

While companies like Red Had do try their best to offer something similar, the Apple type of support is simply not possible with Linux distros given the way they are developed and the variety of platforms/hardware they run upon. Just like we can't offer support for Fedora or Suse here, because most of us here simply can't and we don't want to turn the forums into a huge unusable mess of confusing suggestions.

If Ubuntu, for example, had to offer support only for System76, maybe the support would have been better than any other OS out there. But that's not the case with platforms, hence not the case with support.

### Post by buzzingrobot on 2013-10-13
Red Hat sells support subscriptions for it enterprise Linux releases.  You can buy a subscription for a single desktop but the subscription that actually provides web and phone support is $299 per year. They aren't after the desktop Linux market.

Red Hat did, years ago after its founding, sell retail desktop Linux. They couldn't turn a profit and abandoned the retail market.

### Post by buzzingrobot on 2013-10-20
Root isn't unusable.  You login as root, do what needs to be done, log out, and log back in as your normal user.

Files, directories, etc., owned by root rarely need for an ordinary user to directly access or alter them. When such areas do need to be altered by a user, typically during initial setup or during software installation, the user will be prompted for a password to allow temporary privilege escalation.

It's unclear what you mean by "reset" a "system file", both in terms of what you mean by "reset" and by "system file". These expressions are not used in Linux.

---

### Post by davem1946 on 2013-10-20

reset = adjust settings - add, delete, modify lines

I need to adjust(change/add lines) in a config file as directed from help on a component i installed. The file shows as owned by root and will not allow me in anyway to do anything.

### Post by buzzingrobot on 2013-10-21
Sudo is more generally recommended in Ubuntu. Here's a useful link from the documentation: [https://help.ubuntu.com/community/RootSudo](https://help.ubuntu.com/community/RootSudo).

Use chown, etc., with discretion.  Applications or services that legitimately need a user to make such alterations implicitily assume possession of administrative experience.

### Post by buzzingrobot on 2013-11-02
> **Cycledoc said:**
> You'll know that Ubuntu is focused on users, i.e. not tech oriented people, when there is a simple way to increase the size of the pointer.  


Cursor size can be changed via dconf-editor (sudo apt-get install dconf-tools):  org.gnome.desktop.interface.cursor-size.

Arguably, that might be exposed in a simple GUI in Settings.  On the other hand, users who install new themes might find their changes overwritten.

### Post by jimford on 2013-11-16
It seems that the more features Ubuntu (and probably other distros) get, the more unreliable the system becomes.

I started with Linux in the early 90's with Slakware with a kernel at about 0.9 - certainly before 1.0. As the kernel advanced I downloaded the source and rebuilt it. When I installed programs, I built them from source, usually using the accompanying makefile. From memory it all worked faultlessly, and certainly far more reliable than the Windows OS of the time. I moved to Ubuntu to avoid the rebuilding the ever more complex kernel  - the 'potted' distros looking attractive.

I don't know who the Ubuntu/Xubuntu maintainers are and what their status is regarding the project, but I imagine that they are a loose and changing group of people doing it in their spare time. There's obviously some very talented programmers on the project, and I'm grateful for the work they do, but I wonder whether the project has become too big to handle in the current fashion.

Jim

### Post by alphasoup on 2013-12-08
> **help_me2 said:**
> Since when is having a dock on the left, a phone interface? I have yet to see ONE phone that looks and acts like unity. Surely, my android phone doesn't have a dock. Hmmmm. Apple has had a dock for ages, yet that's OK. Hmmmm. Sounds like sour grapes over gnome2 going away.

It's not a phone interface, it's a desktop interface. When they port ubuntu to the phone, it will *have* to change. You can't use unity as is, on a phone. Just because it has a dock doesn't mean it's a phone interface. If anything, it makes keyboard junkies very happy, since most things can be done very quickly via keyboard. Phone? No.

Actually UNITY will be the basis for the Ubuntu phone GUI, but that was not the main point:
Loosing **secure remote access** to your desktop *from anywhere* is a huge drawback -- like a 30 years back in time regression for Ubuntu.

How to you run a VNC or RDP server on a secondary virtual display with UNITY and access it remotely?
As long as you cannot do that it is a huge regression: X11, Xorg and later Gnome 1,2,3 have provided remote access since the late 80s, etc.

Apparently people are happy to be stuck onto a single physical display, or is everyone moving to laptops and pads, carrying them everywhere they go?

### Post by buzzingrobot on 2013-12-08
> **alphasoup said:**
> 
Loosing **secure remote access** to your desktop *from anywhere* is a huge drawback -- like a 30 years back in time regression for Ubuntu.

How to you run a VNC or RDP server on a secondary virtual display with UNITY and access it remotely?
As long as you cannot do that it is a huge regression: X11, Xorg and later Gnome 1,2,3 have provided remote access since the late 80s, etc.

Yep and yep.  

Running a VNC or RDP server is a need few consumers will ever articulate.  If they do use that capability, it will be disguised in an easy-to-use click-to-play package.

If you check desktop marketshare, you'll see that an awful lot of people are, in fact, switching to a tablet or a phone.  Most things most people did on their desktops or laptops, outside of work, they can do on a phone. (And they can do a lot of work on that phone, too.)

### Post by monkeybrain20122 on 2013-12-15
Bugs that never get fixed despite bug reports on the same problems that span several Ubuntu releases (just take a look at the Compiz bugs, now that they are moving to Unity 8, chances are those bugs will never be fixed)

When fixes finally are released they don't get backported (only some do). I saw many bug reports where people reported problems for Ubuntu version x and fixes were "released", but only for Ubuntu version x + 1. Meanwhile if you are lucky you find ppas to get the fixed versions, or if you are a power user you grab the source and compile it yourself, but  most users are stuck with the broken versions. When the next Ubuntu comes, you update and get those ones fixed, but new bugs appear and you are once again stuck in the same situation.

### Post by marcjknight on 2013-12-21
Crikey this threads seen some days, so here goes. I'm attempting to migrate over to Ubuntu, have been doing so on and off for years but have taken it more seriously of late. The problem for me is the learning curve. Sorry if this offends, but it comes across (and this just might be me) that when a linux guru offers advice it comes over as high end high tech speak and takes some deciphering. For instance, edit out such and such in etc bla bla. I've been a (sorry) Windows user and adviser for years, my example, double click such folder, then double click on some text file, look for such and such text, add this or that to such and such line. 

The thing is, there are many users like myself appreciate the concept of linux and would easily ditch Windows, but it's no secret they don't because not only is Windows easier to use, tech advice is more understandable. I've been in linux forums in the past and whilst I'm not tarring everyone with the same brush, when I've asked for click by click advice I feel like I have been completely ignored.

Today I finally got my scanner to work, took me two days. TWO DAYS I immediately uploaded the package I found and made it available for download. The advice I received was on askubuntu, the adviser named the files to install but they are no longer available from where he specified. This leads me onto my next point, with certain things "to do" with setting up linux, sometimes mountains (not bridges) need to be crossed. I nearly gave up yet again, but since I sorted out the scanner issue I do feel quite proud of the and other achievements since last week, but I have to say it is hard work and very taxing on the old brain cells.

Someone suggested gimp was easy to use, poppicock, try Paint.net in Windows, then you'll understand the term "easy".

### Post by buzzingrobot on 2013-12-21
> **marcjknight said:**
> ...when a linux guru offers advice it comes over as high end high tech speak and takes some deciphering. For instance, edit out such and such in etc bla bla...

Someone suggested gimp was easy to use, poppicock, try Paint.net in Windows, then you'll understand the term "easy".

Instructions to use the command line rather than a GUI happen for several reasons.  First, sometimes an appropriate GUI does not exist. Second, if a GUI does exist, it might not be capable of doing what needs to be done. Third, it is very much simpler to simply provide some copy-and-paste terminal commands than try, in a post, how to use a GUI to accomplish what can be a fairly complex task.  Fourth, web sites and other publications will typically include the terminal instructions because they are much more likely to be applicable to a broad range of readers; otherwise the instructions would often be tied to a particular version of a particular application on a particular distribution. (And, not everyone who puts up a site knows what they're talking about. Many sites and blogs simply repost material from other sources, typically without credit.)

Linux is a version of Unix and Unix was created as, and still is at heart, a text-only non-GUI OS. (Technology didn't support GUI's in any useful or affordable way in the 1970's.) The software layers that build and display GUI's on Linux are separate layers, distinct from the actual OS.  Windows, at least once NT came on the scene, was built from the ground up as a GUI system with an add-on command line capability. You can remove the entire GUI level from Linux/Unix and still have  a perfectly functioning operating system, with access to a very wide range of capable software.  As far as i know, that's not possible in Windows.

One advantage Windows has in this regard is the fact that there is, essentially, one Windows.  Hundreds of Linux distributions exist. Different ways of structuring and executing core components and services exist in Linux. (E.g., advice on disabling system services for Fedora will not work on Ubuntu.) If Microsoft changes Windows, that's it.  If Ubuntu or any other distro changes something, no one else needs to go along if they don't want to.

Add to that the reality that the net is littered with out-of-date, incomplete, and just plain wrong "guidance" about Linux. (No one is in charge, so no one is out there pruning things.) When I troll the net for info, I narrow the search as much as possible:  Specific distro version and releases and limit the timeframe for the search as close as I can to that release.  E.g., if I want info about Ubuntu 13.10, it's useful to focus only on material published on or after its release.

Using Linux effectively requires at least some willingess to become familiar with and use the command line. GUI apps in Windows are often easy to use, but they leave you at a dead end if they can't get the job done. (Your alternative then is to plunge into the the command shells of Windows, which, if anything, are more intimidating than similar Linux shells.)

(Yes, Gimp is not easy to use. it's as complex as Photoshop and harder to learn because there's no market for the kind of 800-page how-to books for $50 a pop that show up every time a new Photoshop release hits.  Limux apps comparable in ease of use to paint.net do exist.)

### Post by apollothethird on 2013-12-21
> **hg-knight said:**
> All i hate about Ubuntu is the fact that my son "stole" my laptop so now i have only a old Toshiba netbook, which i have to run a strippe down version of Ubuntu 12.04
on. 
[https://www.ramon-c.nl/ubuntu-mate/](https://www.ramon-c.nl/ubuntu-mate/)  
Still love it, but I do wish they would slow down the edition releases.You get left behind so quickly.

You'd never be left behind if you would only upgrade to the LTS.  The LTS releases are supported for 5 years.  You hardly get that type of support for Windows.

Would you really see a problem with installing version 13.04 and not having to change the version until 2018?  I would strongly encourage people to consider upgrading their computer hardware after 5 years.

This support cycle is very well defined in Ubuntu.  This should be considered some of the charm, not a hated flaw.  If you didn't know before, hopefully you know now that you have this facility.

You can leave others who wants to test and debut the changes every 6 months to contribute to the best of what will appear in the LTS.  You don't have to participate in that group.  Just do what you just appear to describe you'd prefer.  Just use the LTS and never be left behind for 5 year.

I believe it's great that the development doesn't become stagnate between the 5 year LTS cycle.

When it's time to change/upgrade, I find that to be extremely easy.  All I have to do is backup my personal files (my home directory, or have it on a different filesystem) then install the new version, and just go to work.  All my personal preferences and documents are already intact.

This is something that is not easy to do with Windows.  With Linux it's very easy.  With the Ubuntu flavor, it's even easier.

-- L. James

### Post by apollothethird on 2013-12-21
> **marcjknight said:**
> Crikey this threads seen some days, so here goes. I'm attempting to migrate over to Ubuntu, have been doing so on and off for years but have taken it more seriously of late. The problem for me is the learning curve. Sorry if this offends, but it comes across (and this just might be me) that when a linux guru offers advice it comes over as high end high tech speak and takes some deciphering. For instance, edit out such and such in etc bla bla. I've been a (sorry) Windows user and adviser for years, my example, double click such folder, then double click on some text file, look for such and such text, add this or that to such and such line. 

The thing is, there are many users like myself appreciate the concept of linux and would easily ditch Windows, but it's no secret they don't because not only is Windows easier to use, tech advice is more understandable. I've been in linux forums in the past and whilst I'm not tarring everyone with the same brush, when I've asked for click by click advice I feel like I have been completely ignored.

Today I finally got my scanner to work, took me two days. TWO DAYS I immediately uploaded the package I found and made it available for download. The advice I received was on askubuntu, the adviser named the files to install but they are no longer available from where he specified. This leads me onto my next point, with certain things "to do" with setting up linux, sometimes mountains (not bridges) need to be crossed. I nearly gave up yet again, but since I sorted out the scanner issue I do feel quite proud of the and other achievements since last week, but I have to say it is hard work and very taxing on the old brain cells.

Someone suggested gimp was easy to use, poppicock, try Paint.net in Windows, then you'll understand the term "easy".

I find Linux to be much simpler to work with, support, or give support, than windows.

Windows is extremely complicated.  It's so complicated that most places that give support gives pictures to accommodate the support steps.

Most of the support steps, as you referred, involve clicking on one gui screen, then clicking on next, etc.  However, very often something unexpected comes into the mix, there are unexpected options that confuses the use.  This is why that have to provide the picture so that if you get a difference screen, you  can browse around until you find the desired screen.

This can be a problem with the Linux support steps.  However, Linux has the added bonus of the user being able to, in almost every case, pull up a terminal screen and type command lines that will always be consistent and perform what needs to be done.

We understand that most people are afraid of the black screen.  But it's very powerful and easy to use.  It's different from Windows, because with Windows, you can't get to many things from the console.  You have to have a gui and only click on buttons.  So people have this concept of trying to only do what they have been doing before, and not look at the easy of doing something a different way.

When a person take into consideration that there may be an easier way and test it, they will find how much easier it is to use Linux than windows.

As someone mentioned above, there is the gui method and the console method for just about everything.  Many support people give both.  The console method is almost always substantially easier.  It's just a matter of copying the command from the support page and pasting it onto your console screen.  Then giving the output of the console screen to your support guy if you have problems.

As far as the easy of gimp, it's a very powerful environment, just like Photo shop.  If you take someone who has never used Photo shop, they wouldn't know where to start with that very complex environment.  If they are determined to learn, they could learn to do just about anything in their imagination with it... the same goes with Gimp.

Anything I have ever put into words that I wanted to do with Gimp, I have pasted the string in Google and found a very easy way to do it.  If someone hasn't already asked the question and got an answer, posting my questions on the many support forums would give a very fast response.

There are simple Linux image programs just as there are simple Windows graphic editors.  But many people would benefit better if they can  afford Photo shop to get familiar with it if they are running windows, or get familiar with Gimp if they are running Linux (without having to worry about the cost) and learn how to use the very powerful environments.

With experience, either would soon become easy with the user.

-- L. James

--

### Post by apollothethird on 2013-12-24
[http://looselytyped.blogspot.in/2012/06/enabledisable-unitycompiz-window.html](http://looselytyped.blogspot.in/2012/06/enabledisable-unitycompiz-window.html)> **amir-nukebomb said:**
> I love Ubuntu, but there are some parts which I hate the most is the Compiz part in Unity. I'm love Unity DE, but somehow it would be a total better without silly Compiz compositor like the upcoming Unity8 ones.
> **buzzingrobot said:**
> Agreed. The sooner Compiz goes, the better.

How are you doing, Amir.  How are you doing Buzzingrobot.

I believe you guys are making a serious mistake by your description.  This is some of the charm of Ubuntu.  You can have don't have to use Compiz.  You can use other utilities to configure your Ubuntu Desktop the way you want it.

Loosing it for you is only a few clicks away.  Please take a look at:

Enable/Disable Unity/Compiz Window Animations and Visual Effects in Ubuntu:
[http://looselytyped.blogspot.in/2012/06/enabledisable-unitycompiz-window.htm](http://looselytyped.blogspot.in/2012/06/enabledisable-unitycompiz-window.htm)

Personally, I'd never disable it.  I love it.  But I wouldn't want to force it on your, so the developers have provided a way for you not to have it if you don't want it.

Not many OS' have this freedom.

Hope you appreciate the choice as provided by Ubuntu.

### Post by monkeybrain20122 on 2013-12-24
I have no idea about all the compiz hate either. It is a bit buggy but nothing unmanageable and its plugins make Unity so much more usable. I can't do without edge flip and scale for exposing apps and switching desktops. Gnome shell feels so much more constricted without these features (there are some extensions but they don't work as well and no longer work with gs 10 anyway)

  Compiz is a powerful desktop manager which has a lot more use than providing eye candies and it is a lot less resource demanding than gnome shell or kde in my experience.

However, I have no idea what you (apollothethird) mean by disabling compiz. If you don't use Unity, then you don't have Compiz unless you install it yourself, and if you do use Unity you can't disable it because Unity is a compiz plugin, unless you are still on 12.04 in that case you can log into Unity 2d, but unity 2d is dead after 12.04.

P.S. your link no longer works.

### Post by buzzingrobot on 2013-12-24
> **apollothethird said:**
> [http://looselytyped.blogspot.in/2012/06/enabledisable-unitycompiz-window.html](http://looselytyped.blogspot.in/2012/06/enabledisable-unitycompiz-window.html)


... don't have to use Compiz.




We do.  Unity is implemented as a Compiz plugin.  

I don't hate it, I just find it buggy.  It ought to be the kind of thing that never calls attention to itself.  In a stock install, I get more crashes and apport popups from Compiz than anything else, by a wide margin.

One example: On a new 13.10 install, it's a dead cinch Compiz will crash the first time I click on the workspace icon in the launcher.

That's why I wished it a speedy departure.  

CCSM is a different matter, but I avoid it, as well. I'm not fond of hot corners, edge flipping and such for navigation.

then I came across tarballs
1. download tarballs
2. extract
3. compile

I would be happy if that is all it took, but then I came across errors which I didn't know what it meant, it took some time, googling what to do. Tar balls are *not* program installers.  They're archives, very often of compressed source code.  At heart, they are a development tool, not something for end users.  Totally DIY and almost never necessary. Ubuntu has tens of thousands of programs that can be installed withh a click or two or a tiny stanza on the command line.
