### Post by paulClarke_1967 on 2014-01-12
I hate ubuntu because they are a mirroring Microsoft.  They are more interested in putting out their latest bug filled release rather that fixing their last one.  They have little or no compassion for the end users because they depend on them to not only debug their software but support it.  That wouldn't be so bad except by the time the ubuntu community finds and starts fixing all those bugs,  ubuntu comes out with another exciting new bug riddled release.  This scenario is repeated every 18 months or so.  Meanwhile back in the jungle,  those of us who are a gluttons for punishment keep using the software.  Those with a lesser pain/stress threshold continue to fill Bill Gates pockets with their had earned money.  There's the** real **problem for those of use who love the Linus concept.  The software is in deed free as others have mentioned but the only people using it are the people who have the time, know how and the patience to install/configure and continually debug/repair it.  The rest of the computer world is not so lucky.  They can't use linux because it requires to much of a personal investment.  Because, I believe,  ALL the distros are more interested in making their next version of linux* the next big thing released* instead of just making linux* the best thing ever released.*

---

### Post by buzzingrobot on 2014-01-12
> **paulClarke_1967 said:**
> [SIZE=3]I hate ubuntu...The software is in deed free as others have mentioned but the only people using it are the people who have the time, know how and the patience to install/configure and continually debug/repair it.[/SIZE]

Aren't you confusing the meaning of that word "free"?

The "free software" movement was created by and for developers, to support them in asserting their right to publish and share the source code they write.  Whether or not that software is given away at no cost wasn't part of the mix.  

From a developer's point of view, a primary reason for releasing your program for use is to get it tested and evaluated in as many scenarios and possible, and to collect the bug reports. No developer, no corporation, can accomplish the same thing by in-house testing.

A case in point is Red Hat Enterprise Linux.  Red Hat is a billion-dollar company, so we can expect its resources to be greater than those available to singleton developers or businesses like Canonical. The current version of Enterprise Linux was released several years ago.  It has been essentially locked down since, with changes limited to kernel patches and bug fixes.  Even at this late date in its lifetime, people continue to uncover and report bugs.  That's the nature of software.

If Canonical took your advice, they would need to take over development of each of the tens of thousands of applications, libraries, etc., residing on their servers and available for users.

---

### Post by apollothethird on 2014-01-12
> **paulClarke_1967 said:**
> I hate ubuntu because they are a mirroring Microsoft.  They are more interested in putting out their latest bug filled release rather that fixing their last one.  They have little or no compassion for the end users because they depend on them to not only debug their software but support it.  That wouldn't be so bad except by the time the ubuntu community finds and starts fixing all those bugs,  ubuntu comes out with another exciting new bug riddled release.  This scenario is repeated every 18 months or so.  Meanwhile back in the jungle,  those of us who are a gluttons for punishment keep using the software.  Those with a lesser pain/stress threshold continue to fill Bill Gates pockets with their had earned money.  There's the** real **problem for those of use who love the Linus concept.  The software is in deed free as others have mentioned but the only people using it are the people who have the time, know how and the patience to install/configure and continually debug/repair it.  The rest of the computer world is not so lucky.  They can't use linux because it requires to much of a personal investment.  Because, I believe,  ALL the distros are more interested in making their next version of linux* the next big thing released* instead of just making linux* the best thing ever released.*

Many Windows users describe the same problem with Windows that you're describing with Linux.  The difference, for me, is that Linux is easier to fix, cheaper (free), easier to use, and easier to support.

Every 18 months or so, my Windows customers have to dole out nearly $400.00 for their bug infested upgrade (of the OS and Office).

I'm glad no one can convince the developers to stop developing.  Please feel free to stay with a supported version for 5 years.  You don't have to change so frequently.  That's the good thing about this environment.  After 5 years, you might be ready for a change.  That's only 4 times in a generation.  Things might change from the way it is now.  But if it doesn't, I really don't see such a problem with having to change 4 times in 20 years.

A lot of Ubuntu haters are really missing the memo.  Hope you are starting to get it so that you can help pass it around.

-- L. James

If you don't want breakage, stick with LTS releases. Simple. Incidentally, interim releases come out every nine months now, not eighteen. I've run 12.04 LTS faultlessly for months. Breakage is part of the deal.

### Post by buzzingrobot on 2014-01-13
> **Bucky Ball said:**
> If you install the 'next best thing' two minutes after it has been released I can't see what the complaints are about. Expect breakage.


So right.  There's nothing magic about how bugs and security weaknesses are eliminated.  It happens by getting your software in use by as many people as possible, collecting their bug and problem reports, patching the bugs, and changing as little else as possible.

That's why products like Ubuntu's LTS releases, Debian Stable, and Red Hat's Enterprise Linux have deserved reputations for stability.  

You can't get the newest code and expect that kind of stability, though.  It just can't happen.  So each user, then, needs to work out the best approach for his or her situation. Pretty much all software is full of bugs.  Whether you hit a bug depends on how you use it.

### Post by monkeybrain20122 on 2014-01-13
Well to me LTS is no different from other releases in terms of usability and stability and in fact the best releases I have used are not LTS ( 10.10 > 10.04, 13.04 and 13.10 > 12.04) Here is what I do. I install the latest Ubuntu on an external hard drive (got one of those for $50, or you can get a used one from an old computer) I would tweak it, customize it and set it up maybe for a month or two, making sure everything works, then delete the last Ubuntu from my laptop's harddrive and restore the clone. It is simple, gradual, do it at my own pace, no stress and no nasty surprises. :)

### Post by hodad on 2014-01-14
I love Ubuntu.  I've been using it for many years. Far superior to Windows!!! What I fear, however, is hooking up a new piece of hardware, because it inevitable leads to days of pulling my hair out to get it to work. I realize that this is caused by mfrs not putting enough effort into hardware support.

The reason I mention this is because I inevitably go to this forum to ask for help, and get lots of good suggestions that usually don't work in my case.  What would be most helpful would be a systematic set of procedures for handling such hardware related issues; a "guidebook" on how to proceed, rather than steps that depend on my particular machine (which inevitably leave important steps out).   A good education on Linux helps, but many of us don't have time to properly educate ourselves.  I'm finally "getting there", but many are totally clueless on how to proceed in solving harware related issues.

### Post by Lyfang on 2014-01-14
Unity's search with Amazon and other search results that can compromise privacy. Besides removing unity-scope-home causes lots of problems. I suggest running the following command in the Terminal to disable most of the ads:
```
gsettings set com.canonical.Unity.Lenses disabled-scopes "['more_suggestions- amazon.scope', 'more_suggestions-u1ms.scope', 'more_suggestions-populartracks.scope', 'music-musicstore.scope', 'more_suggestions-ebay.scope', 'more_suggestions-ubuntushop.scope', 'more_suggestions-skimlinks.scope']"

```

### Post by GSF1200S on 2014-03-16
> **varunendra said:**
> WHY COMPROMISE - exactly the reason why we should stick to Open Source software AS LONG AS POSSIBLE, and I'm glad Ubuntu is trying to do just that.

When you give up on Open Source and surrender to Proprietary driver/software which you have no control over, that is a Compromise.


Developing and promoting Open Source is what promises a brighter future for the 'Community', not resorting to proprietary stuff that can kick you off anytime it wishes, just like certain ATI drivers. Although I don't mean to say that Mint is doing the wrong thing. They are just providing an alternative which is good, as long as there are others like Ubuntu trying hard to fill the gaps.

About Unity, just a matter of choice. It's default, but not 'Forced' on us. We can choose a different DE and Ubuntu will happily keep working with it. No DE can please everyone.
As a matter of fact, I like Unity now, and am using that alone, despite having installed Gnome-fallback.

**EDIT:**

Sorry, I don't agree with that. People have different needs and it is very important to let them have the freedom to modify things as long as it doesn't break the system.

Extreme lack of customization is one of the things that I still don't like about Unity. It's okay to provide it the harder way (via dconf, codes etc.) so that newbies don't break their DE 'accidentally', but it's not good to take it away entirely, because that's when it becomes a 'Problem' for many of us who would otherwise like it.
It is a fairly well established trend that new users will always complain about certain things until they realize theyre dealing with a new paradigm and different rules.

However, its also a trend that when the more established members of a community start showing discontent with the community, the community itself is falling apart. It seems that is what is happening here, and it really sucks.

I am beginning to hate any and all corporations of the world, despite the good they can bring in moderation (which they cant ever seem to stick with)

---

### Post by buzzingrobot on 2014-03-16
The willingness of people to inconvenience themselves varies greatly, especially if they are asked to inconvenience themselves over ideology about something most of them consider an applicance, just like a TV or a toaster. That's why 30 years of people such as RMS trying to convince people to inconvenience themselves have made few dents in the real world of applicance users.

Proprietary vendors do abandon products and leave users in the lurch.  So, however, do FOSS developers. Those abandoned efforts do not always find new homes. On the flip side, Microsoft kept XP going for 14 years; Apple supported its legacy OS 9 product for years after the rollout of OS X via emulation.

Commercial and FOSS developers have incentives that don't map perfectly, but we, as users, are subject to their decisions, whatever motivates them.

### Post by varunendra on 2014-03-22
> **sherrod said:**
> anyone that tells you they have no problem with it?

I'm glad to be in the 'Rare' crowd. :)

I don't do very professional level video editing, but whatever I (occasionally) do is certainly much more complex and advanced than what you described (working with multiple audio/video tracks, images, sometimes effects etc.).

And this 'Rare luck' of mine spans across a couple of HP Pavillion DX2480 (E7400 CPU + 1/2 GB RAMs), two HCL (E7500 CPU + 1 GB RAM), even an old Lenovo Think Center (<something> 2100 CPU), a bunch of self assembled Desktops (AMD Phenom2 X2 550/555 + 2 GB RAM on Asus M5A-78M-LE/LX mobo) - all these systems were involved in basic and occasional video editing when I was working in a media house about two years ago (going to work with the assembled ones again in a few days from now). All of them were running Ubuntu 10.04, Ubuntu Studio 10.04 or Linux Mint 10.

Currently I have an Asus X54C laptop with i3-2330M CPU + 2+2 GB RAM, and do similar type of video editing on OpenShot sometimes. These videos range from a 2 min clip to 2+ hour seminar/functions.

So far, I have NOT experienced a SINGLE CRASH on any of these systems on any occasion - with OpenShot. I also have installed KDEnlive, and have used Cinelerra in the past, but have rarely used these.

So.., if you are feeling better after venting your frustration here, maybe you should post your problem in "**Multimedia & Video**" section where you might get some professional advice/insight into why it is happening with you and what you can/should do about it. People like me would almost always suspect the RAM first, but I'm sure your reply would be - "But it works fine on Windows" - to which I may not have an answer. :)

**PS:**
I have no hesitation in admitting that most of the GUI video editors in Linux are not as mature as some Windows ones, at least in my opinion. But as per my personal experience, they are not nearly as bad as you described either.

### Post by xeddog on 2014-04-27
For me, there isn't really anything I HATE about Unity, but there are a few things I dislike.  There was one thing I did hate about it and that was the inability to have the application menu attached to its window instead of the global menu at the top of the screen.  Fortunately, that has been resolved in 14.04.  But now it seems canonical is forcing us into their way of doing things.  For example the lack of customization options.  There are some things that irritate me that seem like they should be customizable.  Like:

1.  Ability to move the window controls (min/max/close) to the right side.  I have been using Unity for a little while, but when I want to close a window my first action is still to look, and start moving the cursor to the upper right corner.  My grand daughter is tired of chastising my language over that one.
2.  Ability to auto-hide the top panel.  Sometimes I would like to move a window up into that area.  I can do it, but the applications window slides under the panel.  As it is now that top panel is just wasted space.
3.  Ability to change things like terminal and file manager to open new windows by default.  I'll have a terminal window open and in focus, and want another one.  I go to the launcher and click the terminal launcher and . . . nothing happens.  After the second or third (or fourth or fifth) click I remember, and again I am reminded about my language.


And then a couple of things just seem to be missing.  

1.  A simple graphic tool to make your own launchers.  I have installed a few things that do not create any menu items.  Then I have to go research all over again how to manually create the launcher, and where to put it.   (language reminder again, but by this time my grand daughter is starting to think that sort of language is ok for her too.  %$#@#$# . . . er . . . uh . . . I mean phooey!!!).
2.  All the applets that gnome2 had for the top panel.  I miss some of those.

Other than these few little things I am ok with Unity for now.

X

### Post by PJs Ronin on 2014-05-01
In many ways I agree with the previous poster. I hated Unity when it first came out, then got to like it because I could customise it to my liking. Not that customisation was easy with at least three tweak tools plus unsettings, gconf/dconf and CCSM all competing for attention. Nonetheless, my Unity was starting to become my Unity... and then came convergence. My desktop is a desktop, not a tablet, nor a phone, nor some other hideous device. It's a desktop and I have a bloody huge monitor in front of me and absolutely no desire to extend my hand beyond the reach of my mouse to slide, grow, double tap or any other digital (the original 'digital', ie fingers) expression of what I want to do. My mouse is intelligent enough that with minimal prompting by me, the appropriate application is launched and happy camper status reigns.
  
 Unity is now becoming a confined spirit such that I am reminded of the colour limitations of the original T model Ford (any colour you want as long as it's black).   Window buttons anywhere you want so long as they are on the right, or is it the left.   You don't have to have file manager handling the desktop, but if you choose any other option things won't work as you expect until convergence is complete and then there'll be an app for that.   The top panel... enough said.   So my love affair with Unity approaches the inevitable status of her becoming my 3rd ex.

 Fortunately, I recently discovered a little gem hiding away, XFCE.   Holy snapping frogpoop this is a class DE, faster than the dickins and I can customise it 600 ways to sunday.   Happy camper status is returning, methinks, or am I just infatuated again?

### Post by buzzingrobot on 2014-05-10
> **debiant said:**
> Audio.

I don't mean headphones or a pair of tinny little speakers, I mean nice stuff.  I need it for recording and stuff which I do in Windows on another hard-drive.

I have spent about 12 hours today and finally got Ubuntu to do what Windows does out of the box...  Its a challenge...

I keep learning stuff in the terminal and trying again but I couldn't run a linux computer for everything... what an effort.

It'a a reality that, with few exceptions, commercial vendors of Windows hardware and software have not, and will not, release equivalent products for Linux. They take this position because, first, they do not believe their Linux sales would be profitable.  Second, they do not want to release the source code for their products, as FOSS licensing very likely would require. They believe that releasing their source code under FOSS licenses would simply result in their software  being copied and used without payment by users who would otherwise have paid for it.(They are almost certainly correct.) (I believe the primary reason is financial:  Linux users are widely seen as reluctant to actually buy software.  This is not an entirely unsubstantiated opinion.)

We can debate those positions, but the debate won't change the fact thay they are very widely held.

So... sometimes it is better to stay with products that provide the support you need rather than try to cajole another product into doing something it very likely is not going to very well, if at all.

Terminal use:  The prevalence of terminal commands on the net gives a false impression.  For routine installation, removal and configuration of software, GUI tools are sufficient.  Sites provide equivalent terminal commands because they are universal (different Ubuntu-based distributions ofer different GUI tools, but Bash -- the program you use in a terminal -- is identical across all of them). They do a disservice to users because they only intend for those commands to by blindly copied and pasted, without offering any explanation of how they work.

---

### Post by monkeybrain20122 on 2014-05-14
Everytime when a new release comes out they break a whole bunch of stuffs that worked perfectly before and they rearrange the furniture in such a way that workarounds that previously worked no longer work but without fixing the bugs that require those workarounds in the first place. It takes several months for these to be fixed or new workarounds to be found but they cut short support for interim releases so that you are left hanging high and dry. 

At this point if someone wants to install Ubuntu which version  should he/she get? 13.10 is the best in terms of performance and reliability but it has only two months of support. 12.04 is supported for a while but terribly out of date and performance wise not that great. After testing 14.04 'stable' release for a month and seriously looking at the bug  reports I don't think it is ready unless your use case is confined to facebooking and email, and preferably have a desktop and use wired connection. 14.04 is very fast but speed alone doesn't make up for the things that have been broken.

This is the condition with all new Ubuntu releases since I started with 10.04, so excuse me, I have little sympathy for people who rush to trade in their solid OS for a new release at the first opportunity and get badly burned,--and no sympathy for people who do "upgrade" with upgrade manager without doing some seriously research first and are left with a broken mess, but that is a different point. My considerations are for people who want to do a new Ubuntu install now. With 18 months of support you have plenty of time to wait til the bugs get fixed and stick with ubuntu -1 in the mean time. It would make more sense if they support interim releases for 12 months, that would smooth over the gap.

---

### Post by Kurt_Alan on 2014-05-18
**[COLOR="#000000"][SIZE=5][/SIZE][/COLOR]**

I am an ex-Unity hater making peace with and adapting to the new DE. However, that does not mean I'm in love. Canonical and Shuttleworth: I get it.  Unity.  Create a simple cross-platform GUI and promulgate it world-wide.  A laudable goal.

However, the "Desktop" is not just a metaphor.  It's a real place in which to do work. But Unity's philosophy of minimalism and ease-of-use gets conflated with "Cleanliness is next to Godliness."  A clean desktop looks great but is sadly lacking in utility.  This is a Canonical choice that impinges on users' ("Sally" in the title) ability to do work from the largest viewable interface--the desktop.

The fact that you cannot drag or pin Terminal to the Desktop is the proof of this bias.  If not the Terminal, what is Linux?

What you can put from the sacred Launcher to the Desktop is a crapshoot.  Most app icons will not transfer.  Non-transferability and non-choice are built into the system for the sake of Unity.

Sure, there are some items you can put on the Desktop. But there is neither rhyme nor reason which you can and which you cannot.  Here are some examples of what you can put on the Desktop: Home, Devices, Dropbox, Folders, Chrome app shortcuts . . . Yeah, so I could completely clutter my desktop with these items.  This is allowed.  But I cannot choose my clutter.  One's clutter is another's ability to do work.  Just ask Sally.

---

### Post by buzzingrobot on 2014-05-19
> **Kurt_Alan said:**
> 

...the "Desktop" is not just a metaphor.  It's a real place in which to do work... looks great but is sadly lacking in utility.  

To each their own, and all that, but I've never found much utility in the desktop.  You cannot actually "do work" there.  All you can do is clutter it up with icons that you cannot see when you are actually doing anything else. Like using an application to get some actual work done. A lot of Windows users seem to be happy minimizing windows to expose the desktop, but that's never struck me as ideal.

> The fact that you cannot drag or pin Terminal to the Desktop is the proof of this bias.  If not the Terminal, what is Linux?

That depends if you think the big selling point of Linux is that you can bang around in a teminal. 

You can put a lot of icons on the desktop and you will need to click to minimize at least one window to get at the icon you want. Meanwhile, in a dock-based system, those icons are always a single click away and you don't need to do any desktop pruning to find them.

'Course, a dock isn't going to help much if someone loads up their desktop with 501 icons of their "most frequently" used apps.   But, that's just bizarre.

### Post by Bill Tetzeli on 2014-05-20
> **Kurt_Alan said:**
> 
The fact that you cannot drag or pin Terminal to the Desktop is the proof of this bias.  If not the Terminal, what is Linux?

What you can put from the sacred Launcher to the Desktop is a crapshoot.  Most app icons will not transfer.  Non-transferability and non-choice are built into the system for the sake of Unity.

The fact that Canonical went to the trouble of inhibiting the ability to pin launchers to the desktop was a tacit admission that they HAD to, because otherwise people would continue doing so, therefore a tacit admission that they were forcing their view of what the desktop should be on users.  No wonder the interface is so unpopular.  People like having choices, not having choices curtailed - especially for reasons purely of personal or corporate taste.  Fortunately you don't have to use Unity, so don't.  That's what really rankles me, and I suspect a lot of other people - that Canonical decided that was a choice that belongs in their hands and not ours.  If someone doesn't like the desktop, they have every right to set it up the way Canonical does.  But I have yet to see a post on this forum titled "How can I make it impossible to create launch shortcuts or put other items on my desktop?"  Like Windows Metro, it's a solution to a problem that doesn't exist.

[QUOTE=buzzingrobot][COLOR=#000000]To each their own, and all that, but I've never found much utility in the desktop. You cannot actually "do work" there. All you can do is clutter it up with icons that you cannot see when you are actually doing anything else. Like using an application to get some actual work done. A lot of Windows users seem to be happy minimizing windows to expose the desktop, but that's never struck me as ideal.[/COLOR][/QUOTE]

"My own" means using the desktop as either a temporary staging area for work I'll be on for the next few hours or days that will eventually get moved to the appropriate folder in Home (video/music/pictures etc.) but I don't want to have to burrow down in there every time I want to access it, or a few docs or files I access with great frequency.  It's just as easy (easier) to clutter the Unity bar with tons of shortcuts, windows, squint for that tiny arrow indicating whether the shortcut indicates an active window or not, etc.  For me it actually involves more effort, scrolling up and down to find what I want, than clicking once on the Show Desktop button in the top panel, finding and double-clicking on the file or shortcut I need.  Xubuntu provides me with a usable desktop that I can work with - the "clutter" I prefer over Shuttleworth's "wasted space" - and a bottom panel with launch icons that's easier to use and accommodates more icons in one viewing than the Unity bar does.  Best of both worlds.

---

### Post by buzzingrobot on 2014-05-20
> **Bill Tetzeli said:**
> The fact that Canonical went to the trouble of inhibiting the ability to pin launchers to the desktop was a tacit admission that they HAD to, because otherwise people would continue doing so, therefore a tacit admission that they were forcing their view of what the desktop should be on users.  No wonder the interface is so unpopular.  People like having choices, not having choices curtailed - especially for reasons purely of personal or corporate taste.  Fortunately you don't have to use Unity, so don't.

Canonical didn't force anything on me because I don't pin icons to the desktop. Seems pointless to me, as I mentioned earlier.

If Canonical had, in fact, designed an interface that depended on placing icons on the desktop, aka Windows, then, by your reasoning, Canonical would have been forcing something on me while it made you happy.

Software design isn't intended to provide a one-size-fits-all universally popular solution.  Nor is it a user-led democracy. That's impossible.  Plenty of alternative interfaces that let you do what you want to do can be installed on Ubuntu in a few minutes (otherwise known as "choice"). No one is forcing anyone to use Unity or any other interface.

### Post by monkeybrain20122 on 2014-05-20
I have just installed lubuntu on my roommate's laptop. I found out that I cannot pin launchers on the desktop either except to copy the .desktop file to the Desktop folder (which works with Unity as well as far as I know, never tried it). There is no drag and drop from the menu or left click "pin to desktop" option. Maybe there are some settings that I am missing, I am not sure. Now for lxde (xfce) there is a legitimate reason to pin launchers on desktop because the menu is a pain to navigate, but not for unity or gnome-shell.

No DE allows infinite customization. They only allow customizations consistent with their design goals. For example, the allegedly infinitely customizable KDE allows you to fool around with decorative stuffs but it is very inflexible if you want to customize what you install, it is not modular and there is a much bigger chance that removing a small piece may break your kde desktop than with other DES. 

So if you insist on working in a way inconsistent with the DE's design you will run into problems. Better just use a different DE.

### Post by buzzingrobot on 2014-05-27
> **TheHesser said:**
> Like many before me, I think the biggest thing I dislike that it isn't easy to download a theme and install it.



If a PPA exists for the theme, use that.

If the theme is available as a packaged Ubuntu deb, use that.

Failing that, download the usual compressed tar file, extract it, move/copy the included theme directory to /usr/share/themes or ~/.themes. 

Unity includes no theme-changing gizmo, so you do need to install and use Unity Tweak Tool or Ubuntu Tweak.

And, of course, themes need to be compatible with the GTK version used in a given Ubuntu release. That's down to the theme writer to update as necessary.

### Post by David_Wright on 2014-06-13
> **Un_Known said:**
> ... one issue left to deal with,and it's very minor, namely i now need to get forum help to find our why, and how to cure xubunuts habbit of reverting back to US eng all the time, and usually not resetting to uk eng at all!!!

Sounds like one of the known release issues.  See [here ]("https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/ibus/+bug/1284635")for solution - I seem to recall that the solution when I upgraded to Lubuntu 14.04 was:

sudo apt-get remove ibus
```

### Post by robin7 on 2014-06-21
> **deadflowr said:**
> What parts are those?

I'm not aware of any!  But I do find it curious that so much cool stuff that Ubuntu has never finds its way "upstream" to Debian, even though Debian and Ubuntu share many of the same developers!

One of the most obvious is the great Ubiquity installer. Elegant, effortless, fast, and ultimately user-friendly, Ubiquity would make Debian a snap to install. Not that the new Debian installer is "hard," it's not. In fact it's really easy now. But it's retro-ugly and a whole lot slower.

Another is Plymouth, at least for some people.  I don't really care about boot times or splash during bootup or any of that. But I have read complaints from some "upstream" users about how Plymouth supposedly slows everything down.  Strange, then, that my Xubuntu boots up to a ready desktop in under 10-15 seconds, while my Debian-based alternate distro, without Plymouth, takes from 30 seconds to a full minute.  Neither distro has a bunch of services enabled at startup on my computers. I have Bluetooth and other stuff that I don't use turned off at startup on both distros. Still Xubuntu goes [COLOR=#800080]*"ZING! Yes Sir, Right away Sir"*[/COLOR] while the AntiX/MX-14 (a superb distro by the way: Debian Stable, Xfce desktop, light and fast) goes [COLOR=#0000cd]*"Uh, okay, in a minute, pal."*[/COLOR] Not that I care about fast boot-up, I go and grab a cup of coffee while it boots. But in the time it takes me to grab a cup and sit down, Xubu has my desktop up and seems to say, [COLOR=#800080]*"The crew awaits your orders, Captain"*[/COLOR] while it's Plymouth-free counterpart is still scrolling through it's wall of text. It seems to say, [COLOR=#0000cd]*"Yeah, wha'd'y'want now?"*[/COLOR] So how does Plymouth get blamed for "slowing things down?"

The cool stuff that Ubuntu does to make Debian simpler and easier without sacrificing speed is just amazing!  Is it just arrogance or is there some good reason that "downstream" improvements never seem to find their way "upstream?" Ubuntu has no "proprietary" bits that I know of, just bits that are unique to Ubuntu and it's family of flavors and derivatives.

### Post by Cbhihe on 2014-06-23
I am new to Ubuntu Desktop. I come from Unix with a too long spell on terminals emulated on Windoze based machines. (just to put things on perspective...). I toil away on an HP laptop workstation.
 I work as much from non login terminal windows or from the login console as I do from the gui. All that follows has to do with the gui. 

Don't likes: 

[B]Number one: 
[/B]_The Unity launch bar can only open on the left edge of the main display_. 
Why is it that Canonical should give no more flexibility than the option to open it on the left screen edge ?
Why do I gripe about that ?
 - I have two screens:
     A) the display of my laptop, which also is where my stdin (keyboard) is. That stays in front of me. 
     B) a larger external display on the left of the laptop, on which I have an extended desktop, courtesy of Unity.
          Why put the ext display on the left ? Because I physically cannot put it right of the laptop. 
 - I go from one screen to the next traversing the common edge they share 
(i.e. on the left of the built in screen on my laptop and on the right of the external display)
  - The launch bar opens on the far left side of the external display, i.e. to the far left of my vision field

The result is I have my head turned 75-80 degress to the left, 80- 90% of the time, in order to use the launch bar and drag all the windows that open in the external display BY DEFAULT, onto the laptop screen in front of me. 
That is because Unity does not allow for the launch bar to open on the right hand side edge of any display.

This becomes physically painful for neck and back after a day, definitely a big no no after a week. 
Has Canonical ever heard the word "usability" ? 

**Number two:**
_Opening windows in the gui always reverts to default pixel location._
It is so even when, for instance in the case of Thunderbird, the application is up and running and it has to do with opening the contacts' window. The contacts' window as any window will not remember in what position it was when it was last closed. So, anyway, a completely arbitrary choice seems to have been made, to always open a new window in the upper left corner of the display in focus. The same happens with Firefox with term windows, with everything that runs in the gui layer.

**Number three:**
_Unusually long boot times_
About 2 months after a fresh install, Ubuntu desktop takes about 57s to boot, significantly longer than the 14 s it took intially (before I modestly tweaked the desktop). By boot I mean the process that gets you to the user session's screen, i.e. where the user enters his/her passwd to actually start a session with the gui. 
Blacklisting HP's or Intel's useless AMT / MEI related service module(s) in the kernel does not help (it just suppresses error messages at boot time). i have not tried "removing" the said kernel modules.

**Number four:**
_Mouse pointer not automatically placed on default button in dialog-boxes_
Instead the pointer is a few millimiters above the default button. I don't think it is a bug. I think it is a "feature" to ensure that if Enter is accidentally hit, then the default button of the dialog box is not automatically activated.
Why does not Canonical in that case offer those two options of config ? The user could define whether he/she wants the button slightly above or smack on the dialog box default button. 
It is not difficult to implement.

=================================
It for now. For the rest I am quite happy to be working with something as nice and friendly as Linux Ubuntu and I find the various communities and the spirit of assistance between users, from noobs to black-hatters, wondrous.

-ced.

---

QUITE RIGHT TOO,I SAY!!! (dont want booted out of the forums quite yet. only been on here for two weeks, though feels like half a blimmin lifetime!!!

so for the benefit of my intrepid fans,here are....
MY TOP TEN THINGS TO HATE ABOUT UBUNTU! PLUS!
1: audio keeps going "mute" on media players,when all indicates the audio's fine system wide!!!! arrrrggghhh!!!
 (and if i EVER get a smooth enough system running to do audio production,i'll eat my hat!)

2: when i installed (from windows 7) to ubuntu 14.04,it came with unity desktop. various weird things happened,and acted like a virussed up,memory over-loaded windows.
latency between clicking and something loading up enough to run properly taking up to 30 or40 seconds, programs that freeze up, then a pop-up that declares and asks "do you want to keepit running.,or continue?!!!!! arrrggghhh!!! the things was, that clicking EITHER button choice DIDNT MAKE A BLIND BIT OF DIFFERENCE!!!

great? um,no....... it's now -after running fine for a week and a half- doing all the stuff stated in point 2:!!!!

well i am in terminal command,and popping in some command or sudo or another, and am asked for my password .ok? MY PASSWORD.; same one i've been using for a few things long before i migrated to ubuntu.  it FAILED my password!!! over twenty times. fail fail fail.INFURIATING when you just wanted to do a 30 second job. failed!! and i knew it was right. and it was right. i was putting in correct password.

after reboot it was fine. but how long till next time?
around this time i got MORE slowness, latency,and so on. programs taking for ever to load. many not loading at all (at least visibly). even now VLC appears only as a tab at top of page on that tasky bar thingy along the top.

4:many interfaces of many programs dont appear to have humanly logical menus on them, and your left feeling totally handicapped. whats the big idea in that?
in for example music production, outwith the short-cut keys, those menus are needed,and should be there. its like redesigning the car, not putting in a wheel, fuel gauge, tires, doors,and no controls for the engine,. then it being expected to be your fault because they are all in the boot,and YOU didnt look there,did you? aaaarrrggghhhh!!!!!!!!!! or having a cupboard,but everything is locked inside,and your not allowed in.

5: wine/playonlinux are really only much good for lightweight work,and gaming(even though many games are heavier on systems than many work programs.
although,it must be said, i enjoy,for those times of meaningless mindless fun times in between work and family ,quite enjoy a dash of secondlife,or need for speed world.
on ubuntu?not a blimmin chance. wine/playonlinux,and that other one, oh, who cares,none of them help me anyhow.

7: why,if there can be repositories of files and bits and bobs,cant there be a terminal command prompt database with a repository(starting to be reminded of certain gastric implant "medecine" which enters ones body,through the not mouth end!) and that data repository acts as a massive FAQs and troubleshooting,and fixes pool?

bringing me to next point,8

8: i spent over 20 hours,missing much sleep last night and a bit of the night before actually,just trying to find cures,fixes,installs/uninstalls and so on to fix things, and everytime i think THATS IT! ITS FIXED..bang, next thing,and usually worse.
i've spent around half an hour doing things I NEED TO DO, spend yet over twenty hours in the past 40 hours faffing around to fix the most piddly stupid annoying thi8ngs that are just robbing my life from me!!!
i thought it was programmers/hackers/developers who's job it is to do all that work before you get to use it as a user?
yeh yeh yeh, i know ubuntu/linux-like platforms are free.but that is NOT the point.

despite claims i'd heard before installing,that ubuntu can load much much faster than any windows system can.for me,this most certainly is NOT the case.
i have an ancient lump of trash PC though the back, which has a basic load of hardware within, basic ancient drivers, and so on, running a VERY old version of windows XP,which boots in well under 20 seconds. and tends to be practically,up and running not much after the desktop appears. and it can still run my music production software reasonably well to at least act as a quality sketch pad,if not genuine mastering suite.

although,it can hang with an old screensaver for anything up to a minute, now and again before desktop items appear.but all in all,it dosnt do that often.
i'd heard,maybe erroneously,that ubuntu would ALWAYS load in under 20 seconds.
well, in my experience,only windows XP does that.

9: volume control for audio vanished off that black bar at the top of the page that has tabs for each running device, wotever you call it.

10:  i havnt successfully run ANYTHING that i actually NEED to run.
and not one single game i enjoy can be run in any form, with any stability, with any type of wine/playonlinux/other-thing too. (virtua box? sh*******azzbatt!

when running windows,i didnt even know i was on a 64bit laptop.but it didnt make much noticable difference. on here it is REALLY infuriating.
what exactly has the point been of releasing 64bit,when not a lot on here works on it?

14: many "libraries" (are they an equivalent, so to speak,of .dll's?) "lib this" and lib thats". many of these for devices i need are now out of date and obsolete,and dont work in 14!!! why should this have to be?

not a stitch on even my very old now cubase, or traktor 2.

16: and lastly, i hope,but not least-  INSTALLING THINGS!
software centre? pfffff, halfd of that seems to install. then go to your wotever-that-menu-on-left-hand-side-of-screen-is-called on xfce,and type the app/program into it to find it... well wadda yah know? nothing.

by terminal- how many files fail each time? really annoying.

### Post by Cbhihe on 2014-06-26
@[URL="http://ubuntuforums.org/member.php?u=1729120"][B][COLOR=#000000]ibjsb4
[/COLOR][/B][/URL]Thank you. I am going to try that this weekend. Hope it can be undone in unsatisfactory case.
@**LastDino**, unfortunately I am not yet to the point/level of setting up a VM. I have thought about XFCE for some time though. It is light weight and I like that.
There is also Classic Menu indicator. That does not replace completely the Lanch bar function but places useful applications right in front of me in the GUI.

But again in the hope of attracting some attention at Canonical, why in the world is the Launch bar not customizable to the mere extent of being on any 3 or 4 sides of any screen ? Again, I am no dev, but this does not seem overly complex to implement. A question of priorities for the Ubuntu dev-teams maybe ?

@**Un_Known**
I was first tempted by a pun reading your shout. Instead, you have my sympathy and allow me one piece of unsollicited advice: don't put something for which you have no technical feel (yet) into production. The most likely result will be frustration and in the end despair as you experienced it already.
Ubuntu is an excellent all-purpose desktop environment and a superb server environment despite its sometimes surprising bashisms. It takes some getting used to it if you come from Windows as seems to be yr case (per yr example, drag-n-drop is not so common in Lx/Ux). Depending on what you want to do, you have to invest +/- time in navigating Lx' unfamiliar waters, again, if you are new to Lx !  In the end it'll pay off. But as already mentionned, you don't have the luxury of time if you are in production from day one. And that again seems to be the case. So you have a choice:
1) sit back and take one problem at a time - explain it in the right forum. Somebodies WILL help you !
2) or - play with Lx on a test machine for as long as you need until you feel confortable with running a system in production. Meanwhile stick to what with which you're plain confortable.

Have a beer, go for a jog, catch a good tube if you are close to any surfy beach, and keep trying. We've all been there.

### Post by buzzingrobot on 2014-06-26
> **Un_Known said:**
> THINGS I HATE ABOUT UBUNTU?

e.g. screen too far to right, have to fix each time i boiot up,then its now too far to left,and have to fix each time to boot up.

the wrong desktop keeps appearing.

audio volume keeps vainishing, (considering my main reasonm for having a laptop if for music/dj production)

Have you posted questions about each specific problem in the appropriate forum here, with details of your system, including any unsupported software you've added and configuration changes you have made? 


> what use is it trying to install stuff when you just get "what do you want to use to open this file" and it points at absolutely nothing useful to open the file with!
most installations dont actually install properly at all. 

If your install packages built for Ubuntu, you should not see that question. Those packages exist in the Ubuntu repositories and on other unoffical third-party servers.

If you try to install software in the typical Windows fashion -- by downloading compressed archives from some website --  you will very likely have problems. In Windows, you very often download a ".zip" archive (perhaps disguised as an ".exe") that contains all the necessary support libraries, etc.  That archive is simply expanded into a new directory and the program will run.  In Linux -- not just Ubuntu -- very little software is packaged this way. If you see Linux software packaged in ".tar.gz" archives, be aware that they almost certainly contain source code, not ready-to-run executables.

> 64 bit was a waste of time as hardly anything 64 bit works wqell., or at all.

Specifics?  What does this mean?  

> media players that cant even recognise mp3 files,cant drag and drop onto them, and they can only recognise ISO file?
i mean,WT*?

wine/playonlinux/virtualbox dont really seem to work well with anything USEFUL.


If, perhaps, you are a gamer trying to use Linux as a direct replacement for Windows, be aware that it is not. Windows games are developed to run on Windows, not Linux.  Efforts like Wine and PlayOnLinux are independent projects that enable a limited subset of Windows apps to run, in some fashion, on Linux. They are not intended to be, and do not claim to be, a substitute for Windows.

If you have added a variety of unsupported and unofficial software from a variety of locations to try to make Windows games work better, perhaps your problems are caused by the alterations those additions have made.

I don't like that, with a freshly installed Ubuntu 14.04, Firefox darkens, pauses, and freezes at random when I click on a new link or attempt to enter a site after in-putting password. It was so bad I just removed Firefox. Switched to Chromium and can now post my dislike.

I also don't like that my graphics card and mouse don't have drivers. Best Razer mouse thread I found is here: [http://www.phoronix.com/scan.php?page=news_item&px=MTU4NTQ](http://www.phoronix.com/scan.php?page=news_item&px=MTU4NTQ)

I'm going to try to switch from Unity to Xubuntu. I want things a little snappier and less liable to pause, fade to darkened screen. 

Back in the Jaunty days, even in the 12.04 days, Ubuntu ran great on my mostly stock Dell XPS machines. Now Windows 7 gives me less production headache than 14.04 (though still steeped in security flaws requiring commercial security programs--Kaspersky, Malwarebytes, Piriform utilities). 

P.S. **Assorted headaches and successes**: 
Xubuntu runs better on my machine than the Unity/Gnome setup in Ubuntu.
I was able to get the GeForce driver to work wonderfully for the GTX 750 Ti via [http://www.binarytides.com/install-nvidia-drivers-ubuntu-14-04/](http://www.binarytides.com/install-nvidia-drivers-ubuntu-14-04/). Great instructions.
Conky is working and I'm having fun tweaking it.
Was able to load Chrome.
Couldn't get Pandora to work in Chromium OoTB, but after some frustration, got Flash working. Soundcloud and Mixcloud work fine. Learned at [https://wiki.ubuntu.com/Chromium/Getting-Flash](https://wiki.ubuntu.com/Chromium/Getting-Flash)[COLOR=#333333] about installing [/COLOR][pepperflashplugin-nonfree]("https://launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/pepperflashplugin-nonfree"). 
Amazon music and Prime music work fine. But no Amazon video.
Have lost all confidence in Firefox. Saw a Mozilla thread with some ideas on possibly getting it to work: [https://support.mozilla.org/en-US/questions/953084](https://support.mozilla.org/en-US/questions/953084). But it's long, a number of suggested things to try, and I haven't had time to get into it. 

**Things I can't do**:
No Amazon video.
No Steam. 
No Netflix (but I gave up on that long ago).

*I dislike that I can't do these things and I globally dislike that the OoTB experience requires so much tuning and fixing*.

[was] Gnome 3.8.4
Intel(R) Core(TM) i7 CPU  930  @ 2.80GHz 
64-bit
8GB

Razer DeathAdder mouse

1.  Google Earth doesn't work completely.  The Panoramio photos don't show.  It works fine in Kubuntu, though.

So I can whine and I will, but I'll keep coming back.

Gnome Shell does allow opening, via middle-click, of a new app in a new workspace, eliminating the need to manually move to the new workspace first. (At least through 3.10.  It seems to be gone in 3.12.)

I disable hot corners, tiling, etc., in Unity. I don't use compositing elsewhere if I can disable it.

### Post by buzzingrobot on 2014-07-31
> **Nikolai508 said:**
> It seems to be trying to separate itself from Linux as much as possible even though obviously it still is Linux.

Many people are scared, or at least wary, of moving to Linux. And, in fact, you don't move to some thing called "Linux".  You move to one of many distributions of Linux (itself something that confuses prospective users). Because Ubuntu is specifically targeting new users, it makes sense to focus on the Ubuntu brand as the identity of a product with their interests in mind, rather than other distributions that refuse to alter their dogmatic approaches and seem to expect choosing to use Linux to be equivalent to adopting a new, rather rigid, ideology and putting up with a a lot of inconveniences just to be faithful to that ideology.

>  When you go to download Ubuntu it takes you to a donation page and the download does NOT start automatically. You have to scroll to bottom and press the text link to download. This just feels very cheap and not the way to ask for donations. The download should of already started by the time the donation page is showed not the other way around.

Well, maybe,  But, there is an equally inappropriate, widespread, and rather romanticized feeling that making and supporting FOSS products ought to be strictly a charity, never a business, and that FOSS users are entitled to gratis products.

### Post by buzzingrobot on 2014-08-10
> **fattyz said:**
> You still have to search and search to fix things. 

It's been my experience with any OS that when I modify something in an unofficial and unsupported fashion, or add unsupported software, then my chances for trouble increase significantly. 

On the other hand, if I let good enough alone,  things remain good enough.

Change always introduces the risk of error.  Altering Ubuntu or Windows or OS X in a ways that are either unsupported by or unknown to the OS vender is setting yourself up for breakage, especially when the vendor applies updates and other changes.  Those changes must assume they are being applied to a standard baseline installation.  If I've altered something and it breaks, I'm to blame, not Canonical or Microsoft or Apple.

One of the down sides of open source is that the internet is littered with bad, wrong, vague and outdated information and advice that never seems to go away. Users need to be careful when trolling the net looking for fixes, etc., that the advice they use actually has been tested successfully with the software they are running.


> Since I upgrade the IOS software on iphone I "have unable to mount/trust this computer" error.

Apple has a strong financial incentive to make sure their software and its updates can get along with Windows.  They have no such incentive in regard to Ubuntu.  Or any other Linux distribution. In fact, as any number of vendors have said, the sheer number of Linux distributions, the differences between them, and the rapid changes that occur are, alone, enough to keep them away from Linux compatibility. 

> I have never used Unity I hate it.

Just about every Linux desktop interface on the planet is available for Ubuntu.

### Post by buzzingrobot on 2014-08-13
> **karl15 said:**
> I don't like the categories in Software Center. I'd like to see all programs at once. 

Synaptic will give you a finer-grained look at things. It displays the individual packages available in the repositories and will also show any dependencies that will be installed and/or if anything will be removed if you go through with the installation of a package.

All the packages for all Ubuntu releases are searchable and browsable at packages.ubuntu.com.

### Post by Dr Kayak on 2014-08-23
I just installed 14.04 on my laptop, resurrecting the struggle to wrest control of my computing environment from unity-crazed developers.  In 12.04 I battled with things like a clunky workspace switcher, crazy global menus, a cartoon-like launcher, disappearance of the applications menu, etc., before I realized that the xfce4 desktop could solve most of my problems.  Along the way, I kept running into Mark Shuttleworth quotes assuring me that these changes were good for me, even if I didn't realize it.

As I used unity to start the customization of my 14.04 desktop, I was astonished to see an Amazon icon in the launcher.  Google searches unearthed the ubuntu 'spyware' issues for me, and I couldn't believe my eyes.  Isn't this sort of corporate whoring the antithesis of what linux is about?  Wait, there's a Mark Shuttleworth quote saying it makes 'perfect sense' to do this, so stop whining and get with the program.  Well I couldn't, so I spent a bunch of time trying to disable as much of the spy/commercial ware as possible, with limited success (e.g., I can't get Other > Amazon off the xfce4 Applications Menu).

It's really disappointing to see how far ubuntu has strayed from an easily customizable, open-source environment to a clunky, do-it-our-way OS with windows-like commercial ambitions.  Shuttleworth's arrogance in defending his crusade is eerily similar to that of the billionaire Seattle-ite with the ten-dollar haircut.  I've had enough, and won't be upgrading my other three machines to 14.04.  I'm looking for a cleaner, purer linux variant without the unity advocacy and commercialization, that lets me do things the way I want to do them -- kind of like ubuntu used to be.  I'd welcome suggestions amidst the flames this post is sure to generate!

---

### Post by buzzingrobot on 2014-08-23
> **Dr Kayak said:**
> I just installed 14.04 on my laptop, resurrecting the struggle to wrest control of my computing environment from unity-crazed developers.  In 12.04 I battled... I was astonished to see an Amazon icon in the launcher... ubuntu 'spyware'.....corporate whoring the antithesis of what linux is about?.. I spent a bunch of time trying to disable as much of the spy/commercial ware as possible, with limited success (e.g., I can't get Other > Amazon off the xfce4 Applications Menu).

...ubuntu has strayed from an easily customizable, open-source environment to a clunky, do-it-our-way OS with windows-like commercial ambitions. 

Three posts here since, at least, 12.04?

The alleged spyware (it's essentially what happens when you click on most Amazon links in blogs, review, etc.: Amazon knows who hosted the link that generated a sale and kickbacks a tiny bit of revenue) is very easily disabled, via System Settings and the Unity Tweak Tool.

Of course, you seem to be running XFCE. Whether installed on Ubuntu Unity or via Xubuntu, the scopes and lenses that are the core of what you call "spyware" in Unity aren't executed. I.e., they are Unity-only.  

As far as I know, there are no ideology police in Linux, and there shouldn't be (although some self-appointed folks expect everyone else to pay attention to them). The driving force of FOSS, its founding and sustaining intent, is to allow developers to freely share code, not to compel anyone delivering something to users to build in endless configurability. (I'm of the opinion that Shuttleworth is on the right track, that defining the "Linux desktop" as it has traditionally been defined is a deadend.)

All the major, and most of the minor, desktop interfaces are available for Ubuntu.  So, if someone doesn't like Unity, for any reason, plenty of alternative are available.

---

### Post by nadrach on 2014-08-23
Try Lubuntu with LXDE. The lightweight window manager works well on old machines (pretty much all I have available) and I have both 32-bit and 64-bit variants on the go. Clear readable text, even on an old ASUS eeePC, and fast in operation.
Default colours can be a bit washed-out, so after some experimentation (and with the assistance of some strongly-opinioned relatives) we settled on Clearlooks with Olive borders, plus Humanity icons. Very readable across a variety of colour balances on assorted screens. Haven't found any spyware in operation (yet - doesn't stop me looking) and the feedback from said relatives has been nothing but positive.
Best Linux I have used, to date, and I've been trying flavours since 1998.

### Post by Dragonbite on 2014-08-25
> **sharks said:**
> What are the things that u hate in Ubuntu?

I can't quite say I "hate" anything in Ubuntu, but Ubuntu is good with some things I don't necessarily like.  Some of the issues are also not Ubuntu-specific, but Linux-related and thus effects Ubuntu as well.

Ubuntu and Linux-related gripes:
[LIST=1]
[*]No Google Drive application (currently use Grive, but I want an official one from Google since they ever-so-nicely PROMISED one...!).  Heck, I could live with a OneDrive integration even!  Until then, I use Copy.com 
[*]Not 100% satisfied with Unity, Gnome, Xfce, KDE or LXDE.  I kinda toss up between Unity and Gnome shell but both of those are a little heavier on my systems. Xfce is good, but something about it ... KDE is good for the most part.... 
[*]That editing the look and feel of individual components has been removed in place of having to change and entire "theme". For example, you used to be able to right-click on the panel and change the color, place an image as the background or make it transparent in addition to being able to add links, cpu/netork graphs, and many, many other means to customize without having to change EVERYTHING, or having to program your own theme.  Xfce and KDE still provide this but like I said I (currently, at least) prefer Gnome shell/Unity.
[*]That Gnome/Unity does not have a built-in wallpaper changing feature that allows you to rotate between your personal pictures. Xfce and KDE have this (not sure about LXDE) and it requires a 3rd party program like Wallch or Variety.
[*]Game performance.  On the same hardware (dual-boot), Linux lags behind Windows in games like Minecraft or trying to run World of Tanks.  At least Minecraft works fine on the family desktop (Ubuntu 12.04) but if I could get my laptop to run just these just as well as it runs in Windows I could almost shut Windows down completely!  (Good thing is the kids PREFER Linux over Windows... so it is only the games that keeps them using it)
[*]That Linux isn't more readily available at work and places where I don't get the chance to choose my operating system. ;)
[/LIST]

My other complaints are more related to Canonical's activities and it is a mix-match because I can understand it when thinking about it from a business point of view, or I like the outcome of it but maybe not the manner in which Canonical rolled the feature out.

### Post by kernalkorn on 2014-08-28
No specific whinges relating to Ubuntu, which with the latest 14.04, is a generally excellent OS, but some niggling problems with specific applicators written for Synaptic/Cannonical.    As far as I can discover, a total lack of AV stream capture automation.  There is no AV stream capture application or suite of applications for recording radio and or video.  I know one could use Audacity or one of various screen recorders that allow a manual single instance of capture.  What I use in windows allows multiple simultaneous streams of audio video packet capture (depending on bandwidth), which is auto starts on a schedule, automatically segments and converts to a universal playback format.  As an aside I congratulate the ease with which the latest few versions of Ubuntu/VLC can playback DVDs.  With a minimal low end Comcast 3.5MBbps connection I can capture 1 video + 4 radio streams or 7 radio streams at once.     (CR)Small annoyances in playback utilities like Rythmbox, which was redesigned with a tiny progress bar that makes precision placement of the playback point very difficult   (CR)A redesigned Libre Writer with obtuse and pointless modifications to a menu system that was already only marginal.  Take a look at the WordPad text util in Win7 as an example of inspired design.     (CR)A less than exemplary Windows application simulator that is opaque and largely unusable to the nervous newbie (WINE).    (CR)Small things that are not directly the responsibility of Ubuntu-Canonical OS have a huge impact on the general confidence of a Windows user to migrate away from what is stable, reliable and known to a questionably inconsistent "Freedom" system.    (CR) carriage return - paragraph break

### Post by chopra on 2014-09-07
I was just looking over this thread.  Two points:
for something annoying about Ubuntu, how about: I can't use the less command to look at directories, as I can in some other linux systems.  Why not?

Also, I read this quote from Mark Shuttleworth:
"*We are not telling Amazon what you are searching for. Your anonymity  is preserved because we handle the query on your behalf. Don’t trust  us?_ **Erm, we have root. You do trust us with your data already**_**.**"*

WTF does this mean?  They "have root"?  What data is there about us that they collect, and how do we know we can trust what they say?  Bottom line, what's really going on in your machine is most likely a black box to most of us, and we only have the word of someone else (who has to put food on the table for themselves, and isn't charging us money for their product) that they're giving us what they claim. Doesn't make me sleep very easily.

### Post by buzzingrobot on 2014-09-08
> **chopra said:**
> ... I can't use the less command to look at directories, as I can in some other linux systems.

"less" is a standard Linux command to display the content of text files.  "ls" is the standard Unix/Linux command to list the content of directories, i.e., files and other directories. Both work just fine in Ubuntu.

> Also, I read this quote from Mark Shuttleworth:
"*We are not telling Amazon what you are searching for. Your anonymity  is preserved because we handle the query on your behalf. Don’t trust  us?_ **Erm, we have root. You do trust us with your data already**_**.**"*

Actually, it doesn't mean much of anything. In the literal sense, Canonical does not have root access to Ubuntu machines because, in the first place, a default Ubuntu install does not create a root user.  Even if it did, Canonical wouldn't have the root password for any Ubuntu install.

When I read that, I took it to be a way of saying that Ubuntu users do their installs from ISO images created by Canonical and maintained on Canonical servers, and download and install software from those same server.  That, as well as many other fundamental processes and daemons that are always executing on any given machine, execute with root privileges (just as we do when we preface commands with "sudo".) That's the way Unix and Linux are intended to work.

The difference is that there's a lot more incentive for shenanegans where computers are involved, with marketing, spying, etc.  So it's just an expression of the reality of the world we live in.


  	 	 	 	   > **buzzingrobot said:**
> "less" is a standard Linux command to display the content of text files. "ls" is the standard Unix/Linux command to list the content of directories, i.e., files and other directories. Both work just fine in Ubuntu.



Actually, it doesn't mean much of anything. In the literal sense, Canonical does not have root access to Ubuntu machines because, in the first place, a default Ubuntu install does not create a root user. Even if it did, Canonical wouldn't have the root password for any Ubuntu install.

When I read that, I took it to be a way of saying that Ubuntu users do their installs from ISO images created by Canonical and maintained on Canonical servers, and download and install software from those same server. That, as well as many other fundamental processes and daemons that are always executing on any given machine, execute with root privileges (just as we do when we preface commands with "sudo".) That's the way Unix and Linux are intended to work.
  
First, in scientific linux at least, which my work uses, the less command is capable of displaying directory contents, just as vim and emacs are.  It doesn't take up your buffer space like ls does ( you could do ls > textfile and less the textfile, I suppose).  If you read the man page for less, you'll see that it's capable of directory viewing, but that some systems don't allow it for some reason (though it doesn't explain why).

Don't get me wrong, that's not a reason to ditch ubuntu, it's a minor point, but the title of the thread is "Things you dislike in Ubuntu" and that's a small annoyance.

As far as Mark Shuttleworth's comment, I figured on a similar interpretation to yours, I just wasn't sure.  He sure used a wierd choice of words (at least, I hope that's what it is).

### Post by buzzingrobot on 2014-09-09
> **chopra said:**
> 

                        
  
First, in scientific linux at least, which my work uses, the less command is capable of displaying directory contents, just as vim and emacs are.  It doesn't take up your buffer space like ls does ( you could do ls > textfile and less the textfile, I suppose).  If you read the man page for less, you'll see that it's capable of directory viewing, but that some systems don't allow it for some reason (though it doesn't explain why).



I have a CentOS system, which is built from the same Red Hat source as Scientific.  Less does not list directory contents, and its manual doesn't mention it. It will attempt to display the *contents* of files whose names have been passed to it by the shell.  E.g., "less *.*" in a directory with PDF's and plain text files results in less displaying the text files and complaining about the pdf's.  It is the shell (Bash) that expands the "*.*" argument and passes the resulting list of filenames to less, as it does for any command.

I don't understand your concern about buffers or the reason for the "ls > textfile" construction? (Unless you want to create a file containing the listing.) If you need to page a lengthy directory listing, why not "ls > less"?

---

### Post by chopra on 2014-09-09
> **buzzingrobot said:**
> I have a CentOS system, which is built from the same Red Hat source as Scientific.  Less does not list directory contents, and its manual doesn't mention it. It will attempt to display the *contents* of files whose names have been passed to it by the shell.  E.g., "less *.*" in a directory with PDF's and plain text files results in less displaying the text files and complaining about the pdf's.  It is the shell (Bash) that expands the "*.*" argument and passes the resulting list of filenames to less, as it does for any command.

I don't understand your concern about buffers or the reason for the "ls > textfile" construction? (Unless you want to create a file containing the listing.) If you need to page a lengthy directory listing, why not "ls > less"?

Here's what I was refering to in the man page:

       -f or --force
              Forces non-regular files to be opened.  (A non-regular file is a
              directory  or a device special file.)  Also suppresses the warn&#8208;
              ing message when a binary file is opened.  By default, less will
              refuse to open non-regular files.  Note that some operating sys&#8208;
              tems will not allow directories to be read, even if -f is set.

Saying that "some systems" will not allow directories to be read implies that others will.  I have bash shell for one of my work computers, and tcsh for another, which also uses a different version of scientific linux.  I don't need to use the -f option for less in either case.  I just type: "less dir_name/" and I get a list of files with permissions and stuff displayed, just as if I type: "ls -l > file ; less file". Both of these are the organization's own versions of scientific linux (actually each version is from a different oragnization, but affiliated), so I can't speak for all SCL.

The difference is just a little less (no pun intended) typing into my keyboard to remove the file when I'm done viewing it, and more commands to clog up my command history.  A very minor point, it's just puzzling as to why this functionality would be disabled for Ubuntu, which I otherwise think is a great system.

### Post by lile001 on 2014-10-23
Here is what I don't like:

Unity Desktop.  I am a Gnome desktop guy, glad that I have the option.  I find it is easier to navigate. 
WINE.  Never had any program successfully run under Wine.  Gave up trying.  
PDF readers. There are PDF features that most of the Linux readers won't read, that are embedded in PDFs I use from some vendors, and I have to switch to Winders to read those files. 
Other stuff that requires Winders to run.  Proprietary vendor software, engineering software, see WINE gripe above. 

My top peeve though:  LACK OF PAID TECHNICAL SUPPORT.  [Canonical ]("http://www.canonical.com/services")"claims" they do this, however repeated attempts at contacting have never produced a return call or email, so obviously this "service" is vaporware. I'd be glad to pay Canonical when I am stuck, if they'd respond to my enquiries.  There are no local companies to my knowledge that offer Linux support.   I run Ubuntu in a business, so it costs money when there is a glitch and I can't fix it myself.  It isn't just some random business, it is MY JOB and MY MONEY that is wasted when I am grinding around at the  Terminal trying to fix some glitch.  I've found a few local Geeks that can help, but at this point I know as much as they do.  When something is beyond my meager skill to fix, often the forums are silent as well.  I wish there was someone who had a business that you could just call up when there is an issue and pay them to tell you how to fix it right now.  Any of you geeks that are in such a business PM me and we'll talk.

### Post by coffeecat on 2014-10-25
> **/ADM said:**
> This may be Linux in general but I would love to see better binary running in Ubuntu.  Lets face it, the .exe in Windows is pretty good, double click and done, it runs.  I get annoyed when sometimes I cannot do this in Ubuntu.  Sure ./ in terminal works but a better binary format that works this way is what I would love too see..  Maybe a format like in os x would be great.

This already exists, but we are spoilt in the Ubuntu world for having so much available in PPA 3rd party repositories. For some apps, not in either the official Ubuntu repositories or in a PPA, you simply download a .deb package and double-click on it. Easy. An example is Google Chrome:

[https://www.google.com/chrome/browser/](https://www.google.com/chrome/browser/)

That one is atypical because the downloaded .deb both installs Chrome and adds a source list so that updates can be applied easily.  

If you are having to compile from source, that is not the fault of Linux generally or Ubuntu in particular. It is a collective failure of the community at large to provide compiled packages in either 3rd party repos or by means of downloadable .deb packages. Now I don't mean to be at all judgemental in using the word failure - that is simply a statement of fact. Perhaps the often single-hand developer of the app simply doesn't have time to compile and package it in all the Linux formats such as .deb, .rpm and so on. And no one in the debian world has seen fit to compile, maintain and package it either. That is just the way things are.

### Post by buzzingrobot on 2014-10-25
> **/ADM said:**
> .deb is only for installing.  I mean an actual click to run format like a mac .app for example.  Essentially a self contained package with binary, dependencies, icon file and meta info.  Click and run.  To me a raw elf by itself well, it feels ancient.

Moving to the OS X approach would require fundamental changes in the way Linux code is written, packaged, and organized on disk, from the kernel up, as well as in packaging and dependency resolution, across many distributions, and including retrofitting tens of thusands of existing applications to work in the new environment. I think the odds of this ever happening are effectively zero. While the OSX approach has its merits, no approach is perfect.  Each one makes its own compromises.

Contemporary GUI desktop Linux standards assert that a GUI application include a ".desktop" file that allows the application to be launched by clicking on an icon.  It is the responsiblity of the developer(s) to ensure this .desktop file exists, just as it is the responsibiilty of OS X developers to follow Apple guideliness to ensure that their apps are clickable in OS X.

### Post by richierich1986 on 2014-10-25
I personally use Linux Mint rather than Ubuntu itself. This is because after long time usage, I just got fed up with Unity. No, Unity is not the devils work and doesn't come straight from hell, but I just really prefer a panel that I can customize and therefore Cinnamon works better for me. I also like how Linux Mint is a little more reserved with updates and doesn't push kernel updates. You have to select a newer kernel for install manually. It also handles PPA's and other additional repositories better. Deleting a PPA actually deletes it, instead of leaving stuff behind in etc/apt/sources.list.d. I like Nemo more than Nautilus as well these days.

BTW, I notice some people really ranting on how terrible Windows 8.1 is, but I have to disagree. It is a wonderful OS, although it doesn't work for me. I like open source software. Microsoft has, in my opinion, really improved Windows a lot with the latest update 8.1 update 1. It's snappy, a lot more secure than windows 7 and I like what the startscreen has become. I only use it in VirtualBox though, for school (System Security Officer). So, while I get the adversity towards Microsoft and proprietary software (it's not my cup of tea either. I like transparency), I seriously can't understand why someone would call Windows 8.1 crap. It is a very solid, stable and reasonably secure OS. 

I'll stick with Mint myself though, haha!

### Post by Xriva on 2014-10-25
I'm running Trusty Server with GNome - when I'm in the file manager and I right click on 'white space' the menu does not offer to create a new file,

I don't like that.

Being able to do that would save a lot of file creating text files as needed.

---

### Post by buzzingrobot on 2014-10-25
> **Xriva said:**
> I'm running Trusty Server with GNome - when I'm in the file manager and I right click on 'white space' the menu does not offer to create a new file,

I don't like that.

Being able to do that would save a lot of file creating text files as needed.

Perhaps this is possible ?

Try this: ```


$ cd ~/Templates/ 
$ touch "New Empty File" 
```

Then, right-clicking in Nautilus should show a new "New Document" menu entry with one option, "New Empty File".

Other file templates, e.g., HTML, LibreOffice, etc., can also be added this way.

---

### Post by Xriva on 2014-10-26
Thanks - that worked great !

### Post by brw2 on 2014-10-26
I bought a new SSD on Friday and decided I wanted to do a fresh install of 14.10 instead of doing an upgrade on my Xubuntu desktop.  I figured I would give Ubuntu a try again to see if anything that drives me batty had been resolved.

The first thing that drove me crazy was the panel down the left side. It was taking up part of the screen, and seemed like a distraction, so I set it to hide itself when not in use.  And since Ubuntu's default them has the min/max/close buttons on the left every time I move my mouse over to the panel I accidentally closed the application I had open when i tried to click on the panel a little too quickly.

The second thing was the missing taskbar.  Because of the work I do I have multiple applications and files open on my desktop at any given time.  I am in the habit of clicking on the taskbar items to restore them.  I know I can use alt-tab, and I know there are things like Tint2 that will give me a taskbar but neither of them really works for me the way I want it to.  

I gave it a day to see if I could get used to it and simply found more and more things that frustrate me.  I am sure I could either get used to the Unity environment, or find ways to get the functionality that I want, but it seems like a lot of work for no benefit.  So I installed Xubuntu 14.10 instead and with the exception of a few applications suddenly looking like they were designed in the 90s, probably a theme issue, I am happy again.

### Post by craig10x on 2014-10-26
@brw2: the unity launcher is a dock...and essentially, a dock is a taskbar...it's just that it's on the left instead of the bottom here....the concept is to put your favorites and most used apps on it and use the dash search for less frequently used items...that way you can get 1 click access to most things you want...the unity launcher (dock) can be autohidden, you can adjust it's sensitivity, you can reduce the icon size (which i do...38 pixels looks perfect on my 17" laptop)... and once you get use to it (like say 2 weeks not 1 day) it can be very pleasant to use...I think the problem is when you go in with a negative attitude and judge quickly...

Oh, and you can even put the menus back in the windows now too (except for the unity panel on the top, which is a good thing because that way it saves space when a web browser is open)...

I don't really get what you said about accidentally minimizing the apps when going to the unity bar...the minimize buttons on top are clear of the unity launcher...

Anyway, good luck with xubuntu...to me, it's kind of old fashioned, but if that's what you love..well then enjoy ;)
