### Post by bro2 on 2014-10-27
Several things I dislike about Ubuntu:

1. Marketed as a simple OS to transition to for Mac/Windows users, but has a horrible UI. "Close" and "minimize" buttons are on the left instead of the right side of windows for some unfathomable reason. I actually don't hate GNOME (I love OpenSUSE's GNOME UI but that's partially because I love the black color scheme) but Ubuntu gets it wrong on every turn IMO. Just not visually appealing, and why are the close/minimize functions on the wrong side of the windows? Yes, I can easily get another DE, but in that case I prefer a distro that has a good default DE so that I actually use it sometimes.

2. Amazon search. I get that Cannonical needs money, but Ubuntu is the only distro (that I'm aware of) that has this kind of spyware built in. Yes, Windows 8/10 have this function too, but in my opinion Cannonical is copying the worst parts of Windows. Furthermore, It's just extra work to go into the Terminal to uninstall the Amazon app, which is unnecessary for other distros.


Can anyone tell me what the benefits of Ubuntu are over other distros/Ubuntu derivatives?

---

### Post by QIII on 2014-10-27
1.  Horrible is in the eye of the beholder.  I don't use it.  Using a different flavor of Ubuntu brings a different default DE.

2.  Again, again, again:  it is not spyware.  No personal information is attached.  Search terms only are used - the user is not compromised and Amazon gets absolutely no information about you.  Besides that, it can be turned off with the click of a mouse -- not generally considered a characteristic of spyware.  Furthermore, Canonical is removing it as opt-out.  Irritating?  Yes.  Spyware? No.
Continuing?  No.

Constructive criticism is a good thing.  Rehashes of old information -- and misinformation -- is not.

### Post by craig10x on 2014-10-27
@bro2: the amazon stuff (and actually all online searching from the "dash" can be turned off in about 5 seconds in system settings..security and privacy section...under "search" tab...it says  
"When Searching in dash include online results"...hit button to "OFF" done!

And as QIII said: not spyware....i turn mine off because i don't care for the clutter...

As far as minimize/maximize buttons the left...the Mac computers have it that way, too...Actually, Unity is rather "mac-like" and it is a dock (except on the left instead of bottom) and you can auto-hide it, reduce the icon sizes and even put the menus back in the windows (all from the settings under appearance)...and again as QIII said "beauty is in the eye of the beholder"  and i think it's lovely and enjoy using it, myself :D
However, you have alternatives...frankly, a lot of people just don't give it a long enough trial (like 2 weeks...not 2 hours..lol) and also don't like things that look different...Although with all the smart phone usage these days, something that is ICON based shouldn't seem so radical anymore...;)

### Post by craig10x on 2014-10-27
Actually, unity is pretty easy to use...most people are use to icons these days because of smart phones and macs, of course...The simplest way to use it, is you put all your favorites and most used apps on the unity "dock" for quick 1 click access, and for stuff you only occasionally use, you bring up in the "dash search" (like searching for an item on the window's menu)...you auto hide it, so it doesn't take up screen space except when needed for a few seconds, and i shrink mine (38 pixels looks real nice on my 17" laptop which is my desktop) and that's it!  (i do have it set for the menus to appear in the windows instead of on the top panel as it is defaulted to do) and find it is very simple and intuitive...

Nothing really radical about it...You can get most things you need from the software center, or deb files from familiar sites like Google Chrome, for example...Ubuntu main edition, which is based on gnome, is very modular in the way it is laid out...actually, it's easier to find things on it then on windows, in my humble opinion ;)

### Post by Dragonbite on 2014-10-28
> **bro2 said:**
> Several things I dislike about Ubuntu:

1. Marketed as a simple OS to transition to for Mac/Windows users, but has a horrible UI. "Close" and "minimize" buttons are on the left instead of the right side of windows for some unfathomable reason. I actually don't hate GNOME (I love OpenSUSE's GNOME UI but that's partially because I love the black color scheme) but Ubuntu gets it wrong on every turn IMO. Just not visually appealing, and why are the close/minimize functions on the wrong side of the windows? Yes, I can easily get another DE, but in that case I prefer a distro that has a good default DE so that I actually use it sometimes.

Boy, would the top (global) panel get cluttered if the close and minimize buttons were the right, along side the other icons traditionally on the right, even in Windows (battery, wi-fi, clock, apps, volume, etc.).  Accidentally go full-volume when trying to minimize your application? Yikes!! ;)

BTW, I go between Ubuntu and openSUSE Gnome on my systems and they run pretty darn close.  Giving Gnome an edge up is I found an extension that auto-changes my desktop background! I know it's dumb, but now Unity and LXDE are the only DEs that don't (as far as I can tell)!

So that is what I dislike about Ubuntu: no auto-changing desktop wallpaper feature built into Unity (I usually use Wallch).

> **bro2 said:**
> Also I'm arguing from the perspective of a switching Windows user. IMO other DEs are easier for Windows users to get into then Unity. I feel like I'm in an alien world when I installed Ubuntu, my first Linux distro :P like 5 years ago. And still scarred by it, as you can tell. Tho Ubuntu is good for the community, because of derivatives :)

My main question is why pick Ubuntu over other distros? The main benefit I've seen is the Software Centre having lots of apps.

I think a good thing about being visually different is that there is little chance of anybody slipping into "older thinking" when using Unity. When you use Unity you KNOW you are using Unity and anybody who knows Unity would KNOW you are using Unity at a glance!  Were you using KDE, however, then people might THINK you are using Windows 7 and even you may THINK you are using Windows 7 or EXPECT it to do something based on your Windows 7 experience that just happens to be different in KDE.  Now you're flustered or having to get out of your groove realizing you goofed and expected one thing to happen (Windows-like) and something else happened (KDE-like)!  Yikes!

Why pick Ubuntu over other distros?  That sounds like a topic for another thread!

As for the Amazon lens, I just want to remove it so that it isn't taking up space, processing or speed.  I do not have the most powerful computers and taking something like this out that I know I am not going to use, makes a difference.

### Post by Kale_Freemon on 2014-11-17
I think PulseAudio needs to go. It's more of a pain than anything else. Had to find a way to deactivate it on my Macbook because it kept misbehaving and there was no other way to fix it. I like to have crystal clear audio, not something that's going to get choppy every few seconds.

Thankfully, deactivating it myself forces everything else through ALSA (or so I understand). So much better.

Other than that, I can't say there's much that I don't like about Ubuntu. Even when using Unity, it's still a pretty fast system on my machines. Much faster than Windows.

### Post by SagaciousKJB on 2014-11-24
> **Kale_Freemon said:**
> I think PulseAudio needs to go. It's more of a pain than anything else. Had to find a way to deactivate it on my Macbook because it kept misbehaving and there was no other way to fix it. I like to have crystal clear audio, not something that's going to get choppy every few seconds.

Thankfully, deactivating it myself forces everything else through ALSA (or so I understand). So much better.

Other than that, I can't say there's much that I don't like about Ubuntu. Even when using Unity, it's still a pretty fast system on my machines. Much faster than Windows.

Agree, I think it's too resources intensive.  I had a dual core laptop with 3 gigs of a RAM, and sometimes using the processor a little bit still makes PulseAudio lag and skip.  I don't think a distribution that's supposed to be friendly to low resources computers should have a sound driver that cannot manage to keep up even on a dual core machine.

I think they should make more "legacy" versions of binary drivers available in the repositories.  I shouldn't have to compile the ATI driver myself just in order to switch to 14.04, especially since the currently packaged kernel won't compile that driver anyway.  Yeah I get that it's "AMD's fault" for dropping support for older cards in the current driver, but couldn't Ubuntu help by making their legacy driver available?

I don't know, Ubuntu is seemingly less and less friendly towards people with older hardware.

### Post by Ko_Char on 2014-12-31
I don't like File Renaming in Linux. 
It is not compatible with Windows, which is important if you have to work with other people. 
The most annoying thing is extra space in front of a file name.
```
$      name
$ name
```

I frequently added an extra space by accident. 
Windows can't rename, delete, move or copy that kind of file. 
When you created the files in Linux and gave these to other people working in Windows, nightmare begins. 

Also, colon. Annoying.

---

### Post by buzzingrobot on 2015-01-01
> **Ko_Char said:**
> I don't like File Renaming in Linux. 
It is not compatible with Windows, which is important if you have to work with other people. 
The most annoying thing is extra space in front of a file name.
```
$      name
$ name
```

I frequently added an extra space by accident. 
Windows can't rename, delete, move or copy that kind of file. 
When you created the files in Linux and gave these to other people working in Windows, nightmare begins. 


How are your creating and naming these files?  I can't find a way to create a filename *with* a leading space.  Any spaces I put there are stripped off. (Unless I delilberately wrap quotes around the filename to tell the shell to keep the spaces. A space is, after all,just another character.)

> Also, colon. Annoying.

In what circumstances?  Unix/Linux/OS X use a colon as delimit directories in a PATH.  Unix began that convention long before Windows was on the scene.

---

### Post by Ko_Char on 2015-01-01
I can recreate space easily by 
1. right-click -> rename > space bar + name
2. right-click -> rename > copy & paste the name with a leading space  (from a text file)
Ubuntu 14.04 - in both nautilus & pcmanfm

They're so deceiving that you won't even notice if there is only one problem file mixed in normal 200 files.

### Post by lew6 on 2015-02-22
**Marketing people, please take note** ):P:  What I don't like are the **transition problems** faced by "people everywhere" who want to "change the world" and "make the move" to Ubuntu.   I think the normal connotation of "people everywhere" includes non-programmers. There are so many of us.

Occasionally I get fed up with W7, and I'm tired of all the precautions needed to protect *myself* against hackers who have it in for *Microsoft*.  My son (a programmer who uses Ubuntu) challenged me to try Linux.  I *very much* like the *idea of  *using an alternative to Windows.  Real, free-market competition for ms -- maybe.  

So last month, after botching it myself, I paid a local tech set up a **dual boot W7/Ubuntu **14.04 LTS system.  He just set up the OSes.  Neither he nor I knew up front all the steps needed to set up my apps *so that I can **keep working*** on my normal non-tech activities in Ubuntu.  I have to say, **this is too painful **for most "people everywhere".  Worse than configuring communications settings for the internet in the early '90s, if you remember.  I'm sure that a dual boot system presents more puzzles than a single-boot.  But **do you expect **most people everywhere -- who have other things to do! -- to want to **drop **their Windows environment and risk taking a quantum *leap* to Ubuntu?  Without a transition period where they can *go back *to Windows to get done the *things in their daily work flow that Linux doesn't do*?    When I can see that I can get my normal work done in a relatively normal way, then I'll feel comfortable dropping Windows.  (I saw one comment [*not *from Canonical] about the inability to use more than one external  monitor on a laptop: "Change your workflow...."  Sounds like Microsoft.)

Example 1:  I started writing this in ubuntu (14.10 LTS), but I'm having to finish from Windows 7.  Why?  Because the Ubuntu links to my files on my Windows partition don't work any more.  Says that the file "doesn't exist".   Why?  No clue at all :confused:.  They worked yesterday.  I had to exit Ubuntu and reboot in Windows to get the password to this forum (and I had to log on to this forum 3 times before it worked :confused:). 

Example 2:  I rely on Windows jumplists to get to frequently used files quickly, without clicking multiple places:  just right click the Open Office icons on my task bar, click the file I want, and I can keep working.  Can't do that with Ubuntu -- have to open LO and hope that the file I want isn't too far down the list to show up on the recent files drop down.  Oh, wait, yes you *can *use jumplists in Linux:  download this and apt-get that, and if it doesn't work search far and wide on the web to see why and hope that you get something more recent than 4 years old that might still apply.  Geez!!

Example 3:  I want to set up Thunderbird so I can hit the same mail data  file from either W7 or Ubuntu.  That way I won't have to get out of Ubuntu (as I did for this piece) to  see my mail, which I use a lot.  I would be able to get more of my normal work done in Ubuntu, and take my time switching over completely.  (Even my programmer son keeps Windows handy "just in case".  Smart move.)   [This article]("http://kb.mozillazine.org/Sharing_a_profile_between_Windows_and_Linux") simply says "Settings specify file locations..." and that "One potential problem is that some extensions (such as Lightning [which I rely on heavily for reminders]) have separate downloads for Linux...."  The article has a link to "the most well known article" on the subject, which is so old it's only available on a "wayback machine" site -- and the document doesn't show a date, so I don't know if it has all the steps I need now or if there is a more recent and simpler approach (as referenced in the first article regarding FAT32 and NTFS disks).  I worked on this -- just TB! -- for many hours, on and off over a couple of days, and now...the mail *does* show up in my Ubuntu TB -- sometimes, but most of the time it doesn't until I do it again (I'm not going to do it *again*...).   I got Lightning installed, but apparently I have to link to storage.sdb.  I'll probably try that out of perverted curiosity (which often works) -- but I already have clear evidence (example 1, broken links) that it's not going to work reliably.  So I don't expect my calendar and reminders to work long term.  


** So, while I'd very much like to use Ubuntu, I don't expect to use it much now**. Just once in a while when all I need is the web or a recently used *.ods or *.odt file.  I don't want to have to figure out why my links don't work.  I think I remember a comment about mounting the Windows partition or directories.  So now how do I do *that*?  *I just want to use my computer* without relying on Windows! I hope that some day Ubuntu will let me get my work done without needing  advanced training.

One more piece of information that marketing people might find useful, about the nature of this user.  I'm not educated in programming, but I'm not totally averse to getting under the hood of my system either.  20 years ago I taught myself DCL (for those who remember VAX computers by DEC) to develop production processes for a publisher.  Then I became comfortable with ksh scripting on unix (after a one-week intro class; the biggest paradigm shift was learning to branch without goto [label:] syntax).  Programming Lite. (A "rm [filename]" statement stopped working in one of my scripts, and  the user asked, "Can you fix it?" -- a little above my pay grade....) Then I became a line manager who supervised people a lot more interested in learning other languages like java, perl, and others. Then I retired -- though I still have some of the programmers' callous in the middle of my forehead.  From beating my head against problems.  I don't want to do that any more, and I suspect that average people everywhere have a similar expectation.

So I'm only a semi-geek -- and that seems to be the minimum skill level required to use Ubuntu for anything other than a simple installation and user workflow. You're probably already doing it, but if not I suggest a **continuous, strategic effort on transition issues** -- how easy can you make it for people to adopt?  Or give it more priority so that common users have an easier time switching over.

---

### Post by Dragonbite on 2015-02-23
Installing for dual-boot is well documented and a lot easier than installing Windows.  Sharing files when Windows wants NTFS and enabling Ubuntu to read/write to them may require some research.  I imagine it would be similar to if you managed a Windows and OS X dual-boot situation.  Of if you dont' want to get technical, use Dropbox.

Thunderbird in one but not the other place? Nobody uses POP mail anymore so if both systems are pointed to an IMAP (Gmail, Outlook.com, etc.) then the mail shows up on both systems. Or use the web browser.  Plus that sounds like a Mozilla Thunderbird issue more than an Ubuntu issue.  

Use what works for you.

Oh, and if you need to open .odt files, you can open them in Microsoft Office or even Office Online if you don't want to install LibreOffice or OpenOffice in Windows (which does work).

---

### Post by apollothethird on 2015-02-23
> **lew6 said:**
> **Marketing people, please take note** ):P:  What I don't like are the **transition problems** faced by "people everywhere" who want to "change the world" and "make the move" to Ubuntu.   I think the normal connotation of "people everywhere" includes non-programmers. There are so many of us.

Occasionally I get fed up with W7, and I'm tired of all the precautions needed to protect *myself* against hackers who have it in for *Microsoft*.  My son (a programmer who uses Ubuntu) challenged me to try Linux.  I *very much* like the *idea of  *using an alternative to Windows.  Real, free-market competition for ms -- maybe.  

So last month, after botching it myself, I paid a local tech set up a **dual boot W7/Ubuntu **14.04 LTS system.  He just set up the OSes.  Neither he nor I knew up front all the steps needed to set up my apps *so that I can **keep working*** on my normal non-tech activities in Ubuntu.  I have to say, **this is too painful **for most "people everywhere".  Worse than configuring communications settings for the internet in the early '90s, if you remember.  I'm sure that a dual boot system presents more puzzles than a single-boot.  But **do you expect **most people everywhere -- who have other things to do! -- to want to **drop **their Windows environment and risk taking a quantum *leap* to Ubuntu?  Without a transition period where they can *go back *to Windows to get done the *things in their daily work flow that Linux doesn't do*?    When I can see that I can get my normal work done in a relatively normal way, then I'll feel comfortable dropping Windows.  (I saw one comment [*not *from Canonical] about the inability to use more than one external  monitor on a laptop: "Change your workflow...."  Sounds like Microsoft.)

Example 1:  I started writing this in ubuntu (14.10 LTS), but I'm having to finish from Windows 7.  Why?  Because the Ubuntu links to my files on my Windows partition don't work any more.  Says that the file "doesn't exist".   Why?  No clue at all :confused:.  They worked yesterday.  I had to exit Ubuntu and reboot in Windows to get the password to this forum (and I had to log on to this forum 3 times before it worked :confused:). 

Example 2:  I rely on Windows jumplists to get to frequently used files quickly, without clicking multiple places:  just right click the Open Office icons on my task bar, click the file I want, and I can keep working.  Can't do that with Ubuntu -- have to open LO and hope that the file I want isn't too far down the list to show up on the recent files drop down.  Oh, wait, yes you *can *use jumplists in Linux:  download this and apt-get that, and if it doesn't work search far and wide on the web to see why and hope that you get something more recent than 4 years old that might still apply.  Geez!!

Example 3:  I want to set up Thunderbird so I can hit the same mail data  file from either W7 or Ubuntu.  That way I won't have to get out of Ubuntu (as I did for this piece) to  see my mail, which I use a lot.  I would be able to get more of my normal work done in Ubuntu, and take my time switching over completely.  (Even my programmer son keeps Windows handy "just in case".  Smart move.)   [This article]("http://kb.mozillazine.org/Sharing_a_profile_between_Windows_and_Linux") simply says "Settings specify file locations..." and that "One potential problem is that some extensions (such as Lightning [which I rely on heavily for reminders]) have separate downloads for Linux...."  The article has a link to "the most well known article" on the subject, which is so old it's only available on a "wayback machine" site -- and the document doesn't show a date, so I don't know if it has all the steps I need now or if there is a more recent and simpler approach (as referenced in the first article regarding FAT32 and NTFS disks).  I worked on this -- just TB! -- for many hours, on and off over a couple of days, and now...the mail *does* show up in my Ubuntu TB -- sometimes, but most of the time it doesn't until I do it again (I'm not going to do it *again*...).   I got Lightning installed, but apparently I have to link to storage.sdb.  I'll probably try that out of perverted curiosity (which often works) -- but I already have clear evidence (example 1, broken links) that it's not going to work reliably.  So I don't expect my calendar and reminders to work long term.  


** So, while I'd very much like to use Ubuntu, I don't expect to use it much now**. Just once in a while when all I need is the web or a recently used *.ods or *.odt file.  I don't want to have to figure out why my links don't work.  I think I remember a comment about mounting the Windows partition or directories.  So now how do I do *that*?  *I just want to use my computer* without relying on Windows! I hope that some day Ubuntu will let me get my work done without needing  advanced training.

One more piece of information that marketing people might find useful, about the nature of this user.  I'm not educated in programming, but I'm not totally averse to getting under the hood of my system either.  20 years ago I taught myself DCL (for those who remember VAX computers by DEC) to develop production processes for a publisher.  Then I became comfortable with ksh scripting on unix (after a one-week intro class; the biggest paradigm shift was learning to branch without goto [label:] syntax).  Programming Lite. (A "rm [filename]" statement stopped working in one of my scripts, and  the user asked, "Can you fix it?" -- a little above my pay grade....) Then I became a line manager who supervised people a lot more interested in learning other languages like java, perl, and others. Then I retired -- though I still have some of the programmers' callous in the middle of my forehead.  From beating my head against problems.  I don't want to do that any more, and I suspect that average people everywhere have a similar expectation.

So I'm only a semi-geek -- and that seems to be the minimum skill level required to use Ubuntu for anything other than a simple installation and user workflow. You're probably already doing it, but if not I suggest a **continuous, strategic effort on transition issues** -- how easy can you make it for people to adopt?  Or give it more priority so that common users have an easier time switching over.

You sound like a great advocate for Microsoft.  I hope you know that as much as you advocate your crutch to them, if you try to communicate with them the way you are offering communication to the Ubuntu developers, most of what you try to get across would fall on deaf ears.

You think you can't function without Microsoft, but you actually could if you weaned yourself away from it.  Believe it or not but there are still people that have strong complaints about computers in general and won't purchase or use one.  They are persistent to use USMail, the telephone (not a smart one)  and travel to knock on a person's door in person to communicate.  I believe they don't know what they are missing, but they survive.  Just as they survive without using a computer, you could also survive without using Windows.  As far as the complaints about the difference and learning curb, I see the same complaints with every generation of Windows, just like the complaints about the difference between Windows 7 and Windows 8.  I personally believe it would be an easier transition to go to Ubuntu than to go to Windows 8 for the previous Microsoft addicts.

What people are doing when they move to Windows 8 is using tech support and other channels to figure out how to get around in it.  You could do the same thing with Ubuntu.  But you won't be able to appreciate it if you are not inclined to get familiar with how to do the job without Windows.  It might take an hour (or a couple of hours) the first time.  But after you learn how to do the chore, you'll find that it would be actually just as easy, and in most cases easier than Windows.

You mentioned having a problem with a link to a drive or a directory.  Making links in Ubuntu is much easier than making links with Windows.  Also, it would be next to impossible to make a link in Windows to your Ubuntu partition.

While there are many, many ways of doing a task in Ubuntu, one way to quickly make a link in GUI is to type the word "disks" in the Ubuntu Launch search field.  Bring it up and select the drive/partition you want to have access.  One of the partition options is to open the location where it mounted (or mount it if it's not mounted).  Then drag the folder you want linked to where you want it linked.  Drop it with the alt-key held.  From the drop down menu click on create link...  there you have it.

If you could describe doing this to someone who has never used Windows before in less than 10 times the text I used, I'd be amazed.  The reason you can do things in Windows relatively easy is because you have spent many years practicing.  If you spent just a little while becoming familiar with Ubuntu, you'll find it won't take so many years to become fluent and soon pass the functionality of what you are locked into with Windows.

If you have a question or problem, create a thread with what you're trying to do and place a link here.  I'll go to the thread and show you the easy way to do it with Linux.  Give this a try and drop the dual boot crutch.

-- L. James

-- 
L. D. James
[email]ljames@apollo3.com[/email]
[www.apollo3.com/~ljames](www.apollo3.com/~ljames)

### Post by Mike_Walsh on 2015-02-24
(Originally posted by lew6)

> [B]So, while I'd very much like to use Ubuntu, I don't expect to use it much now. Just once in a while when all I need is the web or a recently used *.ods or *.odt file. I don't want to have to figure out why my links don't work. I think I remember a comment about mounting the Windows partition or directories. So now how do I do [I]that? [I]I just want to use my computer without relying on Windows! I hope that some day Ubuntu will let me get my work done without needing advanced training.
[/I][/I][/B]

Food for thought, there.

I've used computers since the 'Stone Age' (!); the days of the Commodore 64 & Sinclair ZX-81, the Osborne and the Tandy TRS-80. I think I've used just about every O/S there is going during the course of the last 35 years or so. Since I've swapped around so much, I've never let myself GET to the point of relying on doing things ONE 'comfortable' way; I'm more than happy to learn new ways of doing things.

These days, I confess, I'm no longer using my computer for work-related issues.....more for 'recreational' purposes; browsing, e-mails to and from family and friends, Skype with relatives in the States and New Zealand, and devoting more time to my main passion, graphic design. I moved from Windows XP to Linux in May of last year, when the sinking ship of XP finally started taking on potentially fatal amounts of water..! And since I no longer have deadlines to adhere to, and have always enjoyed learning new ways of doing things (including doing things by command-line, and writing shell-scripts.....which, by the way, are easy to do, and give you a whole new arsenal of tools), I have found the move to Linux relatively painless; almost easy, in fact.

But what one individual may find easy, another may find incredibly difficult. We're all made differently. The best advice I can give, as have so many others in the Forums, is to** [COLOR=#ff0000]use what works for you[/COLOR]**. That's the only statement that makes **ANY** sense at all to someone in your situation. It is completely pointless tying yourself up into knots trying to do things which clearly aren't going to work for you the way you want them to.

The closest analogy I can give for this is to quote the example of my brother. He's been driving for over 30 years.....and he's a much better driver than most. But as soon as the least little thing goes wrong with any of his vehicles, it's STRAIGHT to the main dealer. 'Not my place to fix this', he'll tell them. 'That's YOUR job; that's what I pay you for.' He has NO intention of even attempting to fix little things which I would sort out in 5 minutes. He would far sooner **pay somebody else** to do something he doesn't understand, and can't see any point in learning how to do.

He doesn't WANT to know WHAT makes it work; just that it WILL work. As long as it starts first turn of the key.....he's happy. A bit simplistic perhaps, but it serves to illustrate the point.

If you rely on your machine for work-related matters, and familiarity is important for ease of use, then you would be better served retaining a way of doing things which IS so familiar that it's almost 'second nature'.


Regards,

Mike. :):)

---

### Post by poetical on 2015-02-25
I have an oldish cell phone, and it has been the mainstay of my photographic needs as an artist. There is an aberration in the lens set up so that it is quite unique, and for this reason cannot be changed with any other camera device. Yes I have better camera's, yes there are different camera/phones I can use, but none match the unique quality that this particular camera affords me. In 12.04 the bluetooth synchronization between the camera and the laptop I used was seamless and brilliant. I took a photo session, and activated bluetooth, made all the images viewable by my laptop and bingo downloaded them within seconds onto my laptop, so that I could then do lightwork use on them in Gimp... it was perfect, and seemless, not once did I lose quality or any image in the transfer... **Bluetooth just worked!**

Then comes the upgrade to 14.04... and for over a year or so now, There is now no way to transfer images and files over bluetooth to my laptop anymore... **WTF!**

Do I really need to devolve back to 12.04 in order to use Bluetooth???? ... really? I mean everything else works ok, but in over a year, the use of bluetooth technology cant be integrated to allow transfer of files between a device and the operating system? 

So for over a year, my favourtite camera has been made redundant because I apparently 'UPGRADED' to 14.04, that offers NO ability to transfer files between my camera and my laptop by way of bluetooth. I have no other means of transferring those files, I do not have a USB chord from the camera to the laptop, long lost... 

All I want is an operating system that allows me to transfer files using a basic technology such as bluetooth... is it so hard? I mean, if Ubuntu isn't going to support bluetooth anymore in it's OP upgrades (1) they should say so BEFORE we choose to upgrade. (2) they should surely just put in place the old architecture that allowed for seamless bluetooth use in the previous operating system and leave it alone. 

As an artist, a professional graphics artist, I cannot express my frustration strongly enough. It is all well and good having lots of new bells and whistles, but if one cannot even do as simple a thing as transfer a photograph from one device to another by way of bluetooth, what is the bloody point!

---

### Post by mattharris4 on 2015-02-28
^ Poetical, unfortunately I cannot help with bluetooth issues.  However, with a bit of research on Google you should be able to find what type plug you need on a USB cord by searching using USB cord for XXXXX (XXXXX = the brand and model of phone).  With that info go to Amazon and type in your brand/model phone again and search for USB cord.  Find the cheapest one, look at the picture to verify it has the plug you need then buy it.  It will likely cost less than $5 shipped.  This may be easier than attempting to make Ubuntu connect wirelessly using a likely out of date bluetooth version (I think we are on version 4.0 currently).  You may wish to buy a new battery (usually also about $5) for that phone while you are there, it sounds like you have had the phone for a long time and it probably needs one if it hasn't been replaced before and if you purchase it from the same supplier there is likely a discount on shipping over buying separately.

### Post by sterling-blair on 2015-03-01
I hate that, inorder to install drivers i have to learn to be a programmer. why cant we just click download the click install to make stuff work. if i wanted to learn how to use commands then id make own stupid OS instead of use this piece of junk. I might as well pay 200 bucks for windows

### Post by Bucky Ball on 2015-03-01
> **sterling-blair said:**
> I hate that, inorder to install drivers i have to learn to be a programmer. why cant we just click download the click install to make stuff work. if i wanted to learn how to use commands then id make own stupid OS instead of use this piece of junk. I might as well pay 200 bucks for windows

This thread is about things you dislike in Ubuntu, not a soapbox to bash Ubuntu or any other OS.

The driver issue you bring up has nothing to do with Ubuntu per se. For some devices, lucky to have drivers at all. Please email hardware manufacturers and ask them to provide Linux friendly drivers. It is the manufacturer's responsibility to provide drivers for their hardware, not Ubuntu's, and if manufacturers don't want to play nice with Linux then it's nothing to do with Ubuntu. The fact that there are drivers for any hardware unsupported by its manufacturer is down to the many volunteers who spend their time figuring out how to create functioning open-source drivers for problematic devices. 

It is our responsibility to make manufacturers aware that we want Linux-friendly drivers for their hardware. So let them know.

No-one's forcing you to use 'this (stupid) piece of junk: even if you did create your own Linux OS, you would have EXACTLY the same issue with hardware drivers if you're using the same hardware as you are now. If manufacturers don't make drivers for Linux, that's that, unless someone is willing to volunteer their time, effort and sometimes cold hard cash, to nut out an open-source one.

One good way to avoid your dilema is to research and purchase Linux-friendly hardware that *will* work 'out-of-the-box' in the first place. I build my own desktops and research meticulously when buying a laptop, or any hardware, for this very reason. Problem solved.

If you walk into a store, grab the first wifi dongle or graphics card you see, then can't get it working and complain that it doesn't work with Ubuntu, I can't see that as the fault of Ubuntu or the manufacturer. You don't have to be a programmer, but you do need to do some research and ask some questions prior to purchasing hardware.

Good luck.

### Post by deadflowr on 2015-03-01
> **Dragonbite said:**
> I liked in Ubuntu 12.04 how the login screen's background changed to the selected user's wallpaper.  It doesn't do that in 14.04 and 14.10.  I would love it if that were able to be reset.


It should still work, as it does for me.
You might look at the picture file permissions.
Since the background needs to be able to read the file.
(I think you need to at least set the Other to read-only; I also think if you set the default permissions for your home folder to something like 700 then it can't even access the pictures folder, but if you leave the default permissions -755 it should work)

### Post by phedup on 2015-03-03

Hi Dragonbite,
I'm using Ubuntu 14.4, and my own file permissions except for root are set to 'owner -rwx------'[TABLE]
[TR]
[TD][/TD]
[TD][FONT=inherit]You might look at [https://help.ubuntu.com/community/FilePermissions]("https://help.ubuntu.com/community/FilePermissionshttps://help.ubuntu.com/community/FilePermissionshttps://help.ubuntu.com/community/FilePermissions"), on this forum.
  
When my desktop opens with the icon panel on the left and Dash at the top, I open up 'Image Viewer,' which is available in the software center, navigate to and pick [FONT=monospace]'image,''open'and click [/FONT][FONT=inherit]the image that I want.[/FONT][FONT=inherit]  A drop-down offers for you to pick that as wallpaper.  
OR, 
[/FONT][FONT=inherit]I right click on the desktop and a menu comes up, with 'change desktop background' at the bottom.  A window appears (from the Settings menu) where you're offered a preview image.  If you have images stored in your 'Pictures' file, you can pick one and then pick various settings such as 'tile' or 'stretch' in order to make the image fit the desktop the way you want it to. 

I hope this helps,

### Post by CantankRus on 2015-03-04
**@ Dragonbite**,

Try this....
```
gksudo gedit /usr/share/glib-2.0/schemas/10_unity_greeter_background.gschema.override
```

Should look like this....
```
[com.canonical.unity-greeter]
draw-user-backgrounds=true
[COLOR="#FF0000"]#[/COLOR]background='/path/to/background'
```
The last line is commented out.
Save and close the file, then run... 
```
sudo glib-compile-schemas /usr/share/glib-2.0/schemas/
```


To return to just drawing a single background at the greeter,
edit **10_unity_greeter_background.gschema.override**, change to **false**, remove the [COLOR="#FF0000"]#[/COLOR]
and add a path to an image.
Run... ```
sudo glib-compile-schemas /usr/share/glib-2.0/schemas/
```

### Post by faure212 on 2015-03-04
[COLOR=#000000]I have been using Ubuntu since version 6 (Edgy something), and for a while enjoyed it. [/COLOR]
[COLOR=#000000]Once I discovered (the lightweight) Lubuntu, which ran VERY well on my old hardware, I was happy with it. I tried and hated Unity, and found gnome also to be somewhat quirky. [/COLOR]

Ubuntu on the Yoga machine with the HiDPI display is a disaster. I tried everything-- Lubuntu, KDE, gnome, Ubuntu/Unity-- nothing works correctly under all circumstances. In addition, the sound works only SOMEtimes, the function keys only work correctly in Ubuntu/unity and gnome, but not in Lubuntu or KDE. The auto-rotation does not work, and the touchpad is a disaster. All these things do work correctly in Windows 8.1 on the very same hardware. 

So I might have to throw in the towel, and stick with Windows, which I detest as much as any of you. 
BTW-- I followed to the letter all the arcane instructions on installing Ubuntu on this machine.[/I]

### Post by mattharris4 on 2015-03-04
> **KyleG545 said:**
> I have no complaints with ubuntu, I just wish the the wirless would work out of the box

Maybe you should post a bug report if you have not already with the problem described and the make/model of computer and (if known) the manufacturer and model of the wireless card in your computer.  I just installed Lubuntu and tried Ubuntu, Kubuntu and Xubuntu's test drive option on a Toshiba laptop I bought new seven months ago, the wireless worked just fine with all of them.  IIRC I first tried Ubuntu and Lubuntu on it within the first four months I had it and wireless worked even then.  Therefore unless the wireless card is either something exotic and seldom used or Nvidia (they are notoriously uncooperative with attempts to integrate their products into a Linux OS, even the creator of Linux has some unprintable words for them) I suspect the programmers at Canonical will have a driver in the next version of Ubuntu or maybe even update 14.04 with it since it is the most recent LTS version.  It is possible that some setting needs to be tweaked and it can be made to work now but I don't have any experience with that so I suggest filing a bug report and asking the question wherever it is appropriate, maybe do a search in the Ask Ubuntu section of the Ubuntu site with your computer's name and model and the term wireless as well.

### Post by Mike_Walsh on 2015-03-20
> **poetical said:**
> I have an oldish cell phone, and it has been the mainstay of my photographic needs as an artist. There is an aberration in the lens set up so that it is quite unique, and for this reason cannot be changed with any other camera device. Yes I have better camera's, yes there are different camera/phones I can use, but none match the unique quality that this particular camera affords me. In 12.04 the bluetooth synchronization between the camera and the laptop I used was seamless and brilliant. I took a photo session, and activated bluetooth, made all the images viewable by my laptop and bingo downloaded them within seconds onto my laptop, so that I could then do lightwork use on them in Gimp... it was perfect, and seemless, not once did I lose quality or any image in the transfer... **Bluetooth just worked!**

Then comes the upgrade to 14.04... and for over a year or so now, There is now no way to transfer images and files over bluetooth to my laptop anymore... **WTF!**

Do I really need to devolve back to 12.04 in order to use Bluetooth???? ... really? I mean everything else works ok, but in over a year, the use of bluetooth technology cant be integrated to allow transfer of files between a device and the operating system? 

So for over a year, my favourtite camera has been made redundant because I apparently 'UPGRADED' to 14.04, that offers NO ability to transfer files between my camera and my laptop by way of bluetooth. I have no other means of transferring those files, I do not have a USB chord from the camera to the laptop, long lost... 

All I want is an operating system that allows me to transfer files using a basic technology such as bluetooth... is it so hard? I mean, if Ubuntu isn't going to support bluetooth anymore in it's OP upgrades (1) they should say so BEFORE we choose to upgrade. (2) they should surely just put in place the old architecture that allowed for seamless bluetooth use in the previous operating system and leave it alone. 

As an artist, a professional graphics artist, I cannot express my frustration strongly enough. It is all well and good having lots of new bells and whistles, but if one cannot even do as simple a thing as transfer a photograph from one device to another by way of bluetooth, what is the bloody point!

I can appreciate your problem to a certain extent. I have a Nokia Asha, to which I can transfer items from my desktop OR laptop very easily, but.....it just doesn't work the other way round. So, as matt_harris has said, I simply bought a cheap USB cable with the appropriate end on it for my phone; problem solved. 

Far easier than messing about trying to force something to work.


Regards,

Mike. :)

### Post by Mike_Walsh on 2015-03-20
> **buzzingrobot said:**
> Ubuntu obviously supports bluetooth.

Aye; granted. So do the 'buntu flavours....and so does Mint. Ok, so using a USB lead isn't perhaps as technically 'satisfying' as using the Bluetooth, but hey; who cares? It does the job.....and bear in mind that by the nature of the beast, Bluetooth is essentially a close-range tool. at best.

Neither my old desktop, or even older laptop, came with Bluetooth. So I lashed out the grand total of £3 ($4/5?) about 18 months ago, on a pair of Bluetooth 'dongles', made here in the UK by Cambridge Silicon Radio, Ltd. Under ideal conditions, they have a range of 50m.....in theory, I could transfer files from 'puter to phone all the way from down the bottom of our rather long back garden. But those 'ideal' conditions DON'T include a very tall, thick privet hedge, and half-a-dozen brick walls between the phone and the router... Signal strength drops away like crazy! :)

For day-to-day use, I much prefer the native file-sharing tools to be found in Linux. I have Puppy Linux 'Tahrpup' on my laptop, AND on my desktop, too, alongside Ubuntu. Puppy has a built-in Samba client, and it's a five minute job, literally, to install and set up the Samba-TNG server fork that Puppy uses. The configuration file is as simple as they come....just the bare essentials necessary for it to work. Since it's there, I use it; to my mind, it works BETTER between Linux machines than it does for its intended use....with Windows.

Works for me, anyhow!


Regards,

Mike.

### Post by Yowan on 2015-03-23
I've been using Ubuntu occasionally since 9.04 and those things in Ubuntu/Linux distros in general annoy me to this date:

1. The fact that I have to edit some config file buried deep in the system just to adjust simple things like ALSA/pulseaudio channel mapping/audio remixing. Most of the advanced config stuff in Linux have no GUI counterparts for configuration. In Windows audio channel mapping is done via few clicks, system settings are accessible via simple GUI tools (msconfig, services, regedit, gpedit, device manager, etc). I don't understand why I absolutely have to use the terminal to do the stuff that could otherwise have been done in a GUI.

2. The insane amount of unnecessary confusing abstraction layers (5+ for audio, i.e ALSA drivers>ALSA library/dmix>Pulseaudio server>pulse backends whatnot>application). - Much simpler on Windows.
3. No reliable system-wide *GUI* audio mixer, terrible out of the box surround support, no user-friendly configuration. - 5.1 on my sound card still doesn't work for me due to ALSA/Pulse *limitations*.

3. The obsolete X server codebase that nobody apparently wants to get rid of because 'it just works' despite being full of legacy garbage from the last 30 years or so. What's even the point of X if almost nobody uses most of its core functionality, the stuff that was traditionally handled by X has been moved into the kernel or is now managed by the compositor and developers just seem add more extensions to get around X's limitations. Wayland/Mir initiatives still years away.
4. Still no system-wide mouse scroll wheel acceleration settings, once again an X server limitation or '*feature*' like most die-hard Linux users would call it.
5. Terrible open source graphics drivers that still implement 5 year old deprecated versions of OpenGL, this cripples modern gaming on Linux. Hardware acceleration in Firefox/Chrome is broken with the Nouveau/Mesa drivers/stack.

6. A simple display driver/X server/kernel/software update can render the system unbootable or even wreck Grub itself, on top of that there is zero GUI recovery tools (grub rescue is a joke). On Windows you've got system restore, safe mode and startup/system-repair to handle that for you. There is no easy user-friendly way to roll back system changes without using the terminal, I would very much like a system-restore alike tool in Linux distros. Things like that have occurred with several of my Linux setups and I would rather reinstall everything than waste hours trying to fix things that are inherently nonsensical.
7. The fact that you have to purge/reinstall/rebuild proprietary drivers after each and every kernel upgrade. If done incorrectly that leaves me with a non-functional X environment, I understand that Nvidia drivers use DKMS but why can't display drivers be handled separately like loadable kernel modules like in Android? I don't really like the idea that most drivers must be baked directly into the kernel itself.

---

Upgrading from 1GB to 2GB gives the most pronounced performance improvement, in my experience. ### Post by buzzingrobot on 2015-04-12
> **ubunt72 said:**
> Using ppas.   I find they never work properly and there's always errors.

That's why, among other reasons, PPA's are unofficial and unsupported. I.e., use at your own risk. Packages in PPA's are not vetted by Canonical, only by the developer who maintains the PPA. Users have no assurance PPA software works on any system other than the developer's.  And, in my experience, few users actually visit a PPA's page on LaunchPad to read what the developer(s) say about it and its reason for existence. Most users seem to simply copy-and-paste instructions from one of the many sites touting software from a PPA.  They should not be so trusting.

---

### Post by buzzingrobot on 2015-04-22
> **mrbigmouth502 said:**
> Dealing with the way it deals with time. Why can't it just use the time I have set on the hardware clock, instead of assuming I have it set to UTC time? I don't use UTC time because I don't live in Britain.



Unix and Linux use UTC and Windows uses hardware time. Measuring time, elapsed and otherwise, is an important function of an OS and both platforms do it differently, and have for decades. 

The time as actually displayed should be shown as local time.

---

### Post by Dragonbite on 2015-04-22
I think you can change it from using UTC to local time, and you want to use Local Time if you are dual-booting with Windows.

### Post by electriccandy on 2015-04-24
The only issue I've ever had with Ubuntu is that it seems to run slow/poorly even on decent entry level hardware. I mean, I have a small touchscreen laptop with a fairly modern dual core intel processor and 4 gigs of ram -- nothing to write home about but certainly not outdated or the slowest laptop on the market -- and under Ubuntu the fan was going constantly when I simply had a web browser open, the app store always froze up, etc. I thought maybe the computer was dying, but switched to Linux Mint MATE and all problems solved themselves -- runs cool and quiet now (phew). On my somewhat more powerful Dell, it runs better, though still freezes up doing some pretty basic things, like using LibreOffice. I love Ubuntu, but man, it shouldn't need a $600+ laptop just to run okay.

---

### Post by Bucky Ball on 2015-04-25
> **electriccandy said:**
> I love Ubuntu, but man, it shouldn't need a $600+ laptop just to run okay.

It doesn't. I've installed on lots of machines from high to low end. 90% of the time the install is without issue. Bottom line: it's all about the hardware. Price means diddily squat. If the hardware is not compatible with Linux, it's not. I run a fairly basic set up but I sometimes wonder if I've been blessed by an angel when I read some of the fixes people find themselves in on the forums.

I will say this: prior to purchasing any new hardware I do a LOT of research to make sure it will work 'out of the box' or with the minimum of fuss. For software issues, I also research a LOT and generally things get fixed fairly quick smart.

Have you tried posting here about your Libreoffice crashes and what not?

### Post by buzzingrobot on 2015-04-25
Issues like "... the fan was going constantly..." might point to unusual hardware or hardware about which kernel devs don't have enough information to write adequate thermal and fan control code. Maybe the wrong or misconfigured driver.  I had a 2011 Macbook Pro with AMD video that ran cool and quiet using Apple's hardware but maxed the fans on Linux immediately. That's done as a precaution if there's no way to properly control them.

MATE is less demanding of graphic and 3D rendering capabilities than Unity so the video card won't generate as much heat, and, as far as I know, it won't fallback to pushing graphics off to the CPU for rendering.

I mean, I have a small touchscreen laptop with a fairly modern dual core intel processor and 4 gigs of ram -- nothing to write home about but certainly not outdated or the slowest laptop on the market -- and under Ubuntu the fan was going constantly when I simply had a web browser open, the app store always froze up, etc. I thought maybe the computer was dying, but switched to Linux Mint MATE and all problems solved themselves -- runs cool and quiet now (phew). On my somewhat more powerful Dell, it runs better, though still freezes up doing some pretty basic things, like using LibreOffice. I love Ubuntu, but man, it shouldn't need a $600+ laptop just to run okay.

I find Ubuntu works well on most computers, but not necessarily the best on older computers. Whether it is the requirements of Unity or stuff Ubuntu has running under the hood, either way it is not a light distribution.  I like the full-featured Desktop environments (Gnome, Unity & KDE) which means if there are any issues with the system (age, drivers, etc.) I feel it, probably long before people running quad-core monsters!

I often find Fedora running nicely on hardware, when Ubuntu has problems with it.  I don't know what they do different (drivers, settings, utilities, etc.) but this is just and observation of mine.

Right now I am running a dual-core laptop with 4 GB of RAM and it handles openSUSE w/KDE fine.  It handled the others alright as well, but it didn't feel as snappy.

### Post by buzzingrobot on 2015-04-27
> **Dragonbite said:**
> I find Ubuntu works well on most computers, but not necessarily the best on older computers. 
I often find Fedora running nicely on hardware, when Ubuntu has problems with it..

Fedora moves faster than Ubuntu, and faster than most distributions. Still, the same drivers, etc., are used by both and differ only in release version. If this month's driver fixes a bug in last month's driver that degraded performance on your hardware, you'll be happier with the distribution that uses the newer driver.

Older environments that don't require 3D display capability, or put fewer demands on it, will work better on any less-than-capable (meaning: older) hardware than environments that do require or leverage 3D capabiity.  That's boils down to video capability.  If hardware has an old/weak or poorly supported video card, then best performance is likely to come from something like Lubuntu with LXDE or, perhaps, XFCE with composition disabled.

KDE, Gnome, etc., will revert to a fallback video mode if they detect inadequate video support. This can involve pushing graphics processing off to the CPU, which slows responsiveness across the board. Performance will be distinctly worse, and the display may be rendered poorly. 

Demand on RAM really won't affect performance until the demand exceeds available RAM and triggers swapping. That is, if you have X gigs of RAM and never exceed X-1 gigs of RAM usage, you always have available RAM to allocate. (And many folks always forget that the kernel deliberately keeps bits in RAM -- caches memory -- because accessing memory is orders of magnitude faster than pulling the bits off a drive again.  But, the memory in that cache is available for allocation to any process that requests it. That's why a simple look at total RAM use can be misleading, and why it's good for all the RAM to be used.

---

### Post by Dragonbite on 2015-04-27
Driver updates and improvements are going to be focused on hardware people use currently. Hardware that is currently in use by a large number of people will get fixes faster than people on either ends (older/newer). Even then, people on the end with newer hardware will probably get updates faster than the lower-end because these are the systems that will be growing in use. 

For example, I have 2 laptops that cannot run Ubuntu out-of-the-box because the chipset cannot handle PAE.  Fedora and openSUSE doesn't bat an eyelash.  Eventually this will be a complete non-issue as these non-PAE systems continue to die off.  Similar to how 32bit systems are giving way for 64bit systems. It's harder to find 32bit systems and I wouldn't be surprised if distributions start dropping 32bit support (if they haven't already).

On one of my really old systems (Gateway netbook), I put Lubuntu on it and once it was installed and I rebooted, I could not run Wi-Fi. I ended up having to use the oldest kernel installed (3.16.?) to get Wi-Fi to work.  Haven't a clue what has happened.

Installed Fedora (w/LXDE) and I don't know what they did, but haven't had a problem with Wi-Fi on their kernel (3.19.??).

### Post by Bucky Ball on 2015-04-30
See [here]("https://help.ubuntu.com/community/InstallingSoftware#Installing_packages_without_an_Internet_connection"). Seems simple enough. 

And [here's]("https://help.ubuntu.com/community/Synaptic/PackageDownloadScript") more detail about the package download. 

Basically, mark the apps you want on the offline machine using Synaptics, instead of 'Apply' go File> Generate package download script> Take that script to the online machine> open Synaptics> magic fairy dust, etc., and the upshot is you end up with the .debs on the USB to install on the slow/offline machine ... it's all on the link. I would think installing gdebi on the slow machine might be enough to install .debs, though. Don't quote me.

### Post by buzzingrobot on 2015-04-30
> **Mike_Walsh said:**
> I don't wish to sound like I'm preaching to the converted here; but had you lowered your 'swappiness' settings while you were still using the HDD? It's normally set to about 60 OOTB; it works far better, and with much less swap usage & 'thrashing', once it's lowered down to about 10 or so...


Regards,

Mike. :)

You know, I've never touched 'swappiness'. The only time I've ever seen any actual 'thrashing' is when I actually exceeded available RAM. 'Thrashing' happens when users exceed available RAM, don't reduce that demand, and so trigger constant read/writes to and from the swap partition.

'Swappiness' is often recommended to users with insufficient RAM. Maybe it does something wonderful, but if you exceed available memory, then you exceed available memory. It's not a fuzzy kind of thing.

Browsers: Firefox and Chrome can easily gobble 400+ megs with only a few tabs. Best if RAM is inadequate to try a different browser.

### Post by buzzingrobot on 2015-05-08
> **Jesse_McIlvenna said:**
> I don't like Unity and it's inability to easily create desktop icons. Can someone suggest to me a better alternative?

I don't put icons on my desktop, but this might work:

Before an icon for an app can be displayed, on the desktop or elsewhere, it must have a corresponding .desktop file. The default system-wide .desktop files live in /usr/share/applications. (That's all that's in there.)

Icons cannot be dragged-and-dropped from there because ordinary users lack the needed permissions.  However, try using Nautilus to copy the one(s) you want and then paste onto the desktop.

Dunno if it works, but seems it easy enough to try.

If you want a desktop icon for something that's not in /usr/share/applications, you'll need to research how to write your own .desktop file. It's not difficult.

### Post by sudodus on 2015-05-15
I can make USB drives for openSUSE with [mkusb]("https://help.ubuntu.com/community/mkusb/v9#from_phillw.net"). Such cloned DVD systems have UDF file systems and are read-write, so they are automatically persistent live :-)

### Post by yonnie on 2015-05-16
this supposed to be a mutual admiration society?

I think:

The Dash button is seriously pathetic, was it meant to be a joke?

The panel(s) or lack thereof is under-achieving or under-used and consume desktop space

### Post by buzzingrobot on 2015-05-16
> **yonnie said:**
> this supposed to be a mutual admiration society?
The Dash button is seriously pathetic, was it meant to be a joke?
Why does it seem like 2 panels are taking up space, neither of which get fully utilized?
The panel(s) or lack thereof is under-achieving or under-used and consume desktop space



The Launcher in Unity is a dock, not a panel.  The Dash does what it is intended to do.

Unity is not based on the panel-and-text-menus approach popularized by Windows, that requires users to minimize and maximize windows constantly, accompanied by the visualization of a window "minimizing" or "maximizing" into a panel.

The top panel is not intended to house those little rectangles.  With the dock, there is no need.

Both approaches are equally valid, but trying  to use one design the way you would use the other is a mistake, and criticism of a design using one approach because it does not use the other is misguided.

For users who wish to remain with the traditional menus-and-text-panel approach, or cannot cope with a dock, Ubuntu offers KDE, LXDE, Mate, and XFCE spins, as well as easy installation on a Unity base.

### Post by eric-dunbar on 2015-05-17
Such a great thread. 270 000 views and counting.

What do I dislike about Ubuntu? It's a one word answer: Unity.

It's such a crying shame. Unity is a stable piece of software. You can tell Canonical has devoted engineering resources to it. Unfortunately, they've devoted no DESIGN resources to it. It's absopositivolutely terrible.

The Dash/Dock/whatever it's called? Huh? It's a problem looking for a solution. The fixed menu bar? A missed opportunity. Menus in windows? Why?

Why is there BOTH a Dock AND a fixed menu bar that ISN'T a fixed menu bar? Either have a fixed menu bar, cum launcher OR have a launcher but this half-baked solution doesn't work. And, a fixed menu bar without fixed menus? It's the department of redundancy department. Take a look at Mac OS X. They've done it right. And, the interface is extraordinarily stable.

Windows, well, let's not talk about Windows 8. It's as half-baked as Unity. But, at least if you go to the Windows 8.1 desktop you've also got a reasonable paradigm--not as good as OS X but still functional. Menus in windows (not as good as a fixed menu bar). So so. Decent task bar "fixed launcher" at the bottom (or wherever you put it).

It's a shame Ubuntu didn't have a stronger interface when Windows 8 was going through it's pre-desktop trials and tribulations. It might have been a good opportunity for Ubuntu to pick up disaffected Windows 8 users.

I'm a long-time Ubuntu fan (dating back to the very first release back in 2004) and took a 3-4 year break from Linux because life got in the way of tinkering. Only recently was I motivated to come back because I needed to be able to run some niche (open source) software on a few computers at work. Ubuntu was, of course, my first place to stop. Was always a great Linux. Still is a great Linux.

But, wait, the interface! I started this trial (and, a trial by fire it was) in September. Given that Canonical has a history of doing things right I figured I'd give Unity a go. However, recently I found that I'd reached the end of my rope with Unity. I just didn't see any point to continuing. I'd installed some of the other desktop environments but found them to be less than stable compared to Unity. Then, on a lark I tried Linux Mint because I liked the pictures of the desktop I'd seen and ended up liking it (I'd tried running Cinnamon under Ubuntu 14.10 but found that it was a lot less than stable so I gave up on it). So much so that I've started migrating all my ***personal*** installs of Ubuntu over to Mint. Mint certainly is nowhere near as stable as Ubuntu 14.10, but, it's more stable than Cinnamon under Ubuntu so it's worth sacrificing some stability for a far superior interface :(

I so desperately would prefer to be on Ubuntu. I know Ubuntu. I trust Canonical. But, tragically Canonical made a bad decision when they went in 100% on Unity.

When I first started back into the Linux scene in September I wondered what the fuss was all about with Linux Mint and how a derivative of Ubuntu managed to surpass Ubuntu in terms of popularity. I completely ignored it. Now I understand. It's not that Linux Mint is inherently better. It's because Ubuntu Unity is that much worse :(

Please Canonical, I beg of you, take the time to make Unity work. It's solid. I can tell that. It's got potential. I can see that too. But, the execution is fatally flawed. It's trying to be everything that one person thought was good from Apple and Windows without understanding the design philosophy that drives the two dominant desktop interfaces.

Apple's was built on good research and attention to detail IN THE 1980's! Microsoft's was built on avoiding patent litigation in the 1990's. Apple ultimately had more freedom to develop their interface, especially with the move to Mac OS X. But, Microsoft has done a reasonable job with Windows 7, and, even introduced a few intriguing (albeit poorly executed) ideas in Windows 8. If you're going to steal ideas, steal them from Mac OS X. This strange hybrid isn't working, and, the fact that Linux Mint has now usurped Ubuntu is a testament to that failure :( :( :(

---

### Post by eric-dunbar on 2015-05-17
PS The alt-tabbing between apps in Unity is enough to drive a person batty. I've been using Unity for about 6 months now, long enough to get used to a new way of doing things. I'm still not used to alt-tab in Unity. First it takes you to another app. Then it takes you to another random app. And, if you hold down the alt key long enough it then pops up new images of separate windows open in an app, but, IIRC, you don't even use alt-tab to cycle through them (all I can say is that I never saw a positive to the way Unity implemented alt-tab... and, I can usually find a compelling reason why you do it one way or another, even if I don't personally like it).

Cinnamon's solution isn't great but it's at least predictable.

### Post by Copper Bezel on 2015-06-04
While that's true, the portable hardware that Unity originally targeted was *not *touch friendly, and it's only since then that the driver base for touchscreens has become at all reliable. There are things that are designed with touch in mind in Unity and some things that aren't - I'm pretty aware of this given my primary machine *has *a touchscreen now. Minimizing a window is damn near impossible. = / 

The difference with Windows 8 is that Unity *has* a touch blend in the works, and it doesn't work like desktop Unity at all. Same as Windows 10, of course, which also adapts to its environment. 

But no, Unity developed from something intended for netbook hardware, small screens, but no touch. I never used UNR, even though I had a netbook at the time, but Unity's come a long way since then. What I did use at the time (and before Unity proper's debut) was Synapse for my launcher and AWN+DockbarX for my dock, which were at the time the most reliable and mature versions of the same ideas. 

So the question becomes "why the switch," and I think that honestly, some of us just got tired of fussy tiny little unreadable application icons. = / Browser autocomplete made search and launch come naturally, and the big dock icons and home screen feel a lot like a speed dial page. When you look at Firefox or Chrome, and I mean the desktop versions, all the basic interface conventions of Unity are kinda in there. And Unity came from netbooks, where Chrome or Firefox + webapps were the primary enduser applications. It's not based on phones or tablets (remember that the netbook craze came before the tablet craze.) It's based on web browsers.

---

### Post by buzzingrobot on 2015-06-04
> **Copper Bezel said:**
> ...Minimizing a window is damn near impossible. 

On a desktop, at least, "minimized" windows go into, for lack of a better expression, that application's icon in the Launcher (aka dock).  Visually, it differs from minimizing a window to a panel with a small rectangle now visible in the panel to represent the window. In both cases, the visual on-screen action is simply an effect, an illusion, like everything else on screen.
[/QUOTE]

---

### Post by monkeybrain20122 on 2015-06-04
on the desktops Unity is very keyboard centric. I have no idea how some people would call that a "touchscreen" . People who say that obviously have used  neither a touchscreen nor unity.

---

### Post by Copper Bezel on 2015-06-04
Yes, exactly. 

> **buzzingrobot said:**
> On a desktop, at least, "minimized" windows go into, for lack of a better expression, that application's icon in the Launcher (aka dock).  Visually, it differs from minimizing a window to a panel with a small rectangle now visible in the panel to represent the window. In both cases, the visual on-screen action is simply an effect, an illusion, like everything else on screen.

I'm not sure how to more clearly explain what I said than to quote it again in the context you removed it from.

> [COLOR=#000000]There are things that are designed with touch in mind in Unity and some things that aren't - I'm pretty aware of this given my primary machine [/COLOR]*has* a touchscreen now. Minimizing a window is damn near impossible. = / 

Minimizing a window *in desktop Unity with a touchscreen *is impossible because the click target is too small to tap (and jammed in between two other click targets.) This was an example of a thing in Unity that's designed for desktop / laptop interaction and not for touch. What happens on the backend (either that the window is hidden but still rendered or stops rendering, depending on the desktop in question, with Compiz doing the former) is irrelevant to anything I said or was responding to. Another bit would be, as monkeybrain says, its keyboard-centric search and launch.

And to state the obvious, Unity still uses the global menu and disappearing title bar, which were inherited from screen space problems (and poor trackpads, so buttons extending to screen edges is more important) in the netbook form factor. If anyone wants to derisively shrug off Unity as a [thing] interface, it's something that grew up from a netbook interface, not a tablet one, and the tablet interface *of Ubuntu with Unity itself* is completely different.

---

### Post by buzzingrobot on 2015-06-04
> **Copper Bezel said:**
> Yes, exactly. 



I'm not sure how to more clearly explain what I said than to quote it again in the context you removed it from.





Well, you were addressing touchscreens and I was addressing desktops, which is why I said "desktop" and addressed the myth that minimize is missing from Unity. Some folks seem unable to get their head around using an interface without an actual visual minimize-to-panel effect. 

Touchscreen typically means tablet or phone, and a desktop is something that fits on your desk, not in our hand.  On my desktop display, the window controls are large enough that a finger could select them. On a smaller display, likely not. 

Personally, I think a touchscreen for a legitimate desktop is a nonstarter all around, on any OS. Who wants to poke at icons at arms length?

### Post by Copper Bezel on 2015-06-04
Oh, I know. I associate touchscreens with phones and tablets first off. The fact that I have an 11.6" ubook that came with touch is the only reason I've had the opportunity to bother with using touch in Unity. I do use it, but not extensively. Touch all-in-ones were a failed experiment by Microsoft to bill Windows 8's tablet convergence features as a benefit on the desktop, and they were right to back away from the whole thing in Win10.

And I never liked minimizing by clicking the taskbar item twice (I invoked it more by accident than by intent, because the window would already be on top, etc.), and tweak I my Compiz animations anyway (so the window *does *zoom to the icon) and appreciate that Unity has the icon pop out to show you where the window went anyway, so, yeah, no, I'm not complaining about Unity's use of minimizing, which I think is the best one. = ] (I do set hidden windows to be sticky in Compiz so that minimized windows always restore to the current workspace, because a window that has no physical position anyway shouldn't be dragging me around the wall, but ... yeah, that's because I'm a nitpicky person.)

Edit: And when I say "desktop Unity," of course, I mean not-Ubuntu-touch, and my primary interaction with it is on whatever notebook I'm using at the moment as my primary machine. So, yeah, that ended up unnecessarily ambiguous.

### Post by MartyBuntu on 2015-06-04
> **monkeybrain20122 said:**
> on the desktops Unity is very keyboard centric. I have no idea how some people would call that a "touchscreen" . People who say that obviously have used  neither a touchscreen nor unity.

Because the original approach of Unity was to full-screen selected apps with little or no indication of other running apps (using a "windows list" like panel based desktops use).
This closely resembled mono-tasking (or primarily mono-tasking) platforms like smart phones and tablets.

---

### Post by monkeybrain20122 on 2015-06-04

This closely resembled mono-tasking (or primarily mono-tasking) platforms like smart phones and tablets.

I have been multitasking with Unity since 11.04. You need to enable some features in ccsm for the flow to be smooth though. Just like the much over rated classic menu, the window list never worked really well. I remember in gnome2 it only shows applications in one workspace and the icons are way too small. I feel very constricted multitasking using xfce without compiz on manjaro Linux, it is a similar experience with gnome2 without Compiz.

### Post by Copper Bezel on 2015-06-04
Unity's approach to the dock certainly has advantages over the Windows or Mac implementations, with the collapsing-autoscrolling gimmick. I keep a lot more things pinned than I would otherwise. It of course doesn't do the previews that Windows provides, and that could be considered a drawback. Compared to the old-school window lists that XFCE and others use, it's slightly harder to immediately identify which apps are running and which windows you're looking for, and of course it's sorted by applications - the priority seems to be on keeping pinned apps available over immediately distinguishing where a given open window is. Taskbars on all these systems are just slightly more about launching apps and slightly less about opening running ones than they used to be. (I mean, Mac always was. Slightly more than Windows 95's layout, which the others are based on.)

While Unity did originate with netbooks and with the idea of favoring maximized apps, I don't see any real consequence to that for apps that don't happen to be maximized - it still has the Aero snap, after all, like all non-Mac window managers do. That's not really to do with task lists and things.

---

### Post by buzzingrobot on 2015-06-05
> **MartyBuntu said:**
> Because the original approach of Unity was to full-screen selected apps with little or no indication of other running apps (using a "windows list" like panel based desktops use).
This closely resembled mono-tasking (or primarily mono-tasking) platforms like smart phones and tablets.

There's probably some threshold of minimum size below which it isn't practical to display two windows on the same screen.  That size would vary depending on the user, resolution, etc. Devices as small as phones are really limited as to what kind of GUI they can deploy.

Interesting, too, how "multi-tasking" is often used these days to mean having more than one app open, when, from a technical POV, it means something else in the OS.  I saw a Window 10 machine the other day and noticed it tags its implementation of virtual workspaces as "multi-tasking".

---

### Post by Copper Bezel on 2015-06-05
People noticed the concept when iOS (temporarily) did away with it and offered a consumer computer that would only actively run one enduser application at a time. I think the term got ported from computing into everyday parlance (like, I'm "multitasking" if I'm watching TV and doing a crossword while eating lunch or whatnot) and then got ported back into computing from an enduser, consumer standpoint. Obviously, even iOS was running various services and things while it happened to be using an interface that suspended the applications that weren't the one displayed on the screen.

### Post by vasa1 on 2015-08-03
> **Bucky Ball said:**
> I correct myself, then. Mate is based on a fork of Gnome2. :)

Here's a bit on the future of MATE: > As a self-described "traditional desktop environment" (the tagline that has replaced the project's original, tongue-in-cheek, motto, "a non-intuitive and unattractive desktop") MATE gets the job done without getting in the way, just like GNOME 2 used to do.

But the commitment to simplicity that ensures MATE's popularity also makes future development plans difficult. How do you improve a product whose major selling point is that, unlike alternative choices, it is static and unchanging?

In part, the answer lies in keeping MATE compatible with the latest and greatest software produced elsewhere in the open-source channel. That means, in particular, upgrading MATE's backend to work with gtk+ 3, in order to ensure that the most popular Linux applications will continue to work on MATE. Such plans are already on the table.Source (from 2012): [http://thevarguy.com/open-source-application-software-companies/mate-vs-unity-gnome-3-open-source-desktops-future](http://thevarguy.com/open-source-application-software-companies/mate-vs-unity-gnome-3-open-source-desktops-future)

to do that. I assume by this conversation, that it can't be done. Correct?

### Post by blecha on 2016-03-15
I eventually switched to 15.10 (from 12.04) mainly to have a system with good support for Android development. I installed it in triple-boot with of-the-shelf Windows 7 and 12.04 and I can not refrain (even if this thread is reserved to “hate”) to mention my pleasure to carry on a fast-and-smooth install of wonderful simplicity.
 Hate would be too strong a word to qualify my feeling about some features. I just don't understand why a 1-click manipulation could “evolve” in the multiple-click with no gain for the user. Examples: opening a  second terminal window now requires click to a sub-menu (once you put the icon on the desktop), switching the workspace goes through the full-screen-and-click.
Also the windows behaviour configuration is not included in the standard release and I have the general feeling that look-and-feel is being tweaked at each new release at no purpose. 
My wife, an every-day elementary user, asks me “why they have changed this or that ?” and I have no answer. Do you ?

---
