### Post by sanssadness on 2016-03-24
How about the fact that they took out the alternate installer CD they used to have in 12.04.5 and completely CRIPPLED the main CD's manual partitioning? You can't even set up an encrypted LUKS/LVM partition anymore without commands. If they didn't want an alternate installer the least they could do was to put its features in the main installer. But of course they don't do that. And Ubuntu knows just how to kick me when I'm down: I can't enter the terminal on 16.04 with Ctrl + Alt + F2; it just leads to a black screen with a blinking white cursor. Yes I know it's beta. But I have to use it because it's the ONLY version that my speakers will play sound on. Would you use a computer without sound?

But what's the use? No amount of complaining will lead to them fixing problems that have existed for years. I'm told I'm a participant in an open source project. Basically if I don't like it, I can get out. And that might just be what I have to do. Sad that it has to come to this. I don't exactly love Windows, but at least it works.

---

### Post by Dragonbite on 2016-03-24
That's why I end up going between the "big 3"... when my wireless did not work out of the box in one distribution, I tried another.  With so many distributions based off of Ubuntu, I don't expect a lot of difference between them and Ubuntu itself so I don't bother with them.

I stick to Ubuntu, Fedora and openSUSE.  Between Ubuntu and Fedora I generally can get anything that can work with Linux, to work with Linux.  

I would be curious if Fedora fares any better.  I generally find Fedora is pretty good about working with things using included open source code.  They were the first ones to work with my Broadcomm wireless as well as to supply drivers for Nvidia that worked (may not be great, but worked).

---

### Post by montag dp on 2016-03-24
> **sanssadness said:**
> How about the fact that they took out the alternate installer CD they used to have in 12.04.5 and completely CRIPPLED the main CD's manual partitioning? You can't even set up an encrypted LUKS/LVM partition anymore without commands. If they didn't want an alternate installer the least they could do was to put its features in the main installer. But of course they don't do that. And Ubuntu knows just how to kick me when I'm down: I can't enter the terminal on 16.04 with Ctrl + Alt + F2; it just leads to a black screen with a blinking white cursor. Yes I know it's beta. But I have to use it because it's the ONLY version that my speakers will play sound on. Would you use a computer without sound?

But what's the use? No amount of complaining will lead to them fixing problems that have existed for years. I'm told I'm a participant in an open source project. Basically if I don't like it, I can get out. And that might just be what I have to do. Sad that it has to come to this. I don't exactly love Windows, but at least it works.You might want to try another distro, then.  Arch always has the latest packages, so if 16.04 works with your hardware, so should Arch.  But Arch will take a lot of attention to set up and maintain, so actually I'd suggest a derivative like Manjaro.  There's also Fedora, like Dragonbite suggested, which I think usually is pretty cutting edge though I haven't used it myself.

Anyway, the point is that the newest hardware is not always going to be supported right away.  That's just a fact that will not change any time soon.  Your options are to go back to Windows, try a new distro with the latest kernel and firmware, or wait for the fix in Ubuntu.

### Post by Geoffrey_Arndt on 2016-03-24
Sansa . . . can you not use the "something else" option in the installer of 16.04 anymore to initiate or modify partitioning?   Or use a Live gParted or PartedMagic DVD or USB-FlashStick anymore to do same?

Maybe you should check out the PartedMagic website - it's a pretty awesome rescue system (but don't worry if Skylake sound not supported yet).

BTW, if you want the fastest, most reliable way to create a bootable usb-flash-stick  . . . use mkusb or dd command  - - but watch this video first

 [https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=74A6pVrv0CA](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=74A6pVrv0CA)

### Post by Bucky Ball on 2016-03-24
mksub is preferable. dd takes no prisoners (otherwise known as d(isk)d(estroyer). Be VERY careful with it. It will not ask you to confirm you have selected the correct source and target three times. It won't do that even once. You write the command and hit return and that's it. Whatever the target is will be completely obliterated, no questions asked.

---

### Post by Geoffrey_Arndt on 2016-03-25
Thanks Bucky . . . I agree that mkusb is the better option.   

The video does a great job (other than not defining what IF (input file) and OF (output file) stand for.   On dd, I prefer to do it via a gifted/spare laptop that isn't used for anything important (and single OS (xubuntu) only)).

### Post by ddascalescu+launchpad on 2016-10-01
> **NightwishFan said:**
> Holy crap like nothing. I have it configured just how I like it. :popcorn:

Oh really? Try configuring the following:
[LIST]
[*][move the window minimize/maximize/close buttons to the right]("https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/unity/+bug/1622043") (for whatever reason - you work half a day with a DE that has them like that; or you're left-handed; or Chrome has them like that, or you live in a right-to-left region of the world etc.)
[*][move the panel from the top to the bottom]("https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/unity/+bug/1627394") (because you use a monitor in portrait mode, because 90% of the panel length is wasted empty since you use menus in the app window, not blindly copying Mac OS X to the top etc.)
[/LIST]

(I know I'm replying to an old post, but it's incredible how these customizations have become or remained impossible over the years. Unity should be called Stalinity or something).

Other things I hate about Ubuntu:
[LIST]
[*]dual-monitor story still broken after years (see [this bug]("https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/compiz/+bug/874146") and my comment at the bottom of it - no, it's not fixed)
[*]the network doesn't restore properly after you wake your laptop from standby - again, [years-old bug]("https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/network-manager/+bug/1585863"), with a ton of dupes
[/LIST]

### Post by Lambertus on 2017-09-18
Ubuntu and linux upgrades cause gut wrenching anxiety because they always caused days of work resolving complications (except 14.04, which I should have stuck with), and being unsure if your needed software is going to work with the latest version. I ended up with a cheat sheet of stuff I needed to do each time to get programs and systems to work.  This time going from 14.04 on a GTX board to 17.04 on an ASUS MSA99X motherboard caused a full week of work and still the printer and audio functions do not work. Ubuntu has this joke it plays when doing a fresh install. It asks if you want to keep your existing software installations. Never works. I feel like Charlie Brown landing on my back when Lucy pulls away the football. I fall for it ever time, thinking it will finally work. Ha!

Can't imagine how the developers feel, having to keep up with the endlessly changing and new variants of hardware and software, and feeling they need to have something new and attractive to keep people using the OS. That complexity would be way beyond the hassles I have.

### Post by kademlia on 2017-12-12
I don't like that I have to mark the whole downloads folder or the files manually as they are downloaded safe to execute. The good thing is a CH mod command to the folder will fix that and all new downloaded files will be mark with the permissions you set via chmod. sudo chmod ### ~/Downloads/ and ~/Downloads/* for all the current files. Replace the ### with how ever you want it setup ether its full access or just execute its up to you.

### Post by cmcanulty on 2017-12-21
go into your home/.config/user-dirs.dirs Make sure show hidden files is checked.
Here is mine which eliminates those, you can also do it system wide, I forget that location. I only have one user and this works fine for me. Just edit out the the names of the ones you don't want as shown below


```

# This file is written by xdg-user-dirs-update
# If you want to change or add directories, just edit the line you're
# interested in. All local changes will be retained on the next run
# Format is XDG_xxx_DIR="$HOME/yyy", where yyy is a shell-escaped
# homedir-relative path, or XDG_xxx_DIR="/yyy", where /yyy is an
# absolute path. No other format is supported.
# 
XDG_DESKTOP_DIR="$HOME/Desktop"
XDG_DOWNLOAD_DIR="$HOME/Documents"
XDG_TEMPLATES_DIR="$HOME/Documents"
XDG_PUBLICSHARE_DIR="$HOME/Documents"
XDG_DOCUMENTS_DIR="$HOME/Documents"
XDG_MUSIC_DIR="$HOME/Documents"
XDG_PICTURES_DIR="$HOME/Documents"
XDG_VIDEOS_DIR="$HOME/Documents"
```

### Post by dark-helmet-chris on 2018-01-28
I suppose I dislike Unity. In fairness, I tried it for almost a full month and frankly I just plain don't get it. If I were running a tablet then maybe. I happen to LIKE a bottom-screen-based task bar with a real menu with real categories. That's how I think. Thankfully, Xubuntu is fantastic. It's robust, stable, and makes me happy. I chose the XFCE version of Linux Mint just because it saves me time and effort, but there's no reason I couldn't use Xubuntu if I wanted.

I dislike Gnome3.. badly. But, I suppose that's not really something I can blame on Ubuntu. Can I? hehe. Nah.

I don't like the trend there seems to be in getting away from UI standards. I have a couple of friends with some mobility differences. Using Ubuntu just isn't an option because of how the interface forcibly relies on the mouse now. Keyboard shortcuts and standards are "out the window" (pun intended) in favor of "coolness" or "newness". So, what I really don't like is that standards are just disregarded instead of added on to. There's no reason why long-standing methods of doing things have to be discarded instead of added to. Of course, this is not only Ubuntu.. but it's here too.

---

### Post by dark-helmet-chris on 2018-01-28
> **uRock said:**
> Don't get me started on all of the hardware I have sitting around that is supported in Linux, yet unsupported in Windows 7 or 10.

OMG yes! I have suggested Ubuntu/Linux to many friends. Some of them have stuck with it. However, the ones who don't stick with it are usually the ones who say "this particular thing is very bad in Linux." That "particular thing" varies from person to person and hardware to hardware. One of my friends actually has about 3 different systems and each one has a specific compatibility problem - but that is just shockingly rare. His gear is just very odd. Another friend just couldn't get his inkjet printer to work. After a year, he went back to Windows because of this, instead of just replacing the $75 printer. But, that's a personal choice.

Of course, that was the other side of the coin. All the extra hardware I have laying around is all far more stable and supported with Linux than it was with whatever version of Windows it came with. Linux support has been SO GOOD that I actually can't remember the last time I picked up a driver disk. And I certainly don't miss that.

### Post by animousemusic on 2018-08-06
The overall use of Ubuntu is great. I have used Ubuntu and other Linux distros for more than 10 years. (First install was with a diskette, haha)
I went back to Win and Mac for the use of the professional software.
For the work that I do, I need to record a lot of music and edit videos. Although I tried this on Ubuntu (the last try was in 2010, with success I might add :) there where 3 things that really made me switch.
1. The configuration. To be more precise, the time it takes to set up everything the way it works. Yes, you can end up with a more than a stable system that works exactly the way you want, but the time it takes and the chance of errors is too much for me.
2. Some software is just not "that". I worked with Gimp, Audacity, Some KDE video editors, LMMS, Ardour, Hydrogen, and although they work, there was always something missing. Also, crashes did not make things better either.
3. Updates and possible reconfigurations/errors. It is great that Ubuntu works the way it does. It has a great community, a lot of devs working hard and a ton of professional features. As a day to day desktop system, this works really well!
But as a workstation, that has to be reliable, (almost) error free and as stable as possible when doing music or video production on a daily base, it wasn't working for me anymore. And I will skip all the driver issues I had with my old computer.

After the last update, 3 of my programs stopped working, I had to reconfigure Jack all over again and ended up reinstalling an older version and disabling the updates altogether.
Within a couple of months, I switched to my current Mac and never used Ubuntu again for music or video production. I might try it in the near feature, just to see how things have evolved.
I loved the concept of Ubuntu Studio. A really great system with lots of potential. When I will try Ubuntu again, I will go with the Studio edition and see if Ubuntu can win me back :)

Anyways, just wanted to share this with all of you :)

---

### Post by BlueAZ on 2018-09-06
I've loved using Ubuntu since 2011!  But after I installed 18.04.1, I was baffled by a screen with white arrows occasionally pointing upward.  I clicked on it with my mouse, but nothing happened.  I'd be embarrassed to admit how long it took me to get past that screen.  I went for a walk, came back & tried some more clicks.  Nothing.  Finally I thought to hit the Enter key.  Duh!  And was in. I guess the thing that irks me most is that it's just like Windows 10, which I have on another hard drive.  Same two screen process.  I thought it was Windows stupidity.  Never thought I'd find it in a version of Ubuntu!  Please change back to the former log in procedure!

### Post by Stan_Meissner on 2019-01-04
I first tried it around 2006 on an old computer and when XP support ended I decided to dive in head first.  I was first exposed to computers at work in the mid 80's and by 1989 worked with them as an end user at the railroad all day every day.  In my younger years I used to tinker with home built and/or modified computers so though I'm not a pro I'm far more advanced than the average 67 year old.  I use my desktop for digital photography, layered graphics and Audacity for the final tweaks on MP3 music recordings done on my Tascam 24 track.  I play guitars, bass and keys and use a drum machine to add drums and a voice processor to enhance my vocals.  All of these are fun hobbies as my working background was in accounting so the creativity is for fun.

I'm not a terminal expert but once I figure out how to configure things Ubuntu runs flawlessly until there's a hardware failure or I retire the computer.  I like it so much that I bought my wife a System 76 laptop and myself a Zareason desktop so I guess you could say that I'm all in on Ubuntu.

Now for the negative, and this really isn't a deal breaker but once in a while I'll research compatible hardware, make a purchase and end up struggling with some inexplicable little thing that ends up taking days to resolve.  Case in point, I bought a Logitech C270 webcam a couple months ago and set it aside until I finished a remodeling project.  Now that I'm done with that I finally had time to try out the webcam.  It works right out of the box except I don't get any sound through the speakers.  I get sound if I record a video in Cheese but when I plug it in and try it on one of the webcam test sites it doesn't come through the speakers.  I'm searching and posted a "webcam question" thread in the Hardware section.  The thing works and I can see the volume indicator moving when I speak but nothing out of the speakers so far.  I know it's a simple configuration issue and that the answer will be some simple setting when I figure out the problem but the time spent chasing my tail on something this simple is humbling and frustrating.

---

### Post by maestrobwh1 on 2019-01-08
It has been a while.  I started using Ubuntu since 6.06 Dapper stopped seriously using Ubuntu after 14.04 Trusty.  I had 14.04 on a net-top connected to my TV (asus eb1033 - a dual core atom based box wit nvidia ion) and it worked quite well.  I did not like the later releases.  Stuff just stopped working well with 16.04 - cup fan would rev up all the time, graphics sluggish.  It downright would not run on another machine where unity could not use hardware acceleration if the graphics card driver didn't have 3-d support in Ubuntu.  I think I wanted to like Unity, but it just seemed to sacrifice performance and utility for flair.

I got my hands on a Lenovo X1 Carbon with no OS and I installed 18.04.  I am very impressed with how refined it is running Gnome.  That being said, it "offers" things that don't work, or if they do, they aren't intuitive or reliable:

Google Drive Stream - initially, it worked albeit slowly, but now Gnome Files shows the mount, but just completely locks up now and I have to kill the file manager.  The drive shows mounted when i reopen it, but it simply just stopped working and if I navigate to the folder, Files just locks again and needs to be "killed."

OneDrive - I have no idea.  I signed in when I added my Microsoft account, it shows the file switch being on, but I have no idea where the files are showing.  A OneDrive folder never appeared in my home folder.  I also installed the package onedrive afterwards, but I assume if it were required, it would have installed, so I assume it is a separate package that works in another way?  I searched for half an hour and I cannot fine a definitive answer to either question.  One source just says "your OneDrive folder" like it just appears, and another source says it is read only anyway.  Whatever.

So what I don't like about Ubuntu is minor. I accept it is a polished OS once again that attempts to play nicely with proprietary things and it may or may not have easy to understand/find documentation on such things.  Digging for a lot of time, or just using the same browser to log in and drag and drop the handful of times I need to do it, fine... drag and drop into a browser it is then.

The hardware and the function of the OS are impeccable on this particular machine.  I just wish it would not offer services out of the box that look nice but are funky in dependability or utility.

OneDrive and GoogleDrive still work well via any browser, so these things are not deal breakers.

### Post by oygle on 2019-06-19
The most annoying thing for me in Ubuntu is that it gets slower and slower, and slower. This laptop is quite powerful, but booting takes much longer these days and applications are taking longer to load. Longer than when it was installed.

### Post by BlueAZ on 2019-06-19
> **oygle said:**
> The most annoying thing for me in Ubuntu is that it gets slower and slower, and slower. This laptop is quite powerful, but booting takes much longer these days and applications are taking longer to load. Longer than when it was installed.

This works for me:  Every time Ubuntu updates with a new kernel, some junk is left behind.  I open a Terminal and type:
[HTML]sudo apt autoremove[/HTML]
Then:  [HTML]sudo apt autoclean[/HTML]
Then:  [HTML]sudo apt upgrade[/HTML]

The first command should get rid of some bulky stuff.  The other 2 are just good housekeeping.
Hope it helps!

log out
account parameters

The other user has it's administrator button in white but is not allowed to use sudo as he is not in the sudoers file.
I still have a problem when  connecting to a samba share my name and password are not memorized
and I get asked for another unknown password related to a mysterious key ring thing.

I had to type my password more than 10 times just to write this answer :D

### Post by zerubbabel on 2019-07-15
> **alienprdkt said:**
> Ah! I think what you are looking for is a SOFTPHONE quick google search linux softphone will get you on the right track!

heres a few links 

[http://www.xs4all.nl/~mfnboer/twinkle/]("http://www.xs4all.nl/%7Emfnboer/twinkle/")

[http://voipguides.blogspot.com/2009/07/best-sip-softphones-for-voip-on-linux.html](http://voipguides.blogspot.com/2009/07/best-sip-softphones-for-voip-on-linux.html)

[http://www.go2linux.org/linux-softphones-review-best-free](http://www.go2linux.org/linux-softphones-review-best-free)

hope these help you out

Well, this is a rather old entry, but I was looking for a place to call attention to a wonderful discovery I made a few months ago in my year's-old search for a good softphone for Linux. I had tried Empathy, Ekiga, Twinkle, Jitsi, Linphone, and even paid for the "professional" version of Zoiper, but to my surprise, the softphone that I like the best is the free, but Windows-based, [MicroSIP]("https://www.microsip.org"). It runs perfectly under Wine, and is an active project, with new versions appearing every few months. It's user-interface is a bit dated, but then, I'm a bit old-fashioned, and function is more important to me than appearance. I have found this softphone to be rock solid, has excellent audio, the system-tray indicators are excellent, it imports contacts from a csv file... Highly recommended!

### Post by john_ladasky on 2019-07-20
I loved Ubuntu from 6.06 through 16.10.  Starting with 17.04, the desktop experience has been going downhill, and in 19.04 it became especially acute.  I don't exactly know what changed, because I spend more time thinking about my scientific application software -- but here are the things that are really bothering me as of right now:


1) I can no longer drag and drop from the desktop. 
2) I can cut and paste, but there's a LONG pause when the file is moved.
3) My $HOME folder has been given a permanent icon on the desktop that I don't want.
4) The text interface to dialog boxes in many applications (e.g., GIMP) and even system apps like Nautilus has messed up in a very specific way. When you click inside text, the system thinks that you have selected a location one character to the RIGHT of the position that your eye tells you that you should be selecting.
5) Finally: the Nautilus Save dialog box used by applications has an intermittent bug where you think you should be able to type the name of a file you want (the file name field at the top is in fact highlighted), but in fact you end up typing into a Search field which pops up underneath the Save field when you start typing. After three months of use, this “gotcha” is still getting me.


I'm thinking of checking out other distros to find -- a functional GUI. I never thought I would say that.

### Post by freonpsandoz on 2020-03-05
I'm tech-savvy, but I'm new to Ubuntu, and I'm trying to get an Ubuntu system set up for torrenting. Here are some of the problems I've encountered so far, and I'm nowhere near getting up and running. Most of them aren't really Ubuntu's fault; they just reflect the fact that most developers still don't fully support Linux, and their users are expected to be experienced in Linux.

1. 18.04 doesn't boot on my system. [https://askubuntu.com/questions/1029517/amd-rx480-screen-flicker-after-update-to-18-04/1032198#1032198](https://askubuntu.com/questions/1029517/amd-rx480-screen-flicker-after-update-to-18-04/1032198#1032198) proposes some possible fixes, but I don't know how to run terminal commands on a non-bootable system, so I gave up and installed 16.04.

2. My password manager, Keepass2, isn't fully supported. It's usable, but it can't lock after global inactivity and the scroll wheel doesn't work (I've found this to be a problem in several apps.)

3. My VPN, Surfshark, doesn't provide a GUI app or a kill switch on Ubuntu. Connection is done from the terminal by listing the servers and entering a number, like a 30-year-old DOS program. They give instructions to modify iptables to force apps to only use the VPN connection, but don't tell exactly when to run them or how to undo them.

4. Transmission-remote/daemon won't read .torrent files that work fine with uTorrent. The Transmission GUI app reads them, but provides no way to load a large number of existing torrent downloads (e.g. with a script.) The GUI app and the daemon seem to have separate torrent lists: a torrent loaded with the GUI app isn't listed by transmission-remote. No documentation seems to exist on whether the GUI app and the daemon are actually part of a unified package, or just two completely different apps with a common name.

5. I couldn't get RDP to work with the Ubuntu desktop. I installed xubuntu-desktop (XFCE?), but mouse clicks work only sometimes and the mouse/touchpad settings won't open, so I can't switch mouse buttons.

---

### Post by CelticWarrior on 2020-03-06
> **freonpsandoz said:**
> 1. 18.04 doesn't boot on my system. [https://askubuntu.com/questions/1029517/amd-rx480-screen-flicker-after-update-to-18-04/1032198#1032198](https://askubuntu.com/questions/1029517/amd-rx480-screen-flicker-after-update-to-18-04/1032198#1032198) proposes some possible fixes, but I don't know how to run terminal commands on a non-bootable system, so I gave up and installed 16.04.

No terminal commands are needed for testing the proposed solution. Just edit the Grub menu to temporarily add the additional kernel parameter and try booting. If it works then you should make it permanent by editing 
/etc/default/grub.


> 2. My password manager, Keepass2, isn't fully supported. It's usable, but it can't lock after global inactivity and the scroll wheel doesn't 
work (I've found this to be a problem in several apps.)

It may or may not be related to the same graphical issue the above tries to solve with the kernel parameter.

 
> 3. My VPN, Surfshark, doesn't provide a GUI app or a kill switch on  Ubuntu. Connection is done from the terminal by listing the servers and  entering a number, like a 30-year-old DOS program. They give  instructions to modify iptables to force apps to only use the VPN  connection, but don't tell exactly when to run them or how to undo them.

Windows still uses command line and I would argue now more than ever. Any tech-savy person should know that. VPNs, at least those that depend on OpenVPN can be configured in Gnome easily. Many providers opt for the command line method only because it works everywhere regardless of the desktop environment. In Linux you can have many different ones with different features and what works in one may not work on another whereas the commands are universal. It's very different from Windows where there's only one desktop environment which can0't be uncoupled from kernel. Linux, as you surely know by now, can run without any DE, the default in servers.


> 4. Transmission-remote/daemon won't read .torrent files that work fine  with uTorrent. The Transmission GUI app reads them, but provides no way  to load a large number of existing torrent downloads (e.g. with a  script.) The GUI app and the daemon seem to have separate torrent lists:  a torrent loaded with the GUI app isn't listed by transmission-remote.  No documentation seems to exist on whether the GUI app and the daemon  are actually part of a unified package, or just two completely different  apps with a common name. 

Don't know about scripts but I know they don't have separated torrents lists. Provided the remote is properly configured - very likely it isn't - and also the GUI app that needs to have remote access enabled with the required settings for the intended use. If everything is correctly setup the remote app will show the list of torrents and any torrent file can be uploaded to Transmission from the remote app. There should be no difference in what you see in one or another.
Also the GUI is just that, a GUI. The actual Transmission is the headless daemon, the GUI isn't required (and couldn't work in headless systems (e.g. servers).


> 5. I couldn't get RDP to work with the Ubuntu desktop. I installed  xubuntu-desktop (XFCE?), but mouse clicks work only sometimes and the  mouse/touchpad settings won't open, so I can't switch mouse buttons. 

Can't really help you that, not sure what are talking about. But considering the previous points my educated guess is that you're doing something wrong -or- that is also a side effect of the graphical issues that would supposedly be solved by the kernel parameter mentioned in the AU answer you linked.

### Post by deadflowr on 2021-04-15
Ubuntu's kernel follows a 3 week cadence for stable release updates for non-security related kernel upgrades.
Ubuntu never upgrades to newer kernel base versions though.
(ie, Ubuntu will update from something like  5.4.0-71 to 5.4.0-72 within at least 3 weeks,
but it'll never upgrade from the 5.4 base to 5.5 or newer.
Exceptions being for the hwe stacks, which move up until they reach the next LTS' kernel version then they stay at that for the remainder of the Ubuntu releases support)
General info here: [https://wiki.ubuntu.com/Kernel/StableReleaseCadence]("https://wiki.ubuntu.com/Kernel/StableReleaseCadence")

### Post by proppo001 on 2022-05-22
I am not an IT person but I've used Ubuntu and a couple other flavors for 20+ years. I've spent days at a time looking at a terminal trying to get something or other to work. I am not a neophyte but after all that time and expectations that things would get easier, it hasn't. In all those years, I have NEVER had reliable Printing. It may work for awhile until it doesn't for completely unknown reasons. Currently I have a $500 HP printer that Ubuntu refuses to download drivers for because... (insert a myriad of errors).  Asking for help in the forums is going to be far beyond the scope of the average computer user, I myself don't ask because I couldn't know how to "properly" parse the the details, bringing instant derision and impatience from the forum. For years, every printer I've had, Samsung, Cannon (not sure it ever worked) and HP has ultimately needed to be wired direct which, may or may not work (currently does not) - or just resort to Windows. This is the only reason I cannot live without Windows. Sad. 

PRINTING - networked or not - can they ever get it right???

### Post by #&amp;thj^% on 2022-05-28
I'm not a Hater, just points of pain. To Be Clear.
I don't like Gnome 3/4 but I have choices for that.

I don't like Snaps, my choices are dwindling now. :(

I don't like like Apt hijacking to install Snaps >> instead I hunt for .debs.

I don't like that the installer doesn't offer easy options for encryption, BTRFS with subvolumes, snapshots, compression and checksums.

[B]The only thing *really wrong with Ubuntu is the over-reliance of Snaps.
[/B]
Ubuntu server at home (toy install)
Production server Arch BSD,  Only apache2 servers

 I just write it off as Canonical just being Canonical, so... /shrug.
And for me it's a thing about personal choice more than a distro being good or bad.

Arch is pretty much a kernel and a package manger at it's core (at a high level overview), so you're basically building a custom system.
Not to mention that the Arch documentation is VERY extensive.

I think some more "mainstream" distro's have those problems because they're designed to work for everyone, while an Arch is built by you, for you.
**_It is very much a DIY distro._**

### Post by Tadaen_Sylvermane on 2023-09-27
I enjoy Gnome but along with above I dislike that not everything is consistent. But this is not limited to Ubuntu. The problem is on Debian as well as any other distro I imagine. I can't understand why there can't be a single gui toolkit that can inherit whatever theme is on the system or being used without looking like a cheap hack. The power and choice of linux leads to this situation though so it can't be helped. I dislike the snap concept in general. It's flawed. The flaw is that it gives up one of the biggest strengths of *nix stuff... shared libraries. Without that benefit while I'm no fan of Microsoft I have trouble seeing why one should deal with the limitations (depending on what you do with it) of linux rather than just accept Windows as is. That seems to be the direction, at least at the moment. It does not bode well as far as I am concerned. Why bend over backwards to make something work when I can get a more polished system with far more available software. Without shared libraries it's just not worth the effort for me. Outside of my server at least...

### Post by TheFu on 2023-09-27
Do you use MS-Windows?  Whenever I'm forced to use it, it is so very frustrating. Nothing works right. Programs crash without any warning.  And I can never find the right program to accomplish what I need.  All the MS-Windows programs seem to suffer from "kitchen sink" bloat, meaning they feel some need to add yet another "feature" because someone got a wild hair somewhere that itched.  

I much prefer the Unix way of having small, do one-thing well, tools.

I'm not 100% anti-snap anymore like I was.  The key line for me is whether the snap is the only way do run a specific program or just another option.  I like options, not mandates.  I suppose using Ubuntu isn't a mandate, which is why I've moved my desktop off Ubuntu to a different distro.  

I much prefer AppImages over snaps and flatpaks. This is because with an AppImage, I can add constraints that I control, not have constraints I don't want/need inflicted on me like snaps do.  I've avoided Flatpaks, but my understanding is they allow local control over the constraints AND are only for end-user, desktop, applications.  Canonical trying to make snaps THE answer for all programs, server and desktop is a mistake for their normal desktop.  

I can appreciate someone who wants an OS they don't need to maintain and doesn't need control over where things are located or placed ... you know - like a cell phone user without a 2nd microSD card.  

Don't get me started about how google screwed android when they prevented apps from being setup to handle file types located anywhere on the file system that user has access.  Google is their own worst enemy.  I'm still trying to figure out how to get FBreader to open an epub file that is managed by nextcloud apps on my phone/tablet.  It shouldn't be that hard. I wasted over 30 minutes trying to solve this yesterday and failed.

But I digress, which is normal for me. ;)

### Post by Tadaen_Sylvermane on 2023-09-27
I agree. I prefer the *nix way of course. And I've learned the strength of the system and quite well, maybe not perfectly to mold it to my desires. But at the end of the day it takes more work and I'm just flat lazy. It's hard to justify working twice as hard for the same result. But again that is down the road. Snaps haven't taken over and they certainly aren't becoming main stream. But while I appreciate the purpose of universal packages they are a major threat to as I said before to possibly the *nix stuffs greatest strength in regards to security, efficiency, everything really. If I'm going to end up with a system with holes damn near everywhere why not take the easy road. Laziness will prevail in my case... lol

Windows does irritate me. But laziness == me. And I don't rely on my computer for work anymore so... it's little more than a choice at this point. Not a requirement either way. Flatpak bottles works nice but I spend a fair amount of time gaming still. Why not do it native if there are so few other benefits, in my case anyway.
